name: ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT

topics.title: Fog Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fog Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Fog Computing — Foundational Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECIT746, Module 1):**
> Fog Computing forms the *middle-tier architectural pillar* of any modern IoT stack. The syllabus mandates an understanding of how fog nodes bridge resource-constrained edge devices with latency-intolerant cloud services, especially under **energy-harvesting** and **battery-aware** constraints.

---

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Board-Examiner Approved)

**Fog Computing** is a *horizontally scaled, geographically distributed computing paradigm* that extends cloud-like services — **computation, storage, networking, and analytics** — to the **network edge**, situated *between* end IoT devices and centralized cloud data centers.

It was formally coined by **Cisco Systems in 2012**, conceptualized to overcome the inherent limitations of pure cloud-centric IoT models — namely high latency, bandwidth bottlenecks, and lack of real-time responsiveness.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board-Ready Definition (Verbatim for 3-Mark Answers):**
> *"Fog Computing is a decentralized computing infrastructure in which computing, storage, and networking resources are distributed across the continuum from the cloud down to the edge of the network, enabling location-aware, low-latency, real-time processing for IoT applications."*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Toll Booth vs. The Local Supermarket" 🍎

Imagine you are driving across a long highway:

- **Cloud Computing** = A single, massive **grocery warehouse located 500 km away**. Every time you need even a single apple, you drive all the way to the warehouse and back. Slow, expensive (fuel), and wasteful for small needs.
- **Edge Computing** = A **fruit stand right at your car window**. Extremely close, but tiny — can only hold a few items.
- **Fog Computing** = A **local supermarket on a highway exit ramp, just 5 km away**. It stocks most of what you need, processes transactions instantly, and only forwards very specialized bulk orders to the main warehouse.

The fog layer is *not* on the device (that's edge/mist) and *not* in the distant cloud. It sits in the **middle**, intelligently deciding what to process locally and what to push upward.

---

## 1.3 Core Pillars of Fog Computing (Must-Know Mnemonic: **"LHR-PEAS"**)

| Mnemonic Letter | Pillar | Engineering Significance |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **L** | **Low Latency** | Critical for control loops (e.g., vehicular braking, robotics) |
| **H** | **Heterogeneity** | Supports diverse hardware, OS, and protocols |
| **R** | **Real-time Processing** | Hard/soft deadlines for industrial IoT |
| **P** | **Pervasiveness / Proximity** | Located *one hop* from data source |
| **E** | **Energy Awareness** | Aligns perfectly with **Energy Harvesting & Power Management** (PECIT746 focus) |
| **A** | **Autonomy & Awareness** | Operates independently when cloud connectivity is lost |
| **S** | **Scalability** | Horizontally scaled across thousands of geo-distributed nodes |

---

## 1.4 Why Fog Computing Matters for Energy-Harvested IoT ⚡

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PECIT746 — Direct Context:**
> In battery-less or energy-harvesting IoT devices (solar, RF, vibration), the **most energy-expensive operation is wireless transmission**, not computation. Fog computing dramatically reduces transmission distances, enabling devices to operate on harvested energy alone.

### Key Energy-Saving Mechanisms:
1. **Local Data Aggregation** → Reduces packets transmitted → **Less RF energy consumed**.
2. **Adaptive Duty Cycling** at fog level → Allows edge sensors to remain in deep-sleep states longer.
3. **Intelligent Offloading** → Heavy computation is offloaded to fog nodes only when device's energy budget permits.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

---

## 2.1 The Three-Tier IoT–Fog–Cloud Architecture

The architectural hierarchy of a fog-enabled IoT system is canonically described as a **3-tier vertical stack**:

### **Tier 1 — Edge / IoT Device Layer (Bottom)**
- **Constituents:** Sensors, actuators, microcontrollers (e.g., MSP430, STM32L0), energy harvesters.
- **Functions:** Data acquisition, low-level preprocessing, actuation.
- **Energy profile:** Extremely constrained; operates on harvested energy.

### **Tier 2 — Fog Layer (Middle)**
- **Constituents:** Fog nodes — gateways, routers, embedded servers, Raspberry Pi-class devices, industrial PLCs.
- **Functions:**
  * Stream processing
  * Local analytics
  * Filtering, aggregation, anomaly detection
  * Security enforcement
  * Energy-policy orchestration
- **Energy profile:** Mains- or battery-powered; can manage sub-nodes.

### **Tier 3 — Cloud Layer (Top)**
- **Constituents:** Hyperscale data centers.
- **Functions:** Big-data analytics, ML training, archival storage, fleet management.
- **Energy profile:** Energy-abundant but geographically distant.

> [!NOTE]
> **OpenFog Consortium Reference Architecture (IEEE 1934™):** The 8 pillars of the OpenFog Reference Architecture include security, scalability, openness, autonomy, RAS (reliability, availability, serviceability), agility, hierarchy, and programmability. KTU examiners frequently reference the **hierarchical** pillar.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **must-remember** for Part B (14-mark) analytical questions on Fog Computing in PECIT746.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables Explained | Units | Engineering Insight |
|:-:|:---|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | Cloud Round-Trip Latency | $L_{C} = 2 \cdot (t_{proc,cloud} + t_{prop,wan} + t_{trans,wan})$ | $t_{proc}$= processing time, $t_{prop}$= propagation, $t_{trans}$= transmission | seconds (s) | WAN (Wide Area Network) hops add tens of milliseconds |
| 2 | Fog Round-Trip Latency | $L_{F} = 2 \cdot (t_{proc,fog} + t_{prop,lan} + t_{trans,lan})$ | LAN (Local Area Network) hops only | seconds (s) | LAN propagation ≈ $5 \mu s/km$; typically < 5 ms total |
| 3 | Latency Reduction Ratio | $\eta_{L} = \dfrac{L_{C} - L_{F}}{L_{C}} \cdot 100\%$ | Expresses % latency saved | dimensionless (%) | Often 70–95% in industrial deployments |
| 4 | Transmission Energy (Friis Model) | $E_{tx}(d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{\alpha}$ | $k$=bits, $d$=distance, $\alpha$=path-loss exponent (2–4) | Joules (J) | Energy grows steeply with distance |
| 5 | Bandwidth Saving Ratio | $\eta_{BW} = 1 - \dfrac{B_{fog \rightarrow cloud}}{B_{raw}}$ | $B_{raw}$=raw data rate, $B_{out}$=filtered upstream rate | dimensionless | Aggregation can achieve $\eta_{BW} > 0.95$ |
| 6 | Duty Cycle Energy | $E_{DC} = P_{active} \cdot t_{on} + P_{sleep} \cdot (T - t_{on})$ | $T$=period, $t_{on}$=active interval, $P$=power | Joules (J) | Drives energy-harvesting feasibility |
| 7 | Fog Node Throughput | $\Theta = \dfrac{N_{tasks}}{T_{window}}$ | $N_{tasks}$=tasks completed, $T_{window}$=time window | tasks/s | Used for QoS guarantees |
| 8 | Energy Harvesting Constraint | $P_{harvest,avg} \geq \overline{P}_{consumption}$ | Average harvested power must meet average demand | Watts (W) | Defines feasibility of battery-less design |

---

## 2.3 Fog vs. Cloud vs. Edge — Examiner's Quick Reference Table

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students frequently use "Fog" and "Edge" interchangeably. In KTU valuation, this costs **2 marks minimum**. Edge is **on the device**; Fog is the **intermediate layer**.

| Parameter | Cloud Computing | Edge Computing | Fog Computing |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Location** | Centralized (datacenter) | On the device / sensor | Network edge (1-hop from device) |
| **Latency** | High (50–500 ms) | Ultra-low (< 1 ms) | Low (1–10 ms) |
| **Compute Capacity** | Virtually unlimited | Severely limited | Moderate to high |
| **Storage** | Petabytes | KB–MB | GB–TB |
| **Power Source** | Grid (massive) | Battery / harvested | Grid / battery |
| **Role in IoT** | Heavy analytics, ML training | Sensor pre-processing | Stream analytics, decision gateway |
| **Initiated by** | Industry (AWS, Azure) | Device vendors | **Cisco (2012)** + OpenFog Consortium |

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Fog computing is **not academic theory** — it actively drives:

- 🚗 **Autonomous Vehicles** — sub-10 ms decisions on collision avoidance.
- 🏥 **Remote Surgery & Telehealth** — latency-critical vital-sign processing.
- 🏭 **Industry 4.0** — fog nodes inside factory PLCs orchestrate robotic arms.
- 🌾 **Smart Agriculture** — fog gateways aggregate data from soil/weather sensors in off-grid farms.
- 🔌 **Smart Grid** — fog nodes perform local fault detection in microgrids.
- 🌱 **Energy-Harvested IoT (this course focus)** — enables sustainable, battery-less deployments.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

---

## 3.1 Derivation I — Latency Reduction Ratio $\eta_{L}$

> **Goal:** Quantify how much latency is reduced when a fog node is introduced between an IoT device and the cloud.

### Given Setup

- **Cloud path:** IoT Device $\rightarrow$ Gateway $\rightarrow$ WAN (Internet) $\rightarrow$ Cloud Server $\rightarrow$ back
- **Fog path:** IoT Device $\rightarrow$ Fog Node (1-hop LAN) $\rightarrow$ back

### Step 1 — Write the Cloud Round-Trip Latency

Each leg contributes a delay component. Summing both directions (uplink + downlink):

$$L_{C} = 2 \cdot (t_{proc,cloud} + t_{prop,wan} + t_{trans,wan})$$

**Explanation of terms:**
- $t_{proc,cloud}$ = Time the cloud server spends processing the request (typically 5–50 ms).
- $t_{prop,wan}$ = WAN propagation delay, dominated by long-distance fibre/terrestrial links.
- $t_{trans,wan}$ = WAN transmission delay (queues, congestion windows).

### Step 2 — Write the Fog Round-Trip Latency

$$L_{F} = 2 \cdot (t_{proc,fog} + t_{prop,lan} + t_{trans,lan})$$

**Why smaller?** LAN propagation = $\dfrac{d_{lan}}{v_{signal}}$ where $v_{signal} \approx 2 \cdot 10^{8} \, m/s$ in fibre. For $d_{lan} = 100 \, m$, $t_{prop,lan} \approx 0.5 \, \mu s$.

### Step 3 — Define the Latency Reduction Ratio

$$\eta_{L} = \dfrac{L_{C} - L_{F}}{L_{C}} \cdot 100\%$$

### Step 4 — Substitute

$$\eta_{L} = \dfrac{2(t_{proc,cloud} + t_{prop,wan} + t_{trans,wan}) - 2(t_{proc,fog} + t_{prop,lan} + t_{trans,lan})}{2(t_{proc,cloud} + t_{prop,wan} + t_{trans,wan})} \cdot 100\%$$

### Step 5 — Simplify

$$\eta_{L} = \left[ 1 - \dfrac{t_{proc,fog} + t_{prop,lan} + t_{trans,lan}}{t_{proc,cloud} + t_{prop,wan} + t_{trans,wan}} \right] \cdot 100\%$$

### Step 6 — Numerical Illustration

Assume:
- $t_{proc,cloud} = 30 \, ms$, $t_{prop,wan} = 40 \, ms$, $t_{trans,wan} = 20 \, ms$ → $L_{C} = 2 \cdot 90 = 180 \, ms$
- $t_{proc,fog} = 5 \, ms$, $t_{prop,lan} = 0.1 \, ms$, $t_{trans,lan} = 0.4 \, ms$ → $L_{F} = 2 \cdot 5.5 = 11 \, ms$

$$\eta_{L} = \dfrac{180 - 11}{180} \cdot 100\% \approx 93.9\%$$

> **Conclusion:** A fog layer can eliminate > 90% of round-trip latency for many IoT workloads — the single most cited quantitative justification for fog computing in KTU exam answers.

---

## 3.2 Derivation II — Energy Savings via Fog Aggregation

> **Goal:** Prove that fog nodes reduce total system energy, especially relevant for **energy-harvested IoT devices (PECIT746 focus)**.

### Step 1 — Energy Model for Pure Cloud Path

Each sensor reading $k$ bits must be transmitted all the way to the cloud:

$$E_{cloud}(k, d_{cloud}) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d_{cloud}^{\alpha}$$

where the **Friis-derived** energy model (Heinzelman et al.) gives:
- $E_{elec}$ = Energy/bit for electronic circuitry (≈ 50 nJ/bit)
- $\varepsilon_{amp}$ = Amplifier energy (≈ 100 pJ/bit/m² for free space, $\alpha = 2$)
- $d_{cloud}$ = Distance to cloud gateway (often 10–100 km logical)

### Step 2 — Energy Model for Fog Path

With local aggregation at a fog node, only a *fraction* $\beta$ of raw data is forwarded upward:

$$E_{fog}(k, d_{fog}) = \left[ E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d_{fog}^{\alpha} \right] + \beta \cdot E_{cloud}(k_{agg}, d_{cloud})$$

where $k_{agg} = \gamma \cdot k$ is the aggregated packet size and $\beta \in [0.01, 0.1]$ for typical scenarios.

### Step 3 — Define Energy Saved Per Reading

$$\Delta E = E_{cloud} - E_{fog}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and Approximate

Assume $d_{cloud} \gg d_{fog}$ and $\beta = 0.05$, $\gamma = 0.2$:

$$\Delta E = \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot (d_{cloud}^{\alpha} - d_{fog}^{\alpha}) - 0.05 \cdot \left[ E_{elec} \cdot 0.2k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot 0.2k \cdot d_{cloud}^{\alpha} \right]$$

### Step 5 — Numerical Plug-in

Let $k = 1000$ bits, $\alpha = 2$, $d_{fog} = 50 \, m$, $d_{cloud} = 10 \, km = 10^{4} \, m$:

$$\Delta E = 100 \cdot 10^{-12} \cdot 1000 \cdot \left[ (10^{4})^{2} - (50)^{2} \right] - 0.05 \cdot \left[ 50 \cdot 10^{-9} \cdot 200 + 100 \cdot 10^{-12} \cdot 200 \cdot 10^{8} \right]$$

$$\Delta E = 10^{-7} \cdot (10^{8} - 2500) - 0.05 \cdot (10^{-5} + 2 \cdot 10^{-3})$$

$$\Delta E \approx 10^{-7} \cdot 10^{8} - 10^{-4} \approx 10 - 10^{-4} \approx 9.9999 \, J$$

> **Conclusion:** The energy saved per reading is **~10 J** — orders of magnitude larger than typical sensing energy (~μJ). This validates fog aggregation as a **critical enabler of battery-less, energy-harvested IoT**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — "Where to Process?"

> A KTU 14-mark favorite: *Justify where a given task should be executed — device, fog, or cloud?*

| Decision Criterion | Edge Device | Fog Node | Cloud |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Latency tolerance < 10 ms** | ✅ Best | ✅ Acceptable | ❌ Too slow |
| **Privacy / Locality-sensitive** | ✅ Best | ✅ Good | ❌ Risky |
| **Compute-heavy ML training** | ❌ Impossible | ❌ Inefficient | ✅ Best |
| **Data archival** | ❌ Impossible | ⚠️ Short-term | ✅ Best |
| **Bandwidth conservation** | ✅ Generates | ✅ Aggregates | ❌ Wants raw |
| **Energy-harvesting feasibility** | ✅ Local decision | ✅ Orchestration | ✅ Offload-only |

**Rule of Thumb:** $\text{Fog} \iff \text{Real-time + Bandwidth-Critical + Not Privacy-Sensitive}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics

---

## 4.1 Hierarchical Fog Computing Architecture (3-Tier)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph cloudTier["TIER 3: CLOUD LAYER"]
        cloudNode1["CLOUD DATA CENTER A"]
        cloudNode2["CLOUD DATA CENTER B"]
        cloudNode3["ANALYTICS AND ML ENGINE"]
    end

    subgraph fogTier["TIER 2: FOG LAYER"]
        fogNodeA["FOG NODE 1: GATEWAY"]
        fogNodeB["FOG NODE 2: EDGE SERVER"]
        fogNodeC["FOG NODE 3: INDUSTRIAL PLC"]
        fogCoord["FOG ORCHESTRATOR"]
    end

    subgraph edgeTier["TIER 1: EDGE / DEVICE LAYER"]
        device1["SENSOR NODE A: SOLAR HARVESTED"]
        device2["SENSOR NODE B: RF HARVESTED"]
        device3["ACTUATOR: MOTOR VALVE"]
        device4["WEARABLE: HEALTH MONITOR"]
    end

    cloudNode1 -.downstream policy.-> fogCoord
    cloudNode2 -.downstream policy.-> fogCoord
    cloudNode3 <--> fogCoord

    fogCoord --> fogNodeA
    fogCoord --> fogNodeB
    fogCoord --> fogNodeC

    fogNodeA --- device1
    fogNodeA --- device2
    fogNodeB --- device3
    fogNodeC --- device4

    device1 ==uplink data==> fogNodeA
    device2 ==uplink data==> fogNodeA
    device3 ==control signals==> fogNodeB
    device4 ==vitals stream==> fogNodeC
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - *Solid arrows* = orchestration / control policies.
> - *Double-line arrows* = data plane (uplink sensor readings).
> - *Dotted arrows* = cloud → fog policy distribution.
> - The **Fog Orchestrator** is the master coordinator, typically virtualized.

---

## 4.2 Data-Plane Flow — From Sensor to Cloud with Fog Decision Point

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s["SENSOR READING"] --> a["LOCAL PRE-PROCESSING"]
    a --> q{"AGGR OR ALERT?"}
    q -- YES --> n["NORMAL AGGREGATION"]
    q -- NO --> p["PRIORITY PACKET"]
    q -- EMERGENCY --> e["EMERGENCY ALERT TO FOG"]

    n --> f["FOG NODE BUFFER"]
    p --> f
    e --> f

    f --> c{"CLOUD NEEDED?"}
    c -- YES, BATCH --> up["UPLINK TO CLOUD"]
    c -- NO --> d["DROP OR STORE LOCALLY"]

    up --> ml["CLOUD ML PIPELINE"]
    ml --> pol["POLICY UPDATE TO FOG"]
    pol -.feedback.-> f
```

> **Engineering Insight:** This flow shows the *decision gate* at the fog layer — only **0.5–5%** of raw data typically reaches the cloud after fog aggregation.

---

## 4.3 Energy & Latency Trade-off Surface

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    start["TASK INCOMING AT IOT DEVICE"]

    start --> cost{"ENERGY AND LATENCY BUDGET?"}

    cost -- BOTH LOW --> local["EXECUTE LOCALLY ON EDGE"]
    cost -- ENERGY LOW LATENCY CRITICAL --> fog["OFFLOAD TO FOG NODE"]
    cost -- ENERGY LOW LATENCY FLEXIBLE --> cloud["OFFLOAD TO CLOUD"]
    cost -- ENERGY HIGH --> wait["DEFER TILL HARVESTER CHARGES BUFFER"]
    cost -- URGENT LATENCY --> wake["WAKE FOG NODE IMMEDIATELY"]

    local --> out1["DONE"]
    fog --> out2["DONE"]
    cloud --> out3["DONE WITH DELAY"]
    wait --> out4["DONE WHEN ENERGY AVAILABLE"]
    wake --> out5["DONE WITH PRIORITY"]
```

> **PECIT746 Context:** This is the **canonical energy-aware task scheduler** for harvested IoT — fog computing acts as the *green offload target*.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 🎯 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]** Define Fog Computing. List any four of its defining characteristics.

**Model Answer (Marks Distribution):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** Fog Computing is a horizontally distributed computing paradigm that extends cloud-like services (compute, storage, networking) to the network edge, situated between IoT devices and centralized cloud data centers, thereby enabling low-latency, location-aware, real-time processing.
- **Any 4 characteristics (1 mark for listing):**
  1. Low latency
  2. Location awareness
  3. Real-time processing capability
  4. Heterogeneity support
  5. Mobility support
  6. Predominant role of wireless access
  7. Energy awareness

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** Differentiate between Fog Computing and Edge Computing in any three aspects.

**Model Answer (3 × 1 = 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Edge Computing | Fog Computing |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Location** | On the IoT device itself | Between device and cloud (gateway, switch, server) |
| **Compute Power** | Very limited (μC / SoC) | Moderate to high (gateway-class hardware) |
| **Scope** | Single device's data | Aggregated data from multiple devices |

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** *Internal choice between Question A and Question B.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Q. (a)** Describe the three-tier architecture of Fog Computing with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of each tier. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The fog computing architecture, as standardized by the OpenFog Consortium (IEEE 1934), is typically a **three-tier hierarchical stack** comprising Edge (Device) Layer, Fog Layer, and Cloud Layer.

**Tier 1 — Edge/Device Layer (2 marks):**
- Comprises resource-constrained endpoints — sensors, actuators, microcontrollers, energy harvesters.
- Functions: data acquisition, basic signal conditioning, actuation.
- Operates on harvested or battery power; extremely energy-constrained.

**Tier 2 — Fog Layer (2 marks):**
- Comprises intermediate nodes — gateways, routers, embedded servers, industrial controllers.
- Functions: stream analytics, data aggregation, filtering, security policy enforcement, energy orchestration.
- Sits 1-hop from devices; provides low-latency decisioning.

**Tier 3 — Cloud Layer (2 marks):**
- Comprises hyperscale data centers.
- Functions: long-term storage, big-data analytics, ML training, fleet-level orchestration.
- Provides heavy compute and global coordination.

*(Refer to Section 4.1 diagram for illustration.)*

---

**(b)** With a suitable example, explain how Fog Computing improves latency and energy efficiency in an IoT-based Smart Agriculture system. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Scenario (1 mark):** A smart agriculture deployment has 100 soil-moisture sensors distributed across a 1 km² farm, each powered by a miniature solar energy harvester.

**Latency Improvement (3 marks):**
- **Without Fog:** Every soil reading (1 KB) is transmitted over a cellular link (~10 km to cloud). Round-trip latency ≈ 180 ms.
- **With Fog:** A fog gateway placed at the farm center aggregates readings over Wi-Fi/LoRa (≤ 1 km). Round-trip latency to fog ≈ 11 ms.
- **Latency reduction:** $\eta_{L} \approx 93.9\%$ (as derived in Section 3.1).
- *Practical benefit:* Real-time irrigation valves can be triggered within tens of milliseconds.

**Energy Efficiency (3 marks):**
- **Without Fog:** Each sensor transmits 1 KB raw data every minute. Energy per transmission ≈ $\varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{\alpha} = 100 \, pJ \cdot 1000 \cdot (10^{4})^{2} = 10 \, J$. This is **prohibitive** for a 1 cm² solar cell (~10 mW average).
- **With Fog:** Local LoRa transmission over 1 km consumes ~$0.1 \, J$ per packet; only $5\%$ of aggregated data is forwarded upstream.
- **Net savings:** ~99.5% energy reduction per sensor, enabling continuous battery-less operation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:**
> Students often **omit the numerical energy calculation**, which is the core 14-mark differentiator. Always anchor fog benefits to **quantified numbers** — examiners reward "hard math" over vague prose.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**

**Q. (a)** Explain the OpenFog Consortium's eight pillars of fog computing architecture. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

The OpenFog Consortium (merged with Industrial Internet Consortium in 2019) defined **8 architectural pillars** as a design benchmark for fog systems (IEEE 1934-2018):

1. **Security (1 mark):** End-to-end encryption, attestation, secure boot across all fog nodes.
2. **Scalability (1 mark):** Horizontal and vertical scaling across geographically distributed nodes.
3. **Openness (1 mark):** Interoperability through open standards (no vendor lock-in).
4. **Autonomy (1 mark):** Fog nodes operate independently during cloud disconnection.
5. **RAS — Reliability, Availability, Serviceability (1 mark):** Fault-tolerance and hot-swap capability.
6. **Agility (1 mark):** Rapid redeployment of services via containerization (Docker, Kubernetes).
7. **Hierarchy (1 mark):** Multi-level fog (e.g., micro-fog at device → metro-fog at city → cloud) for cascading compute.
8. **Programmability (Bonus mark):** Multi-language, multi-OS support via abstraction layers.

---

**(b)** Discuss the role of Fog Computing in enabling energy-harvested IoT devices. Use appropriate equations. **[7 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

**Context (1 mark):**
Energy-harvested IoT devices operate under the strict feasibility constraint $P_{harvest,avg} \geq \overline{P}_{consumption}$. Fog computing acts as the *enabler* by offloading expensive tasks from energy-poor devices.

**Mechanism 1 — Reduced Transmission Energy (2 marks):**
Using the Friis model:
$$E_{tx}(d) = E_{elec} \cdot k + \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{\alpha}$$
Fog proximity reduces $d$ from kilometres (cellular) to tens of metres (Wi-Fi/LoRa), slashing $d^{\alpha}$ by **6–8 orders of magnitude** for $\alpha = 2$.

**Mechanism 2 — Duty-Cycle Extension (2 marks):**
$$E_{DC} = P_{active} \cdot t_{on} + P_{sleep} \cdot (T - t_{on})$$
By aggregating data at the fog, devices can extend $t_{on}$ to minutes instead of seconds, increasing the sleep fraction and reducing $\overline{P}_{consumption}$.

**Mechanism 3 — Adaptive Offloading (2 marks):**
A fog node can buffer data when harvested energy is low and forward it only when the device's supercapacitor voltage exceeds a threshold:
$$V_{cap}(t) > V_{threshold} \implies \text{Uplink permitted}$$
This forms the **closed-loop energy-aware scheduler** — a board-favorite concept.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common 14-mark Trap:**
> Students forget to **link fog computing back to PECIT746's energy-harvesting core**. Always explicitly state *how* the fog layer solves a power-management problem — examiners expect this dual-domain integration.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print-this-Before-Walking-Into-the-Exam-Hall** Checklist for Fog Computing (Module 1, PECIT746):

- ✅ **Fog = Middle layer** (NOT edge, NOT cloud). Cloud is *above*, Edge is *below*.
- ✅ **Origin:** Coined by **Cisco in 2012**; standardized by **OpenFog Consortium → IEEE 1934**.
- ✅ **Three tiers:** Edge / Fog / Cloud — remember "EFC" bottom-to-top.
- ✅ **Latency reduction** can reach **~90%+** in real deployments (LAN vs WAN).
- ✅ **Fog saves energy** by exploiting $\varepsilon_{amp} \cdot k \cdot d^{\alpha}$ — distance squared/cubed dependency.
- ✅ **8 OpenFog Pillars:** Security, Scalability, Openness, Autonomy, RAS, Agility, Hierarchy, Programmability.
- ✅ **Fog ≠ Edge:** Edge = on-device; Fog = intermediate infrastructure.
- ✅ **Energy-harvesting constraint:** $P_{harvest,avg} \geq \overline{P}_{consumption}$.
- ✅ **Duty-cycle formula:** $E_{DC} = P_{active} t_{on} + P_{sleep}(T - t_{on})$ — memorize verbatim.
- ✅ **Real-world exam scenarios:** Smart Agriculture, Industry 4.0, Smart Grid, Autonomous Vehicles, Remote Health.
- ✅ **Always quantify benefits:** Numbers like 93.9% latency reduction, 99.5% energy saving win 14-mark questions.
- ✅ **Use the LHR-PEAS mnemonic** for characteristics if asked to "list features."

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Definition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Definition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Energy Harvesting? — The Formal KTU Definition

**Energy Harvesting (EH)**, also widely referred to as *Energy Scavenging*, *Ambient Energy Capture*, or *Power Harvesting*, is formally defined as the process by which energy is derived from **external ambient sources** (such as solar radiation, thermal gradients, mechanical vibrations, RF signals, or fluid flow), captured, and stored for small, autonomous, wireless devices—particularly those operating within the **Internet of Things (IoT)** ecosystem.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECIT746 - Module 1):**
> Energy harvesting is the process of capturing minute amounts of energy from one or more naturally occurring or ambient sources, accumulating and storing it for later use, typically to power low-energy electronic devices and sensors that would otherwise require grid power or battery replacement.

The energy quantities involved are **extremely small**—often in the range of:
- **µW (microwatts)** for indoor light or vibration harvesting
- **mW (milliwatts)** for outdoor solar or thermal gradient harvesting
- Rarely exceeding **1–10 W** in large-scale EH systems

## 1.2 What is Power Management? — The Formal Definition

**Power Management (PM)** in IoT refers to the set of **circuit-level and system-level strategies, algorithms, and hardware techniques** used to optimally regulate, condition, store, and dispatch the harvested electrical energy to the load (sensor, microcontroller, transceiver) such that:
- Energy conversion efficiency is maximized.
- Operational lifetime is extended.
- Duty cycling and sleep modes are intelligently controlled.
- Voltage regulation and current limiting are precisely maintained.

## 1.3 What is IoT? — The Connecting Context

The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is a **network of physical objects (things)** embedded with sensors, software, and communication technologies, intended to **sense, collect, process, and exchange data** over the internet without requiring human-to-human or human-to-computer interaction.

The synergy is critical: **IoT devices are typically deployed in inaccessible, remote, or large-scale environments** where battery replacement is **infeasible, uneconomical, or impossible**. This is precisely why **Energy Harvesting and Power Management become foundational pillars** of sustainable IoT design.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Trinity of Sustainable IoT:**
> 1. **Source** — Where the energy comes from (sun, vibration, heat).
> 2. **Harvester** — The transducer converting ambient energy to electrical energy.
> 3. **Manager** — The circuitry that conditions, stores, and delivers the energy to the load.

## 1.4 The Conceptual Analogy — "The Self-Watering Plant"

Imagine a potted plant in a remote desert garden where no gardener can visit. The plant has a **small built-in reservoir** (the storage capacitor/battery) that collects **dew from the air** every morning (energy harvesting). A **tiny wick and valve system** (the power management circuit) decides **when and how much water to release** to the roots throughout the day, ensuring the plant survives even when the dew comes in trickles rather than buckets.

**Just like the plant:**
- IoT sensors cannot wait for "big" energy events — they must sip from the ambient trickle.
- Power management acts as the "valve" that prevents over-harvesting, under-utilization, or destructive energy spikes.
- Storage is finite, so every microjoule counts.

## 1.5 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Strict Definition | Relevance |
|------|------------------|-----------|
| **Ambient Source** | Any naturally available or waste energy source in the environment | The "fuel" of an EH system |
| **Transducer** | A device that converts one form of energy into another | E.g., piezoelectric, photovoltaic |
| **Harvesting Circuit** | The interface electronics between the transducer and storage element | Maximizes power transfer |
| **Storage Element** | A capacitor, supercapacitor, or thin-film battery that stores harvested energy | Energy buffer |
| **Load** | The end IoT device (sensor node, MCU, transceiver) | Energy consumer |
| **Duty Cycling** | Operating the load for brief active periods followed by deep sleep | Energy conservation strategy |
| **MPPT** | Maximum Power Point Tracking | Algorithm to extract peak power from variable sources |
| **Net-Zero Energy Node** | A node that harvests more energy than it consumes over a duty cycle | The ideal goal |

## 1.6 Why This Definition Matters in KTU 2024 Scheme

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 2024 Scheme emphasizes **Outcome-Based Education (OBE)** and **NEP 2020 alignment**. Understanding the *definition* is not merely rote memorization — it forms the **CO1 (Course Outcome 1)** baseline:
> *"Recall and explain the fundamental concepts of energy sources, harvesting mechanisms, and power management strategies for IoT."*
> This topic is the **anchor for all subsequent modules** on transducers, storage, and PMIC design.

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Hierarchical Architecture of EH-PM-IoT

Any energy harvesting and power management system for IoT can be decomposed into **three logical tiers**:

### **Tier 1: The Energy Source (Ambient Domain)**
- **Photonic (Light):** Solar (outdoor ~100 mW/cm²), Indoor (~100 µW/cm²)
- **Thermal (Heat):** Human body (~20–50 µW/cm² at skin), Industrial machinery
- **Mechanical (Motion):** Vibrations (piezoelectric ~50–200 µW/cm³)
- **Electromagnetic (RF/Wi-Fi):** ~1–10 µW/cm² (very low density)
- **Fluidic (Flow):** Wind, water flow, blood flow in medical implants
- **Chemical/Biochemical:** Glucose in biofuel cells

### **Tier 2: The Transduction + Conditioning Stage**
- **Transducer:** Converts ambient energy → electrical (AC usually for vibration, DC for solar)
- **Rectifier (AC-DC):** Bridge rectifiers, Schottky diodes, or active MOSFET rectifiers
- **Voltage Regulation:** LDO, buck/boost converters
- **MPPT Controller:** Tracks peak power point of the source
- **Storage Charging Circuit:** Constant-current/constant-voltage (CC-CV) profile

### **Tier 3: The Load (IoT Node)**
- **Sensing Subsystem:** Temperature, humidity, pressure, gas sensors
- **Processing Subsystem:** Microcontroller (e.g., ARM Cortex-M0+)
- **Communication Subsystem:** BLE, LoRa, Zigbee, NB-IoT transceivers
- **Wake-up/Sleep Controller:** Manages duty cycle

## 2.2 The Power Flow Equation — Fundamental Energy Balance

The **single most important equation** governing any energy harvesting IoT system is the **Energy Neutrality Condition**:

$$E_{harvested} \geq E_{consumed} + E_{losses}$$

Where:
- $E_{harvested}$ is the total energy captured over a duty cycle period $T$
- $E_{consumed}$ is the total energy demanded by the IoT load over $T$
- $E_{losses}$ accounts for conversion, leakage, and storage inefficiencies

For **Energy Neutral Operation (ENO)**, we need:

$$E_{harvested} = E_{consumed} + E_{losses}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Interpretation:** If harvested energy is *less than* consumption, the storage element will eventually deplete and the node will die. If harvested energy is *much greater* than consumption, the excess can extend operational lifetime indefinitely — this is the design goal.

## 2.3 The Average Power Equation

If the source provides a time-varying power $P_s(t)$ and the conversion efficiency is $\eta$, then the average harvested power is:

$$P_{avg} = \eta \cdot \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T P_s(t) \, dt$$

The duty-cycled load consumes:

$$P_{load} = P_{active} \cdot D + P_{sleep} \cdot (1 - D)$$

Where $D$ is the **duty cycle ratio** (fraction of time active):

$$D = \frac{t_{active}}{T}$$

## 2.4 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master the following table — it covers ~80% of numerical problems asked in KTU University Exams for this module.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Harvested Power | $P_h = \eta \cdot P_{source}$ | Watts (W) | Basic EH power calculation |
| 2 | Energy Neutrality | $E_h \geq E_c + E_{loss}$ | Joules (J) | Lifetime prediction |
| 3 | Average Power | $P_{avg} = \eta \cdot \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T P_s(t)\,dt$ | W | Time-varying sources |
| 4 | Duty Cycle | $D = t_{active}/T$ | Dimensionless | Sleep mode design |
| 5 | Load Average Power | $P_L = P_{act}\cdot D + P_{sl}\cdot(1-D)$ | W | Lifetime estimation |
| 6 | Storage Capacity | $C = \frac{2 \cdot E_{store}}{V^2}$ | Farads (F) | Capacitor sizing |
| 7 | Battery Capacity | $E_{bat} = V_{bat} \cdot Q_{bat}$ | Joules (J) | Battery selection |
| 8 | Energy per Cycle | $E_{cycle} = P_{act} \cdot t_{act} + P_{sl} \cdot t_{sl}$ | Joules (J) | Per-transmission energy |
| 9 | Conversion Efficiency | $\eta = P_{out}/P_{in}$ | Dimensionless | Rectifier/transducer efficiency |
| 10 | Energy Density | $\rho_E = E/V$ or $E/m$ | $J/cm^3$ or $Wh/kg$ | Source/storage comparison |
| 11 | Power Density | $\rho_P = P/A$ or $P/V$ | $W/cm^2$ or $W/cm^3$ | Source comparison |
| 12 | Maximum Power Transfer | $R_{load} = R_{source}$ | Ohms ($\Omega$) | Impedance matching |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The definitions and equations above are not abstract — they directly enable:
1. **Smart City Sensor Deployment** — Solar + vibration EH nodes on bridges.
2. **Wearable Health Monitors** — Body heat + motion EH.
3. **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** — Vibration EH on rotating machinery for predictive maintenance.
4. **Agricultural IoT** — Solar + soil-based microbial fuel cells.
5. **Aerospace & Defense** — RF harvesting in remote surveillance nodes.
6. **Implantable Biomedical Devices** — Glucose biofuel cells powering pacemakers.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Neutral Operation (ENO) Condition

We start from the fundamental principle that for an **energy autonomous** IoT node to operate indefinitely, the **total energy input must equal or exceed the total energy demand over any complete duty cycle period $T$**.

### **Step 1: Define the Energy Harvested Over One Period**

The transducer delivers instantaneous power $P_s(t)$ to the harvesting circuit. The harvested electrical energy over one period $T$ is:

$$E_{harvested} = \int_0^T \eta \cdot P_s(t) \, dt$$

### **Step 2: Define the Energy Consumed by the Load**

The IoT load operates in two distinct modes:
- **Active mode** for duration $t_{act}$ at power $P_{act}$
- **Sleep mode** for duration $t_{sl}$ at power $P_{sl}$

With $T = t_{act} + t_{sl}$:

$$E_{consumed} = P_{act} \cdot t_{act} + P_{sl} \cdot t_{sl}$$

### **Step 3: Introduce the Duty Cycle Definition**

The duty cycle is defined as:

$$D = \frac{t_{act}}{T}$$

Therefore:

$$t_{act} = D \cdot T \quad \text{and} \quad t_{sl} = (1 - D) \cdot T$$

### **Step 4: Substitute into the Energy Equation**

$$E_{consumed} = P_{act} \cdot (D \cdot T) + P_{sl} \cdot [(1 - D) \cdot T]$$

Factoring out $T$:

$$E_{consumed} = T \cdot [P_{act} \cdot D + P_{sl} \cdot (1 - D)]$$

### **Step 5: Apply the ENO Condition**

For energy-neutral operation:

$$\int_0^T \eta \cdot P_s(t) \, dt = T \cdot [P_{act} \cdot D + P_{sl} \cdot (1 - D)]$$

If the source is **constant** ($P_s(t) = P_s$, a valid assumption for steady sources like constant light or steady thermal gradients):

$$\eta \cdot P_s \cdot T = T \cdot [P_{act} \cdot D + P_{sl} \cdot (1 - D)]$$

### **Step 6: Solve for the Maximum Allowable Duty Cycle**

$$\eta \cdot P_s = P_{act} \cdot D + P_{sl} - P_{sl} \cdot D$$

$$\eta \cdot P_s - P_{sl} = D \cdot (P_{act} - P_{sl})$$

$$\boxed{D_{max} = \frac{\eta \cdot P_s - P_{sl}}{P_{act} - P_{sl}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This equation is **the single most powerful result** for EH-IoT system design. It tells the designer the **maximum fraction of time** the node can stay awake, given the harvested power and the load's active/sleep power profile.

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Storage Capacitance

A supercapacitor (or capacitor) is often used as the **intermediate storage buffer**. Given that the energy stored in a capacitor is:

$$E_{C} = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$$

If the node requires a minimum operational voltage $V_{min}$ and the storage is pre-charged to $V_{max}$, the **available energy per discharge cycle** is:

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} C (V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2)$$

### **Step 1: Set the Available Energy Equal to Per-Cycle Demand**

$$\frac{1}{2} C (V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2) = P_{act} \cdot t_{act}$$

### **Step 2: Solve for $C$**

$$\boxed{C = \frac{2 \cdot P_{act} \cdot t_{act}}{V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Check:** $[C] = \frac{W \cdot s}{V^2} = \frac{J}{V^2} = F$ (Farads) ✓

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Problem:** An IoT sensor node consumes $P_{act} = 50$ mW during active mode and $P_{sl} = 5$ µW during sleep mode. The node operates with a duty cycle of $D = 0.1$% and the energy harvesting source delivers an average $P_s = 100$ µW with conversion efficiency $\eta = 0.6$. The capacitor is charged to $V_{max} = 3.3$ V and must operate down to $V_{min} = 2.0$ V.
> **(a)** Check whether the system satisfies the ENO condition.
> **(b)** Calculate the required storage capacitance for one active cycle lasting $t_{act} = 100$ ms.

### **Solution (a): ENO Check**

Step 1: Compute the average load power.

$$P_L = P_{act} \cdot D + P_{sl} \cdot (1 - D)$$

$$P_L = (50 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (0.001) + (5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (0.999)$$

$$P_L = 50 \times 10^{-6} + 4.995 \times 10^{-6} = 54.995 \times 10^{-6} \, W$$

Step 2: Compute the available harvested power.

$$P_h = \eta \cdot P_s = 0.6 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-6} = 60 \times 10^{-6} \, W$$

Step 3: Compare.

$$P_h = 60 \, \mu W > P_L = 54.995 \, \mu W \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The system satisfies ENO with a margin of $\Delta P = 5.005$ µW. The node can operate indefinitely.

### **Solution (b): Required Capacitance**

Step 1: Apply the formula.

$$C = \frac{2 \cdot P_{act} \cdot t_{act}}{V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2}$$

Step 2: Substitute values.

$$C = \frac{2 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (100 \times 10^{-3})}{(3.3)^2 - (2.0)^2}$$

$$C = \frac{2 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 0.1}{10.89 - 4.0}$$

$$C = \frac{0.01}{6.89} = 1.451 \times 10^{-3} \, F = 1.451 \, mF$$

**Conclusion:** A capacitor of approximately **1.5 mF** (or 1451 µF) is required.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EH-PM-IoT System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete functional flow of an energy harvesting and power management system, from ambient source to IoT load.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[AMBIENT SOURCE] --> A2[TRANSDUCER]
    A2 --> A3[RECTIFIER AC-DC]
    A3 --> A4[MPPT CONTROLLER]
    A4 --> A5[VOLTAGE REGULATOR]
    A5 --> A6[STORAGE ELEMENT]
    A6 --> A7[POWER MANAGEMENT UNIT]
    A7 --> A8[IOt LOAD SENSOR MCU RADIO]
    A8 --> A9[DATA TO CLOUD OR GATEWAY]
    A6 -.FEEDBACK.-> A4
    A7 -.CONTROL SIGNALS.-> A8
```

## 4.2 Hierarchical Tier Diagram (Source to Sink)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1["TIER 1: AMBIENT SOURCES"]:::tier1
    T2["TIER 2: TRANSDUCTION AND CONDITIONING"]:::tier2
    T3["TIER 3: STORAGE AND POWER MANAGEMENT"]:::tier3
    T4["TIER 4: IOT LOAD"]:::tier4
    T1 --> T2 --> T3 --> T4
    classDef tier1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,color:#000
    classDef tier2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,color:#000
    classDef tier3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,color:#000
    classDef tier4 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Is Energy Harvesting Feasible?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is grid power available at deployment site?}
    Q1 -- NO --> Q2{Is ambient energy abundant and consistent?}
    Q2 -- YES --> Q3[Use Energy Harvesting]
    Q2 -- NO --> Q4[Use primary battery or hybrid]
    Q1 -- YES --> Q5{Is mobility required?}
    Q5 -- YES --> Q3
    Q5 -- NO --> Q6[Use grid power with battery backup]
    Q3 --> Q7[Design EH PM IoT system]
    Q4 --> Q8[Long life primary cell]
    Q6 --> Q9[Standard wall powered design]
```

## 4.4 Energy Neutral Operation Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Energy Source] --> H[Harvester Circuit]
    H --> ST[Storage Supercapacitor]
    ST --> PMU[Power Management IC]
    PMU --> L[IoT Load]
    L --> DC[Duty Cycle Controller]
    DC -. Adjusts Activity .-> L
    ST -. Voltage Feedback .-> PMU
    PMU -. Charge Rate .-> ST
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In the Mermaid schematics above, solid arrows represent **power/energy flow**, while dashed arrows represent **control/feedback signals**. This dual-flow architecture is a hallmark of well-designed EH-PM-IoT systems.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define Energy Harvesting. List any four ambient energy sources used for harvesting.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Energy harvesting is the process of capturing small amounts of energy from ambient sources in the environment—such as sunlight, vibrations, heat, or RF signals—and converting it into usable electrical energy to power low-energy devices, particularly wireless sensor nodes in IoT systems.

**Four ambient energy sources:**
1. **Solar (Photovoltaic)** — converts light into electricity
2. **Thermal (Thermoelectric)** — converts temperature gradients via Seebeck effect
3. **Vibration (Piezoelectric/Electromagnetic)** — converts mechanical motion
4. **RF (Electromagnetic)** — captures ambient radio frequency energy

---

### **Q2. What is Power Management in the context of IoT? Why is it essential?** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Power management in IoT refers to the set of techniques, algorithms, and circuits used to efficiently regulate, condition, store, and deliver harvested energy to the sensor, microcontroller, and radio subsystems of an IoT node.

**Why it is essential:**
- It **maximizes energy conversion efficiency** from the harvester.
- It **enables energy-neutral operation** by matching consumption to harvesting.
- It **protects storage elements** from over-charge/discharge damage.
- It **extends operational lifetime**, often to decades.
- It **manages duty cycling** to keep sleep-mode current ultra-low.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** Define Energy Harvesting and Power Management. Explain with a neat block diagram the architecture of a typical energy harvesting system for an IoT node. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An IoT sensor node has an active power consumption of 60 mW and sleep power of 10 µW. It operates with a duty cycle of 0.2%. A solar EH source provides an average of 150 µW with a conversion efficiency of 70%.
- (i) Verify whether the system is energy neutral. **(3 Marks)**
- (ii) Calculate the required storage capacitance if $V_{max} = 3.6$ V, $V_{min} = 2.4$ V, and $t_{act} = 200$ ms. **(4 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A:**

### **Part (a) — Definition + Block Diagram (7 Marks)**

**Definition of Energy Harvesting (2 Marks):**
Energy harvesting is the process of capturing, accumulating, and storing minute amounts of energy from ambient environmental sources—such as sunlight, thermal gradients, mechanical vibrations, or RF signals—and converting them into usable electrical energy to power autonomous electronic devices, especially IoT sensor nodes.

**Definition of Power Management (2 Marks):**
Power management encompasses the circuit-level and system-level strategies used to optimally condition, store, regulate, and dispatch harvested energy to the load, ensuring maximum efficiency, energy-neutrality, and prolonged operational lifetime of the IoT node.

**Block Diagram Explanation (3 Marks):**

The architecture consists of four cascaded blocks:

1. **Ambient Source (e.g., solar, vibration):** Provides the raw environmental energy in a non-electrical form.
2. **Transducer + Rectifier + MPPT:** Converts ambient energy to electrical DC and extracts maximum available power.
3. **Storage Element (Supercapacitor/Battery):** Buffers the harvested energy to handle the intermittency of the source.
4. **Power Management Unit (PMU) + IoT Load:** Regulates voltage, controls duty cycling, and supplies stable power to the sensor, MCU, and radio.

(Refer to the block diagram in SECTION 4.1 of these notes.)

---

### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $P_{act} = 60$ mW $= 60 \times 10^{-3}$ W
- $P_{sl} = 10$ µW $= 10 \times 10^{-6}$ W
- $D = 0.2\% = 0.002$
- $P_s = 150$ µW $= 150 \times 10^{-6}$ W
- $\eta = 0.70$

#### **(i) Energy Neutrality Check (3 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Compute average load power.

$$P_L = P_{act} \cdot D + P_{sl} \cdot (1 - D)$$

$$P_L = (60 \times 10^{-3})(0.002) + (10 \times 10^{-6})(0.998)$$

$$P_L = 120 \times 10^{-6} + 9.98 \times 10^{-6} = 129.98 \times 10^{-6} \, W$$

$$P_L \approx 129.98 \, \mu W$$

**Step 2:** Compute harvested power.

$$P_h = \eta \cdot P_s = 0.7 \cdot 150 \times 10^{-6} = 105 \times 10^{-6} \, W$$

$$P_h = 105 \, \mu W$$

**Step 3:** Compare.

$$P_h = 105 \, \mu W < P_L = 129.98 \, \mu W \quad \times$$

**Conclusion:** The system is **NOT energy neutral**. Harvested power falls short by $\Delta P = 24.98$ µW. The storage will deplete over time. To achieve ENO, either increase the source, improve efficiency, or reduce the duty cycle.

#### **(ii) Required Capacitance (4 Marks):**

**Given:** $V_{max} = 3.6$ V, $V_{min} = 2.4$ V, $t_{act} = 200$ ms $= 0.2$ s.

**Step 1:** Apply the storage capacitance formula.

$$C = \frac{2 \cdot P_{act} \cdot t_{act}}{V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$C = \frac{2 \cdot (60 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (0.2)}{(3.6)^2 - (2.4)^2}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the denominator.

$$(3.6)^2 - (2.4)^2 = 12.96 - 5.76 = 7.2 \, V^2$$

**Step 4:** Compute the numerator.

$$2 \cdot 0.060 \cdot 0.2 = 0.024 \, J$$

**Step 5:** Final calculation.

$$C = \frac{0.024}{7.2} = 3.333 \times 10^{-3} \, F = 3.333 \, mF$$

**Conclusion:** A storage capacitor of approximately **3.33 mF (3333 µF)** is required.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper as per 2024 Scheme]`**

**(a)** Define the Internet of Things (IoT). Discuss why energy harvesting is critical for sustainable IoT deployment. List and briefly explain any three ambient energy sources. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A wireless IoT node operates with active power 80 mW (for 50 ms) and sleep power 5 µW (for the remaining time). The energy harvester delivers an average 200 µW with 80% efficiency.
- (i) Find the maximum allowable duty cycle for energy-neutral operation. **(4 Marks)**
- (ii) If the system is to operate with a duty cycle of 0.05%, what is the energy surplus or deficit per second? **(3 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B:**

### **Part (a) — IoT Definition + EH Criticality (7 Marks)**

**Definition of IoT (2 Marks):**
The Internet of Things is a network of interconnected physical devices—embedded with sensors, microcontrollers, software, and communication modules—that collect, process, and exchange data over the internet with minimal human intervention.

**Why EH is Critical for IoT (2 Marks):**
- IoT deployments are massive (billions of nodes) and often in **inaccessible locations**.
- **Battery replacement is impractical, costly, and environmentally harmful.**
- EH provides **virtually unlimited operational lifetime** under ambient energy availability.
- It enables **truly autonomous, "deploy-and-forget" IoT systems.**

**Three Ambient Sources (3 Marks):**
1. **Solar Energy:** Photovoltaic cells convert sunlight into DC electricity. Power density ~10–100 mW/cm² outdoors.
2. **Vibration/Piezoelectric:** Piezoelectric transducers convert mechanical vibrations (from machines, footsteps, vehicles) into AC voltage. Power density ~50–200 µW/cm³.
3. **Thermal Energy:** Thermoelectric generators (TEGs) exploit the Seebeck effect to convert temperature differences into voltage. Power density ~20–60 µW/cm² for human body heat.

### **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 Marks)**

**Given:**
- $P_{act} = 80$ mW $= 80 \times 10^{-3}$ W
- $P_{sl} = 5$ µW $= 5 \times 10^{-6}$ W
- $t_{act} = 50$ ms $= 0.05$ s
- $P_s = 200$ µW $= 200 \times 10^{-6}$ W
- $\eta = 0.80$

#### **(i) Maximum Allowable Duty Cycle (4 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Apply the ENO duty cycle formula derived in SECTION 3.1:

$$D_{max} = \frac{\eta \cdot P_s - P_{sl}}{P_{act} - P_{sl}}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values.

$$D_{max} = \frac{(0.8)(200 \times 10^{-6}) - (5 \times 10^{-6})}{(80 \times 10^{-3}) - (5 \times 10^{-6})}$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator and denominator.

$$\text{Numerator} = 160 \times 10^{-6} - 5 \times 10^{-6} = 155 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\text{Denominator} = 80 \times 10^{-3} - 0.005 \times 10^{-3} = 79.995 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 4:** Final calculation.

$$D_{max} = \frac{155 \times 10^{-6}}{79.995 \times 10^{-3}} = 1.9375 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{D_{max} \approx 0.19375\%}$$

#### **(ii) Energy Surplus/Deficit at D = 0.05% (3 Marks):**

**Given:** $D = 0.05\% = 5 \times 10^{-4}$.

**Step 1:** Compute average load power.

$$P_L = P_{act} \cdot D + P_{sl} \cdot (1 - D)$$

$$P_L = (80 \times 10^{-3})(5 \times 10^{-4}) + (5 \times 10^{-6})(0.9995)$$

$$P_L = 40 \times 10^{-6} + 4.9975 \times 10^{-6} = 44.9975 \times 10^{-6} \, W$$

$$P_L \approx 45 \, \mu W$$

**Step 2:** Compare with harvested power $P_h = 160$ µW.

$$P_h - P_L = 160 - 45 = 115 \, \mu W \text{ (surplus)}$$

**Step 3:** Convert to energy surplus per second.

$$E_{surplus} = 115 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1 \, s = 115 \, \mu J \text{ per second}$$

**Conclusion:** The system operates with an energy **surplus of 115 µJ per second**, indicating healthy energy-neutral operation with margin for additional functionality.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Students frequently mix mW, µW, and W in the same equation. Always convert all quantities to a **single unit system (preferably SI: Watts)** before substituting.
> 2. **Forgetting the Sleep Power:** Many students compute load power as $P_{act} \cdot D$ only and ignore $P_{sl} \cdot (1-D)$. For ultra-low duty cycles, the sleep term can become **comparable or even dominant**.
> 3. **Misidentifying ENO Direction:** A common error is to claim ENO is satisfied when $P_h < P_L$. Remember: **ENO requires $P_h \geq P_L$**, not the reverse.
> 4. **Confusing Voltage in the Capacitor Formula:** The expression $C = 2E/V^2$ uses the **squared voltage difference** when computing usable energy from a pre-charged capacitor discharging to $V_{min}$. Students often erroneously use $(V_{max} - V_{min})$ linearly — this loses **3–4 marks instantly**.
> 5. **Skipping the Block Diagram:** In 7-mark definition questions, **a labeled block diagram is mandatory**. A text-only answer without a diagram typically gets capped at 4–5 marks by the KTU examiner.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Revise Before the Exam:**

- [x] **Energy Harvesting (EH)** = capturing ambient energy → electrical energy for low-power devices.
- [x] **Power Management (PM)** = conditioning, storing, regulating, and dispatching harvested energy to the load.
- [x] **IoT** = networked physical objects with sensors/communication, often battery-inaccessible.
- [x] **The Trinity:** Source → Transducer → Manager (load is the beneficiary).
- [x] **Ambient Sources:** Solar, Thermal, Vibration, RF, Fluidic, Biochemical.
- [x] **Energy Neutrality Equation:** $E_h \geq E_c + E_{loss}$.
- [x] **Average Load Power:** $P_L = P_{act}\cdot D + P_{sl}\cdot(1-D)$.
- [x] **Maximum Duty Cycle:** $D_{max} = (\eta P_s - P_{sl}) / (P_{act} - P_{sl})$.
- [x] **Storage Capacitance:** $C = 2 P_{act} t_{act} / (V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2)$.
- [x] **Power Density Units:** $W/cm^2$ (for areal sources), $W/cm^3$ (for volumetric).
- [x] **Conversion Efficiency:** $\eta = P_{out}/P_{in}$, typically 0.5–0.85 in practical EH systems.
- [x] **MPPT** is essential for variable sources (solar under clouds, vibration under changing conditions).
- [x] **Duty cycling** is the #1 energy-saving strategy in IoT nodes.
- [x] **Storage trade-off:** Capacitors (fast charge, limited energy) vs. Batteries (slow charge, high energy).
- [x] **Net-zero energy node** = the design ideal: harvests ≥ consumes over every duty cycle.
- [x] **Block diagram must include:** Source → Transducer → Rectifier → MPPT → Storage → PMU → Load.
- [x] **Real-world applications:** Smart cities, wearables, IIoT, agriculture, aerospace, implants.

> **End of Module 1 - Topic: Definition | PECIT746 - Energy Harvesting & Power Management in IoT**
