**Step 2 — Aggregated Data Rate (Fog-Processed)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{fog \rightarrow cloud} &= 10 \text{ KB/s} \\
&= 10 \times 1024 \times 8 \\
&= 81.92 \text{ Kbps} \\
&= 0.08192 \text{ Mbps}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Bandwidth Savings Ratio**

$$\eta_{BW} = 1 - (D_{fog \rightarrow cloud} \div D_{raw})$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{BW} &= 1 - (0.08192 \div 8.192) \\
&= 1 - 0.01 \\
&= 0.99
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

> A **99% reduction** in upstream bandwidth is achieved. This is the **single most important economic driver** for fog adoption in large-scale IoT deployments.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cloud vs. Fog vs. Edge

| Parameter | Cloud Computing | Fog Computing | Edge Computing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Centralized data center | Network edge (gateway, micro-DC) | On/near the device |
| **Typical Latency** | 50–300 ms | 1–10 ms | <1 ms |
| **Compute Power** | Virtually unlimited | Moderate | Limited |
| **Storage** | Petabytes | Terabytes | Gigabytes |
| **Bandwidth Need** | High (raw data streamed) | Low (aggregated) | Very low |
| **Energy Profile** | High (data center) | Moderate | Low (device-level) |
| **Best Use Case** | Big-data analytics, training ML | Local control, real-time response | Sensor-level pre-filtering |
| **KTU Ranking (by latency)** | Slowest (3rd) | Medium (2nd) | Fastest (1st) |
| **Scalability Model** | Vertical (scale up server) | Horizontal (add more fog nodes) | Per-device |
| **Failure Impact** | Single point of failure | Graceful degradation | Device-level isolation |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram 1 — Hierarchical Fog Computing Architecture

The following Mermaid block renders the canonical **Mist → Edge → Fog → Cloud** four-tier architecture used in KTU 2024 scheme answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    dev1[IoT Sensor 1]
    dev2[IoT Sensor 2]
    dev3[IoT Sensor 3]
    dev4[IoT Sensor 4]
    dev5[IoT Sensor 5]

    mistNode[Mist Layer - Sensor Level Pre-Processing]

    edgeGW1[Edge Gateway - Building A]
    edgeGW2[Edge Gateway - Building B]

    fogNode1[Fog Node - Campus Micro-DC]
    fogNode2[Fog Node - District Micro-DC]

    cloudDC[Cloud Data Center - Global Aggregation]

    analytics[Big Data Analytics and ML Training]
    storage[Long-Term Cloud Storage]

    dev1 --> mistNode
    dev2 --> mistNode
    dev3 --> mistNode
    dev4 --> mistNode
    dev5 --> mistNode

    mistNode --> edgeGW1
    mistNode --> edgeGW2

    edgeGW1 --> fogNode1
    edgeGW2 --> fogNode2

    fogNode1 --> cloudDC
    fogNode2 --> cloudDC

    cloudDC --> analytics
    cloudDC --> storage
```

**Reading the Diagram (Top-Down):**
- **Top tier (Mist):** Raw sensor data is filtered and lightly pre-processed.
- **Upper-middle tier (Edge):** Local protocol translation (e.g., ZigBee → IP).
- **Lower-middle tier (Fog):** Aggregated regional processing, decision making, ML inference.
- **Bottom tier (Cloud):** Global aggregation, model training, archival.

---

### 4.2 Diagram 2 — Functional Block Architecture: Fog Node Internal Structure

This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** describing the internal modules of a single fog node — a common KTU 14-mark diagram question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inData[Incoming IoT Data Stream]
    inQueue[Ingress Buffer and Classifier]
    secMod[Security Module - Auth, Encryption]
    procMod[Compute Engine - VM, Container]
    storeMod[Local Storage - SSD, In-Memory Cache]
    aiMod[AI Inference Engine]
    polMod[Policy Engine - Rules, Thresholds]
    outLocal[Local Response to Actuator]
    outCloud[Compressed Summary to Cloud]
    monMod[Monitoring and Telemetry]
    mgrMod[Resource Manager and Scheduler]

    inData --> inQueue
    inQueue --> secMod
    secMod --> procMod
    procMod --> storeMod
    procMod --> aiMod
    procMod --> polMod
    polMod --> outLocal
    polMod --> outCloud
    procMod --> monMod
    mgrMod --> procMod
    mgrMod --> inQueue
    monMod --> cloudDC2[Cloud Telemetry Receiver]
```

**Functional Description:**

| Module | Function |
|---|---|
| **Ingress Buffer** | Receives raw data packets, performs priority tagging |
| **Security Module** | Authenticates devices, decrypts payload, applies access control |
| **Compute Engine** | Runs containers/VMs that host the application logic |
| **Local Storage** | Temporarily persists data for batched processing |
| **AI Inference Engine** | Runs trained ML models for real-time classification/detection |
| **Policy Engine** | Applies threshold-based rules (e.g., "if temp > 90°C, send alert") |
| **Resource Manager** | Allocates CPU, RAM, and network to workloads |
| **Monitoring** | Emits telemetry metrics to the cloud |

---

### 4.3 Diagram 3 — Decision Flow: When to Use Fog vs. Cloud

This decision flowchart helps students answer "justify fog vs. cloud" type questions in 14-mark problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[New IoT Application]
    q1{Latency requirement below 10 ms?}
    q2{Bandwidth constrained or expensive?}
    q3{Privacy or data-sovereignty concern?}
    q4{Local autonomous operation required?}

    fogAns[Deploy Fog Node at Edge]
    cloudAns[Deploy on Cloud]
    hybridAns[Hybrid Fog plus Cloud]

    start --> q1
    q1 -- Yes --> fogAns
    q1 -- No --> q2
    q2 -- Yes --> fogAns
    q2 -- No --> q3
    q3 -- Yes --> fogAns
    q3 -- No --> q4
    q4 -- Yes --> fogAns
    q4 -- No --> hybridAns
    q1 -- No and Q2 No and Q3 No and Q4 No --> cloudAns
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Marking Tip:** When justifying fog in 14-mark answers, always reference at least **two of the four decision criteria** above and map them to the application's specific requirements.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
**Define fog computing. List any four reasons why fog computing is required in IoT systems.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Fog Computing** is a distributed computing paradigm that extends cloud computing capabilities — namely compute, storage, and networking — to the **intermediate layer between end IoT devices and centralized cloud data centers**. It was introduced by Cisco in 2012 to address the limitations of pure cloud-based IoT architectures.

**Four reasons fog computing is required:**

1. **Latency Reduction:** Time-critical applications (autonomous vehicles, industrial control) require sub-10 ms response, which the cloud cannot deliver due to long propagation distances.
2. **Bandwidth Optimization:** Fog performs local aggregation, reducing WAN bandwidth usage by 80–99%.
3. **Enhanced Security & Privacy:** Sensitive data can be processed and anonymized locally, reducing exposure during transit.
4. **Improved Reliability:** Fog nodes operate autonomously during cloud outages, ensuring continuous service.

*(Other valid reasons: Scalability, Mobility support, Reduced energy consumption, Geographic distribution.)*

**Marks Distribution:**
- Definition: 1 mark
- Four reasons (0.5 each): 2 marks

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, SAMPLE]**
**Differentiate between Cloud Computing and Fog Computing on the basis of (i) location, (ii) latency, and (iii) primary use case.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Cloud Computing | Fog Computing |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Location** | Centralized mega data centers (often 1000s of km from devices) | Distributed micro-data centers at the network edge (1–50 km from devices) |
| **(ii) Latency** | 50–300 ms (high) | 1–10 ms (low) |
| **(iii) Primary Use Case** | Big-data analytics, ML model training, long-term archival storage | Real-time control, local decision making, latency-sensitive IoT applications |

**Marks Distribution:**
- Three parameters (1 mark each): 3 marks

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Rule:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and typically has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. The student must answer **one full question** (both sub-parts) from a Module-internal choice.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the **hierarchical architecture of fog computing** with a neat diagram. Discuss the role of each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A smart factory deploys **500 sensors**, each generating **2 KB/s** of raw data. The fog node aggregates the data and transmits only **summary packets of 50 KB/s** to the cloud. Compute the **bandwidth savings ratio** and the **annual cost savings** if WAN bandwidth costs ₹0.50 per GB. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hierarchical Architecture (7 marks):**

The fog computing architecture is organized into **four hierarchical layers**, each playing a distinct role:

1. **Mist Layer (Sensors/Actuators):** The innermost tier — raw data generation and immediate actuation. Performs the most basic filtering and pre-processing.
2. **Edge Layer (Gateways):** Protocol translation, local buffering, light analytics. Acts as the bridge between heterogeneous sensor protocols (ZigBee, BLE, LoRa) and IP networks.
3. **Fog Layer (Micro Data Centers):** The heart of the system. Performs aggregation, real-time analytics, ML inference, and policy enforcement. Provides compute, storage, and networking services.
4. **Cloud Layer (Central Data Centers):** Global aggregation, big-data analytics, model training, archival storage, and cross-region coordination.

*Include a labeled four-tier diagram (refer to Section 4.1). Marks: 1 for diagram + 6 for explanation (1.5 per layer).*

**(b) Bandwidth Savings Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Number of sensors: $N = 500$
- Raw data rate per sensor: $S_{raw} = 2$ KB/s
- Aggregated data rate: $S_{fog} = 50$ KB/s
- WAN cost: $C = ₹0.50$ per GB
- Time period: 1 year $= 365 \times 24 \times 3600$ s $= 31,536,000$ s

**Step 1 — Total Raw Data (No Fog)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{raw} &= 500 \times 2 \text{ KB/s} \\
&= 1000 \text{ KB/s} \\
&= 1 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Aggregated Data (With Fog)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{fog} &= 50 \text{ KB/s} \\
&= 0.05 \text{ MB/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Bandwidth Savings Ratio**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta_{BW} &= 1 - (D_{fog} \div D_{raw}) \\
&= 1 - (0.05 \div 1) \\
&= 0.95 \text{ or } 95\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Annual Data Volume (Cloud-Path Without Fog)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{raw} &= 1 \text{ MB/s} \times 31,536,000 \text{ s} \\
&= 31,536,000 \text{ MB} \\
&= 31,536 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Annual Data Volume (With Fog)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{fog} &= 0.05 \text{ MB/s} \times 31,536,000 \text{ s} \\
&= 1,576,800 \text{ MB} \\
&= 1,576.8 \text{ GB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — Cost Savings**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Savings} &= (V_{raw} - V_{fog}) \times C \\
&= (31,536 - 1,576.8) \times ₹0.50 \\
&= 29,959.2 \times ₹0.50 \\
&= ₹14,979.60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** **95% bandwidth savings** achieved; **annual cost savings of ₹14,979.60**.

*Marks: Step 1 (1) + Step 2 (1) + Step 3 (1.5) + Step 4 (1) + Step 5 (1) + Step 6 (1.5) = 7 marks*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Discuss the **key drivers and reasons** for adopting fog computing in modern IoT systems. Categorize them into technological, economic, and application-driven reasons. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An IoT device is **20 km** away from a fog node and **1500 km** away from the cloud. The signal propagation speed in fiber is $2 \times 10^8$ m/s. Compute the **propagation delay** in both cases. If the cloud processing time is **40 ms** and fog processing time is **3 ms**, compute the **total round-trip latency difference** (assume transmission and queuing delays are negligible). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Key Drivers and Reasons (7 marks):**

The adoption of fog computing is driven by **three categories of factors**:

**1. Technological Drivers (2.5 marks)**
- **5G/6G Networks:** Enable URLLC (Ultra-Reliable Low-Latency Communication), which mandates local processing.
- **AI/ML at the Edge:** Inference engines deployed in fog nodes reduce dependence on cloud round-trips.
- **Mature Containerization:** Technologies like Docker and Kubernetes allow lightweight services on fog hardware.
- **Heterogeneous Device Support:** Fog gateways can interface with diverse sensor protocols.

**2. Economic Drivers (2.5 marks)**
- **Bandwidth Cost Reduction:** Aggregated data transmission saves 80–99% of WAN costs.
- **Cloud Storage Cost Avoidance:** Local archival reduces cloud egress and storage fees.
- **Reduced Energy Cost:** Less long-haul transmission = lower energy bills for battery-powered IoT devices.
- **CAPEX/OPEX Optimization:** Distributed micro-DCs are cheaper to deploy than scaling centralized clouds.

**3. Application-Driven Drivers (2 marks)**
- **Smart Cities:** Real-time traffic signal control, pollution monitoring.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Sub-millisecond control loops in manufacturing.
- **Healthcare Wearables:** Privacy-preserving local processing of vitals.
- **Autonomous Vehicles:** V2X communication needs <10 ms response.
- **Disaster Response:** Fog operates when cloud connectivity is lost.

**(b) Propagation Delay Numerical (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Distance to fog: $d_{fog} = 20$ km $= 20,000$ m
- Distance to cloud: $d_{cloud} = 1500$ km $= 1,500,000$ m
- Propagation speed: $v = 2 \times 10^8$ m/s
- Cloud processing time: $t_{proc,cloud} = 40$ ms
- Fog processing time: $t_{proc,fog} = 3$ ms
- Transmission and queuing delays: negligible

**Step 1 — Propagation Delay to Fog**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{prop,fog} &= d_{fog} \div v \\
&= (2 \times 10^4) \div (2 \times 10^8) \\
&= 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} \\
&= 0.1 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Propagation Delay to Cloud**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{prop,cloud} &= d_{cloud} \div v \\
&= (1.5 \times 10^6) \div (2 \times 10^8) \\
&= 7.5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} \\
&= 7.5 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Round-Trip Latency (Cloud)**
Round-trip = 2 × (propagation + processing)

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{RT,cloud} &= 2 \times (L_{prop,cloud} + t_{proc,cloud}) \\
&= 2 \times (7.5 + 40) \\
&= 2 \times 47.5 \\
&= 95 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Total Round-Trip Latency (Fog)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{RT,fog} &= 2 \times (L_{prop,fog} + t_{proc,fog}) \\
&= 2 \times (0.1 + 3) \\
&= 2 \times 3.1 \\
&= 6.2 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Latency Difference**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta L &= L_{RT,cloud} - L_{RT,fog} \\
&= 95 - 6.2 \\
&= 88.8 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:** Fog saves **88.8 ms** in round-trip latency, representing a **93.5% improvement** over cloud-only processing.

*Marks: Step 1 (1) + Step 2 (1) + Step 3 (1.5) + Step 4 (1.5) + Step 5 (2) = 7 marks*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Fog Computing Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Fog with Edge:** Many students treat fog and edge as synonyms. *Edge = device-adjacent (single device); Fog = infrastructure-level (gateway/micro-DC serving many devices).* Losing **1–2 marks** in 14-mark answers.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Round-Trip Multiplier:** When asked for "total latency," students often compute only one-way latency. Remember: $L_{RT} = 2 \times L_{one-way}$. Loses **1.5 marks** in numericals.
>
> 3. **Unit Mismatches:** Mixing up **bits vs. bytes** and **seconds vs. milliseconds**. KTU examiners are strict — **0.5 mark deducted** for every unit inconsistency.
>
> 4. **Skipping the Diagram:** For 7-mark sub-questions asking for an "explanation with diagram," a missing diagram costs **at least 1 mark** even if the text is perfect.
>
> 5. **Vague Justification:** Writing "fog is better than cloud" without quantifying the benefit (e.g., "92% latency reduction") loses **up to 2 marks**. Always include numbers from the formula sheet.
>
> 6. **Not Categorizing Drivers:** When asked to "discuss reasons," do not give a flat bullet list. **Categorize** into Technological / Economic / Application. Examiners reward structured answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Need and Reasons for Fog Computing"**

- [x] **Definition:** Fog computing = intermediate distributed layer between IoT devices and cloud, introduced by **Cisco in 2012**.
- [x] **Hierarchy (innermost → outermost):** Mist → Edge → Fog → Cloud.
- [x] **Fog ≠ Edge:** Edge is on/near the device; Fog is an infrastructure layer serving multiple edge gateways.
- [x] **Five core needs:** Latency, Bandwidth, Security, Reliability, Scalability.
- [x] **Three driver categories:** Technological (5G, AI), Economic (bandwidth cost), Application (smart cities, IIoT, healthcare, vehicles).
- [x] **Key formulas to memorize:**
  - $L_{total} = L_{prop} + L_{trans} + L_{queue} + L_{proc}$
  - $L_{prop} = d \div v$
  - $\eta_{BW} = 1 - (D_{fog-cloud} \div D_{raw})$
  - Round-trip latency = $2 \times$ one-way latency
- [x] **Typical latency ranges:** Cloud 50–300 ms; Fog 1–10 ms; Edge <1 ms.
- [x] **Bandwidth savings:** Typically **80–99%** via local aggregation.
- [x] **Use the four-tier diagram** in every hierarchical architecture answer.
- [x] **Always include numerical justification** (e.g., "92% latency reduction") in fog-vs-cloud comparison answers.
- [x] **Use the formula sheet's exact notation** — write units in **bold** and equations in proper LaTeX.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fog Computing and Edge Computing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fog Computing and Edge Computing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Edge Computing — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Edge Computing** is a distributed computing paradigm that brings computation and data storage closer to the *physical location* of the data source (the "edge" of the network), thereby reducing communication latency, bandwidth consumption, and central cloud dependency.

In the context of IoT, the **edge** refers to the layer consisting of *sensor nodes, actuators, gateways, and embedded microcontrollers* that sit at the boundary between the physical world and the digital network. Edge computing processes data *on or near the device that generated it*, often without round-tripping to a remote data center.

**Standard KTU Metrics (as per 2024 syllabus terminology):**
- **Latency Target:** **< 10 ms** (mission-critical, real-time control)
- **Processing Location:** *On-device / On-premise gateway*
- **Data Volume Processed:** Filtered/aggregated (typically **< 5%** of raw sensor data is forwarded upstream)

---

## 1.2 Fog Computing — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fog Computing**, originally coined by **Cisco (2012)**, is a *horizontal, system-level architecture* that distributes computing, storage, control, and networking functions *between the device and the cloud* — closer to end-nodes than the cloud, but more powerful than a single edge device.

Fog computing is *not* a replacement for edge computing. Rather, it is a **hierarchical intermediate layer** that orchestrates multiple edge devices, performs aggregation, runs lightweight analytics, and acts as a secure buffer before forwarding data to the cloud.

**Standard KTU Metrics:**
- **Latency Target:** **< 50 ms** (near-real-time, coordination layer)
- **Processing Location:** *LAN-level nodes, fog nodes, micro-data centers*
- **Key Role in IoT:** Acts as the **energy-aware orchestrator** for battery-powered edge clusters

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Smart Restaurant 🍽️

Imagine a busy smart restaurant during dinner rush:

| Layer | Real-World Equivalent | Computing Counterpart |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer placing order at table** | Patron taps tablet to order | IoT sensor reading |
| **Waiter processing order on the floor (Edge)** | Waiter brings soup immediately, no chef needed | **Edge Computing** — local micro-controller handles trivial logic |
| **Sous-chef coordinating dishes (Fog)** | Coordinates multiple waiters, partial cooking on the line | **Fog Computing** — gateway aggregates, runs ML inference, schedules |
| **Main cloud kitchen** | Slow-cooked specials, billing analytics | **Cloud Computing** — long-term storage, heavy training, global dashboards |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Edge = *do it right here, right now.* Fog = *coordinate the neighborhood of edges.* Cloud = *think globally over weeks/months.* In a power-constrained IoT deployment, doing work at the **edge or fog** drastically cuts the energy cost of wireless transmission — which is the *single largest power drain* in most battery nodes.

---

## 1.4 Why These Paradigms Matter for Energy Harvesting

Because PECIT746 is centered on **Energy Harvesting & Power Management**, the relevance is direct:

- **Wireless transmission power (Tx)** is proportional to distance squared: $P_{tx} \propto d^{n}$ where $n \in [2, 4]$ (free-space to urban path-loss).
- Transmitting 1 bit over 100 m can consume the same energy as executing **~1000 CPU instructions** locally.
- Therefore: *Compute locally → transmit less → harvest less frequently → battery lasts longer.*

This is the foundational energy argument for edge and fog architectures.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Tier Architectural Hierarchy

A canonical IoT architecture used in KTU 2024 Scheme papers is structured as follows:

### **Tier 1 — Cloud Layer**
- Massive storage, heavy training (e.g., LSTM models on months of data).
- Latency: **100 – 500 ms** (geographically distant data centers).
- Energy profile: *Low per-bit compute energy, very high per-bit Tx energy at IoT end.*

### **Tier 2 — Fog Layer**
- **Fog Nodes** (e.g., industrial gateways, Raspberry-Pi class devices, 5G MEC servers).
- Functions: **Data aggregation, local ML inference, security enforcement, energy-aware task offloading.**
- Latency: **5 – 50 ms** within LAN/metro scale.

### **Tier 3 — Edge Layer**
- **Edge Devices** (e.g., STM32 + sensor, ESP32 nodes, smart cameras).
- Functions: **Filtering, thresholding, transient control, time-critical actuation.**
- Latency: **< 1 – 10 ms.**

> [!NOTE]
> The fog layer is *especially* important in **energy-harvested** IoT because it can dynamically decide **which edge nodes** should sleep, harvest, transmit, or compute — based on residual energy states.

---

## 2.2 Comparison Matrix (KTU Board-Favorite Table)

| Parameter | **Edge Computing** | **Fog Computing** | **Cloud Computing** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Location of Compute** | On the sensor / device | LAN / Gateway / MEC server | Remote data center |
| **Latency** | **< 10 ms** (very low) | **5 – 50 ms** (low) | **100 – 500 ms** (high) |
| **Compute Power** | Very low (kB RAM, mW) | Medium (GB RAM, 1–10 W) | Massive (TB RAM, kW) |
| **Storage** | KB – few MB | GB scale | PB – EB scale |
| **Power Source** | Battery / harvested (mW) | Mains / large battery | Grid (MW) |
| **Network Reach** | 0 – 1 hop | 1 – few hops | Internet-scale |
| **Role in IoT** | Time-critical decisions | Local coordination, analytics | Long-term insights, training |
| **Energy-Awareness Role** | Sleep/wake control | Task offloading decisions | None directly |

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula / Concept Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept / Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{tx}(d) = P_{tx,0} + 10\,n \log_{10}(d) + X_\sigma$ | Log-distance path-loss model for wireless Tx energy | dBm, **n = 2–4** |
| 2 | $E_{tx}(b,d) = b \cdot (E_{elec} + E_{amp} \cdot d^{n})$ | Energy to transmit *b* bits over *d* m | Joules/bit (Heinzelman model) |
| 3 | $E_{rx}(b) = b \cdot E_{elec}$ | Energy to receive *b* bits | Joules |
| 4 | $E_{cpu}(N) = N \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2}$ | CPU energy ≈ *Capacitance × Voltage² × Cycles* | Joules (CMOS energy model) |
| 5 | $\text{Speedup}(N_{edge}, N_{fog}) = \frac{T_{cloud}}{T_{local}}$ | Parallel speedup at edge/fog | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $P_{avg} = P_{active} \cdot d_{active} + P_{sleep} \cdot (1 - d_{active})$ | Duty-cycled average power | Watts |
| 7 | $E_{harvest} = \int_{0}^{T} P_{harv}(t)\, dt$ | Energy harvested over interval T | Joules |
| 8 | $\eta = \dfrac{E_{useful}}{E_{harvested}}$ | End-to-end energy efficiency | 0 ≤ η ≤ 1 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Rule (CRITICAL):** Never use the vertical pipe `|x|` inside a table — it breaks markdown. Always use `\vert` or `\mid`. This cheat sheet complies.

---

## 2.4 Why "Why" — The Engineering Rationale

1. **Latency-bound applications** (closed-loop motor control, ADAS, robotic surgery) **cannot** wait for a cloud round-trip → must use **edge**.
2. **Bandwidth-bound applications** (video surveillance, 4K vision) generate gigabits/day — impossible to stream raw → must **pre-process at edge**, **aggregate at fog**.
3. **Energy-bound applications** (battery-less, harvested IoT) cannot afford always-on radios → **compute locally**, transmit sparingly, orchestrated by **fog**.
4. **Privacy-bound applications** (medical, industrial) cannot leave the premises → **edge/fog** keeps data local.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 Scheme, an examiner will award full marks only if the answer **explicitly links** the paradigm to *energy awareness, latency, OR bandwidth*. Generic definitions score partial marks only.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy Saved by Edge Processing

We derive the **energy saving ratio (ESR)** — a key analytical metric for deciding *how much* processing should be done at the edge versus the cloud.

### **Step 1 — Define raw data generation rate**
Let a sensor generate data at rate $R$ bits/second, continuously, for a total observation window of $T$ seconds.

$$R_{total} = R \cdot T \quad \text{[bits]}$$

**Explanation:** This is the *unprocessed* bit volume. If we forwarded every bit to the cloud, this is the radio load.

---

### **Step 2 — Apply edge-side compression ratio**
Let $C_{edge}$ be the **compression/filtering ratio** at the edge. For example, thresholding, event-detection, or feature extraction that retains only meaningful events.

$$C_{edge} \in (0, 1]$$

**Explanation:** $C_{edge} = 0.05$ means only 5 % of raw bits are forwarded; 95 % are discarded locally after local processing.

---

### **Step 3 — Bits actually transmitted**
After edge filtering, only a fraction of the raw data needs wireless transmission:

$$R_{tx} = C_{edge} \cdot R \cdot T \quad \text{[bits]}$$

**Explanation:** This is the radio burden — the *only* contributor to high-cost transmission energy.

---

### **Step 4 — Energy cost of transmission (Heinzelman model)**
Using the standard first-order radio energy model from low-power IoT literature (valid for KTU use):

$$E_{tx}(b, d) = b \cdot E_{elec} + b \cdot E_{amp} \cdot d^{n}$$

**Explanation of terms:**
- $b$ = number of bits
- $E_{elec}$ = energy per bit in the radio electronics (≈ **50 nJ/bit**)
- $E_{amp}$ = transmit amplifier energy (≈ **100 pJ/bit/m²** for free space)
- $d$ = transmission distance in metres
- $n$ = path-loss exponent (2 free space, 3–4 indoor/urban)

---

### **Step 5 — Total Tx energy without edge processing**
Without edge filtering, $b = R \cdot T$, so:

$$E_{tx, cloud} = (R \cdot T) \cdot (E_{elec} + E_{amp} \cdot d^{n})$$

---

### **Step 6 — Total Tx energy with edge processing**
With edge filtering at ratio $C_{edge}$:

$$E_{tx, edge} = (C_{edge} \cdot R \cdot T) \cdot (E_{elec} + E_{amp} \cdot d^{n})$$

---

### **Step 7 — Define Energy Saving Ratio (ESR)**

$$\text{ESR} = \frac{E_{tx, cloud} - E_{tx, edge}}{E_{tx, cloud}}$$

---

### **Step 8 — Substitute and simplify**

$$\text{ESR} = \frac{(R \cdot T)(E_{elec} + E_{amp} d^{n}) - (C_{edge} \cdot R \cdot T)(E_{elec} + E_{amp} d^{n})}{(R \cdot T)(E_{elec} + E_{amp} d^{n})}$$

---

### **Step 9 — Cancel common terms**
The factor $(R \cdot T)(E_{elec} + E_{amp} d^{n})$ appears in both numerator and denominator:

$$\text{ESR} = 1 - C_{edge}$$

**The result is elegantly simple:** energy savings from edge processing are *linearly proportional* to the edge compression ratio.

---

### **Step 10 — Worked numerical example**
Suppose:
- $C_{edge} = 0.05$ (only 5 % forwarded)
- $E_{elec} = 50 \text{ nJ/bit}$, $E_{amp} = 100 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^2$, $d = 50 \text{ m}$, $n = 2$

$$\text{ESR} = 1 - 0.05 = 0.95 = 95\,\%$$

**Interpretation:** 95 % of the wireless transmission energy is *eliminated* by processing at the edge. For a 100 mW average radio, the harvested energy budget can now sustain the node for **20× longer** — directly motivating the edge/fog paradigm in **PECIT746**.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which Paradigm

| Scenario | Recommended Tier | Energy Reasoning |
|---|---|---|
| Threshold-based alarms (e.g., temp > 80°C) | **Edge** | Single comparison, no Tx needed |
| Daily aggregation of 1000 sensor nodes | **Fog** | Local summing, single bulk Tx |
| Monthly yield forecasting model retraining | **Cloud** | Heavy batch, latency-tolerant |
| Closed-loop motor control @ 1 kHz | **Edge** | Hard real-time, < 1 ms |
| Video frame anomaly detection | **Fog + Edge** | Edge extracts frames, fog runs CNN |
| Battery-less BLE beacon | **Edge** | Compute on event, deep-sleep otherwise |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Tier IoT Architecture (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph cloudLayer[Cloud Tier - Data Center]
        cloudA["Big Data Storage"]
        cloudB["Deep Learning Training"]
        cloudC["Global Dashboards"]
    end

    subgraph fogLayer[Fog Tier - LAN / Gateway]
        fogA["Fog Node 1 Aggregator"]
        fogB["Fog Node 2 ML Inference"]
        fogC["Energy-Aware Scheduler"]
    end

    subgraph edgeLayer[Edge Tier - Field Devices]
        edgeA["Edge Node 1 Sensor MCU"]
        edgeB["Edge Node 2 Smart Camera"]
        edgeC["Edge Node 3 Actuator"]
    end

    subgraph phyLayer[Physical World]
        phyA["Temperature Sensor"]
        phyB["Camera Feed"]
        phyC["Motor / Valve"]
    end

    phyA --> edgeA
    phyB --> edgeB
    phyC --> edgeC

    edgeA --> fogA
    edgeB --> fogB
    edgeC --> fogC

    fogA --> cloudA
    fogB --> cloudB
    fogC --> cloudC
```

**Interpretation:** Arrows represent data/control flow. The cloud tier is *passive consumers*; the fog tier *orchestrates*; the edge tier *interacts with the physical world.*

---

## 4.2 Decision Flow — Should Computation Go to Edge, Fog, or Cloud?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["New IoT Task Arrives"] --> q1{"Latency Requirement?"}
    q1 -->|less than 10 ms| edge1["Assign to EDGE node"]
    q1 -->|10 to 100 ms| q2{"Aggregation Needed?"}
    q1 -->|more than 100 ms| cloud1["Assign to CLOUD"]

    q2 -->|Yes, multi-node| fog1["Assign to FOG node"]
    q2 -->|No, single node| edge2["Assign to EDGE node"]

    edge1 --> endNode["Energy check: Sleep vs Active"]
    edge2 --> endNode
    fog1 --> endFog["Fog schedules and harvests energy"]
    cloud1 --> endCloud["Cloud logs and trains model"]
```

---

## 4.3 Fog Computing as Energy Orchestrator — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sensor["Sensor Cluster A"] --> mcuA["MCU Edge A"]
    sensorB["Sensor Cluster B"] --> mcuB["MCU Edge B"]
    sensorC["Sensor Cluster C"] --> mcuC["MCU Edge C"]

    mcuA --> harvestA["Energy Harvester A"]
    mcuB --> harvestB["Energy Harvester B"]
    mcuC --> harvestC["Energy Harvester C"]

    mcuA -->|Status: residual energy| fogN["FOG ENERGY MANAGER"]
    mcuB -->|Status: residual energy| fogN
    mcuC -->|Status: residual energy| fogN

    fogN --> decision{"Who Transmits Next?"}
    decision -->|Highest residual energy| txA["Node A transmits"]
    decision -->|Others sleep| sleepB["Nodes B and C sleep"]
```

**Engineering Insight:** This is the *exact* block topology used in energy-harvested WSNs (e.g., solar-powered agricultural sensors). The fog acts as a **TDMA-style scheduler** based on energy state, maximizing network lifetime.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**"Differentiate between Edge Computing and Cloud Computing with respect to latency, energy, and processing location."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
| Aspect | Edge Computing | Cloud Computing |
|---|---|---|
| **Latency** | **< 10 ms** (very low) | **100 – 500 ms** (high) |
| **Energy Profile** | Battery/harvested; energy-efficient | Grid-powered; high per-bit Tx cost |
| **Processing Location** | On/near the sensor device | Remote, centralized data center |

> Edge computing processes data at the source, while cloud computing centralizes processing in distant data centers. *(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for the table.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**"List any three advantages of Fog Computing in IoT systems."** (3 Marks)

**Model Answer:**
1. **Reduced Latency** — Local processing achieves 5–50 ms response versus 100+ ms for cloud. *(1 mark)*
2. **Bandwidth Conservation** — Aggregates and filters data before cloud upload, saving up to **95 %** of uplink traffic. *(1 mark)*
3. **Energy Awareness** — Acts as a central orchestrator managing sleep/wake cycles of energy-harvested edge nodes. *(1 mark)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> Students often *describe* edge and fog without **numerical examples or energy analysis**. KTU 2024 Scheme mandates outcome-based answers linking concepts to *energy/power metrics*. A definition-only answer typically scores **6–7 / 14**; the remaining 7 marks are reserved for **quantitative reasoning, architecture diagrams, and IoT-specific justification**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **three-tier IoT architecture** consisting of Edge, Fog, and Cloud layers. Discuss the **role of each layer** with at least one real-time IoT use case. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Energy Saving Ratio (ESR)** when edge processing is performed before wireless transmission, and evaluate it for $C_{edge} = 0.08$, $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $E_{amp} = 100$ pJ/bit/m², $d = 30$ m, and $n = 2$. Comment on the impact of ESR on **battery lifetime** in energy-harvested IoT nodes. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks**

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    P[Physical Sensors and Actuators] --> E[Edge Layer: MCU + Local Decisions]
    E --> F[Fog Layer: Gateway + Aggregation + ML]
    F --> C[Cloud Layer: Data Center + Training]
```

**Description (4 marks):**

- **Edge Layer (2 marks):** Sits closest to the physical world. Comprises microcontrollers (e.g., STM32, ESP32), sensors, and actuators. Performs **time-critical operations** such as threshold detection, closed-loop control, and event-triggered wake-up. *Use case:* In a **smart motor control system**, the edge MCU reads current/voltage at 10 kHz and shuts down the motor within **< 1 ms** of an overcurrent event — impossible over the cloud.

- **Fog Layer (1 mark):** Intermediate layer of gateway-class devices. Aggregates data from hundreds of edge nodes, runs **lightweight ML inference**, and decides what to forward. *Use case:* In a **solar farm**, fog nodes aggregate panel telemetry from 500 edge devices and run a daily MPPT optimization locally.

- **Cloud Layer (1 mark):** Long-term storage, batch training, global analytics. *Use case:* A utility company uses cloud training over 6 months of data to retrain its forecasting model, then pushes updated weights back down to the fog.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Define raw bit volume $R_{total} = R \cdot T$.

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Define edge compression ratio $C_{edge}$, giving transmitted bits $R_{tx} = C_{edge} \cdot R \cdot T$.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Write Heinzelman energy equation for transmission:

$$E_{tx}(b, d) = b \cdot E_{elec} + b \cdot E_{amp} \cdot d^{n}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Derive ESR:

$$\text{ESR} = \frac{E_{tx, cloud} - E_{tx, edge}}{E_{tx, cloud}} = 1 - C_{edge}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Substitute $C_{edge} = 0.08$:

$$\text{ESR} = 1 - 0.08 = 0.92 = 92\,\%$$

**Comment on battery lifetime (bonus point if mentioned):** A **92 % reduction** in transmission energy means the node's battery (or supercapacitor buffer) is depleted **12.5× slower** for the same data throughput — a transformative gain for energy-harvested nodes where the average harvest rate is only **milliwatts**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the concept of **Fog Computing**. Discuss how fog nodes act as **energy-aware orchestrators** in a battery-less or energy-harvested IoT deployment. Include a block diagram. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Compare Edge and Fog computing on the basis of **latency, scalability, energy profile, computational capability, and role in IoT power management**. Compute the **per-bit transmission energy** for a sensor transmitting 1 byte over 20 m and 200 m using $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $E_{amp} = 100$ pJ/bit/m², and $n = 2$. Comment on the result. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (2 marks):** Fog Computing is a distributed architecture (Cisco, 2012) where compute, storage, and networking functions are placed *between the edge and the cloud*, providing low-latency local services.

**Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Edge Node 1] --> FN[Fog Node: Energy Manager]
    E2[Edge Node 2] --> FN
    E3[Edge Node 3] --> FN
    FN --> CL[Cloud]
    FN -->|Sleep wake schedule| E1
    FN -->|Sleep wake schedule| E2
    FN -->|Sleep wake schedule| E3
```

**Energy-Aware Orchestration (3 marks):**
- The fog node receives **residual energy reports** from each edge device.
- It maintains a global **TDMA-like schedule**, ensuring only the node with the highest residual energy transmits.
- Nodes with depleted buffers are forced into **deep sleep** until they harvest sufficient energy.
- This technique is known as **Energy-Neutral Operation (ENO)** — the average consumed energy ≤ average harvested energy.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Edge | Fog |
|---|---|---|
| Latency | < 10 ms | 5 – 50 ms |
| Scalability | Per-device | Multi-device coordinator |
| Energy Profile | mW (battery/harvested) | 1–10 W (mains or large battery) |
| Compute Capability | TinyML, fixed logic | Linux-class, ML inference |
| Role in Power Mgmt | Local sleep/wake | Cluster energy balancing |

**Computation (4 marks):**

For **1 byte = 8 bits**, $b = 8$.

$$E_{tx}(8, d) = 8 \cdot E_{elec} + 8 \cdot E_{amp} \cdot d^{2}$$

**Case 1: d = 20 m**

$$E_{tx}(8, 20) = 8 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-9}) + 8 \cdot (100 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (20)^{2}$$

$$E_{tx}(8, 20) = 400 \text{ nJ} + 8 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 400$$

$$E_{tx}(8, 20) = 400 \text{ nJ} + 320 \text{ nJ} = 720 \text{ nJ}$$

**Case 2: d = 200 m**

$$E_{tx}(8, 200) = 8 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-9}) + 8 \cdot (100 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (200)^{2}$$

$$E_{tx}(8, 200) = 400 \text{ nJ} + 8 \cdot 100 \times 10^{-12} \cdot 40000$$

$$E_{tx}(8, 200) = 400 \text{ nJ} + 32\,000 \text{ nJ} = 32\,400 \text{ nJ} = 32.4 \,\mu\text{J}$$

**Result:** Increasing the transmission distance **10× (20 m → 200 m)** increases the transmission energy by **~45×** (720 nJ → 32.4 µJ), confirming the **quadratic path-loss penalty** in free-space propagation. This justifies pushing compute to the **edge** and using **fog gateways** placed physically close to the edge cluster.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 1)**

- ✅ **Edge = at/near the device; Fog = LAN gateway; Cloud = remote data center.**
- ✅ Edge latency is **< 10 ms**; Fog latency is **5–50 ms**; Cloud latency is **100+ ms**.
- ✅ Fog is a **horizontal architecture (Cisco, 2012)**, not a replacement for edge.
- ✅ Edge computing uses **on-device filtering/compression** to reduce uplink bits.
- ✅ Fog computing acts as an **energy-aware orchestrator** for harvested IoT clusters.
- ✅ **Energy Saving Ratio (ESR) = 1 − C_edge** — directly proportional to compression.
- ✅ Heinzelman model: $E_{tx}(b, d) = b \cdot E_{elec} + b \cdot E_{amp} \cdot d^{n}$, with $n = 2$ (free space) to $n = 4$ (urban).
- ✅ Transmitting over **10× the distance** can cost **~100× the energy** (quadratic law).
- ✅ CMOS CPU energy scales with $V_{dd}^{2}$ — voltage scaling at edge saves power.
- ✅ **Energy-Neutral Operation (ENO):** Average consumed energy ≤ Average harvested energy.
- ✅ Always link your answer to **energy, latency, or bandwidth** — KTU 2024 examiners explicitly award marks for outcome-based reasoning.
- ✅ Use **Mermaid-style block diagrams** showing all three tiers in any architecture question.
- ✅ Remember: **cloud handles training; fog handles inference; edge handles actuation.**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: IoT, Fog, and Cloud Benefits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** IoT, Fog, and Cloud Benefits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Internet of Things (IoT) — The Sensing Edge Layer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** The **Internet of Things (IoT)** is a globally interconnected network of physical devices, sensors, actuators, and embedded systems — each uniquely identifiable — that collect, exchange, and act upon data over communication networks without requiring direct human-to-human or human-to-computer interaction.

### Conceptual Analogy
Think of IoT as the **"nervous system of a smart city"**. Just as nerve endings in a human body detect heat, pressure, or pain at the fingertip and send signals toward the brain, IoT sensors at the **edge** (a traffic camera, a soil moisture probe, a smartwatch) detect physical phenomena (light, temperature, motion) and convert them into electrical/digital signals for further processing.

### Key Architectural Roles of IoT Devices
- **Sensing** — Transducing physical phenomena (heat, motion, gas) into analog/digital data.
- **Actuation** — Triggering physical responses (switching a relay, rotating a motor).
- **Local Pre-processing** — Running lightweight filtering, thresholding, or compression.
- **Communication** — Forwarding data via Wi-Fi, BLE, LoRa, ZigBee, NB-IoT, etc.
- **Identity** — Each node carries a unique IPv6/IPv4 address or URI for traceability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric (IEEE / IETF):** A single IoT "thing" typically draws between **1 mW** (passive RFID tag) to **10 W** (edge gateway with onboard AI). This power envelope is the central motivation behind energy harvesting strategies covered later in PECIT746.

---

## 1.2 Cloud Computing — The Global Brain Layer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** **Cloud Computing** is a model that enables ubiquitous, on-demand network access to a shared pool of configurable computing resources (servers, storage, applications, and services) that can be rapidly provisioned and released with minimal management effort (NIST SP 800-145, adapted).

### Conceptual Analogy
The cloud is like a **"central hospital's super-specialty department"** in a smart city. A local clinic (the IoT edge) handles routine check-ups (sensing), but critical cases — cancer diagnosis, genome analysis, long-term patient history mining — are referred to the super-specialty hospital (the cloud). The hospital has virtually **unlimited diagnostic machines, storage archives, and specialist doctors**, but it is **far away** (high network latency) and **expensive to consult for trivial issues**.

### Five Essential Characteristics of Cloud (NIST Model)
1. **On-Demand Self-Service** — Resources provisioned automatically.
2. **Broad Network Access** — Available over standard networks.
3. **Resource Pooling** — Multi-tenant hardware sharing.
4. **Rapid Elasticity** — Scale up/down instantly.
5. **Measured Service** — Pay-per-use metering.

---

## 1.3 Fog Computing — The Local Intelligence Layer (Cisco, 2012)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** **Fog Computing** (also called *Edge Computing* or *Mist Computing* in some texts) is a distributed computing paradigm that extends cloud capabilities to the network edge — placing compute, storage, and networking resources **between the IoT device and the cloud** — to provide low-latency, location-aware, and mobility-supportive services.

### Conceptual Analogy
Fog is like the **"district-level diagnostic lab"** that sits between the local clinic (IoT) and the super-specialty hospital (cloud). It handles **intermediate-complexity analysis** — like blood tests, X-rays, and ECG analysis — that don't need the full power of a central hospital but are too complex for the local clinic. Patients get **faster results**, the hospital isn't overloaded, and ambulances don't waste time traveling long distances.

### The Origin of the Term
The term *"Fog Computing"* was coined by **Cisco in 2012**. The metaphor: just as **fog** is a cloud that is closer to the ground, **fog computing** is cloud computing that is closer to the data source.

---

## 1.4 Unified Three-Tier View — How They Fit Together

| Tier | Location | Primary Role | Latency | Compute Power |
|------|----------|--------------|---------|---------------|
| **IoT (Edge / Mist)** | On/near the device | Sensing, fast actuation | < 1 ms | Tiny (kB RAM) |
| **Fog** | LAN / Gateway / On-prem server | Aggregation, local analytics | 1–10 ms | Moderate (GB RAM) |
| **Cloud** | Internet data center | Big-data analytics, AI/ML training, archival | 50–500 ms | Massive (TB RAM, GPUs) |

> [!TIP]
> **Energy Harvesting Note (Course Context):** Each tier has *different* energy constraints. The IoT tier is **energy-starved** (often battery/solar-powered), the fog tier is **mains-powered but thermally constrained**, and the cloud tier is **power-rich but carbon-expensive**. The course PECIT746 dedicates significant study to the IoT-tier energy problem.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Continuum Model — Cloud → Fog → Edge → IoT

Modern IoT architectures are best understood as a **continuum of compute resources** distributed across the network. The **Why** behind this distribution is driven by four engineering tensions:

### The "Why" of Layered Architecture
- **Latency Pressure** — Autonomous vehicles, robotic surgery, and industrial closed-loop control require **end-to-end latency below 10 ms**. A round-trip to the cloud (often > 100 ms) is unacceptable.
- **Bandwidth Economics** — A single autonomous car generates ~**4 TB/day**. Uploading raw sensor streams to the cloud is prohibitively expensive. Local filtering (at the fog) reduces uploads by 90–99%.
- **Energy Budget** — Wireless transmission is the single largest energy consumer in battery-powered IoT nodes (often **> 70%** of node energy). Local processing + summary transmission saves Joules.
- **Reliability / Privacy** — Manufacturing plants and hospitals cannot depend on internet uptime. Fog nodes provide **local autonomy** when the WAN link fails.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Benefits of Each Tier

### 2.2.1 Benefits of the IoT Tier (Edge)
- **Real-Time Response** — Microsecond-scale actuation for safety-critical loops.
- **Energy Autonomy** — Compatible with energy harvesting (solar, vibration, RF).
- **Privacy by Default** — Raw personal/biometric data never leaves the device.
- **Offline Operation** — Functions without internet (a hard requirement for remote agriculture, mines, undersea).
- **Massive Scalability** — Billions of devices can be deployed; each is self-sufficient.

### 2.2.2 Benefits of Fog Computing
- **Reduced Latency** — Brings compute within **1 network hop** of the device.
- **Bandwidth Aggregation** — Combines 1000s of sensor streams and uploads only summaries.
- **Geographic Distribution** — Fog nodes can be deployed per-region for **data sovereignty** (GDPR/India DPDP Act compliance).
- **Context Awareness** — Fog node knows *local* conditions (weather, time-of-day) better than the cloud.
- **Mobility Support** — Predictable handoff as mobile users move between fog cells.
- **Heterogeneous Protocol Bridging** — Translates between BLE/ZigBee/Modbus on one side and MQTT/HTTPS on the other.

### 2.2.3 Benefits of Cloud Computing
- **Virtually Unlimited Resources** — Elastic provisioning of CPUs, GPUs, TPUs.
- **Big-Data Analytics** — Hadoop/Spark clusters process petabytes.
- **Global AI/ML Training** — Train deep models on aggregated datasets from millions of devices.
- **Long-Term Archival** — Cheap cold storage (S3 Glacier, ~$0.001/GB/month).
- **Centralized Device Management** — OTA firmware updates, fleet monitoring dashboards.
- **High Availability** — Service-level agreements of 99.99% via geographic redundancy.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following equations are central to analyzing latency, energy, and bandwidth trade-offs in IoT–Fog–Cloud systems. They are **frequently tested** in KTU ESE papers of PECIT746.

### 2.3.1 End-to-End Latency

$$
T_{\text{total}} \;=\; T_{\text{sense}} \;+\; T_{\text{proc,edge}} \;+\; T_{\text{tx,local}} \;+\; T_{\text{proc,fog}} \;+\; T_{\text{tx,wan}} \;+\; T_{\text{proc,cloud}}
$$

**Units:** milliseconds (ms). **Engineering Utility:** Quantifies whether a workload can be placed at edge, fog, or cloud to meet a service-level objective.

### 2.3.2 Energy Cost of Wireless Transmission

$$
E_{\text{tx}} \;=\; P_{\text{tx}} \,\cdot\, t_{\text{tx}} \;\approx\; \big(P_{\text{tx,elec}} + P_{\text{amp}}\big)\,\cdot\, t_{\text{tx}}
$$

For short-range (Friis model):

$$
P_{\text{amp}} \;\approx\; P_{\text{tx0}}\,\cdot\, d^{2}
$$

For long-range (two-ray ground):

$$
P_{\text{amp}} \;\approx\; P_{\text{tx0}}\,\cdot\, d^{4}
$$

**Units:** Joules (J). **Engineering Utility:** Shows why *minimizing transmitted bits* via edge/fog compression is critical for battery lifetime.

### 2.3.3 Bandwidth Reduction Ratio (Fog Benefit)

$$
\eta_{\text{BW}} \;=\; 1 \;-\; \dfrac{B_{\text{wan}}}{B_{\text{sensor}}}
$$

where $B_{\text{sensor}}$ is the raw sensor data rate and $B_{\text{wan}}$ is the post-fog upload rate. Typical values: **0.85 ≤ η ≤ 0.99** when fog performs feature extraction.

### 2.3.4 Device Battery Lifetime

$$
L_{\text{bat}} \;=\; \dfrac{E_{\text{bat}}}{\overline{P_{\text{node}}}}
$$

where $\overline{P_{\text{node}}}$ is the time-averaged power consumption. For a solar-harvested node:

$$
\overline{P_{\text{node}}} \;=\; P_{\text{harvest}} \;-\; P_{\text{loss,storage}} \;-\; P_{\text{load}}
$$

A node is **energy-neutral** when $\overline{P_{\text{node}}} = 0$.

### 2.3.5 Cloud Cost-per-Query (Pay-as-you-go)

$$
C_{\text{cloud}} \;=\; \sum_{i}\, n_{i}\,\cdot\, u_{i}
$$

where $n_i$ is the number of invocations of resource type $i$ and $u_i$ is its unit cost. Fog can **offload** expensive cloud functions when:

$$
C_{\text{fog}} + E_{\text{offload}}\,\cdot\, C_{\text{energy}} \;<\; C_{\text{cloud}}
$$

### 2.3.6 Summary Table — Formula Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Unit | Engineering Use |
|--------|----------|--------------|------------------|
| $T_{\text{total}}$ | End-to-end latency | ms | SLA compliance |
| $E_{\text{tx}}$ | Transmit energy | J | Battery sizing |
| $\eta_{\text{BW}}$ | Bandwidth reduction | dimensionless | Fog placement decision |
| $L_{\text{bat}}$ | Battery lifetime | hours/days/years | Energy harvesting design |
| $C_{\text{cloud}}$ | Cloud OPEX | USD/query | Offloading policy |
| $P_{\text{harvest}}$ | Harvested power | mW | Source selection |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Substitution Rule for Tables:** When writing absolute values or norms inside a table row, **never use** the pipe character `\vert`. Instead, write `\\vert x \\vert` or `\\lvert x \\rvert`. Example: $\lvert V_{\text{dd}} \rvert$.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Grid (India's UDAY Scheme)** — Millions of smart meters at the IoT tier; state-level fog servers aggregate for demand-response; central cloud for grid analytics.
- **Predictive Maintenance (Siemens, GE)** — Vibration sensors (IoT) → on-prem fog (FFT + anomaly detection) → cloud (fleet-wide ML model training).
- **Smart Agriculture** — Soil sensors (IoT) → farm-gateway fog (irrigation scheduling) → cloud (multi-farm yield prediction).
- **Healthcare Wearables** — ECG patch (IoT) → smartphone fog (real-time arrhythmia alert) → cloud (longitudinal cardiology analytics).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimal Placement of a Compute Task (Edge vs. Fog vs. Cloud)

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup
Consider a single IoT node that must execute a task of **$W$ CPU cycles** with input data of size **$D$ bits** and acceptable output size **$D'$ bits**. The node has three placement options:
- **(a) Local Edge:** Execute on the IoT node's own MCU.
- **(b) Fog Offload:** Transmit $D$ bits to a fog node, get $D'$ bits back.
- **(c) Cloud Offload:** Transmit $D$ bits to the cloud, get $D'$ bits back.

We want to derive the **decision rule** that minimizes total energy consumption.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Energy of local (edge) execution**

$$
E_{\text{edge}} \;=\; P_{\text{proc,edge}} \cdot t_{\text{exec}} \;=\; P_{\text{proc,edge}} \cdot \frac{W}{f_{\text{clk,edge}}}
$$

where $f_{\text{clk,edge}}$ is the MCU clock frequency. For CMOS, $P_{\text{proc,edge}} \approx C_{\text{eff}} \cdot V_{\text{dd}}^{2} \cdot f_{\text{clk,edge}}$, giving:

$$
E_{\text{edge}} \;\approx\; C_{\text{eff}} \cdot V_{\text{dd}}^{2} \cdot W
$$

> This is independent of clock frequency — a counter-intuitive but canonical result in energy-aware computing (the *energy per cycle* is fixed by $V_{\text{dd}}$ and switching capacitance).

**Step 2 — Energy of offloading to fog**

$$
E_{\text{fog}} \;=\; E_{\text{tx,up}}(D, d_{\text{fog}}) \;+\; E_{\text{proc,fog}} \;+\; E_{\text{tx,down}}(D', d_{\text{fog}})
$$

where $d_{\text{fog}}$ is the (short) distance to the fog node. Using the Friis model for $d_{\text{fog}}$:

$$
E_{\text{tx,up}} \;\approx\; \big(P_{\text{tx0}} + P_{\text{amp0}} \cdot d_{\text{fog}}^{2}\big) \cdot \frac{D}{R_{\text{fog}}}
$$

Since fog servers are mains-powered, $E_{\text{proc,fog}}$ is **borne by the infrastructure**, not the IoT node — so from the IoT node's perspective:

$$
E_{\text{fog,node}} \;\approx\; \big(P_{\text{tx0}} + P_{\text{amp0}} \cdot d_{\text{fog}}^{2}\big) \cdot \frac{D}{R_{\text{fog}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Energy of offloading to cloud**

$$
E_{\text{cloud,node}} \;\approx\; \big(P_{\text{tx0}} + P_{\text{amp0}} \cdot d_{\text{cloud}}^{4}\big) \cdot \frac{D}{R_{\text{cloud}}}
$$

Note the use of the $d^{4}$ two-ray model — IoT-to-cloud links typically pass through multiple reflections and the long path is closer to planar-earth geometry.

**Step 4 — Decision Rule**

The IoT node should **offload to fog** if:

$$
E_{\text{fog,node}} \;<\; E_{\text{edge}}
$$

which simplifies (assuming $D \gg D'$) to:

$$
\big(P_{\text{tx0}} + P_{\text{amp0}} \cdot d_{\text{fog}}^{2}\big) \cdot \frac{D}{R_{\text{fog}}} \;<\; C_{\text{eff}} \cdot V_{\text{dd}}^{2} \cdot W
$$

Similarly, **offload to cloud** if:

$$
\big(P_{\text{tx0}} + P_{\text{amp0}} \cdot d_{\text{cloud}}^{4}\big) \cdot \frac{D}{R_{\text{cloud}}} \;<\; C_{\text{eff}} \cdot V_{\text{dd}}^{2} \cdot W
$$

**Step 5 — Comparative Analysis**

Since $d_{\text{fog}} \ll d_{\text{cloud}}$ and $R_{\text{fog}} \approx R_{\text{cloud}}$ in modern networks, the **fog offload energy is dramatically lower** than cloud offload energy — often by **3–4 orders of magnitude** for outdoor deployments.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical illustration:** Suppose $P_{\text{tx0}} = 50$ mW, $P_{\text{amp0}} = 1.5 \cdot 10^{-6}$ mW/m², $d_{\text{fog}} = 10$ m, $d_{\text{cloud}} = 10$ km, $R = 1$ Mbps, $D = 1$ Mbit, $C_{\text{eff}} = 10^{-9}$ J/cycle, $V_{\text{dd}} = 3.3$ V, $W = 10^{9}$ cycles.
>
> Fog transmit energy ≈ $50 \cdot 10^{-3} \cdot 1 = 50$ mJ.
> Edge execution energy ≈ $10^{-9} \cdot (3.3)^{2} \cdot 10^{9} = 10.89$ J.
> → **Fog wins by ~200×.**

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Benefit Trade-Off Across Tiers

| Benefit / Metric | IoT Edge | Fog | Cloud | Best Tier |
|------------------|:--------:|:---:|:-----:|:---------:|
| Latency | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | Edge |
| Energy efficiency (node-side) | ★★ (if awake) | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | Fog/Cloud |
| Compute power | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | Cloud |
| Storage capacity | ★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | Cloud |
| Privacy / Data sovereignty | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ | Edge |
| Bandwidth savings (aggregation) | ★ | ★★★★★ | — | Fog |
| Global AI/ML training | — | ★ | ★★★★★ | Cloud |
| Autonomy / Offline survival | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★ | Edge/Fog |
| Geographic distribution | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | Fog |
| Cost-per-device (CAPEX) | ★★★★★ (cheap) | ★★★ | ★★★★★ (OPEX) | Edge/Cloud |

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Fog's Bandwidth-Saving Effect

**Setup:** $N$ IoT nodes stream at rate $R_{i}$ bits/s each to a fog aggregator. The fog runs a feature-extraction algorithm that compresses the stream by a factor $K \geq 1$.

$$
\eta_{\text{BW}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{B_{\text{wan}}}{B_{\text{sensor}}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} R_{i}/K}{N \cdot \bar{R}} \;=\; 1 - \frac{1}{K}
$$

For an ECG signal sampled at 250 Hz with 16-bit resolution:

$$
R_{\text{sensor}} \;=\; 250 \cdot 16 \;=\; 4{,}000 \; \text{bits/s}
$$

After fog extracts R-R intervals (1 number/min = 0.0167 bits/s equivalent):

$$
K \;=\; \frac{4000}{0.0167} \;\approx\; 240{,}000
$$

Hence $\eta_{\text{BW}} \approx 0.999996$ — a virtually perfect compression, exactly the kind of win that motivates fog architectures for body-area networks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Three-Tier IoT–Fog–Cloud Architecture (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the complete functional flow of data, control signals, and energy across the three tiers. Node IDs follow the alphanumeric-prefix rule; labels contain no markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DEV["DEVICE TIER - IOT EDGE"]
        S1["Sensor 1 - Temperature"]
        S2["Sensor 2 - Vibration"]
        S3["Sensor 3 - Humidity"]
        ACT["Actuator Relay"]
        MCU["Edge MCU - ARM Cortex M0"]
        HARV["Energy Harvester - Solar PV"]
        BATT["Li-ion Battery 3.7V 600mAh"]
    end

    subgraph FOG["FOG TIER - GATEWAY LAYER"]
        GW["Fog Gateway - Raspberry Pi Class"]
        AGG["Data Aggregator Buffer 256MB"]
        ANA["Local Analytics Engine"]
        FLT["Threshold and Anomaly Filter"]
        PROT["Protocol Bridge BLE to MQTT"]
    end

    subgraph CLD["CLOUD TIER - GLOBAL LAYER"]
        API["REST API Gateway"]
        DB["Time Series Database InfluxDB"]
        ML["AI ML Training Cluster GPU Pool"]
        DASH["Web Dashboard Grafana"]
        OTA["OTA Firmware Service"]
    end

    S1 --> MCU
    S2 --> MCU
    S3 --> MCU
    HARV --> BATT
    BATT --> MCU
    MCU --> ACT
    MCU -->|Raw BLE Stream| GW
    GW --> AGG --> FLT --> ANA
    ANA --> PROT
    ANA -->|Summary Metrics| GW
    GW -->|Encrypted MQTT TLS| API
    API --> DB
    DB --> ML
    ML --> DASH
    OTA -->|Firmware Push| GW
    GW -->|OTA Relay| MCU
```

---

## 4.2 Data Path Decision Flow — Where Should a Task Run?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["New Task Arrives at IoT Node"] --> B{"Latency SLA less than 10 ms"}
    B -- YES --> C["Execute at EDGE - Local MCU"]
    B -- NO --> D{"Cloud Connectivity Available"}
    D -- NO --> E["Execute at FOG - Local Gateway"]
    D -- YES --> F{"Computation Heavy - Needs GPU or TB RAM"}
    F -- YES --> G["Offload to CLOUD - AWS or Azure"]
    F -- NO --> H{"Data Size greater than 1 MB and Bandwidth scarce"}
    H -- YES --> E
    H -- NO --> I{"Privacy Sensitive - PII or PHI"}
    I -- YES --> E
    I -- NO --> G
```

---

## 4.3 Energy Flow Across Tiers (Vertical Power Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1["Harvested Power - 100 mW Solar"] --> B1["Battery Buffer"]
    B1 --> N1["IoT Node - 10 mW Avg"]
    N1 -- TX 1 mW --> F1["Fog Gateway - 5 W Mains"]
    F1 -- TX 100 mW --> C1["Cloud DC - 10 MW Facility"]
    C1 -. AI Result Back .-> F1
    F1 -. Control Command .-> N1
    N1 --> A1["Actuator - 50 mW Peak"]
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this:** The harvested power at the IoT tier (mW scale) is **4–5 orders of magnitude smaller** than the cloud facility's draw. This power asymmetry is exactly why energy harvesting is *only* relevant at the IoT tier — fog and cloud are infrastructure-tier problems, not harvesting-tier problems.

---

## 4.4 Benefit-Centric Comparison Matrix (Schematic View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph B1["Latency Benefits"]
        L1["Edge - Microsecond"]
        L2["Fog - Millisecond"]
        L3["Cloud - 100ms Plus"]
    end
    subgraph B2["Compute Benefits"]
        C1["Edge - kB RAM"]
        C2["Fog - GB RAM"]
        C3["Cloud - TB RAM plus GPU"]
    end
    subgraph B3["Energy Benefits"]
        E1["Edge - MicroJoule"]
        E2["Fog - Mains Powered"]
        E3["Cloud - MegaWatt Facility"]
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Style]**
**Differentiate between Cloud Computing and Fog Computing. List any three advantages of deploying a Fog layer between IoT devices and the Cloud.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
| Aspect | Cloud Computing | Fog Computing |
|--------|-----------------|---------------|
| **Location** | Centralized internet data centers | Distributed, on-premise or LAN |
| **Latency** | High (50–500 ms) | Low (1–10 ms) |
| **Proximity to device** | Far (multi-hop WAN) | One hop from devices |
| **Resource scale** | Virtually unlimited | Moderate (limited per node) |
| **Primary role** | Big-data analytics, AI training, archival | Aggregation, local analytics, protocol bridging |

**Three Fog advantages (any 3):**
1. **Reduced latency** for time-sensitive applications (closed-loop control, AR/VR).
2. **Bandwidth optimization** through local data aggregation and filtering.
3. **Improved reliability** — system continues to operate when the WAN link fails.
4. **Enhanced privacy** — sensitive data can be processed locally without leaving the premises.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, KTU 2024 Scheme Style]**
**Explain the term "Energy Harvesting" in the context of IoT and state its primary benefits for edge devices.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks):
**Definition:** Energy harvesting is the process by which ambient energy sources — solar, thermal gradients, vibration, RF, wind — are captured and converted into usable electrical energy to power IoT edge devices, eliminating or reducing dependence on batteries.

**Primary benefits (any 3):**
1. **Extended / indefinite device lifetime** — no manual battery replacement in inaccessible deployments.
2. **Enables deployment in harsh/remote locations** — undersea, inside walls, on rotating machinery.
3. **Reduces maintenance cost** for large-scale fleets (smart cities, structural monitoring).
4. **Environmental sustainability** — fewer battery disposals, lower carbon footprint.
5. **Enables energy-neutral operation** — average harvested power ≥ average consumption.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Topic-Specific Warning:**
> - When listing *benefits*, students often write vague marketing-style answers ("it is fast, scalable, secure"). This fetches **0 marks** under KTU's keyword-based valuation. Always pair each benefit with a **quantitative metric** (e.g., "latency reduced from 200 ms to 5 ms" or "bandwidth reduced by 95%").
> - In derivation questions, students often skip the **assumption statements** (e.g., Friis vs. two-ray ground model). Under KTU's step-marking scheme, **assumptions carry 1–2 marks**.
> - When drawing the IoT–Fog–Cloud architecture, students frequently omit the **energy/harvesting block** in the IoT tier — but this course (PECIT746) **mandates** its inclusion. Up to **3 marks** may be lost.
> - Always state **units** in numerical answers. A correct formula with missing units gets partial credit only.

---

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]**

**A1. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the **three-tier IoT–Fog–Cloud architecture**. Discuss the role of each tier and the typical latency, compute power, and energy profile of devices in each tier. **(7 Marks)**

**A1. (b)** A remote IoT weather station harvests solar power at an average rate of **80 mW**. Its MCU + sensor + radio consumes an average of **45 mW**. If a 2000 mAh, 3.7 V Li-ion battery is used as a buffer, calculate:
- (i) The net daily energy surplus or deficit in Joules.
- (ii) The maximum battery-only runtime (in days) if harvesting suddenly stops. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Block Diagram & Tier Roles (7 Marks):**

The three-tier architecture consists of:

**1. IoT / Edge Tier (3 Marks):**
- Comprises sensors, actuators, microcontrollers (e.g., ARM Cortex M0/M4), and energy harvesters.
- Roles: sensing the physical world, local pre-processing (filtering, thresholding), and actuation.
- Latency: **< 1 ms** for local actuation.
- Compute: limited (kB RAM, tens of MHz clock).
- Energy: **1 mW – 100 mW** (often battery or harvest-powered).

**2. Fog Tier (2 Marks):**
- Comprises gateways, on-prem servers, or edge micro-datacenters.
- Roles: data aggregation, protocol translation (BLE → MQTT), local analytics, reduced-latency services.
- Latency: **1–10 ms**.
- Compute: moderate (GB RAM, multi-core CPUs, sometimes small GPUs).
- Energy: typically **mains-powered** (5–50 W per node).

**3. Cloud Tier (2 Marks):**
- Comprises hyperscale data centers (AWS, Azure, GCP).
- Roles: big-data analytics, AI/ML training, archival storage, fleet management, OTA firmware.
- Latency: **50–500 ms**.
- Compute: massive (TB RAM, GPU/TPU clusters).
- Energy: **megawatt-scale** facilities.

*Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes for the visual block diagram (3 marks for neat, fully-labeled diagram with all blocks).*

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- $P_{\text{harvest}} = 80$ mW
- $P_{\text{load}} = 45$ mW
- Battery capacity: $Q = 2000$ mAh, $V = 3.7$ V

**Step 1 — Net Power (1 Mark):**
$$
P_{\text{net}} \;=\; P_{\text{harvest}} - P_{\text{load}} \;=\; 80 - 45 \;=\; 35 \; \text{mW}
$$

**Step 2 — Daily Net Energy (2 Marks):**
$$
E_{\text{net,day}} \;=\; P_{\text{net}} \cdot t_{\text{day}} \;=\; 35 \cdot 10^{-3} \,\text{W} \cdot 86400 \,\text{s} \;=\; 3024 \,\text{J}
$$
**Surplus of 3024 J/day** — the node is energy-positive; the battery will gradually fill if not full.

**Step 3 — Total Battery Energy (2 Marks):**
$$
E_{\text{bat}} \;=\; Q \cdot V \cdot 3600 \;=\; 2 \,\text{Ah} \cdot 3.7 \,\text{V} \cdot 3600 \,\text{s} \;=\; 26640 \,\text{J}
$$

**Step 4 — Battery-Only Runtime (2 Marks):**
$$
L_{\text{bat}} \;=\; \frac{E_{\text{bat}}}{P_{\text{load}}} \;=\; \frac{26640}{45 \cdot 10^{-3}} \;\approx\; 592000 \,\text{s} \;\approx\; 6.85 \,\text{days}
$$

**Answer:** Net surplus = **+3024 J/day**; battery-only runtime ≈ **6.85 days**.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model]**

**B1. (a)** Compare and contrast **Cloud Computing, Fog Computing, and Edge Computing** along the dimensions of architecture, latency, scalability, security, and ideal use cases. **(7 Marks)**

**B1. (b)** Derive the energy consumption expression for an IoT node that **offloads a task of $W$ CPU cycles** to a fog node versus executing it locally on the MCU. State the conditions under which fog offloading is more energy-efficient than local execution. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Comparison Table (7 Marks — 1 mark per correct filled dimension × 5 dimensions, 2 marks for "ideal use cases" statement):**

| Dimension | Edge Computing | Fog Computing | Cloud Computing |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|-----------------|
| **Architecture** | Distributed on devices | Distributed at gateways/LAN | Centralized in data centers |
| **Latency** | < 1 ms (real-time) | 1–10 ms (near real-time) | 50–500 ms (best-effort) |
| **Scalability** | Limited per device | Scales per fog node cluster | Virtually unlimited |
| **Security** | High (data stays local) | Medium-High (LAN-controlled) | Medium (depends on provider + transit) |
| **Ideal Use Case** | Closed-loop control, wearables | Smart factory, smart grid substations | Big-data analytics, AI training |

**Use-case elaboration (2 marks):**
- **Edge** is best when *latency and privacy dominate* — autonomous vehicles, implanted medical devices.
- **Fog** is best when *aggregation and protocol-bridging dominate* — building automation, smart farming.
- **Cloud** is best when *scale and analytics dominate* — fraud detection across millions of transactions, genome research.

**(b) Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Assumptions (1 Mark):**
- CMOS-based MCU; clock-independent energy per cycle model applies.
- Fog transmit uses short-range Friis path-loss ($d^{2}$ dependence).
- Downlink payload $D' \ll D$ (uplink-dominated), so downlink energy is neglected.

**Step 1 — Local execution energy (2 Marks):**
$$
E_{\text{edge}} \;=\; C_{\text{eff}} \cdot V_{\text{dd}}^{2} \cdot W
$$

**Step 2 — Fog-offload energy from the IoT node's perspective (2 Marks):**
$$
E_{\text{fog,node}} \;\approx\; \big(P_{\text{tx0}} + P_{\text{amp0}} \cdot d_{\text{fog}}^{2}\big) \cdot \frac{D}{R_{\text{fog}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Decision inequality (1 Mark):**
$$
E_{\text{fog,node}} \;<\; E_{\text{edge}}
$$

**Step 4 — Conditions (1 Mark):**
- The task must be **compute-heavy** ($W$ large) so that $E_{\text{edge}}$ is significant.
- The data input $D$ must be **small** (or already-compressed) so that $E_{\text{tx}}$ is small.
- The distance $d_{\text{fog}}$ must be **short** (Wi-Fi/BLE/LoRa within 10–100 m).
- The radio must support a **high enough $R_{\text{fog}}$** to keep transmission time short.

> **Final inequality** (1 mark):
> $$
> \big(P_{\text{tx0}} + P_{\text{amp0}} \cdot d_{\text{fog}}^{2}\big) \cdot \frac{D}{R_{\text{fog}}} \;<\; C_{\text{eff}} \cdot V_{\text{dd}}^{2} \cdot W
> $$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **three-tier model** (IoT → Fog → Cloud) is the canonical architecture for modern IoT systems.
- ✅ **IoT / Edge tier** = sensing + actuation; **< 1 ms** latency; **mW** power; energy-harvesting-relevant.
- ✅ **Fog tier** = aggregation + local analytics; **1–10 ms** latency; **mains-powered**.
- ✅ **Cloud tier** = big-data + AI/ML training + archival; **50–500 ms** latency; **MW-scale** facilities.
- ✅ **Fog was coined by Cisco (2012)** — a frequent 1-mark question.
- ✅ **Energy asymmetry** across tiers is what makes *energy harvesting* meaningful *only* at the IoT tier.
- ✅ Wireless transmission dominates IoT node energy: **$E_{\text{tx}} \propto d^{2}$ (short) or $d^{4}$ (long)**.
- ✅ Fog's primary quantitative benefit is **bandwidth reduction** $\eta_{\text{BW}} = 1 - 1/K$, often **> 95%**.
- ✅ Decision rule for offloading: offload when **transmit energy < local compute energy**.
- ✅ In KTU ESE answers, **always include numerical units, assumptions, and a labeled diagram**.
- ✅ **NIST Five Characteristics of Cloud** = On-demand, Broad access, Pooling, Elasticity, Measured service.
- ✅ **Latency-pressure applications** (autonomous vehicles, robotic surgery) cannot use the cloud — they need edge/fog.
- ✅ **Energy-neutral operation** = harvested power = load power averaged over the duty cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Working Procedure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Working Procedure # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition The **Working Procedure of an Energy Harvesting System in IoT** refers to the systematic, end-to-end sequence of physical and electronic operations that converts ambient, otherwise wasted, environmental energy (solar, thermal, mechanical, RF, or vibrational) into a regulated, stable, and storable electrical form suitable for energizing the ultra-low-power circuitry of IoT sensor nodes. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this working procedure is a **four-stage transducer–conditioning–storage–management chain**: > [!IMPORTANT] > **Energy Source → Transducer (Harvesting Element) → Power Conditioning Circuit (PCC) → Energy Storage / Direct Load (with Power Management Unit - PMU)** Every energy harvesting system, irrespective of the ambient source, must traverse these four functional stages. The efficiency, output voltage levels, and load matching characteristics differ across sources, but the **functional architecture remains invariant**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The Rainwater Harvesting System on a Rooftop** > > Think of an IoT energy harvester exactly like a **rainwater harvesting setup** on a Kerala-style tiled roof: > > 1. **Rain (Ambient Energy)** — The sun, vibration, heat, or RF waves that constantly surround the IoT node but are "going to waste." > 2. **Roof + Gutter (Transducer)** — Captures the rain and channels it. The transducer (solar cell, piezoelectric cantilever, thermoelectric generator, or RF antenna) captures the ambient energy. > 3. **Filter + Storage Tank (Power Conditioning + Storage)** — The collected water is dirty, so it passes through a filter (rectifier + DC-DC converter) and is stored in an overhead tank (battery or supercapacitor). > 4. **Tap with Pressure Regulator (Power Management Unit)** — The tap only releases water when needed, at the right pressure. The PMU enables **duty-cycled** delivery to the IoT sensor, maximizing battery life. > > Just as a rooftop system gives a home **energy independence**, an energy harvester gives an IoT node **battery independence** — the entire purpose of PECIT746. ## 1.3 Why "Working Procedure" Matters in KTU Examinations | Aspect | Significance | |---|---| | **Module Outcome (CO2)** | Maps directly to "Illustrate the working of various energy harvesting transducers and their interface circuits" | | **Bloom's Level** | L3 – Apply / L4 – Analyze | | **Typical Question Form** | "Explain with block diagram the working procedure of XYZ energy harvester" (14 marks) | | **Valuation Weight** | Block diagram (4 marks) + Stage explanation (6 marks) + Formula/Application (4 marks) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Universal Constants to Memorize:** > - Boltzmann constant **k_B = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K** > - Electron charge **q = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C** > - Standard solar insolation **G = 1000 W/m²** (AM1.5G) > - Seebeck coefficient unit **μV/K** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Stage-by-Stage Operational Logic ### **Stage 1 — Energy Source Identification & Characterization** - The first sub-step is **surveying the available ambient energy** at the deployment site of the IoT node. - Indoor IoT nodes typically rely on **RF (≈ 0.01–1 mW/cm²)** and **vibration (≈ 0.1–10 mW/cm³)**. - Outdoor nodes (agricultural sensors in Kerala) primarily use **solar (≈ 10–100 mW/cm²)** and **thermal gradients (≈ 1–10 mW/cm²)**. - The harvester topology is selected *only after* this characterization. > [!NOTE] > **Power Density Rule of Thumb (KTU High-Yield):** > Solar (outdoor) > Piezo (machine-mounted) > Thermoelectric > RF (indoor WiFi) ### **Stage 2 — Transduction (Energy Conversion)** - The transducer converts one form of energy directly into electrical energy. - Each transducer obeys a **specific physical principle**: - Photovoltaic cell → **photovoltaic effect** (photon → electron-hole pair) - Piezoelectric cantilever → **piezoelectric effect** (strain → charge) - Thermoelectric Generator (TEG) → **Seebeck effect** (ΔT → V) - RF antenna + rectifier → **rectenna principle** (EM wave → AC → DC) ### **Stage 3 — Power Conditioning Circuit (PCC)** - Raw transducer output is **unregulated** — it can be AC (piezo, RF), very low voltage (mV range for TEG, RF), or variable (solar under clouds). - The PCC performs three sub-functions: 1. **Rectification** — AC to DC (bridge rectifier / Schottky / synchronous MOSFET) 2. **Voltage Boosting / Step-Down** — DC-DC converter (boost for low-voltage harvesters; buck for solar MPPT) 3. **Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT)** — For solar: Perturb & Observe (P&O) or Incremental Conductance algorithm matches load impedance to source impedance. ### **Stage 4 — Energy Storage & Power Management** - Output is stored in either a **rechargeable battery (Li-ion, Li-Po, thin-film)** or a **supercapacitor**. - Supercapacitors are preferred for high-cycle, short-burst applications (wireless sensor transmissions). - The **Power Management Unit (PMU)** schedules the load: the sensor sleeps, wakes, harvests, transmits, and sleeps again — a duty cycle that can be as low as **0.01% to 1%**. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | Used For | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $P_{out} = \eta \cdot P_{in}$ | Harvested power = efficiency × input power | W (Watts) | Generic harvester | | 2 | $V_{oc} = S \cdot \Delta T$ | TEG open-circuit voltage (Seebeck) | V | Thermoelectric | | 3 | $P_{TEG,max} = \dfrac{S^{2} \cdot \Delta T^{2}}{4 R_{int}}$ | Max TEG power transfer | W | Thermoelectric | | 4 | $V_{piezo} = d_{33} \cdot \sigma \cdot g_{33} \cdot t$ | Piezo voltage from stress | V | Piezoelectric | | 5 | $P_{solar} = \eta_{PV} \cdot A \cdot G$ | Solar cell power output | W | Photovoltaic | | 6 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Fill Factor of solar cell | dimensionless | Solar MPPT | | 7 | $V_{out,boost} = V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{1}{1-D}$ | Boost converter output | V | DC-DC stage | | 8 | $E_{cap} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ | Energy stored in supercapacitor | J | Storage | | 9 | $P_{RF,rec} = \dfrac{V_{oc,ant}^{2}}{4 R_{ant}}$ | Max RF harvested power | W | RF harvesting | | 10 | $\eta_{rect} = \dfrac{P_{DC,out}}{P_{RF,in}}$ | Rectifier efficiency | % | RF rectenna | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Notation Note:** Vertical bars in math (e.g., absolute value) are written as $\vert x \vert$ to preserve markdown table integrity. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility -
