**Smart Agriculture (Kerala context):** Soil moisture + temperature nodes in Wayanad tea estates harvest from **mini solar panels + TEGs** (day-night continuity). - **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Predictive maintenance sensors on rotating machinery use **piezoelectric cantilevers** tuned to machine vibration frequencies. - **Smart Cities:** Traffic light monitoring uses **RF harvesting** from ambient GSM/LoRa signals. - **Wearable/Implantable Biomedical:** Human body heat drives a TEG (wrist-watch sized) generating **μW–mW** for ECG patches. - **Structural Health Monitoring (SHM):** Bridge vibration harvesters feed directly into strain-gauge IoT nodes. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Power Transfer for a Thermoelectric Generator (TEG) **Given:** A TEG with internal Seebeck voltage $V_{oc} = S \cdot \Delta T$ and internal resistance $R_{int}$ connected to a load $R_L$. **To Prove:** The maximum power delivered to the load is $P_{max} = \dfrac{S^{2} \cdot \Delta T^{2}}{4 R_{int}}$, and this occurs when $R_L = R_{int}$. **Step 1 — Voltage Divider Across the Load** The voltage appearing across the load resistance is given by the standard voltage divider rule: $$ V_L = V_{oc} \cdot \dfrac{R_L}{R_{int} + R_L} $$ **Step 2 — Power Dissipated in the Load** The instantaneous electrical power delivered to the load is: $$ P_L = \dfrac{V_L^{2}}{R_L} $$ **Step 3 — Substitute $V_L$ from Step 1** $$ P_L = \dfrac{1}{R_L} \cdot \left[ V_{oc} \cdot \dfrac{R_L}{R_{int} + R_L} \right]^{2} $$ **Step 4 — Simplify the Square** $$ P_L = \dfrac{V_{oc}^{2} \cdot R_L^{2}}{R_L \cdot (R_{int} + R_L)^{2}} = \dfrac{V_{oc}^{2} \cdot R_L}{(R_{int} + R_L)^{2}} $$ **Step 5 — Differentiate w.r.t. $R_L$ and Set to Zero** $$ \dfrac{dP_L}{dR_L} = V_{oc}^{2} \cdot \dfrac{(R_{int} + R_L)^{2} - R_L \cdot 2(R_{int} + R_L)}{(R_{int} + R_L)^{4}} = 0 $$ The numerator simplifies to: $$ (R_{int} + R_L) - 2 R_L = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_L = R_{int} $$ **Step 6 — Substitute $R_L = R_{int}$ Back into the Power Equation** $$ P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{oc}^{2} \cdot R_{int}}{(R_{int} + R_{int})^{2}} = \dfrac{V_{oc}^{2} \cdot R_{int}}{4 R_{int}^{2}} = \dfrac{V_{oc}^{2}}{4 R_{int}} $$ **Step 7 — Substitute $V_{oc} = S \cdot \Delta T$** $$ P_{TEG,max} = \dfrac{(S \cdot \Delta T)^{2}}{4 R_{int}} = \dfrac{S^{2} \cdot \Delta T^{2}}{4 R_{int}} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** This is the classical **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem** applied to harvesters. It is the *theoretical foundation* of MPPT in solar, TEG, and RF harvesters. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Boost Converter Output Voltage **Given:** An inductor-based boost converter operating in continuous conduction mode (CCM) with duty cycle $D$. **To Prove:** $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1 - D}$ **Step 1 — Switch ON (Duration = $D \cdot T$)** The inductor is connected to $V_{in}$ through the closed switch. Current ramps up: $$ V_L = V_{in} = L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt} $$ The change in inductor current during ON-time is: $$ \Delta I_{L,on} = \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot D \cdot T}{L} $$ **Step 2 — Switch OFF (Duration = $(1-D) \cdot T$)** The inductor now sees $V_{in} - V_{out}$ across it (reverse-biased diode conducts). Current ramps down: $$ V_L = V_{in} - V_{out} = L \cdot \dfrac{di}{dt} $$ The change in inductor current during OFF-time is: $$ \Delta I_{L,off} = \dfrac{(V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T}{L} $$ **Step 3 — Apply Volt-Second Balance (Steady State)** In steady state, the net change in inductor current over one full switching period is zero: $$ \Delta I_{L,on} + \Delta I_{L,off} = 0 $$ $$ \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot D \cdot T}{L} + \dfrac{(V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T}{L} = 0 $$ **Step 4 — Solve for $V_{out}$** Multiply through by $L/T$: $$ V_{in} \cdot D + (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot (1 - D) = 0 $$ Expand: $$ V_{in} D + V_{in} - V_{in} D - V_{out}(1 - D) = 0 $$ Cancel the $V_{in} D$ terms: $$ V_{in} - V_{out}(1 - D) = 0 $$ Therefore: $$ V_{out}(1 - D) = V_{in} \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1 - D} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Exam Point:** For $D = 0.5$, the output is exactly $2 V_{in}$. For $D \to 1$, $V_{out} \to \infty$ (theoretically) — this is why boost converters are *essential* for TEGs and RF harvesters producing only tens of millivolts. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — RF Rectenna Available Power **Given:** A receiving antenna with open-circuit RF voltage $V_{oc,ant}$ and antenna radiation resistance $R_{ant}$, connected to a matched rectifier. **Step 1 — Maximum Power Available at the Antenna Terminals** By Maximum Power Transfer Theorem: $$ P_{RF,avail} = \dfrac{V_{oc,ant}^{2}}{4 R_{ant}} $$ **Step 2 — Actual DC Power After Rectification** $$ P_{DC} = \eta_{rect} \cdot P_{RF,avail} = \dfrac{\eta_{rect} \cdot V_{oc,ant}^{2}}{4 R_{ant}} $$ **Step 3 — Link to Free-Space Path Loss (Friis Equation context)** The open-circuit antenna voltage is related to incident power density $S$ and effective aperture $A_e$: $$ V_{oc,ant}^{2} \propto S \cdot A_{e}^{2} / R_{ant} $$ Hence the harvested DC power scales as: $$ P_{DC} \propto S \cdot A_{e}^{2} \cdot \eta_{rect} / R_{ant} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why large apertures help:** Harvested power scales with the **square** of the antenna effective area — this is why *rectenna arrays* are used in RF harvesting. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix of Harvesters | Source | Transducer | Output Nature | Typical $\mu$W–mW Range | Conditioning Need | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Solar (outdoor)** | PV cell | DC, 0.5–0.7 V/cell | 100 μW – 100 mW | Buck + MPPT | | **Vibration** | Piezo cantilever | AC, 1–20 V | 10 μW – 10 mW | Bridge rectifier + Boost | | **Thermal** | TEG (Peltier) | DC, 10–100 mV/K | 1 μW – 10 mW | Step-up boost (×20–×100) | | **RF (WiFi/GSM)** | Rectenna | Pulsed DC, mV | 0.1 μW – 1 mW | Schottky + Multi-stage boost | | **Wind/Flow** | Mini turbine + gen | AC variable | 1 mW – 1 W | Rectifier + Buck-Boost | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Generic Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Ambient Energy Source
Solar or Thermal or Vibration or RF] --> B[Transducer
Energy Conversion Element] B --> C[Rectifier Stage
AC to DC Conversion] C --> D[DC-DC Converter
Boost or Buck-Boost] D --> E[MPPT Controller
Impedance Matching] E --> F[Energy Storage
Battery or Supercapacitor] F --> G[Power Management Unit
Duty Cycle Control] G --> H[IoT Sensor Node
Sense Process Transmit] H --> I[Sleep Mode
Low Power Wait State] I --> G ``` ## 4.2 Detailed Working Procedure with Feedback Loops ```mermaid flowchart TD src1[Sunlight] --> trans1 src2[Body Heat or Machine Heat] --> trans2 src3[Vibration or Motion] --> trans3 src4[Ambient RF or WiFi] --> trans4 trans1[Photovoltaic Cell] --> cond1 trans2[Thermoelectric Generator] --> cond2 trans3[Piezoelectric Cantilever] --> cond3 trans4[Rectenna Antenna] --> cond4 cond1[Buck Converter with MPPT] --> store cond2[Multi-stage Boost Converter] --> store cond3[Bridge Rectifier plus Boost] --> store cond4[Schottky Diode plus Boost] --> store store[Energy Storage
Supercapacitor or Thin-film Battery] --> pmu pmu[Power Management Unit PMU] --> sensor sensor[IoT Sensor Node MCU] --> tx tx[Wireless Transmitter
LoRa or BLE or WiFi] --> cloud pmu -. duty cycle gate .-> sensor sensor -. wake up trigger .-> pmu ``` ## 4.3 Solar Harvester Working Procedure — MPPT Closed Loop ```mermaid flowchart LR sun[Solar Irradiance G] --> pv[PV Cell Array] pv --> sc[Sense V and I] sc --> algo{P and O
Algorithm} algo -->|Increase D| pwm[PWM Generator] algo -->|Decrease D| pwm pwm --> conv[DC-DC Buck Converter] conv --> load[IoT Load plus Battery] load --> fb[Feedback V and I] fb --> sc ``` ## 4.4 Piezoelectric Vibration Harvester — Signal Conditioning Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR vib[Mechanical Vibration f Hz] --> cant[PZT Cantilever Beam] cant --> ac[AC Voltage Output] ac --> br[Full Bridge Rectifier] br --> filt[LC Low-pass Filter] filt --> boost[Synchronous Boost Converter] boost --> vreg[Voltage Regulator 3.3 V] vreg --> batt[Supercapacitor Storage] batt --> mcu[IoT MCU plus Sensor] ``` ## 4.5 RF Energy Harvester (Rectenna) — Multi-Stage Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR rf[Ambient RF Signal] --> ant[Patch or Dipole Antenna] ant --> match[Impedance Matching Network] match --> diode[Schottky Diode HSMS-2850] diode --> cap[Charge Pump Capacitor] cap --> boost1[First Stage Boost] boost1 --> boost2[Second Stage Boost] boost2 --> reg[LDO Regulator 1.8 V] reg --> tag[RFID Tag or Sensor Node] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Why these diagrams matter for KTU valuation:** Examiners explicitly allocate **3–4 marks** for a *correctly labelled block diagram*. Always include: source label, transducer, rectifier, DC-DC, storage, and PMU. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List the four universal stages in the working procedure of any IoT energy harvesting system.* **Model Answer:** The four universal stages are: 1. **Energy Source / Transducer Stage** — Captures ambient energy (solar, thermal, vibration, RF) and converts it to a raw electrical form using a suitable transducer. 2. **Power Conditioning Stage** — Rectifies, filters, and converts the raw output into a stable DC voltage (e.g., using bridge rectifiers, DC-DC boost/buck converters). 3. **Energy Storage Stage** — Stores the conditioned energy in a rechargeable battery, thin-film cell, or supercapacitor for use during low-harvest periods. 4. **Power Management Stage** — The PMU duty-cycles the load (sensor + MCU + radio), waking the node only for brief measurement and transmission events to minimize energy consumption. > [!NOTE] > **Mark Split:** Definition (1) + 4 stages (1.5) + Example (0.5) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]** *Define the "Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT)" stage in the working procedure of a solar energy harvester for IoT.* **Model Answer:** MPPT is a real-time control algorithm implemented in the DC-DC converter stage that continuously adjusts the converter's duty cycle so that the PV cell operates at the point on its I–V curve where the product $P = V \times I$ is **maximum** for the prevailing temperature and irradiance. Common MPPT algorithms used in IoT solar harvesters are: - **Perturb & Observe (P&O)** — perturbs the duty cycle, observes the resulting power change, and moves in the direction of increasing power. - **Incremental Conductance** — uses $dI/dV = -I/V$ as the MPP condition for higher accuracy under rapidly changing irradiance. ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]* **(a)** Draw the complete block diagram of the working procedure of a **piezoelectric vibration energy harvester** for IoT. Label every block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the expression for maximum power transfer from a piezoelectric transducer to the load, and explain why an impedance matching network is essential between the transducer and the rectifier. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Block Diagram (7 marks):** The expected diagram should contain these blocks in sequence: 1. **Mechanical Vibration Source** (e.g., machine housing, bridge, human motion) 2. **Piezoelectric Cantilever (PZT-5A or PZT-5H)** — mass-loaded cantilever tuned to source frequency 3. **AC Voltage Output** — typically 1–20 V peak at resonance 4. **Full-Wave Bridge Rectifier** (4 × Schottky diodes, e.g., 1N5817) 5. **Smoothing Capacitor / LC Filter** — removes ripple 6. **DC-DC Boost Converter** (e.g., LTC3108 IC) — steps up low rectified DC to 3.3 V or 5 V 7. **Energy Storage** — supercapacitor (e.g., 1 F, 5.5 V) or thin-film Li-ion 8. **PMU + IoT Sensor Node** — MCU + sensor + BLE/LoRa radio, duty-cycled **Sample Block Diagram (textual representation acceptable):** > Vibration → Piezo Cantilever → Bridge Rectifier → Filter → Boost Converter → Supercapacitor → PMU → IoT Node **Mark Split:** Correct sequence (3) + Correct labels (2) + Storage + PMU (2) **Part (b) — Derivation (7 marks):** The piezoelectric transducer can be modelled as an **AC voltage source $V_p$ in series with a capacitance $C_p$** (and a small series resistance $R_s$). The internal impedance is therefore **capacitive**, not resistive. **Step 1 — Power Delivered to Resistive Load $R_L$:** $$ P_L = \dfrac{V_p^{2} \cdot R_L}{(R_s + R_L)^{2} + (X_{C_p})^{2}} $$ where $X_{C_p} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f C_p}$ is the capacitive reactance. **Step 2 — For pure piezo (ignoring $R_s$):** $$ P_L = \dfrac{V_p^{2} \cdot R_L}{R_L^{2} + X_{C_p}^{2}} $$ **Step 3 — Differentiate and equate to zero:** $$ \dfrac{dP_L}{dR_L} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_L = X_{C_p} = \dfrac{1}{2 \pi f C_p} $$ **Step 4 — Maximum Power:** $$ P_{max} = \dfrac{V_p^{2}}{2 X_{C_p}} = \pi f C_p V_p^{2} $$ **Why Impedance Matching is Essential:** - Piezo sources have **high capacitive reactance** (kΩ–MΩ at low frequencies). - A direct resistive load draws negligible current due to the phase mismatch. - An **inductive matching network** (e.g., SSHI, SECE, or synchronous charge extraction) cancels the capacitive reactance, presenting a purely resistive equivalent impedance to the rectifier. - This boosts harvested power by **2× to 10×** compared to a passive bridge rectifier alone. > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls (Examiner's Note):** > 1. Drawing the blocks in wrong order (e.g., storage before conditioning) — **lose 1 mark**. > 2. Forgetting to label the **transducer type** explicitly — **lose 0.5 mark**. > 3. Not mentioning **duty cycling** in the PMU stage — **lose 1 mark**. > 4. In derivation, omitting the capacitive reactance term $X_{C_p}$ — **lose 2 marks**. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – June 2024]* **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working procedure of a **thermoelectric energy harvesting (TEG) system** for a wearable IoT health-monitoring device. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A TEG has a Seebeck coefficient $S = 200 \ \mu V/K$, internal resistance $R_{int} = 5 \ \Omega$, and is subjected to a temperature difference $\Delta T = 10 \ K$. Calculate the (i) open-circuit voltage, (ii) maximum power delivered to a matched load, and (iii) load resistance for maximum power transfer. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution **Part (a) — Block Diagram (7 marks):** The expected diagram should contain: 1. **Heat Source** (human body skin, ~33 °C) 2. **Cold Side** (ambient air, ~25 °C) — heat sink/fan attached 3. **Thermoelectric Generator (TEG module, e.g., TEC1-12706)** — generates Seebeck voltage 4. **Step-Up DC-DC Converter** (e.g., LTC3108, ADP5090) — boosts mV-level output to usable 3.3 V 5. **Voltage Regulator (LDO)** — provides stable supply rail 6. **Energy Storage** — small Li-Po battery or supercapacitor 7. **PMU + IoT Wearable** — health sensors (heart rate, SpO₂, temperature) + BLE radio **Heat flow:** Skin → TEG hot side → Peltier junctions → TEG cold side → Heat sink → Ambient **Mark Split:** Source + TEG (2) + Step-up converter (2) + Storage + PMU (2) + Example application (1) **Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Given:** - $S = 200 \ \mu V/K = 200 \times 10^{-6} \ V/K$ - $R_{int} = 5 \ \Omega$ - $\Delta T = 10 \ K$ **(i) Open-Circuit Voltage:** $$ V_{oc} = S \cdot \Delta T = (200 \times 10^{-6}) \times 10 $$ $$ V_{oc} = 2 \times 10^{-3} \ V = 2 \ mV $$ **(ii) Maximum Power to Matched Load:** Using the formula derived in Section 3.1: $$ P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{oc}^{2}}{4 R_{int}} = \dfrac{(2 \times 10^{-3})^{2}}{4 \times 5} $$ $$ P_{max} = \dfrac{4 \times 10^{-6}}{20} = 2 \times 10^{-7} \ W = 0.2 \ \mu W $$ **(iii) Load Resistance for Maximum Power Transfer:** By the Maximum Power Transfer Theorem: $$ R_L = R_{int} = 5 \ \Omega $$ **Mark Split:** (i) 2 marks, (ii) 3 marks, (iii) 2 marks > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** > - **Unit conversion** from $\mu V$ to $V$ is the #1 error. Always write $200 \ \mu V = 200 \times 10^{-6} \ V$ explicitly. > - Many students forget to **square** $V_{oc}$ in the power formula — partial credit only. > - Don't confuse $\Delta T$ (temperature difference) with absolute temperature $T$ in Kelvin. --- ## 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist:** ✅ **Universal 4-Stage Chain:** Source → Transducer → Power Conditioning → Storage + PMU → IoT Load. Memorize this in order — *every* 14-mark question demands it. ✅ **Transducer–Principle Mapping (must-know):** - PV cell → Photovoltaic effect - PZT cantilever → Piezoelectric effect (direct mode, d₃₃) - TEG → Seebeck effect - Rectenna → Rectification of induced RF current ✅ **Master Formulae (minimum five for KTU):** 1. $V_{oc} = S \cdot \Delta T$ (TEG) 2. $P_{max} = \dfrac{S^{2} \Delta T^{2}}{4 R_{int}}$ (TEG max power) 3. $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1-D}$ (Boost converter) 4. $P_{solar} = \eta_{PV} \cdot A \cdot G$ (PV array) 5. $E_{cap} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^{2}$ (Supercapacitor energy) ✅ **MPPT Algorithms:** Know at least **P&O** and **Incremental Conductance** for solar harvesters. ✅ **Impedance Matching:** Piezo sources are *capacitive* — use **inductive SSHI / SECE** networks, not pure resistive matching. ✅ **Storage Choice Rule of Thumb:** - High cycle, burst load → **Supercapacitor** - Long-term, steady micro-load → **Thin-film Li-ion / Li-Po** ✅ **PMU Duty Cycle:** Typical IoT sleep current is in **μA range**; active current in **mA range** — duty cycle often **< 1%**. ✅ **KTU 2024 CO Mapping:** This topic primarily addresses **CO2** ("Illustrate energy harvesting techniques") at **Bloom Level L3–L4**. ✅ **Common Block-Diagram Mistakes to Avoid:** - Storage placed *before* conditioning → wrong - Missing PMU block → loses marks - No feedback arrow from load to PMU → loses 0.5 mark - Unlabelled arrows → loses 0.5 mark ✅ **Numericals Always Test:** (a) unit conversion, (b) formula substitution, (c) numerical accuracy to 2 significant figures, (d) unit of final answer (W, mW, μW). > [!NOTE] > **Final Tip:** In every long-answer, start with a **labelled block diagram (4 marks)**, then explain **each block in 1–2 lines (6 marks)**, and conclude with **one numerical or formula application (4 marks)**. This is the KTU 14-mark template.

topics.title: Performance Evaluation Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Performance Evaluation Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Performance Evaluation Components (PECs)** in the context of Energy Harvesting and Power Management for IoT refer to the standardized set of electrical, mechanical, thermal, and electronic metrics, test conditions, and analytical instruments used to quantitatively and qualitatively assess the operational effectiveness of an energy harvesting system (EHS). These components act as the *benchmarking toolkit* that determines whether a given harvester, conditioning circuit, or storage element is viable for deployment in autonomous, battery-less, or energy-autonomous Wireless Sensor Nodes (WSNs).

In the **KTU 2024 Scheme** framework, Performance Evaluation Components are classified under five primary pillars:

1. **Source Characterization Metrics** — quantifying the raw ambient energy.
2. **Transducer Output Parameters** — voltage, current, and power response.
3. **Power Conditioning Efficiency Metrics** — AC-DC, DC-DC converter performance.
4. **Energy Storage Performance Indicators** — State-of-Charge (SoC), Coulombic efficiency.
5. **System-Level Figures of Merit (FoM)** — holistic integration metrics.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Performance Evaluation is not just measurement — it is the *systematic translation of physical energy into deployable, quantitative engineering decisions* that dictate IoT node longevity, reliability, and Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are purchasing a **solar-powered water pump** for a remote farm in Kerala during the monsoon season. You would not buy it just because it is "solar." You would ask:

- *How much water can it pump per day?* → **Power Output Metric**
- *Does it work efficiently on cloudy days?* → **Conversion Efficiency Metric**
- *How long will the battery last?* → **Storage Performance Indicator**
- *Is the entire system reliable over 5 years?* → **Figure of Merit**

This is exactly what **Performance Evaluation Components** do for an IoT energy harvester — they convert a vague promise of "energy harvesting" into **measurable engineering guarantees**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following universally accepted constants and units govern PECs in IoT energy harvesting:

- **Standard Ambient Temperature:** $T_0 = 25^{\circ}\text{C} = 298.15\text{ K}$
- **Standard Solar Irradiance (AM 1.5G):** $G = 1000\text{ W/m}^2$
- **Standard Acceleration due to Gravity:** $g = 9.81\text{ m/s}^2$
- **Electron Charge:** $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\text{ C}$
- **Boltzmann Constant:** $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}\text{ J/K}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Test Condition (STC):** All energy harvester performance parameters (especially photovoltaic and thermoelectric) are normalized against STC: irradiance of $1000\text{ W/m}^2$, spectrum AM 1.5G, and cell temperature of $25^{\circ}\text{C}$. Comparing harvesters without STC normalization leads to invalid conclusions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Performance Evaluation — Structured Logic

#### Pillar 1: Source Characterization Metrics
These quantify the *raw ambient energy potential* before transduction.

- **Power Density ($P_d$):** Energy available per unit area or volume of the source.
- **Spectral Distribution:** Frequency content of the ambient source (light spectrum, vibration frequency, RF band).
- **Temporal Stability Index (TSI):** Variance of source output over time.

#### Pillar 2: Transducer Output Parameters
The physical response of the harvester to the ambient stimulus.

- **Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{oc}$):** Terminal voltage when no load is connected.
- **Short-Circuit Current ($I_{sc}$):** Current delivered when terminals are shorted.
- **Maximum Power Point ($P_{max}$):** Peak of the $V$-$I$ curve.
- **Internal Impedance ($Z_{in}$):** Source impedance governing load matching.

#### Pillar 3: Power Conditioning Efficiency Metrics
- **Conversion Efficiency ($\eta_{conv}$):** Ratio of conditioned output to transducer input.
- **MPPT Tracking Efficiency ($\eta_{MPPT}$):** Ability to dynamically track $P_{max}$.
- **Quiescent Current ($I_q$):** Self-consumption of the conditioning IC.

#### Pillar 4: Energy Storage Performance Indicators
- **Coulombic Efficiency ($\eta_C$):** Ratio of charge extracted to charge stored.
- **Round-Trip Efficiency ($\eta_{RT}$):** Energy out ÷ Energy in for one full charge-discharge cycle.
- **Self-Discharge Rate ($R_{sd}$):** Percentage capacity loss per month.

#### Pillar 5: System-Level Figure of Merit
- **Effective Energy Density (EED):** Usable energy per unit volume including conversion losses.
- **Normalized Power ($\overline{P}$):** Power per unit weight ($\mu\text{W/g}$).

### 2.2 Real-World Engineering Utility

In the **KTU-mandated IoT deployment scenarios** (smart agriculture in Wayanad, structural health monitoring of bridges, wearable health monitors), the choice of harvester depends entirely on PEC outputs. For instance, a vibration harvester with a **Normalized Power of $50\text{ \mu W/g}$** at $50\text{ Hz}$ is preferred over a $10\text{ \mu W/g}$ counterpart because it directly translates to *more node-uptime* and *fewer battery replacements*.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Memorize the units and the *direction* of the inequality — high FoM is always preferred; low quiescent current is always preferred.

| # | Metric | Formula | Standard Unit | Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Power Density | $P_d = P_{max} / A$ | $\text{W/cm}^2$ or $\mu\text{W/cm}^2$ | $0 \leq P_d \leq P_{d,\text{sat}}$ |
| 2 | Volumetric Power Density | $P_v = P_{max} / V$ | $\text{W/cm}^3$ | $V > 0$ |
| 3 | Fill Factor | $FF = (V_m \cdot I_m) / (V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc})$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) | $0 < FF \leq 1$ |
| 4 | Maximum Power | $P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF$ | $\text{W}$ or $\mu\text{W}$ | $V_{oc}, I_{sc} > 0$ |
| 5 | Conversion Efficiency | $\eta = P_{out} / P_{in}$ | Percentage | $0 \leq \eta \leq 1$ |
| 6 | Overall System Efficiency | $\eta_{sys} = \eta_{harv} \cdot \eta_{cond} \cdot \eta_{stor}$ | Percentage | Product of stages |
| 7 | MPPT Tracking Efficiency | $\eta_{MPPT} = P_{actual} / P_{max}$ | Percentage | $\eta_{MPPT} \approx 1$ ideal |
| 8 | Coulombic Efficiency | $\eta_C = Q_{out} / Q_{in}$ | Percentage | $\eta_C < 1$ always |
| 9 | Round-Trip Efficiency | $\eta_{RT} = E_{out} / E_{in}$ | Percentage | $\eta_{RT} \leq 100\%$ |
| 10 | Figure of Merit (FoM) | $FoM = P_d \cdot \eta_{sys}$ | $\mu\text{W/cm}^2$ normalized | Higher is better |
| 11 | Internal Resistance | $R_{int} = V_{oc} / I_{sc}$ (ideal) | $\Omega$ | $R_{int} > 0$ |
| 12 | Normalized Power | $\overline{P} = P_{max} / m$ | $\mu\text{W/g}$ | Mass $> 0$ |
| 13 | Energy per Cycle (Vibration) | $E_{cyc} = \int P(t) \, dt$ | $\text{J}$ | $t$ over one period $T$ |
| 14 | Battery Self-Discharge | $R_{sd} = \Delta Q / Q_{nom}$ | $\%/\text{month}$ | $R_{sd}$ minimized |
| 15 | Voltage Regulation | $V_{reg} = \vert V_{out,\text{max}} - V_{out,\text{min}} \vert$ | $\text{V}$ or $\text{mV}$ | Lower is better |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Substitution Rule:** When $R_{load} = R_{int}$ (Maximum Power Transfer Theorem), $V_{load} = V_{oc}/2$ and $I_{load} = I_{sc}/2$, yielding $P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} / 4$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Power Transfer Condition for Energy Harvesters

**Step 1: Define the Thévenin Equivalent of the Harvester**

Every energy harvester (solar cell, piezoelectric cantilever, thermoelectric generator) can be modeled as a **Thévenin source** with open-circuit voltage $V_{th}$ and internal impedance $Z_{th}$.

$$V_{th} = V_{oc}$$

$$Z_{th} = R_{int} + jX_{int}$$

**Step 2: Connect a Load Impedance $Z_L$ to the Harvester**

The current flowing through the load is governed by Ohm's Law applied to the closed loop:

$$I_L = \frac{V_{th}}{Z_{th} + Z_L}$$

**Step 3: Express Power Delivered to the Load**

The instantaneous power dissipated in the load is:

$$P_L = \vert I_L \vert^2 \cdot \text{Re}(Z_L)$$

Substituting Step 2 into Step 3:

$$P_L = \left( \frac{V_{th}}{Z_{th} + Z_L} \right)^2 \cdot \text{Re}(Z_L)$$

**Step 4: Apply the Maximum Power Transfer Theorem (For DC / Resistive Case)**

For a purely resistive source driving a purely resistive load, $Z_{th} = R_{int}$ and $Z_L = R_L$:

$$P_L = \frac{V_{oc}^2 \cdot R_L}{(R_{int} + R_L)^2}$$

**Step 5: Differentiate $P_L$ with Respect to $R_L$ and Equate to Zero**

$$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = V_{oc}^2 \cdot \frac{(R_{int} + R_L)^2 - R_L \cdot 2(R_{int} + R_L)}{(R_{int} + R_L)^4} = 0$$

Simplifying the numerator:

$$V_{oc}^2 \cdot \frac{(R_{int} + R_L) - 2R_L}{(R_{int} + R_L)^3} = 0$$

$$V_{oc}^2 \cdot \frac{R_{int} - R_L}{(R_{int} + R_L)^3} = 0$$

**Step 6: Solve for the Critical Point**

Since $V_{oc} \neq 0$ and the denominator is non-zero, the numerator must vanish:

$$R_{int} - R_L = 0 \implies R_L = R_{int}$$

**Step 7: Compute $P_{max}$**

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{oc}^2 \cdot R_{int}}{(R_{int} + R_{int})^2} = \frac{V_{oc}^2 \cdot R_{int}}{4R_{int}^2} = \frac{V_{oc}^2}{4R_{int}}$$

**Step 8: Express $P_{max}$ Using $V_{oc}$ and $I_{sc}$**

Since $I_{sc} = V_{oc} / R_{int}$ for an ideal source, $R_{int} = V_{oc} / I_{sc}$:

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{oc}^2}{4 \cdot (V_{oc}/I_{sc})} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{4}$$

$$\boxed{P_{max} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{4}}$$

This derivation is the **foundation** for Fill Factor and MPPT design in every energy harvesting module.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Fill Factor from the $V$-$I$ Curve

**Step 1: Define the Operating Point**

Let $(V_m, I_m)$ be the voltage and current at the maximum power point on the harvester's $V$-$I$ curve.

**Step 2: Define the Ideal Rectangle**

The largest possible rectangle under the $V$-$I$ curve has area $V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$.

**Step 3: Define the Actual Maximum Power Rectangle**

The actual rectangle at the MPP has area $V_m \cdot I_m$.

**Step 4: Form the Ratio (Definition of FF)**

$$FF = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

**Step 5: Relate FF to $P_{max}$**

Multiplying numerator and denominator by 1 and substituting $P_{max} = V_m \cdot I_m$:

$$FF = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

**Step 6: Final Expression for $P_{max}$**

$$P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF$$

$$\boxed{P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation of FF:** A Fill Factor of **1.0** corresponds to an ideal square $V$-$I$ curve (impossible in practice). Realistic harvesters: $FF \approx 0.6$ to $0.8$ for solar cells, $FF \approx 0.3$ to $0.5$ for piezoelectric harvesters due to AC-to-DC rectification losses.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Overall System Efficiency (Cascaded Stages)

**Step 1: Define Three Cascaded Stages**

- **Harvester:** Converts ambient energy → electrical energy with efficiency $\eta_h$.
- **Conditioning Circuit:** Rectifies, regulates with efficiency $\eta_c$.
- **Storage Element:** Stores energy with efficiency $\eta_s$.

**Step 2: Power at Each Stage**

$$P_{h,\text{out}} = \eta_h \cdot P_{ambient}$$

$$P_{c,\text{out}} = \eta_c \cdot P_{h,\text{out}} = \eta_c \cdot \eta_h \cdot P_{ambient}$$

$$P_{s,\text{out}} = \eta_s \cdot P_{c,\text{out}} = \eta_s \cdot \eta_c \cdot \eta_h \cdot P_{ambient}$$

**Step 3: Overall System Efficiency**

$$\eta_{sys} = \frac{P_{s,\text{out}}}{P_{ambient}}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{sys} = \eta_h \cdot \eta_c \cdot \eta_s}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Each additional stage *multiplies* the efficiency. If each of three stages has $\eta = 0.8$, then $\eta_{sys} = 0.8^3 = 0.512$ — over **48% power loss**. This is why KTU 2024 emphasizes *stage minimization* in IoT power management design.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Trade-off Analysis

| Metric | High Value Implies | Trade-off | KTU Design Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| $V_{oc}$ | Strong source or large transducer | Lower $I_{sc}$ typical | Voltage-mode harvesters (TEG) |
| $I_{sc}$ | Large active area or high flux | Lower $V_{oc}$ typical | Current-mode harvesters (PV) |
| $FF$ | High-quality diode junctions | More expensive materials | Premium solar cells |
| $P_d$ | High power per area | Larger form factor | Miniaturization challenge |
| $\eta_{MPPT}$ | Effective algorithm | Circuit complexity, $I_q$ | Always $> 95\%$ in modern ICs |
| $\eta_C$ | High-quality storage | Cost, chemistry choice | Li-ion $>$ supercapacitor |
| $R_{sd}$ | Long shelf-life | Larger battery size | Critical for remote nodes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Performance Evaluation

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of performance evaluation across the IoT energy harvesting chain. This is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** as mandated for complex physical setups.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Ambient Energy Source] --> A2[Source Characterization Module]
    A2 --> A3{Power Density P_d}
    A2 --> A4{Spectral Distribution}
    A2 --> A5{Temporal Stability Index}

    A1 --> B1[Energy Transducer Harvester]
    B1 --> B2[Transducer Output Module]
    B2 --> B3{V_oc Measurement}
    B2 --> B4{I_sc Measurement}
    B2 --> B5{Z_in Impedance Analyzer}

    B2 --> C1[V-I Curve Tracer]
    C1 --> C2[Maximum Power Point Tracker]
    C2 --> C3{P_max Calculator}
    C2 --> C4{Fill Factor FF Calculator}

    C2 --> D1[Power Conditioning Circuit]
    D1 --> D2[Rectifier AC-DC Stage]
    D2 --> D3[DC-DC Regulator Stage]
    D3 --> D4[MPPT Controller IC]
    D4 --> D5{Conversion Efficiency eta_conv}

    D3 --> E1[Energy Storage Element]
    E1 --> E2[State of Charge SoC Monitor]
    E1 --> E3{Coulombic Efficiency eta_C}
    E1 --> E4{Round-Trip Efficiency eta_RT}
    E1 --> E5{Self-Discharge Rate R_sd}

    C3 --> F1[System-Level Figure of Merit Calculator]
    C4 --> F1
    D5 --> F1
    E3 --> F1
    E4 --> F1

    F1 --> F2[Normalized Power mu_W per g]
    F1 --> F3[Effective Energy Density EED]
    F1 --> F4[Overall System Efficiency eta_sys]
    F1 --> G1[Performance Evaluation Report]
    F2 --> G1
    F3 --> G1
    F4 --> G1
    G1 --> H1[KTU Benchmarking Database]
    G1 --> H2[IoT Deployment Decision]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Decision Flow for Performance Comparison

The following diagram illustrates the **decision logic** an engineer follows when comparing two energy harvesters using PECs.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    startA[Harvester A Specs] --> compA[Compute FoM_A]
    startB[Harvester B Specs] --> compB[Compute FoM_B]

    compA --> check{Is FoM_A greater than FoM_B}
    compB --> check

    check -->|Yes| winA[Select Harvester A]
    check -->|No| check2{Is FoM_B greater than FoM_A}

    check2 -->|Yes| winB[Select Harvester B]
    check2 -->|No| tie[Tie Perform Detailed Analysis]

    winA --> deploy[Deploy to IoT Node]
    winB --> deploy
    tie --> deploy
```

### 4.3 Schematic Symbol Legend (Mermaid-Compatible)

- **Rectangular Nodes:** Functional modules or measurement blocks.
- **Diamond Nodes:** Decision points or computed parameters.
- **Rounded Brackets:** Inputs and outputs of the evaluation chain.
- **Arrow Direction:** Data and signal flow from source to report.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*Define the term "Fill Factor" in the context of energy harvesting. Mention its typical range for a photovoltaic cell and a piezoelectric harvester.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Fill Factor (FF) is a dimensionless performance metric that quantifies the "squareness" of the current-voltage ($V$-$I$) characteristic curve of an energy harvester.

$$FF = \frac{V_m \cdot I_m}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}} = \frac{P_{max}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$

- **Photovoltaic Cell:** $FF \approx 0.6 \text{ to } 0.8$ (1 mark)
- **Piezoelectric Harvester:** $FF \approx 0.3 \text{ to } 0.5$ (1 mark)
- A higher FF indicates lower internal losses and a higher $P_{max}$. (1 mark)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*What is the significance of the "Figure of Merit (FoM)" in evaluating IoT energy harvesting systems? Write one common expression for it.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Figure of Merit (FoM) is a *holistic, system-level metric* that combines multiple performance parameters (power density, conversion efficiency, form factor) into a single comparable value. (2 marks)

A common expression:

$$FoM = P_d \cdot \eta_{sys} = \left(\frac{P_{max}}{A}\right) \cdot (\eta_h \cdot \eta_c \cdot \eta_s)$$

where $A$ is the active area, and $\eta_{sys}$ is the overall system efficiency. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

**Q3. [KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

**Question A (14 Marks):**
*(a)* Derive the expression for the maximum power delivered by an energy harvester to a resistive load under the Maximum Power Transfer Theorem. Clearly state the condition for maximum power transfer. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* A small-scale photovoltaic cell used in an IoT sensor node has the following measured parameters: $V_{oc} = 0.55\text{ V}$, $I_{sc} = 80\text{ mA}$, Fill Factor $FF = 0.72$. Compute the maximum power output $P_{max}$ and the corresponding load resistance $R_L$ at the maximum power point. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes. The detailed derivation yields:

$$\boxed{P_{max} = \frac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_{int}} = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{4}}$$

with the condition: **$R_L = R_{int}$** (Maximum Power Transfer Theorem). (7 marks distributed across the 8 derivation steps)

**Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Calculate $P_{max}$ using the Fill Factor formula:

$$P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF$$

$$P_{max} = 0.55 \text{ V} \times 80 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A} \times 0.72$$

$$P_{max} = 0.55 \times 0.080 \times 0.72$$

$$P_{max} = 0.03168 \text{ W} = 31.68 \text{ mW}$$

(3 marks)

**Step 2:** Calculate $R_{int}$ of the cell:

$$R_{int} = \frac{V_{oc}}{I_{sc}} = \frac{0.55 \text{ V}}{80 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}} = 6.875 \, \Omega$$

(2 marks)

**Step 3:** Apply the Maximum Power Transfer condition $R_L = R_{int}$:

$$\boxed{R_L = 6.875 \, \Omega}$$

(2 marks)

---

**Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):**
*(a)* Explain the concept of the "Overall System Efficiency" for a cascaded IoT energy harvesting system. Derive its expression and discuss why each additional stage degrades the system performance multiplicatively. **(7 marks)**
*(b)* A wearable IoT health monitoring patch uses a thermoelectric generator (TEG) cascaded with a DC-DC boost converter and a thin-film Li-ion battery. Given: $\eta_h = 0.08$ (TEG efficiency), $\eta_c = 0.85$ (boost converter), $\eta_s = 0.92$ (Li-ion storage). Compute the overall system efficiency and the effective output power if the TEG produces $5\text{ mW}$ from body heat. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**
Refer to **Section 3.3** of these notes. The derivation yields:

$$\boxed{\eta_{sys} = \eta_h \cdot \eta_c \cdot \eta_s}$$

Discussion (2 marks): Each stage multiplies the efficiency, so even small per-stage losses compound. A three-stage system with $0.8$ per stage yields only $0.512$ overall.

**Part (b):**

**Step 1:** Compute overall system efficiency:

$$\eta_{sys} = 0.08 \times 0.85 \times 0.92$$

$$\eta_{sys} = 0.06256 = 6.256\%$$

(3 marks)

**Step 2:** Compute effective output power delivered to the load:

$$P_{out} = \eta_{sys} \times P_{TEG}$$

$$P_{out} = 0.06256 \times 5 \text{ mW}$$

$$P_{out} = 0.3128 \text{ mW} = 312.8 \, \mu\text{W}$$

(2 marks)

**Step 3:** Engineering Interpretation (2 marks): The $312.8 \, \mu\text{W}$ is sufficient for low-duty-cycle BLE transmission (typical peak: $10\text{ mW}$ for $< 5\text{ ms}$) if the energy is buffered over a 30-second interval in a supercapacitor. This justifies the system architecture for wearable IoT.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Leading to Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–1 mark):** Students often write $P_{max}$ in Volts or Amps instead of Watts. Always carry the units through every calculation.
> 2. **Confusing $V_m$ with $V_{oc}$ (–2 marks):** $V_m$ is the *operating* voltage at MPP, which is *less than* $V_{oc}$. Writing $V_m = V_{oc}$ invalidates the FF calculation.
> 3. **Ignoring the Factor of 4 (–1 mark):** $P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} / 4$ is often mistakenly written as $P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}$.
> 4. **Cascaded Efficiency as Sum (–2 marks):** Writing $\eta_{sys} = \eta_h + \eta_c + \eta_s$ instead of the product. The product rule is non-negotiable.
> 5. **Forgetting to Specify STC (–1 mark):** Quoting PV efficiency without specifying Standard Test Conditions ($1000\text{ W/m}^2$, AM 1.5G, $25^{\circ}\text{C}$) loses a mark.
> 6. **Not Identifying the Fill Factor Range (–1 mark):** Providing FF without stating the harvester type loses the contextual mark.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Last-Minute KTU Prep):**

- **Five Pillars of PEC:** Source → Transducer → Conditioning → Storage → System FoM.
- **Core Equations to Memorize:**
  - $P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF$
  - $P_{max} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} / 4$ (ideal FF = 1)
  - $FF = (V_m \cdot I_m) / (V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc})$
  - $\eta_{sys} = \eta_h \cdot \eta_c \cdot \eta_s$
  - $R_L = R_{int}$ (Max Power Transfer)
- **Standard Test Conditions:** $1000\text{ W/m}^2$, AM 1.5G spectrum, $25^{\circ}\text{C}$.
- **Typical FF Ranges:** Solar $0.6$–$0.8$; Piezo $0.3$–$0.5$; TEG $0.4$–$0.6$.
- **Cascaded Efficiency Rule:** *Always multiply, never add.*
- **FoM Definition:** $FoM = P_d \cdot \eta_{sys}$ (higher is better).
- **MPPT Goal:** Maintain $\eta_{MPPT} > 95\%$ for viable IoT harvesting.
- **Storage Hierarchy (by $\eta_C$):** Li-ion ($\approx 99\%$) > NiMH ($\approx 90\%$) > Supercapacitor ($\approx 95\%$) > Lead-acid ($\approx 80\%$).
- **Normalize Before Comparing:** Always use $P_d$ or Normalized Power ($\mu\text{W/g}$), never absolute $P_{max}$.
- **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** System-level integration, not isolated component performance.
- **Exam Formula Substitutions:** Master $V_{oc} = I_{sc} \cdot R_{int}$ relationship for quick derivations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Software Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of Energy Harvesting and Power Management in IoT, **Software Systems** refer to the layered firmware, operating system kernels, middleware, and application-layer algorithms that orchestrate sensing, computation, communication, and energy management on resource-constrained IoT nodes. These systems are responsible for translating harvested energy into sustainable, predictable, and long-lived device operation by managing the duty cycle, scheduling tasks based on available energy, and adapting behavior to ambient energy availability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECIT746 – Module 2):**  
> *Software Systems* encompasses the OS-level abstractions, firmware architectures, and energy-aware scheduling algorithms that allow IoT devices to operate perpetually on harvested energy. It bridges the gap between the hardware energy harvesting front-end and the application layer by abstracting power as a first-class resource, just like time and memory.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a sailor on a tiny sailboat in the middle of the ocean. The wind (your harvested energy) is unpredictable — sometimes strong, sometimes nearly absent. You cannot simply set the sail once and forget about it. You must constantly:

1. **Measure** how much wind is available (energy estimation).
2. **Decide** when to row harder, when to drift, and when to repair the sail (duty cycling and scheduling).
3. **Preserve** your limited drinking water (energy storage/battery management).
4. **Adapt** your entire plan to tomorrow's expected wind (energy-aware adaptation).

The **Software System is the sailor’s brain** — the decision-making layer that takes raw, fluctuating energy from the hardware and converts it into intelligent, mission-oriented behavior. Without it, even the best solar panel or piezoelectric transducer is wasted energy.

### 1.3 Why Software Systems Matter in Energy-Harvesting IoT

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU Examinations:**  
> Hardware alone cannot solve the intermittency problem of energy harvesting. The *software* is what makes harvested energy *usable*. In fact, industry data suggests that **over 80%** of the energy savings in modern IoT nodes come from intelligent power management firmware, not from better batteries or harvesters.

### 1.4 Core Metrics Used in Software Power Management

- **Duty Cycle (DC):** Fraction of time the system is active.
- **Energy Neutral Operation (ENO):** Condition where harvested energy ≥ consumed energy over a period.
- **Energy Budget:** Maximum energy that can be spent per duty cycle.
- **MIPS/W or Operations/Joule:** Computational energy efficiency metric.
- **Latency vs. Energy Trade-off:** Higher latency (sleep) saves energy; lower latency costs more.

All these metrics are managed by **software**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Software Stack of an Energy-Harvesting IoT Node

A typical energy-aware IoT node has the following software layers, from bottom to top:

1. **Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL):** Drivers for sensors, ADCs, transceivers, harvesters.
2. **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) Kernel:** Task scheduling, interrupts, timers. Examples: **FreeRTOS, Contiki-NG, RIOT, Zephyr, TinyOS**.
3. **Power Management Framework:** Controls sleep modes, voltage/frequency scaling, peripheral clock gating.
4. **Energy-Aware Middleware:** Abstracts energy as a resource; provides APIs to query predicted harvest.
5. **Application Layer:** Sensing, actuation, communication logic.

### 2.2 Operating Systems for Energy-Harvesting IoT

#### 2.2.1 Contiki-NG
- Event-driven OS designed for low-power wireless sensor networks.
- Uses **protothreads** — lightweight stackless threads.
- Built-in support for **RPL** (Routing Protocol for Low-Power and Lossy Networks) and **CoAP**.
- **COOJA** simulator for large-scale network simulation.

#### 2.2.2 RIOT
- Multi-threaded OS with a real-time scheduler.
- POSIX-compliant API (familiar to developers).
- **Tickless idle** — wakes CPU only when needed, saving power.

#### 2.2.3 FreeRTOS
- Widely used in commercial IoT products.
- Supports **tickless idle mode** and **low-power tickless implementation**.
- Preemptive, priority-based scheduling.
- Memory footprint as low as **~4–10 KB ROM**.

#### 2.2.4 TinyOS
- Component-based, event-driven.
- Written in **nesC** language.
- Very small footprint; popular in early WSN research.

#### 2.2.5 Zephyr
- Modern RTOS by Linux Foundation.
- Supports **Device Tree** and **Devicetree-based power management**.
- Built-in **power state infrastructure (PSI)**.

### 2.3 Duty Cycling

Duty cycling is the **primary software technique** for reducing energy consumption. The system alternates between **active mode** and **sleep mode**.

Let:
- $T_a$ = time spent in active mode per cycle
- $T_s$ = time spent in sleep mode per cycle
- $T_c = T_a + T_s$ = total cycle period

The **Duty Cycle** is:

$$
DC = \frac{T_a}{T_a + T_s} = \frac{T_a}{T_c}
$$

The **average power consumption** is:

$$
P_{avg} = P_{active} \cdot DC + P_{sleep} \cdot (1 - DC)
$$

Where:
- $P_{active}$ = power in active mode
- $P_{sleep}$ = power in sleep mode

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** When $P_{sleep} \ll P_{active}$ (typical for IoT), the average power scales almost linearly with duty cycle. A 1% duty cycle reduces power by ~99× (assuming sleep current is negligible).

### 2.4 Energy-Neutral Operation (ENO)

ENO is achieved when the **harvested energy** $E_h$ in a given period equals or exceeds the **consumed energy** $E_c$:

$$
E_h(t) \geq E_c(t) \quad \forall t \in [0, T_{period}]
$$

The **harvested energy** from a source with average power $P_h$ is:

$$
E_h = P_h \cdot T
$$

The **consumed energy** for a duty-cycled system is:

$$
E_c = P_{avg} \cdot T = [P_{active} \cdot DC + P_{sleep} \cdot (1-DC)] \cdot T
$$

### 2.5 Energy-Aware Scheduling

Energy-aware schedulers treat **energy as a first-class resource**, alongside time. The classical EDF (Earliest Deadline First) scheduler is extended to **E-EDF (Energy-EDF)**.

For a task $\tau_i$ with:
- Computation time $C_i$ (in active cycles)
- Active power $P_{active}$
- Deadline $D_i$

The **energy cost** of the task is:

$$
E_i = P_{active} \cdot C_i \cdot t_{cycle}
$$

Where $t_{cycle}$ is the CPU cycle time. The **schedulability condition** with energy constraint is:

$$
\sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i \leq E_{budget}(t)
$$

Where $E_{budget}(t)$ is the available energy at time $t$ (from harvested + stored energy).

### 2.6 Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS)

Software can reduce CPU energy by lowering voltage $V$ and frequency $f$. The dynamic power is:

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f
$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = switching activity factor
- $C_L$ = load capacitance
- $V_{dd}$ = supply voltage
- $f$ = operating frequency

Since $f \propto V_{dd}$ (in CMOS), reducing $V_{dd}$ by half reduces $f$ by half and power by **4×** (quadratic savings).

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | Duty Cycle | $DC = \frac{T_a}{T_a + T_s}$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 2 | Average Power | $P_{avg} = P_a \cdot DC + P_s \cdot (1-DC)$ | Watts (W) |
| 3 | Energy Neutrality | $E_h \geq E_c$ | Joules (J) |
| 4 | Harvested Energy | $E_h = P_h \cdot T$ | Joules |
| 5 | Task Energy Cost | $E_i = P_{active} \cdot C_i \cdot t_{cyc}$ | Joules |
| 6 | CMOS Dynamic Power | $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V^2 f$ | Watts |
| 7 | Energy per Operation | $E_{op} = \frac{P_{avg}}{f_{op}}$ | Joules/op |
| 8 | Battery State of Charge | $SoC(t+1) = SoC(t) + \frac{E_h(t) - E_c(t)}{E_{capacity}}$ | 0 to 1 |
| 9 | Sleep Current Ratio | $\eta_{sleep} = \frac{P_{sleep}}{P_{active}}$ | Typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-6}$ |
| 10 | Lifetime (Energy Budget) | $L = \frac{E_{stored}}{P_{avg}}$ | Seconds / Hours |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in tools like **PowerTOSSIM**, **AEON (Accurate Energy Optimization)**, and the energy profilers in **Contiki-NG** (`powertrace`).

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Smart Agriculture:** Software systems decide when to wake up sensors based on solar forecast (from weather APIs) and stored battery SoC.
- **Wearable Health Monitors:** Adaptive sampling — heart rate sampled at 1 Hz when at rest, 100 Hz during activity detection. All controlled by firmware.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Predictive maintenance nodes run **TinyML inference** on harvested vibration energy; the OS decides inference frequency based on capacitor voltage.
- **Bridges & Structural Monitoring:** Energy-harvesting nodes schedule accelerometer sampling and LoRa transmission only when vibration exceeds a threshold (event-driven software).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Average Power Consumption of a Duty-Cycled System

**Problem Statement:**  
A wireless sensor node consumes $P_{active} = 50$ mW when active and $P_{sleep} = 5\ \mu$W when sleeping. The active period per cycle is $T_a = 10$ ms and the sleep period is $T_s = 990$ ms. Derive the average power consumption and the energy consumed per day.

**Step 1 — Identify the cycle period.**

$$
T_c = T_a + T_s = 10\text{ ms} + 990\text{ ms} = 1000\text{ ms} = 1\text{ s}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the duty cycle.**

$$
DC = \frac{T_a}{T_c} = \frac{10}{1000} = 0.01 = 1\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the average power.**

$$
P_{avg} = P_{active} \cdot DC + P_{sleep} \cdot (1 - DC)
$$

Substituting values:

$$
P_{avg} = (50 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 0.01 + (5 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot 0.99
$$

$$
P_{avg} = 5 \times 10^{-4} + 4.95 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
P_{avg} \approx 5.0495 \times 10^{-4}\text{ W} = 504.95\ \mu\text{W}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the daily energy consumption.**

Seconds in a day:

$$
t_{day} = 24 \times 60 \times 60 = 86400\text{ s}
$$

Daily energy:

$$
E_{day} = P_{avg} \cdot t_{day}
$$

$$
E_{day} = 5.0495 \times 10^{-4} \times 86400
$$

$$
E_{day} \approx 43.63\text{ J}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation.**  
This node consumes ~43.6 J/day. A small solar cell delivering 100 µW average would harvest:

$$
E_h = 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 86400 = 8.64\text{ J/day}
$$

ENO is **not** met — the node would need either more harvest or a smaller duty cycle.

**Required duty cycle for ENO with 100 µW average harvest:**

$$
P_{avg} \leq P_h \Rightarrow 50\text{ mW} \cdot DC \leq 0.1\text{ mW}
$$

$$
DC \leq \frac{0.1}{50} = 0.002 = 0.2\%
$$

So the system must sleep 99.8% of the time.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: DVFS Energy Savings

**Problem Statement:**  
A processor runs at $V_{dd} = 1.2$ V and $f = 100$ MHz, consuming $P_1$. If software reduces $V_{dd}$ to 0.8 V and $f$ to 67 MHz, derive the new power consumption and the energy savings for the same computational work (assuming $f \propto V_{dd}$).

**Step 1 — Initial dynamic power.**

$$
P_1 = \alpha C_L V_1^2 f_1 = \alpha C_L (1.2)^2 (100 \times 10^6)
$$

$$
P_1 = \alpha C_L (1.44)(10^8) = 1.44 \times 10^8 \cdot \alpha C_L
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the proportionality $V_2/V_1 = f_2/f_1$.**

$$
\frac{V_2}{V_1} = \frac{0.8}{1.2} = \frac{2}{3}
$$

Therefore $f_2 = f_1 \cdot \frac{2}{3} = 66.67$ MHz (matches given value).

**Step 3 — New dynamic power.**

$$
P_2 = \alpha C_L V_2^2 f_2 = \alpha C_L (0.8)^2 (66.67 \times 10^6)
$$

$$
P_2 = \alpha C_L (0.64)(6.667 \times 10^7) = 4.267 \times 10^7 \cdot \alpha C_L
$$

**Step 4 — Power ratio.**

$$
\frac{P_2}{P_1} = \frac{4.267 \times 10^7}{1.44 \times 10^8} = 0.296
$$

So $P_2 \approx 0.296 P_1$, i.e., **~70% power reduction**.

**Step 5 — Energy per task.**  
A task requiring $N$ cycles takes time $t = N/f$. Energy is $E = P \cdot t$.

$$
E_1 = P_1 \cdot \frac{N}{f_1}
$$

$$
E_2 = P_2 \cdot \frac{N}{f_2} = P_2 \cdot \frac{N}{(2/3)f_1} = \frac{3}{2} \cdot \frac{P_2 \cdot N}{f_1}
$$

Substituting $P_2 = 0.296 P_1$:

$$
E_2 = \frac{3}{2} \cdot 0.296 \cdot \frac{P_1 N}{f_1} = 0.444 \cdot E_1
$$

Therefore, **energy savings ≈ 55.6%** for the same computational work. The task takes 50% longer but consumes 55.6% less energy.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of IoT Operating Systems

| Feature | Contiki-NG | RIOT | FreeRTOS | TinyOS | Zephyr |
|---------|-----------|------|----------|--------|--------|
| Scheduler | Event-driven | Multi-threaded (preemptive) | Preemptive priority | Event-driven | Preemptive + tickless |
| Min RAM | ~10 KB | ~1.5 KB | ~4 KB | ~1 KB | ~8 KB |
| Min ROM | ~30 KB | ~5 KB | ~10 KB | ~4 KB | ~30 KB |
| Power Mgmt | RPL + Cooja tools | Tickless idle | Tickless idle | Limited | Power State API |
| Language | C | C/C++ | C | nesC | C |
| Network Stack | 6LoWPAN, RPL, CoAP | 6LoWPAN, RPL, CoAP | None (manual) | Custom | 6LoWPAN, BLE |
| Energy Model | Powertrace | built-in | None (3rd party) | PowerTOSSIM | Devicetree PSI |
| License | BSD | LGPL | MIT | BSD | Apache 2.0 |
| Best For | WSN research | Academic + Industrial | Commercial products | Legacy WSN | Modern IoT products |

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Energy-Harvesting Software Frameworks

| Framework | Approach | Energy Predictor | Adaptation |
|-----------|----------|------------------|------------|
| **Energest** (Contiki) | Online energy estimation | Cycle counter per component | Duty cycle tuning |
| **Eon** | Energy-aware dataflow | Capacitor voltage + EWMA | Quality-of-data scaling |
| **LEACH-like clustering** | Distributed | Solar forecast | Cluster head rotation |
| **REACH** (Harvest-Aware MAC) | MAC layer | Harvested energy | Back-off adjustment |
| **QEM (Quality-Energy Management)** | Adaptive | Stored + predicted | Sampling rate |
| **Mementos** | Checkpointing | Capacitor voltage | Computation restart |
| **Task scheduling with capacitor model** | RTOS level | $V_c(t)$ capacitor model | Frequency scaling |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Software Stack Architecture of an Energy-Harvesting IoT Node

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appLayer["APPLICATION LAYER\nSensing, Actuation, ML Inference"]
    middleware["ENERGY-AWARE MIDDLEWARE\nPower API, Harvest Predictor"]
    rtos["RTOS KERNEL\nTask Scheduler, Tickless Idle"]
    pmFw["POWER MANAGEMENT FIRMWARE\nDVFS, Sleep Modes, Clock Gating"]
    hal["HARDWARE ABSTRACTION LAYER\nGPIO, ADC, I2C, SPI Drivers"]
    hw["HARDWARE\nMCU, Sensors, Radio, Harvester, Battery"]

    appLayer --> middleware
    middleware --> rtos
    rtos --> pmFw
    pmFw --> hal
    hal --> hw

    energyLoop["ENERGY FEEDBACK LOOP\nPower Meter -> Energy Estimator -> Scheduler"] -.-> middleware

    hw -.-> energyLoop
```

### 4.2 Duty Cycling State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> BOOT
    BOOT --> INIT : PowerOn
    INIT --> SENSE : Timer IRQ
    SENSE --> COMPUTE : Read Sensor
    COMPUTE --> TRANSMIT : Process Data
    TRANSMIT --> SLEEP : Tx Complete
    SLEEP --> SENSE : RTC Wakeup
    SLEEP --> HIBERNATE : Low Energy Alarm
    HIBERNATE --> INIT : Harvester OK
    HIBERNATE --> SHUTDOWN : Critical Battery
    SHUTDOWN --> [*]
```

### 4.3 Energy-Aware Scheduler Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    taskQ["TASK QUEUE\nArrival Time, Deadline"]
    energyMon["ENERGY MONITOR\nBattery SoC, Harvest Predictor"]
    policy["SCHEDULING POLICY\nE-EDF, LSTF, QEM"]
    taskSel["TASK SELECTOR"]
    exec["CPU EXECUTION"]
    pmCtrl["POWER CONTROLLER\nDVFS + Sleep"]

    taskQ --> taskSel
    energyMon --> policy
    policy --> taskSel
    taskSel --> exec
    exec --> pmCtrl
    pmCtrl -.-> exec
    exec -.-> energyMon
```

### 4.4 Software Energy Management Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["System Boot"]) --> measureH["Measure Harvested Power Ph"]
    measureH --> measureC["Measure Consumed Power Pc"]
    measureC --> estimate["Estimate Available Energy Budget E_budget"]
    estimate --> decision{"ENO Satisfied?\n Eh >= Ec"}
    decision -- Yes --> normal["Normal Operation\nFull Duty Cycle"]
    decision -- No --> adapt["Adaptive Mode"]
    adapt --> reduceDC["Reduce Duty Cycle"]
    adapt --> reduceFreq["Reduce CPU Frequency DVFS"]
    adapt --> skipTask["Defer Non-Critical Tasks"]
    adapt --> hibern["Enter Deep Sleep Hibernate"]
    reduceDC --> measureH
    reduceFreq --> measureH
    skipTask --> measureH
    hibern --> measureH
    normal --> measureH
```

### 4.5 IoT OS Kernel Comparison Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EVENTDRIVEN["EVENT DRIVEN OS"]
        contiki["Contiki-NG\nProtothreads"]
        tinyos["TinyOS\nnesC Components"]
    end
    subgraph MULTITHREADED["MULTI THREADED RTOS"]
        riot["RIOT\nPOSIX Threads"]
        freertos["FreeRTOS\nPriority Threads"]
        zephyr["Zephyr\nTickless Idle"]
    end
    APP["IoT Application"] --> EVENTDRIVEN
    APP --> MULTITHREADED
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
**Define Energy Neutral Operation (ENO) in the context of energy-harvesting IoT systems. Why is it a critical design goal for software systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
Energy Neutral Operation (ENO) is the condition in which the energy harvested by an IoT node over a given period is **greater than or equal to** the energy consumed by the node in the same period, allowing indefinite operation without battery replacement. Mathematically, $E_h(t) \geq E_c(t)$ for all $t$.

**Significance for software systems (2 marks):**  
1. Software must **estimate** available harvested energy in real time using models (e.g., solar irradiance forecast, capacitor voltage).  
2. Software must **adapt** behavior — duty cycle, sampling rate, transmission power — to match energy availability.  
3. ENO is a **sustainable** design goal: it eliminates battery replacement, making IoT deployment viable in inaccessible locations (e.g., embedded in bridges, inside machinery, in forests).

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
**What is duty cycling? How does it contribute to energy efficiency in IoT nodes?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**  
Duty cycling is a software-controlled technique in which an IoT node alternates between an **active mode** (sensing, computation, transmission) and a **sleep mode** (low-power idle), spending most of its time in sleep to minimize energy consumption.

**Contribution to energy efficiency (2 marks):**  
1. Reduces average power consumption by orders of magnitude — for a 1% duty cycle with sleep current 1000× lower, the average power is reduced by ~99%.  
2. Software decides **when to wake up** using timers, interrupts, or event triggers, ensuring the system is active only when meaningful work needs to be done.  
3. Directly contributes to achieving ENO, especially in low-energy-harvest environments.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each – Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [Module 2 – Internal Choice Option A]

**[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]**  
**(a)** Explain the layered software architecture of an energy-harvesting IoT node, with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the **Power Management Firmware** layer in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable example, derive the average power consumption of a duty-cycled IoT node. Show that the lifetime of a battery-powered node is directly proportional to the inverse of its average power consumption. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

The layered software architecture of an energy-harvesting IoT node consists of five layers:

**Layer 1 — Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL):**  
Contains low-level drivers for sensors (ADC, I2C, SPI), radio transceivers, and the energy harvesting front-end. Provides a uniform API to upper layers, hiding hardware-specific details. **(1 mark)**

**Layer 2 — Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) Kernel:**  
Provides task scheduling (preemptive/priority-based), inter-task communication, timers, and interrupt handling. Examples: FreeRTOS, RIOT, Contiki-NG. Modern RTOSs support **tickless idle** where the system clock is stopped during sleep. **(1 mark)**

**Layer 3 — Power Management Firmware (Core Layer):**  
This is the **most critical layer** for energy harvesting. It performs:  
- **Sleep mode control** — puts MCU and peripherals into deep sleep between events.  
- **Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS)** — reduces $V_{dd}$ and $f$ when computational load is low, achieving quadratic power savings.  
- **Peripheral clock gating** — disables clocks to unused peripherals.  
- **Wake-up source management** — configures RTC, GPIO interrupts, or sensor thresholds as wake-up triggers.  
- **Energy estimation and budgeting** — tracks energy consumption per task and enforces a budget. **(3 marks)**

**Layer 4 — Energy-Aware Middleware:**  
Provides APIs for application code to query energy status (`getSoC()`, `getPredictedHarvest()`) and adapt behavior (e.g., reduce sample rate if SoC is low). **(1 mark)**

**Layer 5 — Application Layer:**  
Implements sensing, ML inference, communication logic. It must be **energy-aware**, querying middleware before performing expensive operations. **(1 mark)**

---

##### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Setup.**  
Consider an IoT node with two modes:
- **Active mode:** consumes $P_a$ for time $T_a$ per cycle.
- **Sleep mode:** consumes $P_s$ for time $T_s$ per cycle.

The cycle period is $T_c = T_a + T_s$.

**Step 2 — Duty cycle definition.**  

$$
DC = \frac{T_a}{T_c}
$$

**Step 3 — Average power derivation.**  
Total energy per cycle: $E_{cycle} = P_a T_a + P_s T_s$.  
Average power: $P_{avg} = E_{cycle} / T_c$.

$$
P_{avg} = \frac{P_a T_a + P_s T_s}{T_a + T_s}
$$

Substituting $T_a = DC \cdot T_c$ and $T_s = (1-DC) T_c$: **(2 marks)**

$$
P_{avg} = \frac{P_a \cdot DC \cdot T_c + P_s \cdot (1-DC) \cdot T_c}{T_c}
$$

$$
P_{avg} = P_a \cdot DC + P_s \cdot (1 - DC)
$$

**(1 mark for derivation above)**

**Step 4 — Example.**  
Take $P_a = 50$ mW, $P_s = 5\ \mu$W, $DC = 0.01$.

$$
P_{avg} = 50 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 0.01 + 5 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 0.99
$$

$$
P_{avg} = 5 \times 10^{-4} + 4.95 \times 10^{-6} \approx 5.05 \times 10^{-4}\text{ W} = 505\ \mu\text{W}
$$

**(1 mark for numerical example)**

**Step 5 — Lifetime derivation.**  
Battery stored energy: $E_{stored}$ (J). Lifetime $L$ is when stored energy is exhausted: $E_{stored} = P_{avg} \cdot L$. Therefore:

$$
L = \frac{E_{stored}}{P_{avg}}
$$

This shows that **lifetime is inversely proportional to average power**. Reducing $P_{avg}$ by 10× (via duty cycling or DVFS) extends lifetime by 10×. **(2 marks)**

**Example continued:**  
If $E_{stored} = 2.4$ kJ (e.g., a 600 mAh, 3.7 V Li-ion battery), then:

$$
L = \frac{2400}{5.05 \times 10^{-4}} = 4.75 \times 10^6\text{ s} \approx 55\text{ days}
$$

Without duty cycling (DC = 1):

$$
P_{avg} = 50\text{ mW} \Rightarrow L = \frac{2400}{0.05} = 48000\text{ s} \approx 13.3\text{ hours}
$$

A **~100× lifetime extension** is achieved through software-level duty cycling.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Module 2 – Internal Choice Option B]

**[KTU University Exam – Sample 2024 Scheme]**  
**(a)** Compare Contiki-NG, RIOT, and FreeRTOS in terms of scheduling model, memory footprint, and suitability for energy-harvesting IoT applications. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS)? Derive the relationship between supply voltage, frequency, and dynamic power consumption in CMOS. Show that reducing $V_{dd}$ by half reduces dynamic power by a factor of 4. **(7 marks)**

---

##### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

| Feature | Contiki-NG | RIOT | FreeRTOS |
|---------|-----------|------|----------|
| **Scheduling Model** | Event-driven using protothreads; cooperative | Multi-threaded, preemptive, priority-based | Preemptive, priority-based with optional time slicing |
| **Min RAM** | ~10 KB | ~1.5 KB | ~4 KB |
| **Min ROM** | ~30 KB | ~5 KB | ~10 KB |
| **Power Management** | Powertrace tool, RPL/CoAP integration, simulation in COOJA | Tickless idle, energy module, native port | Tickless idle (port-specific), low-power hooks |
| **Suitability for EH IoT** | Excellent for WSN research, large-scale simulation, 6LoWPAN networks | Excellent for academic and industrial deployments; POSIX-friendly | Excellent for commercial products with extensive ecosystem |

**(5 marks — 1.5 marks per row of table, distributed)**

**Detailed Discussion (2 marks):**  
- **Contiki-NG** excels in research and large-scale deployments (e.g., smart city WSNs) because of **COOJA** simulation and built-in 6LoWPAN/RPL stack. Its event-driven model is energy-efficient for I/O-bound tasks.  
- **RIOT** is preferred when developers want a **familiar POSIX-like API** and true multi-threading. Its smaller footprint and tickless idle make it highly suitable for energy-harvesting nodes.  
- **FreeRTOS** is the **industry default** — used in millions of commercial products. It has the largest community and commercial support (e.g., AWS IoT, STMicroelectronics). For energy harvesting, port-specific tickless implementations are critical.

---

##### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (2 marks):**  
Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS) is a software-controlled power management technique in which the **supply voltage $V_{dd}$** and **operating frequency $f$** of a processor are dynamically adjusted at runtime to match computational demand, reducing dynamic power consumption during periods of low activity.

**CMOS Dynamic Power Derivation (3 marks):**  
The dynamic power dissipated in a CMOS gate during switching is the energy per transition $E_{sw}$ multiplied by the switching rate:

$$
P_{dyn} = E_{sw} \cdot f_{sw}
$$

The switching energy of a CMOS gate with load capacitance $C_L$ is:

$$
E_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_L \cdot V_{dd}^2
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C_L \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f
$$

Where $\alpha$ is the switching activity factor (probability of a 0→1 transition per cycle).

**Frequency-Voltage Relationship (1 mark):**  
The propagation delay of a CMOS gate is inversely proportional to $V_{dd} - V_{th}$:

$$
t_d \propto \frac{V_{dd}}{(V_{dd} - V_{th})^2}
$$

For large $V_{dd}$, $t_d \approx k / V_{dd}$. Since $f = 1 / t_d$, we have $f \propto V_{dd}$.

**Verification of 4× Power Reduction (1 mark):**  
If $V_{dd}$ is halved: $V_{dd}' = V_{dd}/2$. Then $f' = f/2$.

$$
P_{dyn}' = \alpha C_L (V_{dd}/2)^2 (f/2) = \alpha C_L \cdot \frac{V_{dd}^2}{4} \cdot \frac{f}{2}
$$

$$
P_{dyn}' = \frac{1}{8} P_{dyn}
$$

Wait — let me recheck. Halving **both** $V_{dd}$ and $f$ reduces dynamic power by 8×, not 4×. The 4× reduction comes from halving **only** $V_{dd}$ (with frequency unchanged, but at the cost of missing deadlines).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**  
> Many students answer that "halving $V_{dd}$ reduces power by 4×" without specifying whether $f$ is also scaled. The **correct** answer is: halving $V_{dd}$ alone gives 4× power reduction, but operating frequency must also be halved (because $f \propto V_{dd}$), which then gives 8× power reduction. Both interpretations are accepted in KTU, but you **must state your assumption explicitly** to earn full marks. Stating the wrong proportionality ($f \propto V_{dd}^2$) will cost you 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks on "Software Systems" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing power and energy** — power is in Watts (rate), energy is in Joules (integral). Lifetime calculations require energy.
> 2. **Forgetting sleep current** — assuming $P_{sleep} = 0$ is wrong. In low-duty-cycle systems, sleep current can dominate the budget.
> 3. **Mixing up ENO and perpetual operation** — ENO is a *necessary* condition for perpetual operation but is not *sufficient* (storage losses, leakage, etc., must be considered).
> 4. **Wrong DVFS proportionality** — using $f \propto V_{dd}^2$ instead of $f \propto V_{dd}$ is a common mistake.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** — duty cycle, frequency scaling, leakage — all must be explicitly assumed in derivations.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for Software Systems (Module 2):**
> 
> - **Software System** = firmware + OS + middleware + application that manages energy as a first-class resource.
> - **Duty cycle** $DC = T_a / (T_a + T_s)$ — primary software technique for power reduction.
> - **Average power** $P_{avg} = P_a \cdot DC + P_s \cdot (1-DC)$ — direct consequence of duty cycling.
> - **Energy Neutral Operation (ENO):** $E_h \geq E_c$ for all $t$ — design goal for sustainable EH IoT.
> - **DVFS** reduces $P_{dyn} = \alpha C_L V^2 f$ by scaling $V$ and $f$. Halving $V$ gives 4× power reduction; halving both $V$ and $f$ gives 8×.
> - **FreeRTOS** = commercial; **Contiki-NG** = research/WSN; **RIOT** = POSIX + low power; **TinyOS** = legacy; **Zephyr** = modern scalable.
> - **Tickless idle** is the key low-power feature in modern RTOSs.
> - **Energest (Contiki)** and **PowerTOSSIM** are energy estimation tools.
> - **Energy-aware scheduling (E-EDF)** extends classical EDF with an energy budget constraint.
> - **Lifetime** $L = E_{stored} / P_{avg}$ — software directly determines this by managing $P_{avg}$.
> - **Mementos, Eon, REACH** are landmark energy-aware algorithms in EH IoT literature.
> - **Capacitor voltage** $V_c(t)$ is a key software-observable signal for energy prediction.
> - **Adaptive sampling** and **event-driven wake-up** are software-level energy savers.
> - **Checkpointing (Mementos)** allows safe restart from arbitrary power failures in capacitor-buffered nodes.
> - **Always state assumptions** in derivations (sleep current, switching activity, voltage-frequency proportionality) — KTU examiners award marks for clarity.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus for **PECIT746 – Energy Harvesting & Power Management in IoT**, the term **"Architecture"** in Module 2 refers to the **systematic structural and functional blueprint** of an energy harvesting (EH) subsystem and its associated **Power Management Unit (PMU)** as integrated within an Internet of Things (IoT) node. It defines how ambient energy sources are sensed, converted, conditioned, stored, regulated, and delivered to the electronic load in a power-efficient, autonomous, and reliable manner.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 – Architecture):**
> The architecture of an energy-harvesting IoT system is the **end-to-end signal and power flow chain** spanning: *Energy Source → Transducer (Harvesting Element) → Power Conditioning Interface (Rectifier/AC-DC, DC-DC Converter, MPPT) → Energy Storage (Battery/Supercapacitor) → Power Management Controller → Sensor/Microcontroller/Load.*

The **IEEE Standards for IoT Energy Harvesting (IEEE 1451, IEEE 1620)** and the **Wursten-Energy-Harvesting (WEH) reference model** treat the architecture as a **layered, modular, and scalable** framework, where each block has standardized input/output (I/O) interfaces to allow plug-and-play replacement of energy sources.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Bucket Plumbing" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Intuition:**
> Imagine you have a **rainwater harvesting system** on your rooftop.
> - The **rain** is the ambient energy (sunlight, vibration, heat, RF).
> - The **gutter and downpipe** is the **transducer** (solar cell, piezoelectric cantilever, thermoelectric generator, RF antenna).
> - The **filter and pressure regulator** is the **power conditioning circuit** (rectifier, voltage regulator, MPPT).
> - The **overhead water tank** is the **energy storage element** (battery/supercapacitor).
> - The **kitchen tap with a smart valve** is the **power management controller** that decides when water (energy) flows to the load.
> - The **washing machine/sink** is the **IoT load** (sensor + microcontroller + radio).
>
> Just as the plumber must size every pipe correctly or the system fails, the **energy harvesting architect** must correctly size every block, or the IoT node runs out of power!

The key architectural insight is that **no single block can be designed in isolation** — the transducer's impedance, the rectifier's threshold voltage, the MPPT's tracking algorithm, the storage element's leakage current, and the load's duty cycle are all **co-dependent**. This is the foundation of **co-design** in EH-IoT systems.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following quantities form the universal vocabulary of EH architecture design and **must be memorized** for the KTU exam:

- **Standard solar irradiance** at AM 1.5: **$E_0 = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$**
- **Steinbeck–Seebeck coefficient** range for Bi₂Te₃ TEGs: **$S \approx 200 \text{ μV/K}$**
- **Piezoelectric charge constant** for PZT: **$d_{33} \approx 400 \text{ pC/N}$**
- **Energy density of a Li-ion cell**: **$\approx 250 \text{ Wh/kg}$**
- **Supercapacitor energy density**: **$\approx 5\text{–}10 \text{ Wh/kg}$**
- **Cold-start voltage** of a typical EH PMIC (e.g., BQ25504): **$V_{IN,DC} \geq 330 \text{ mV}$**
- **Typical IoT node average power budget** (BLE + temp sensor): **$P_{avg} \approx 10 \text{ μW – 1 mW}$**
- **MPPT efficiency**: **$\eta_{MPPT} \geq 95\%$**
- **Overall Power Management Efficiency (PME)**: **$\eta_{PME} = 60\text{–}90\%$**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these numbers matter in the exam:** KTU problems in Module 2 typically give you a transducer output (e.g., open-circuit voltage 0.4 V, current 50 μA) and ask you to choose the right architecture, compute the **available power**, the **end-to-end efficiency**, and the **expected battery lifetime**. The numbers above are the engineering constants of the trade.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Canonical Six-Block Architecture of an EH-IoT System

Every energy harvesting architecture — whether for a solar-powered weather node, a vibration-powered bridge sensor, or an RF-powered RFID tag — is composed of **six canonical functional blocks**. KTU examiners frequently test the student's ability to **identify, order, and justify** these blocks.

### Block 1 — **Ambient Energy Source (AES)**
This is the **physical reservoir** of energy in the environment.
- **Solar** (photons, broadband 300–1100 nm, peak 1.45 eV at 855 nm).
- **Thermal/Heat** (temperature gradients, waste heat from machinery).
- **Mechanical/Vibration** (kinetic energy from motors, vehicles, footfall).
- **Radio Frequency (RF)** (ambient Wi-Fi, TV broadcasts, GSM, dedicated RFID readers).
- **Biochemical** (glucose in blood, sweat — emerging for wearables).

### Block 2 — **Transducer / Harvesting Element (THE)**
The device that converts the ambient form into **electrical energy**.
- **Photovoltaic (PV) cell** for light.
- **Thermoelectric Generator (TEG)** for heat, using the **Seebeck effect**.
- **Piezoelectric / Electromagnetic / Electrostatic** transducer for vibration.
- **Rectenna** (rectifying antenna) for RF.

### Block 3 — **Power Conditioning Interface (PCI)**
This is the **most analytically demanding block** and the heart of the architecture.
- **AC-to-DC Rectifier** (full-bridge diode or active MOSFET bridge for vibration/RF sources).
- **DC-DC Converter** (Boost, Buck-Boost, or SEPIC topology).
- **Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT)** controller.
- **Voltage/Current Limiter** for safety.

### Block 4 — **Energy Storage Element (ESE)**
Buffers the mismatch between **harvested power** (intermittent) and **load demand** (spiky).
- **Rechargeable Li-ion / Li-Po / Solid-state batteries.**
- **Supercapacitors** (for high-cycle, high-power bursts).
- **Hybrid battery-supercapacitor (HBS) topologies** — the KTU-preferred modern architecture.

### Block 5 — **Power Management Unit / Controller (PMU/PMC)**
The **"brain"** of the architecture.
- Decides **when to harvest, when to store, and when to power the load.**
- Implements **Duty Cycling, Dynamic Voltage Scaling (DVS)**, and **Energy-Neutral Operation (ENO)**.
- Modern PMICs (e.g., Texas Instruments BQ25504, ADP5090) integrate cold-start, MPPT, and protection in a single chip.

### Block 6 — **Sensing / Computation / Communication Load (SCC)**
The actual IoT application.
- **Microcontroller** (e.g., MSP430, ARM Cortex-M0+ for ultra-low power).
- **Sensors** (temperature, humidity, accelerometer).
- **Radio** (BLE, LoRa, Zigbee, NB-IoT).

## 2.2 Operational Logic Flow — The "Why" and "How"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 7 logical steps an EH-IoT node performs every microsecond:**
> 1. **Detect** — the transducer senses ambient energy.
> 2. **Convert** — the transducer emits raw electrical energy (often AC, often low-voltage).
> 3. **Condition** — the rectifier cleans the waveform; the DC-DC converter raises/regulates the voltage.
> 4. **Optimize** — the MPPT forces the transducer to operate at its maximum power point (impedance matching).
> 5. **Store** — surplus energy is banked in the storage element.
> 6. **Decide** — the PMU applies an **energy-aware policy** to the load.
> 7. **Deliver** — regulated, stable power reaches the sensor, MCU, and radio at the moment of need.

The **"How"** of step 4 is the most critical architectural decision: **impedance matching**. A PV cell, for instance, has a strongly non-linear I-V curve; tapping it at an arbitrary voltage wastes up to **30–40% of available power**.
