The MPPT block, typically using **Perturb & Observe (P&O)** or **Incremental Conductance (IncCond)** algorithms, dynamically adjusts the input impedance of the DC-DC converter to keep the transducer operating at its **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are KTU 2024 high-yield items.** Memorize units and sign conventions. Note: every `|` (absolute value / magnitude) is written as `\vert` to keep markdown tables intact.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|---------------|
| 1 | $P_{avail} = V_{OC} \cdot I_{SC} \cdot FF$ | Available power from a generic transducer | **Watts (W)**; $FF$ = fill factor |
| 2 | $P_{PV} = \eta_{PV} \cdot A \cdot E_0$ | Solar PV output power | W; $A$ = cell area, $E_0$ = irradiance |
| 3 | $P_{TEG} = \frac{(S \cdot \Delta T)^2}{4 R_{int}}$ | Thermoelectric generator power (matched load) | W; $S$ = Seebeck coeff. |
| 4 | $P_{piezo} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C_p \cdot V_p^2 \cdot f$ | Piezoelectric harvested power per cycle | W; $C_p$ = piezo capacitance, $f$ = vibration freq. |
| 5 | $\eta_{rect} = \frac{P_{DC,out}}{P_{AC,in}}$ | Rectifier efficiency | Dimensionless, **typically 60–90%** |
| 6 | $\eta_{DC\text{-}DC} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}$ | DC-DC converter efficiency | Dimensionless, **80–95%** |
| 7 | $\eta_{MPPT} = \frac{P_{PV,MPP}}{P_{PV,actual}}$ | MPPT tracking efficiency | Dimensionless, **target $\geq 95\%$** |
| 8 | $\eta_{SYS} = \eta_{rect} \cdot \eta_{DC\text{-}DC} \cdot \eta_{MPPT} \cdot \eta_{storage}$ | **End-to-end system efficiency** | Dimensionless, **target 50–80%** |
| 9 | $E_{bat} = V_{bat} \cdot Q_{bat}$ | Energy stored in a battery | Joules (J) or Wh |
| 10 | $E_{cap} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot C \cdot V^2$ | Energy stored in a supercapacitor | Joules (J) |
| 11 | $P_{avg,load} = \frac{E_{cycle}}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{P_{active} \cdot t_{on} + P_{sleep} \cdot t_{off}}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$ | Average load power with duty cycling | W; **key for ENO design** |
| 12 | $ENO: \; P_{harvested,avg} \geq P_{load,avg}$ | **Energy-Neutral Operation condition** | Inequality; **fundamental design goal** |
| 13 | $V_{IN,min} = V_{th,rect} + V_{DS,ON}$ | Minimum cold-start voltage of EH-PMIC | V; **e.g., 330 mV for BQ25504** |
| 14 | $R_{match} = R_{int,source}$ | Maximum power transfer condition | Ω; **impedance matching** |
| 15 | $D = \frac{t_{on}}{T}$ | Duty cycle of the load | Dimensionless, $0 \leq D \leq 1$ |
| 16 | $t_{lifetime} = \frac{E_{store}}{P_{load,avg} - P_{harv,avg}}$ | Battery lifetime (when $P_{load} > P_{harv}$) | seconds / hours / years |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The architecture framework is **not academic fluff** — it is the **design checklist** used by industry for:
- **Smart agriculture nodes** powered by solar + supercap (e.g., Libelium Waspmote).
- **Structural Health Monitoring (SHM)** of bridges using piezoelectric harvesters from traffic vibration.
- **Wearable health monitors** powered by body heat via flexible TEGs.
- **Battery-less IoT** for Industry 4.0 — eliminating billions of throwaway coin cells.
- **Energy-autonomous Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)** — the architectural goal is *never to change the battery again*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section develops the **three most important KTU derivations** for Module 2 — Architecture. Every algebraic step is shown explicitly with explanatory text. No shortcuts.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — End-to-End System Efficiency $\eta_{SYS}$

**Problem Statement:** A solar-powered IoT node has a PV panel, a Schottky-diode full-bridge rectifier, a boost DC-DC converter with MPPT, and a Li-ion battery. Given individual block efficiencies, derive the expression for the **net electrical power delivered to the load** and the **overall system efficiency**.

**Step 1 — Define the input power.** The PV panel delivers raw DC power $P_{PV}$:
$$P_{PV} = \eta_{PV} \cdot A \cdot E_0$$

**Step 2 — Power after the rectifier.** The rectifier converts DC to a regulated DC bus (with some conduction loss):
$$P_{rect,out} = P_{PV} \cdot \eta_{rect}$$

**Step 3 — Power after the DC-DC converter with MPPT.** The converter steps up the voltage and tracks the maximum power point. The MPPT introduces an additional gain that captures the difference between the *actual* operating point and the true MPP:
$$P_{DC,out} = P_{rect,out} \cdot \eta_{DC\text{-}DC} \cdot \eta_{MPPT}$$

**Step 4 — Power into the storage element.** The battery/supercapacitor has its own charging efficiency (Coulombic efficiency $\eta_C$ and overcharge protection losses):
$$P_{store} = P_{DC,out} \cdot \eta_{storage}$$

**Step 5 — Power delivered to the load.** The PMU delivers this to the load with a final distribution efficiency $\eta_{dist}$:
$$P_{load} = P_{store} \cdot \eta_{dist}$$

**Step 6 — Combine all steps into a single expression.** Multiplying them all together:
$$P_{load} = P_{PV} \cdot \eta_{rect} \cdot \eta_{DC\text{-}DC} \cdot \eta_{MPPT} \cdot \eta_{storage} \cdot \eta_{dist}$$

**Step 7 — Define the overall system efficiency.** By definition, $\eta_{SYS} = P_{load} / P_{PV}$:
$$\boxed{\eta_{SYS} = \eta_{rect} \cdot \eta_{DC\text{-}DC} \cdot \eta_{MPPT} \cdot \eta_{storage} \cdot \eta_{dist}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Summary:** The cascaded efficiency formula is mathematically exact because **power is a multiplicative conserved quantity** through the chain — every block's loss simply multiplies the surviving fraction. If any single block has a low $\eta$, it dominates the entire chain. **Engineering takeaway:** A $50\%$ rectifier on an otherwise $95\%$ chain gives $\eta_{SYS} \approx 0.50 \times 0.95^4 \approx 0.41$ (i.e., **59% of harvested energy is wasted**).

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy-Neutral Operation (ENO) and Battery Lifetime

**Problem Statement:** An IoT node spends $t_{on}$ seconds in active mode (drawing $P_{active}$) and $t_{off}$ seconds in sleep mode (drawing $P_{sleep}$). The harvester delivers a constant $P_{harv}$ on average. Derive the **duty cycle $D$** required for **Energy-Neutral Operation (ENO)**, and the **battery lifetime** if ENO is violated.

**Step 1 — Average load power.** By the definition of average power over a periodic duty cycle:
$$P_{load,avg} = \frac{P_{active} \cdot t_{on} + P_{sleep} \cdot t_{off}}{t_{on} + t_{off}}$$

**Step 2 — Express $t_{on} = D \cdot T$ and $t_{off} = (1 - D) \cdot T$,** where $T = t_{on} + t_{off}$ is the period. Substituting:
$$P_{load,avg} = \frac{P_{active} \cdot D \cdot T + P_{sleep} \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T}{T}$$

**Step 3 — Cancel $T$ and simplify.**
$$P_{load,avg} = P_{active} \cdot D + P_{sleep} \cdot (1 - D)$$

**Step 4 — Expand.**
$$P_{load,avg} = P_{active} \cdot D + P_{sleep} - P_{sleep} \cdot D$$

**Step 5 — Collect terms in $D$.**
$$P_{load,avg} = P_{sleep} + D \cdot (P_{active} - P_{sleep})$$

**Step 6 — Apply the ENO condition.** ENO requires that the **harvested power equals the average load power** over a long period, so that the battery is neither depleted nor overcharged:
$$P_{harv,avg} = P_{load,avg} = P_{sleep} + D \cdot (P_{active} - P_{sleep})$$

**Step 7 — Solve algebraically for $D$.**
$$D \cdot (P_{active} - P_{sleep}) = P_{harv,avg} - P_{sleep}$$
$$\boxed{D = \frac{P_{harv,avg} - P_{sleep}}{P_{active} - P_{sleep}}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Summary:** The duty cycle $D$ represents the **maximum fraction of time** the node can be active while still surviving on harvested energy alone. If $D > 1$, the design is **energetically infeasible** — the harvester is too small. If $D < 0$, the harvester is **over-specified** — wasted potential. ENO is the **fundamental design equation** of every battery-less IoT node.

**Step 8 — Battery lifetime when ENO is violated.** Suppose the node consumes more than it harvests: $P_{load,avg} > P_{harv,avg}$. The net discharge power is:
$$P_{discharge} = P_{load,avg} - P_{harv,avg}$$

The battery's stored energy is $E_{store} = V_{bat} \cdot Q_{bat}$ (in Wh). The lifetime $t_{lifetime}$ (in hours) is:
$$t_{lifetime} = \frac{E_{store}}{P_{load,avg} - P_{harv,avg}}$$

**Step 9 — Final compact form.**
$$\boxed{t_{lifetime} = \frac{V_{bat} \cdot Q_{bat}}{P_{load,avg} - P_{harv,avg}}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Power Transfer & MPPT Necessity

**Problem Statement:** A transducer with internal voltage $V_{int}$ and internal resistance $R_{int}$ drives a load $R_L$. Show that **maximum power is delivered when $R_L = R_{int}$**, and quantify the power loss if impedance matching is not used.

**Step 1 — Thevenin equivalent circuit.** The transducer is modelled as:
$$V_{int} \longrightarrow R_{int} \longrightarrow R_{load}$$

**Step 2 — Current through the loop.** By Ohm's law:
$$I = \frac{V_{int}}{R_{int} + R_L}$$

**Step 3 — Power delivered to the load.**
$$P_L = I^2 \cdot R_L = \frac{V_{int}^2 \cdot R_L}{(R_{int} + R_L)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate $P_L$ with respect to $R_L$ and set to zero.** Let $x = R_L$ for clarity.
$$\frac{dP_L}{dx} = \frac{V_{int}^2 (R_{int} + x)^2 - V_{int}^2 x \cdot 2(R_{int} + x)}{(R_{int} + x)^4} = 0$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the numerator.**
$$V_{int}^2 \left[ (R_{int} + x)^2 - 2x(R_{int} + x) \right] = 0$$
$$(R_{int} + x) \left[ (R_{int} + x) - 2x \right] = 0$$
$$(R_{int} + x)(R_{int} - x) = 0$$

**Step 6 — Discard the trivial solution $R_L = -R_{int}$.** This gives:
$$\boxed{R_L = R_{int}}$$

**Step 7 — Maximum power value.** Substituting $R_L = R_{int}$ back:
$$P_{L,max} = \frac{V_{int}^2 \cdot R_{int}}{(2 R_{int})^2} = \frac{V_{int}^2}{4 R_{int}}$$

**Step 8 — Quantify mismatch loss.** If $R_L \neq R_{int}$, the relative loss is:
$$\frac{P_L}{P_{L,max}} = \frac{4 R_{int} R_L}{(R_{int} + R_L)^2}$$

**Step 9 — Example: $R_L = 0.1 R_{int}$ (very low load).**
$$\frac{P_L}{P_{L,max}} = \frac{4 \cdot 1 \cdot 0.1}{(1 + 0.1)^2} = \frac{0.4}{1.21} \approx 0.33 \; (33\%)$$

This shows that **mismatched impedance can waste 67% of the available power** — the architectural justification for including an MPPT block in every EH system.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Summary:** The Maximum Power Transfer Theorem is a **necessary but not sufficient** design rule for EH systems because transducers are often non-linear (PV cells) or frequency-dependent (piezoelectrics). The DC-DC converter + MPPT **dynamically synthesizes the optimal $R_L$** seen by the source, fulfilling the theorem in real-time.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Canonical Block Diagram of an Energy Harvesting IoT Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **complete end-to-end architecture** as per KTU Module 2 specification, showing power flow, control signals, and feedback paths.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AES[Ambient Energy Source] --> THE[Transducer Harvesting Element]
    THE --> PCI[Power Conditioning Interface]
    PCI --> MPPT[MPPT Controller]
    MPPT --> DCB[DC DC Converter]
    DCB --> ESE[Energy Storage Element]
    ESE --> PMU[Power Management Unit]
    PMU --> SCC[Sensing Computation Communication Load]
    PMU -.feedback.- MPPT
    PMU -.enable disable.- SCC
    ESE -.state of charge.- PMU

    classDef source fill:#FFF4CC,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef harvest fill:#CCE5FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px
    classDef condition fill:#D5E8D4,stroke:#82B366,stroke-width:2px
    classDef store fill:#FFD6D6,stroke:#A82B2B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef control fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#9673A6,stroke-width:2px
    classDef load fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#D2691E,stroke-width:2px

    class AES source
    class THE harvest
    class PCI,MPPT,DCB condition
    class ESE store
    class PMU control
    class SCC load
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Solid arrows** = power flow (forward).
- **Dashed arrows** = control/feedback signals.
- The PMU is the **central brain** that closes the feedback loop with the MPPT and gates the load.

## 4.2 Architecture Variants by Energy Source

The KTU syllabus expects students to know that the **architecture morphs** depending on the ambient source. The following diagram compares four common variants.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Solar[Architecture A: Solar PV Node]
        SOL1[Sunlight] --> SOL2[PV Cell Array]
        SOL2 --> SOL3[Schottky Bridge + MPPT Boost]
        SOL3 --> SOL4[Li ion Battery]
        SOL4 --> SOL5[PMIC BQ25504]
        SOL5 --> SOL6[MCU + NB IoT]
    end

    subgraph Vibration[Architecture B: Vibration Node]
        VIB1[Machine Vibration] --> VIB2[Piezo Cantilever]
        VIB2 --> VIB3[Active MOSFET Rectifier]
        VIB3 --> VIB4[SSHI Interface]
        VIB4 --> VIB5[Supercapacitor]
        VIB5 --> VIB6[Cold Start PMU]
        VIB6 --> VIB7[BLE SoC]
    end

    subgraph Thermal[Architecture C: Thermal Gradient Node]
        THR1[Hot Pipe 100C] --> THR2[TEG Module Bi2Te3]
        THR2 --> THR3[Low V Boost LTC3108]
        THR3 --> THR4[Thin Film Battery]
        THR4 --> THR5[PMU ADP5090]
        THR5 --> THR6[LoRa Sensor]
    end

    subgraph RF[Architecture D: RF Harvesting Node]
        RF1[Ambient Wi Fi 2.4GHz] --> RF2[Dual Band Rectenna]
        RF2 --> RF3[Schottky Diode HSMS 2852]
        RF3 --> RF4[Charge Pump x10]
        RF4 --> RF5[Micro Supercapacitor]
        RF5 --> RF6[Passive RFID Tag]
    end

    classDef sourceA fill:#FFEB99
    classDef harvestA fill:#B3D9FF
    classDef storageA fill:#FFB3B3
    classDef loadA fill:#C2F0C2
```

**Architectural insights from the comparison:**
- **Solar and Thermal** use **DC-DC boost converters** (sources are DC).
- **Vibration and RF** use **AC-DC rectifiers first**, then DC-DC (sources are AC).
- **Piezo** requires **SSHI (Synchronized Switch Harvesting on Inductor)** to recover the usually-lost $Q^2/2C$ charge.
- **RF** uses **voltage multiplier charge pumps** because harvested voltages are typically **< 200 mV**.

## 4.3 Power Management Unit (PMU) Internal Architecture

The PMU is the **architectural heart** of any EH-IoT system. The Mermaid block diagram below shows its internal sub-blocks.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VIN[Input from DC DC] --> CS[Cold Start Circuit 330mV]
    CS --> REG[Internal LDO Regulator]
    REG --> MPPT2[MPPT Algorithm Block]
    MPPT2 --> CHG[Battery Charger Controller]
    CHG --> BAT[Li ion or SuperCap]
    BAT --> MON[Battery Monitor SOC SOH]
    MON --> LDO2[Output LDO 1.8V 3.3V]
    LDO2 --> LOAD2[IoT Load]
    MON -.SOC feedback.- MPPT2
    MON -.overvoltage protection.- CHG
    MON -.undervoltage lockout.- LDO2

    classDef pmu fill:#E1D5E7,stroke:#6C3A91
    classDef batt fill:#FFD6D6,stroke:#A82B2B
    classDef out fill:#C2F0C2,stroke:#2E7D32
    class CS,REG,MPPT2,CHG,MON,LDO2 pmu
    class BAT batt
    class LOAD2,VIN out
```

**Key architectural functions of the PMU:**
- **Cold start** from as low as **330 mV** without any battery.
- **MPPT** to harvest maximum available energy.
- **SOC monitoring** to prevent over-charge / over-discharge.
- **Output regulation** at stable 1.8 V / 3.3 V for digital loads.
- **Load gating** to duty-cycle the application processor.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper PECIT746]**
*List and briefly explain the six canonical blocks of an energy harvesting IoT system architecture.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — one mark per two blocks, half-mark each for naming + brief explanation):**

The six blocks are:
1. **Ambient Energy Source (AES):** The physical reservoir — solar, thermal, vibration, RF.
2. **Transducer (THE):** Converts ambient energy to electrical form (PV cell, TEG, piezo, rectenna).
3. **Power Conditioning Interface (PCI):** Rectifies, boosts, and tracks MPP via MPPT.
4. **Energy Storage Element (ESE):** Buffers intermittency — battery, supercapacitor, or hybrid.
5. **Power Management Unit (PMU):** Decides when to harvest, store, or power the load; runs ENO.
6. **Sensing / Computation / Communication (SCC) Load:** The IoT application's MCU + sensors + radio.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** A clean numbered list with a one-line description per block scores full marks. Do **not** write paragraphs — KTU board examiners reward crispness.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PECIT746 Supplementary]**
*State and explain the Energy-Neutral Operation (ENO) condition for an EH-IoT node.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement:** An EH-IoT node operates in **Energy-Neutral Mode (ENM)** when, over a long time horizon, the **average harvested power equals or exceeds the average load power**:
$$P_{harv,avg} \geq P_{load,avg}$$

**Explanation:** Under ENO, the storage element is never fully depleted and never overcharged — the node can theoretically operate **indefinitely without battery replacement**. This is achieved by **duty cycling** the load and using a **closed-loop PMU** that adapts the duty cycle to the available harvested power. The maximum sustainable duty cycle is:
$$D_{max} = \frac{P_{harv,avg} - P_{sleep}}{P_{active} - P_{sleep}}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Format:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each. **Internal choice** is permitted, so you may attempt **either OR** of the two full questions provided below.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model PECIT746, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **architecture of a solar-powered IoT node** and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A solar panel of area $10 \text{ cm}^2$ operates at **30% efficiency** under irradiance $E_0 = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$. The rectifier efficiency is 75%, the boost converter efficiency is 90%, MPPT efficiency is 95%, and the storage efficiency is 85%. Compute the **available PV power** and the **end-to-end system efficiency**. If the load requires 1 mW average, is ENO satisfied? **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Block Diagram Description (7 marks):**

The architecture of a solar-powered IoT node consists of the following blocks in series:

**Block 1 — PV Cell Array:** A series-parallel connection of silicon cells (typically 6–8 cells for 3 V output) that converts solar irradiance $E_0$ into DC electrical power $P_{PV} = \eta_{PV} A E_0$. **(1 mark)**

**Block 2 — Schottky Bridge Rectifier:** A full-bridge rectifier using low-forward-voltage Schottky diodes (e.g., BAT54S) to allow cold-start at low input voltages; the rectifier prevents reverse current flow at night. **(1 mark)**

**Block 3 — MPPT-Enabled Boost Converter:** A DC-DC boost converter (e.g., TPS61236 from TI) that steps up the low PV voltage (often 0.5–1.5 V) to a stable 3.3–4.2 V battery bus. The MPPT algorithm (P&O or IncCond) dynamically adjusts the converter's duty cycle so the PV cell operates at its maximum power point. **(1 mark)**

**Block 4 — Energy Storage:** A Li-ion / Li-Po cell (or supercapacitor for short-term buffering) that stores surplus energy and supplies the load during night/cloudy periods. **(1 mark)**

**Block 5 — Power Management Unit (PMU):** A dedicated PMIC (e.g., BQ25504) that performs cold-start from 330 mV, monitors battery SOC, protects against over-voltage/over-current, and generates the regulated output voltage. **(1 mark)**

**Block 6 — Load (MCU + Sensor + Radio):** An ultra-low-power MCU (e.g., MSP430) reads sensors (temperature, humidity) and transmits via BLE/LoRa. The PMU duty-cycles the load using GPIO enable signals. **(1 mark)**

**Block Diagram (drawn in exam — should resemble Section 4.1 above). (1 mark)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Calculate the available PV power.**
$$P_{PV} = \eta_{PV} \cdot A \cdot E_0 = 0.30 \times (10 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2) \times 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$$
$$P_{PV} = 0.30 \times 0.001 \times 1000 = 0.30 \text{ W} = 300 \text{ mW}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the cascaded efficiency formula.**
$$\eta_{SYS} = \eta_{rect} \cdot \eta_{DC\text{-}DC} \cdot \eta_{MPPT} \cdot \eta_{storage}$$
$$\eta_{SYS} = 0.75 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 \times 0.85$$

**Step 3 — Multiply step by step.**
$$0.75 \times 0.90 = 0.675$$
$$0.675 \times 0.95 = 0.64125$$
$$0.64125 \times 0.85 = 0.5450625$$

**Step 4 — State the result.**
$$\eta_{SYS} \approx 0.545 \; (54.5\%)$$

**Step 5 — Calculate power delivered to the load.**
$$P_{load,delivered} = P_{PV} \times \eta_{SYS} = 300 \text{ mW} \times 0.545 = 163.5 \text{ mW}$$

**Step 6 — Check ENO condition.**
$$P_{load,delivered} = 163.5 \text{ mW} \gg P_{load,required} = 1 \text{ mW}$$
Since $P_{delivered} \gg P_{required}$, **ENO is comfortably satisfied.** The node is grossly over-provisioned; a smaller PV area could be used to reduce cost.

**Step 7 — Conclude with engineering insight (extra credit).**
> The 45.5% power loss is dominated by the rectifier (25% loss) — replacing the Schottky bridge with an **active MOSFET rectifier** ($\eta_{rect} \approx 92\%$) would raise $\eta_{SYS}$ to about **66%**, significantly improving the energy budget.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, PECIT746, Adapted]**

**(a)** Compare the **architectures of solar, vibration, thermal, and RF energy harvesting systems** in a tabular form. Highlight the differences in transducer, rectifier, and storage choice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **condition for maximum power transfer** from a Thevenin-equivalent transducer to a load, and show that **MPPT is essential** in EH-IoT architectures. A piezoelectric transducer has $V_{int} = 1.2 \text{ V}$ and $R_{int} = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$. Compute the maximum deliverable power. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Comparative Architecture Table (7 marks):**

| Feature | **Solar** | **Vibration** | **Thermal** | **RF** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Source type | DC (irradiance) | AC (vibration) | DC (heat flux) | AC (EM wave) |
| Transducer | PV cell (Si, GaAs) | Piezo / EM / Electrostatic | TEG (Bi₂Te₃, PbTe) | Rectenna (antenna + diode) |
| Voltage level | 0.5 – 5 V | 1 – 20 V (AC) | 0.1 – 0.5 V (DC) | 0.05 – 0.3 V (AC) |
| Rectifier required | Optional (DC source) | **Yes — full-bridge** | Optional (DC source) | **Yes — Schottky + charge pump** |
| DC-DC topology | Boost / Buck-Boost | SSHI + Boost | **Low-V boost (LTC3108)** | Charge pump (Villard) |
| MPPT? | **Yes** (P&O) | **Yes** (SSHI sync) | Limited (linear) | Limited (impedance match) |
| Storage | Li-ion / Li-Po | Supercapacitor (fast cycle) | Thin-film battery | Micro-supercapacitor |
| Typical power | 1 – 100 mW | 10 μW – 10 mW | 10 μW – 10 mW | 1 μW – 1 mW |
| Cold-start challenge | Low | Medium | **High** (needs special IC) | **Very high** (needs < 100 mV IC) |
| Use case | Outdoor IoT, agri | Industrial SHM | Wearables, boilers | Smart cards, indoor BLE |

**Marking:** 1 mark for correctly identifying source type + transducer; 1 mark for rectifier/DC-DC; 1 mark for storage; 1 mark for power range; 1 mark for cold-start challenge; 2 marks for engineering insight / use case.

**Part (b) — Maximum Power Transfer Derivation + Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Thevenin model.** The transducer is modelled as $V_{int}$ in series with $R_{int}$, driving a load $R_L$. **(1 mark)**

**Step 2 — Load current and power.**
$$I = \frac{V_{int}}{R_{int} + R_L}, \quad P_L = I^2 R_L = \frac{V_{int}^2 R_L}{(R_{int} + R_L)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate and set to zero.**
$$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = \frac{V_{int}^2(R_{int} - R_L)}{(R_{int} + R_L)^3} = 0 \implies \boxed{R_L = R_{int}}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum power formula.**
$$P_{L,max} = \frac{V_{int}^2}{4 R_{int}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical computation.**
$$P_{L,max} = \frac{(1.2)^2}{4 \times 10{,}000} = \frac{1.44}{40{,}000} = 3.6 \times 10^{-5} \text{ W} = 36 \text{ μW}$$

**Step 6 — Explain why MPPT is essential.** Real transducers have **non-linear I-V characteristics** (PV cells) or **frequency-dependent impedance** (piezos). The optimal $R_L$ varies with temperature, vibration frequency, and aging. A fixed $R_L$ results in **30–60% power loss** due to impedance mismatch. An MPPT controller **dynamically adjusts** the DC-DC converter's input impedance to maintain $R_{L,opt} = R_{int}(t)$ in real time, capturing the maximum available energy. **(2 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfalls that cost students 2–3 marks per question:**
> 1. **Forgetting the units** in the final answer of numerical problems. Always write W or mW or μW explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing P with E.** Power is in Watts; Energy is in Joules or Wh. The duty cycle is dimensionless.
> 3. **Writing the ENO inequality backwards.** It is $P_{harv} \geq P_{load}$, never the reverse.
> 4. **Omitting the MPPT block** in the block diagram. The MPPT is **mandatory** in KTU's expected architecture for solar/vibration systems.
> 5. **Confusing $\eta_{MPPT}$ with $\eta_{SYS}$.** $\eta_{MPPT}$ is the efficiency of the tracking algorithm alone (95%+); $\eta_{SYS}$ is the cascaded efficiency of every block (typically 50–80%).
> 6. **Not justifying the choice of storage element.** Always state *why* you chose a supercapacitor (high cycle life, fast charge) or a battery (high energy density, slow discharge).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for "Architecture" — Module 2:**

- ☐ The architecture has **six canonical blocks**: AES → THE → PCI → ESE → PMU → SCC.
- ☐ **Always draw the block diagram** first in any 7-mark theory question — it earns you 2–3 marks instantly.
- ☐ The **MPPT block is non-negotiable** for solar, vibration, and high-power RF systems.
- ☐ The **cold-start voltage** of modern PMICs is around **330 mV** (BQ25504 / ADP5090).
- ☐ **Solar and thermal** sources are DC → need DC-DC only. **Vibration and RF** are AC → need AC-DC rectifier first.
- ☐ The cascaded efficiency formula is:
  $$\eta_{SYS} = \eta_{rect} \cdot \eta_{DC\text{-}DC} \cdot \eta_{MPPT} \cdot \eta_{storage} \cdot \eta_{dist}$$
- ☐ **Energy-Neutral Operation (ENO):** $P_{harv,avg} \geq P_{load,avg}$. The maximum duty cycle is:
  $$D_{max} = \frac{P_{harv,avg} - P_{sleep}}{P_{active} - P_{sleep}}$$
- ☐ Battery lifetime under violated ENO:
  $$t_{lifetime} = \frac{V_{bat} \cdot Q_{bat}}{P_{load,avg} - P_{harv,avg}}$$
- ☐ **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem:** $P_{L,max} = V_{int}^2 / (4 R_{int})$ when $R_L = R_{int}$.
- ☐ Energy stored in a supercapacitor: $E_{cap} = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ (in Joules).
- ☐ Energy stored in a battery: $E_{bat} = V_{bat} \cdot Q_{bat}$ (in Wh).
- ☐ The **duty cycle $D$** is defined as $D = t_{on} / T$ where $T = t_{on} + t_{off}$.
- ☐ A typical IoT node's **average power budget** is **10 μW to 1 mW**; the harvester must match or exceed this.
- ☐ **Mismatched impedance wastes 30–67% of power** — justify every block in your design.
- ☐ The **PMU is the brain** — it enables MPPT, monitors SOC, and duty-cycles the load.
- ☐ Use the **architecture comparison table** in Question B(a) to revise source-specific differences.
- ☐ **KTU 2024 mark distribution reminder:** Part A = 3 marks × 2 Q = 6 marks; Part B = 14 marks × 1 Q (with internal choice) = 14 marks; total Module 2 weightage follows KTU's continuous + ESE split as per the 2024 scheme regulations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modeling and Simulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Modeling and Simulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **PECIT746 – Energy Harvesting & Power Management in IoT**, **Modeling and Simulation** refers to the systematic process of constructing a mathematically rigorous, computationally tractable abstraction of a physical energy harvesting transducer (e.g., piezoelectric, photovoltaic, thermoelectric, RF), its associated power conditioning circuitry, and the load/IoT node, in order to predict, analyse, and optimise system behaviour under realistic operating conditions *before* fabrication.

According to standard systems-engineering taxonomy adopted in KTU 2024 scheme syllabi, the workflow consists of three coupled stages:

1. **Modelling** – Deriving governing differential/algebraic equations that map physical inputs (vibration, solar irradiance, thermal gradient, RF field strength) to electrical outputs (voltage, current, power).
2. **Simulation** – Numerically solving those equations using tools such as **MATLAB/Simulink**, **LTspice**, **PSpice**, **COMSOL Multiphysics**, or **ANSYS Maxwell** to obtain time-domain and frequency-domain responses.
3. **Verification & Validation (V&V)** – Comparing simulated outputs against analytical predictions and experimental measurements to establish credibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The course outcome for Module 2 mandates that the student must be able to *develop lumped and distributed parameter models* of energy harvesters and *simulate the complete power management path* from transducer to battery/IoT load.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to know whether a small rooftop solar panel (5 W) can keep an outdoor weather sensor alive forever. Building a prototype, mounting it on the roof, and waiting for six months of cloudy weather is slow and expensive.

Instead, you **model** the panel as a math equation (current depends on sunlight and temperature), **simulate** that equation on a computer across thousands of weather conditions, and *predict* how much energy you will harvest every hour of the year. Modelling is the *recipe*; simulation is *tasting the dish* before serving it to the customer.

### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in IoT

IoT nodes are typically **wireless**, **battery-constrained**, and deployed in inaccessible locations. Designing a harvester without a validated model leads to:
- Over-designed (bulky, expensive) harvesters, or
- Under-designed harvesters that cannot sustain the duty cycle of the node.

A good model reduces design iterations from dozens to one or two prototypes, slashing **time-to-market** and **bill-of-materials cost**.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics (KTU-Expected Terminology)

The following metrics are repeatedly evaluated in board questions and must be memorised:

- **Output power:** $P_{\text{out}}$ in **watts (W)** or **milliwatts (mW)**. For IoT harvesters, typical range: **10 µW – 100 mW**.
- **Power conversion efficiency:** $\eta = P_{\text{out}} / P_{\text{in}} \times 100\%$.
- **Bandwidth (Hz):** the frequency range over which a vibration harvester delivers ≥ 50% of its peak power.
- **Figure of Merit (FoM):** transducer-specific dimensionless number used to compare harvesters.
- **Time constant:** $\tau = RC$ (seconds) governing how fast the storage element charges.
- **State of Charge (SoC):** percentage of usable energy in the battery/super-capacitor.

> [!NOTE]
> Always state units explicitly in board answers. Examiner deduction of marks is often triggered by missing units in final numerical values.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Levels of Modelling

Energy-harvester modelling is conventionally organised into a four-level hierarchy. Mastering each level is essential because KTU questions often require you to identify *which level* a particular simulation operates at.

- **Level 1 – Distributed-Parameter (Physics) Model**
  Uses partial differential equations (PDEs) derived from Maxwell's equations, solid mechanics, or heat-transfer equations. Examples: finite-element modal analysis of a piezoelectric cantilever in **COMSOL**, electromagnetic field distribution in an RF rectenna via **HFSS**.

- **Level 2 – Lumped-Parameter (Equivalent-Circuit) Model**
  Replaces the distributed physics with discrete R, L, C, and controlled sources. Example: Butterworth-Van-Dyke (BVD) equivalent circuit of a piezoelectric cantilever. Suitable for **SPICE-class simulators**.

- **Level 3 – Behavioural / Macro-Model**
  A black-box model with input-output mapping (e.g., harvested power as a function of acceleration). Often implemented in **MATLAB/Simulink** or **Simscape**.

- **Level 4 – System-Level / Co-Simulation**
  Couples transducer macro-models, power-electronics switches, and battery models in a single time-domain simulation (e.g., **PLECS**, **PSIM**, or co-simulation between Simulink and LTspice).

### 2.2 Canonical Models for Major Energy Harvesters

The KTU syllabus emphasises three primary transduction mechanisms. Below is the consolidated formula sheet.

#### 2.2.1 Photovoltaic (PV) Cell – Single-Diode Model

A PV cell is modelled as a photo-generated current source $I_{ph}$ in parallel with a diode, a series resistance $R_s$, and a shunt resistance $R_{sh}$.

$$I = I_{ph} - I_{0}\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{q(V + IR_s)}{n k T}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$

where:

- $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}$ (electron charge)
- $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}\ \text{J/K}$ (Boltzmann constant)
- $n$ = diode ideality factor (typically 1 – 2)
- $T$ = cell temperature in **kelvin (K)**
- $I_{0}$ = diode saturation current

#### 2.2.2 Piezoelectric Vibration Harvester – BVD Equivalent Circuit

For a cantilever beam with a piezoelectric patch operating near resonance, the Butterworth-Van-Dyke model gives:

$$Z(\omega) = \frac{1}{j\omega C_p}\ \Big\|\ \left(R_s + j\omega L_m + \frac{1}{j\omega C_m}\right)$$

- $C_p$ : parasitic (clamped) capacitance
- $L_m$ : motional inductance (mass analogue)
- $C_m$ : motional capacitance (compliance analogue)
- $R_m$ : mechanical loss (often written inside the series branch)

#### 2.2.3 Thermoelectric Generator (TEG)

A TEG with $N$ thermocouples produces a Seebeck voltage:

$$V_{\text{oc}} = N \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

The internal resistance is $R_{\text{int}} = N \cdot (\rho L / A)$. Maximum power transfer occurs when load resistance equals $R_{\text{int}}$, giving:

$$P_{\max} = \frac{V_{\text{oc}}^{2}}{4 R_{\text{int}}}$$

#### 2.2.4 RF / Electromagnetic Harvester

The harvested DC power at the load after the rectifier is:

$$P_{\text{DC}} = \eta_{\text{rect}} \cdot P_{\text{avail}} = \eta_{\text{rect}} \cdot S \cdot A_e \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^{2}$$

where $S$ = power density (W/m²), $A_e$ = effective aperture, $G_r$ = receive antenna gain, $d$ = distance, $\lambda$ = wavelength.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Domain | Governing Equation | Key Variables | Typical Units |
|---|--------|-------------------|---------------|----------------|
| 1 | PV cell I–V | $I = I_{ph} - I_{0}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\tfrac{q(V+IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] - \tfrac{V+IR_s}{R_{sh}}$ | $I_{ph}, I_{0}, n, T, R_s, R_{sh}$ | A, V, K, Ω |
| 2 | PV Fill Factor | $FF = \tfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$ | $V_{mp}, I_{mp}$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Piezo resonance | $f_n = \tfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{L_m C_m}}$ | $L_m, C_m$ | Hz |
| 4 | Piezo BVD impedance | $Z(\omega) = \tfrac{1}{j\omega C_p} \Big\vert\vert \bigl(R_m + j\omega L_m + \tfrac{1}{j\omega C_m}\bigr)$ | $R_m, L_m, C_m, C_p$ | Ω |
| 5 | TEG open-circuit voltage | $V_{oc} = N \alpha \Delta T$ | $N, \alpha, \Delta T$ | V |
| 6 | TEG maximum power | $P_{\max} = \tfrac{(N\alpha\Delta T)^{2}}{4 N \rho L / A}$ | $\rho, L, A$ | W |
| 7 | RF Friis harvested | $P_{r} = P_t G_t G_r \bigl(\tfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\bigr)^{2}$ | $P_t, G_t, G_r, \lambda, d$ | W |
| 8 | Rectifier efficiency | $\eta_{\text{rect}} = \tfrac{P_{DC}}{P_{RF,in}}$ | — | dimensionless |
| 9 | Super-cap charge time | $V_c(t) = V_{\infty}\bigl(1 - e^{-t/RC}\bigr)$ | $R, C, t$ | V, s |
| 10 | Battery SoC | $\text{SoC}(t) = \text{SoC}_0 + \tfrac{1}{E_{\text{nom}}}\!\int_0^{t}\!\bigl(P_{\text{in}} - P_{\text{load}}\bigr)dt$ | $E_{\text{nom}}, P$ | % |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Vibration harvesters on helicopter rotor blades are modelled using distributed-parameter FEM to predict fatigue and power output simultaneously.
- **Wearables:** Flexible PV cells on smart watches are simulated with multi-physics (optical + electrical) tools to balance efficiency against skin-temperature rise.
- **Industrial IoT:** Predictive maintenance sensors in factories use TEG models to estimate energy autonomy from waste-heat gradients around steam pipes.
- **Smart Agriculture:** Soil-moisture sensors buried in fields rely on RF-harvesting models to size the antenna for the available cellular/LoRa back-scatter energy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section demonstrates the kind of derivation KTU examiners expect for full marks. Two classical derivations are worked out exhaustively.

### 3.1 Derivation: Maximum Power Point of a Photovoltaic Cell

**Statement:** Show that the maximum power delivered by a PV cell to an external load occurs when the differential of power with respect to terminal voltage vanishes.

**Step 1 – Define terminal power.**
The instantaneous electrical power delivered by the cell to the load is:

$$P = V \cdot I$$

For an ideal single-diode cell with $R_s \to 0$ and $R_{sh} \to \infty$ (simplification justified when the cell operates near its maximum-power region):

$$I = I_{ph} - I_{0}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 2 – Substitute into the power expression.**

$$P(V) = V \cdot I_{ph} - V \cdot I_{0}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 3 – Differentiate with respect to voltage and set to zero.**
The maximum-power condition is $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$.

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = I_{ph} - I_{0}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] - V_{mp}\,I_{0}\,\frac{q}{nkT}\exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right)$$

Grouping the exponential terms:

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = I_{ph} - I_{0}\!\left[\left(1 + \frac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right)\exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] = 0$$

**Step 4 – Solve for the transcendental relationship.**
Rearranging the previous equation gives the canonical implicit MPP condition used in every Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) algorithm:

$$I_{ph} + I_{0} = I_{0}\!\left(1 + \frac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right)\exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right)$$

This transcendental equation cannot be solved in closed form; iterative numerical methods (Newton–Raphson) are used inside simulators such as **LTspice** or **MATLAB/Simulink**.

**Step 5 – Define Fill Factor for clarity.**
For a quick analytical estimate, the **Fill Factor** summarises the "squareness" of the I–V curve:

$$FF = \frac{V_{mp}\,I_{mp}}{V_{oc}\,I_{sc}}$$

The conversion efficiency then becomes:

$$\eta = \frac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{P_{\text{in}}}$$

where $P_{\text{in}}$ is the incident solar irradiance multiplied by the cell area.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Optimal Load Resistance for a Thermoelectric Generator

**Statement:** Prove that maximum electrical power is transferred from a TEG to an external resistive load when the load resistance equals the TEG's internal resistance.

**Step 1 – Write the Thevenin equivalent of the TEG.**
A TEG behaves as a voltage source $V_{\text{oc}} = N\alpha\Delta T$ in series with an internal resistance $R_{\text{int}} = N\rho L / A$.

**Step 2 – Express the load current.**
Applying Kirchhoff's voltage law around the source-load loop:

$$I = \frac{V_{\text{oc}}}{R_{\text{int}} + R_L}$$

**Step 3 – Express power dissipated in the load.**

$$P_L = I^{2} R_L = \frac{V_{\text{oc}}^{2}\,R_L}{(R_{\text{int}} + R_L)^{2}}$$

**Step 4 – Differentiate with respect to $R_L$ and set to zero.**

$$\frac{dP_L}{dR_L} = V_{\text{oc}}^{2}\cdot \frac{(R_{\text{int}} + R_L)^{2} - 2R_L(R_{\text{int}} + R_L)}{(R_{\text{int}} + R_L)^{4}}$$

**Step 5 – Simplify numerator.**
Factor $(R_{\text{int}} + R_L)$ from the numerator:

$$\text{Numerator} = (R_{\text{int}} + R_L)\bigl[(R_{\text{int}} + R_L) - 2R_L\bigr] = (R_{\text{int}} + R_L)(R_{\text{int}} - R_L)$$

**Step 6 – Identify the optimum.**
Setting the numerator to zero (with the trivial solution $R_L = -R_{\text{int}}$ rejected because resistance is positive):

$$R_L^{\text{opt}} = R_{\text{int}}$$

**Step 7 – Compute the maximum power.**
Substituting $R_L = R_{\text{int}}$ back into the load-power expression:

$$P_{L,\max} = \frac{V_{\text{oc}}^{2}\,R_{\text{int}}}{(2R_{\text{int}})^{2}} = \frac{V_{\text{oc}}^{2}}{4R_{\text{int}}} = \frac{(N\alpha\Delta T)^{2}}{4\,N\rho L / A}$$

The result $\boxed{P_{\max} = \dfrac{(N\alpha\Delta T)^{2}}{4N\rho L/A}}$ is the single most-tested TEG formula in KTU board papers.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Simulation Tool

| Transducer | Best Physics Tool | Best Circuit Tool | Best System Tool | KTU Comment |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------------|-------------|
| Photovoltaic | COMSOL / FDTD | LTspice (single-diode) | MATLAB/Simulink + Simscape Electronics | Always cite $I_{ph}$ vs irradiance |
| Piezoelectric | ANSYS Modal / COMSOL | LTspice (BVD model) | Simulink + Stateflow | Watch resonance drift with temperature |
| Thermoelectric | COMSOL Heat + Electric | LTspice (Thevenin) | Simulink | Couple hot-side & cold-side temperatures |
| RF / Rectenna | HFSS / FEKO | ADS / AWR (harmonic balance) | Simulink + RF Blockset | Match impedance at –10 dBm typical |
| Wind / Flow | OpenFOAM (CFD) | LTspice (PMSG model) | Simulink | Include turbine $C_p$–$\lambda$ curve |

### 3.4 Verification & Validation (V&V) Workflow

A KTU-quality answer to a "describe the simulation workflow" question should mention the following five V&V steps:

1. **Code-to-model traceability** – ensure every line of the simulation script corresponds to an equation in the model documentation.
2. **Unit & dimensional checks** – verify that all intermediate quantities carry SI units.
3. **Convergence study** – reduce time-step / mesh size until the result no longer changes within 1%.
4. **Sensitivity analysis** – sweep parameters (irradiance, temperature, acceleration) to identify dominant variables.
5. **Experimental correlation** – overlay simulated waveforms on measured oscilloscope traces.

> [!NOTE]
> A complete simulation report in a KTU lab viva should include *all five* V&V steps. Missing step (4) or (5) is the most common reason for partial-marks deduction.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Modelling and Simulation Workflow

The following block diagram captures the full modelling-to-deployment pipeline as expected in KTU module-2 answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real Physical Transducer] --> B[Distributed Parameter PDE Model]
    B --> C[Lumped Equivalent Circuit Model]
    C --> D[Behavioural Macro Model]
    D --> E[System Level Co Simulation]
    E --> F[Power Electronics and PMU Model]
    F --> G[Storage Element Model]
    G --> H[IoT Load Duty Cycle Model]
    H --> I[Simulation Engine MATLAB LTspice COMSOL]
    I --> J[Results Time Domain Frequency Energy]
    J --> K[Verification vs Analytical]
    K --> L[Validation vs Experiment]
    L --> M{Model Accepted}
    M -- Yes --> N[Prototype Fabrication]
    M -- No --> O[Refine Parameters]
    O --> B
```

### 4.2 Piezoelectric Harvester Equivalent-Circuit Mapping

The block diagram below illustrates how the four branches of the Butterworth-Van-Dyke equivalent circuit map to physical phenomena inside a cantilever beam harvester.

```mermaid
    E --> F
    F --> G[Clamped Capacitance Cp]

```

The block diagram below shows the typical signal chain modelled inside **MATLAB/Simulink** or **PSpice** for a complete PV-powered IoT node.


### 4.4 Simulation Tool Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start What Do You Want to Model] --> B{Physics Level Required}
    B -- Field PDE --> C[COMSOL ANSYS HFSS]

```

### 4.5 Co-Simulation Architecture for an IoT Energy Harvester

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    end

        E1[Rectifier]
        E2[DC DC Converter]
        E3[Energy Storage]
        E4[Load]
    end
    subgraph Control["Control Domain MATLAB Simulink"]
        C1[MPPT Algorithm]
        C2[Battery Management Logic]
        C3[Duty Cycle of IoT Node]
    end
    P1 -- Look Up Table or S Parameter --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> E3
    E3 --> E4
    C1 --> E2
    C2 --> E3
    C3 --> E4
    E4 -- Feedback SoC --> C2
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Diagram Fallback Rule Used:** For module-level system architecture (Section 4.3 – 4.5), a block-level functional architecture flow is provided because the topic is inherently cross-domain. Piezo-cantilever physical drawings cannot be rendered inside Mermaid, so the BVD mapping diagram is used instead.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
*Define the term "Lumped-Parameter Model" as applied to energy harvesters. Give one example.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A lumped-parameter model represents a spatially distributed physical system (e.g., a vibrating piezoelectric cantilever) by a finite set of discrete elements — resistors, inductors, capacitors, and controlled sources — whose values are chosen so that the input–output behaviour of the circuit matches the first few resonant modes of the actual structure. *Example:* the Butterworth-Van-Dyke (BVD) equivalent circuit of a piezoelectric cantilever, where the motional inductance $L_m$, motional capacitance $C_m$, mechanical resistance $R_m$, and clamped capacitance $C_p$ reproduce the impedance spectrum of the beam around its first resonance. (3 marks)

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
*List any three simulation tools used for energy-harvester design and state one specific strength of each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **LTspice** – free, fast SPICE-class transient simulator; excellent for power-electronics and rectifier modelling. (1 mark)
2. **COMSOL Multiphysics** – finite-element solver; couples structural, thermal, and electromagnetic physics in one environment. (1 mark)
3. **MATLAB/Simulink** – block-diagram system simulator; convenient for control-loop design, MPPT algorithms, and battery models. (1 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 Scheme rule: Module-2 carries a single 14-mark question with internal choice. Either **Question A** *or* **Question B** must be answered.

---

#### ❖ Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the maximum-power-transfer condition for a thermoelectric generator (TEG) connected to a resistive load, and compute the maximum power for a TEG with $N = 200$ thermocouples, Seebeck coefficient $\alpha = 200\ \mu\text{V/K}$, temperature difference $\Delta T = 40\ \text{K}$, leg length $L = 1.5\ \text{mm}$, cross-section $A = 1\ \text{mm}^{2}$, and resistivity $\rho = 1 \times 10^{-5}\ \Omega\cdot\text{m}$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain, with the aid of a block diagram, the typical workflow for modelling and simulating a complete IoT energy-harvesting node (transducer → power management → storage → IoT load). Mention the role of *Verification* and *Validation* in this workflow.

---

##### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation and Calculation (7 marks):**

The Thevenin equivalent of the TEG is a voltage source $V_{\text{oc}}$ in series with internal resistance $R_{\text{int}}$. The load current is:

$$I = \frac{V_{\text{oc}}}{R_{\text{int}} + R_L}$$

The power delivered to the load is:

$$P_L = I^{2} R_L = \frac{V_{\text{oc}}^{2}\,R_L}{(R_{\text{int}} + R_L)^{2}}$$

Differentiating $P_L$ with respect to $R_L$ and equating to zero yields the well-known matching condition:

$$R_L^{\text{opt}} = R_{\text{int}}$$

Substituting $R_L = R_{\text{int}}$ gives:

$$P_{\max} = \frac{V_{\text{oc}}^{2}}{4 R_{\text{int}}} = \frac{(N\alpha\Delta T)^{2}}{4 N \rho L / A}$$

**Numerical evaluation:**

First compute $V_{\text{oc}}$:

$$V_{\text{oc}} = N\alpha\Delta T = 200 \times (200 \times 10^{-6})\times 40 = 1.6\ \text{V}$$

Then compute $R_{\text{int}}$:

$$R_{\text{int}} = \frac{N\rho L}{A} = \frac{200 \times (1 \times 10^{-5})\times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 3\ \Omega$$

Therefore:

$$P_{\max} = \frac{(1.6)^{2}}{4 \times 3} = \frac{2.56}{12} \approx 0.2133\ \text{W} \approx 213\ \text{mW}$$

(7 marks — full credit when derivation, substitution, and unit consistency are shown.)

**Part (b) — Workflow Explanation (7 marks):**

The modelling-and-simulation workflow proceeds in five stages:

1. **Distributed-physics modelling** — Solve PDEs (heat conduction, structural vibration, Maxwell) in **COMSOL/ANSYS** to obtain the transducer's input–output transfer function.
2. **Equivalent-circuit extraction** — Reduce the distributed model to a SPICE-compatible netlist (e.g., Thevenin equivalent for the TEG, BVD circuit for a piezo cantilever).
3. **System-level co-simulation** — Couple the transducer macro-model, rectifier, DC-DC converter, MPPT controller, and battery in **MATLAB/Simulink + PLECS/LTspice**.
4. **Verification** — Compare simulated waveforms (voltage, current, SoC) against closed-form analytical solutions (e.g., the $P_{\max}$ expression derived above).
5. **Validation** — Build a hardware prototype, measure the same quantities with calibrated instruments, and compute the error metric (RMSE, normalised RMS) between measured and simulated data. Models with error < 5 % are considered validated.

A typical block diagram was shown in **Section 4.3**. (7 marks)

---

#### ❖ Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Pattern]

**(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of the single-diode equivalent circuit of a photovoltaic cell, derive the expression for the cell I–V characteristic. Define *Fill Factor* and state the analytical condition for the Maximum Power Point (MPP).

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare *Lumped-Parameter*, *Behavioural*, and *System-Level* models of energy harvesters. For each model, name the simulation tool most commonly used and justify the choice.

---

##### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — PV Cell Derivation (7 marks):**

The single-diode equivalent circuit consists of a photo-generated current source $I_{ph}$ in parallel with a diode $D$, series resistance $R_s$, and shunt resistance $R_{sh}$. Applying Kirchhoff's Current Law at the output node:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_{D} - I_{sh}$$

where

$$I_{D} = I_{0}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1\right],\quad I_{sh} = \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$

Hence the cell equation:

$$I = I_{ph} - I_{0}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}\right) - 1\right] - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$$

The **Fill Factor (FF)** quantifies the "squareness" of the I–V curve:

$$FF = \frac{V_{mp}\,I_{mp}}{V_{oc}\,I_{sc}}$$

The **Maximum Power Point (MPP)** is reached when $dP/dV = 0$, leading to the transcendental condition (derived in Section 3.1):

$$I_{ph} + I_{0} = I_{0}\!\left(1 + \frac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right)\exp\!\left(\frac{qV_{mp}}{nkT}\right)$$

(7 marks)

**Part (b) — Comparison Table (7 marks):**

| Model Type | What it Captures | Best Tool | Justification |
|-----------|-------------------|-----------|----------------|
| **Lumped-Parameter** | Discrete R/L/C elements reproducing the dominant resonance or DC behaviour of the transducer | **LTspice / PSpice** | Native SPICE engine, fast transient, accurate switching-device models, free licence |
| **Behavioural** | Input-output transfer function (e.g., harvested power vs acceleration) without internal physics | **MATLAB / Simulink + Simscape** | Block-diagram environment ideal for control-loop and signal-chain design |
| **System-Level** | Coupled electro-thermal-mechanical behaviour of transducer + power electronics + storage + IoT load | **PLECS, PSIM, or co-simulation (Simulink + LTspice)** | Handles multi-domain, multi-rate simulation; supports state-machine and finite-state models for the IoT duty cycle |

(7 marks — full credit when the table is accompanied by a one-sentence explanation of *why* the tool suits the model level.)

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Points in Module-2 Answers:**
> 1. **Missing units** — Always append V, A, W, Hz, K, etc. to final numerical answers. Examiner routinely deducts **0.5 – 1 mark** per missing unit.
> 2. **Forgetting $R_s$ and $R_{sh}$** — Board answer that derives the *ideal* diode equation without acknowledging parasitic resistances is marked down by up to **2 marks** because it does not reflect realistic cell behaviour.
> 3. **Confusing verification with validation** — *Verification* = "are we solving the equations right?"; *Validation* = "are we solving the right equations?" This distinction is asked almost every semester.
> 4. **No diagram in 14-mark answers** — A neat, labelled block diagram is worth at least **1 – 2 marks** even when the textual explanation is excellent.
> 5. **Using outdated tool names** — Calling it "PSpice for Students" is acceptable; saying "Multisim blue" is acceptable; avoid vague terms such as "any circuit simulator".

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] Modelling = deriving equations; Simulation = solving those equations numerically on a computer.
- [ ] Four modelling levels: **Distributed → Lumped → Behavioural → System**.
- [ ] Canonical PV equation uses **single-diode model** with $I_{ph}$, $I_{0}$, $n$, $R_s$, $R_{sh}$.
- [ ] Fill Factor: $FF = V_{mp} I_{mp} / (V_{oc} I_{sc})$; Efficiency: $\eta = FF \cdot V_{oc} I_{sc} / P_{\text{in}}$.
- [ ] MPP condition: $dP/dV = 0$, gives a transcendental equation solved iteratively.
- [ ] Piezo BVD circuit contains $L_m$, $C_m$, $R_m$ in series, parallel with $C_p$.
- [ ] TEG optimum load = internal resistance; $P_{\max} = V_{\text{oc}}^{2} / (4 R_{\text{int}})$.
- [ ] RF harvested power follows Friis transmission equation with $\lambda / (4\pi d)$ path-loss factor.
- [ ] **LTspice** for circuit; **COMSOL** for PDEs; **MATLAB/Simulink** for system; **PLECS/PSIM** for power-electronics-heavy systems.
- [ ] Verification = numerical correctness; Validation = physical correctness against measurement.
- [ ] Always include **units, a block diagram, and a clear conclusion** in 14-mark answers.
- [ ] Common V&V methods: convergence study, sensitivity analysis, experimental overlay.
- [ ] The fill factor of a typical crystalline silicon cell is **0.7 – 0.85**; of an amorphous silicon cell is **0.5 – 0.7** — remember this for descriptive questions.
- [ ] Standard metric for IoT harvester comparison is the **Figure of Merit (FoM)**: power density per unit volume or per unit cost.
- [ ] State-of-Charge dynamics: $V_c(t) = V_{\infty}(1 - e^{-t/RC})$ — derivable, high-yield.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for the Board Exam — "DLSB"**
> **D**istributed → **L**umped → **S**ystem → **B**ehavioural
> (Hierarchy of model abstraction, top-down refinement.)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the KTU 2024 syllabus for **PECIT746**, the term **"Challenges"** in Module 2 refers to the fundamental physical, electrical, environmental, architectural, and economic obstacles that constrain the design, deployment, and long-term operation of **self-powered IoT nodes** that rely on ambient energy harvesting. These challenges span the entire energy chain — from the source (solar, vibrational, thermal, RF), through the transducer, the power conditioning circuitry, the energy storage element, to the IoT load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Challenges in energy harvesting and power management for IoT include — but are not limited to — source intermittency, low power density of ambient sources, conversion inefficiency, energy storage limitations, impedance mismatches, unpredictable load profiles, and the absence of universal power management standards."*

The challenges are typically classified into **four engineering domains**:

1. **Source-Side Challenges** — Variability, unpredictability, low density.
2. **Transducer / Conversion Challenges** — Low efficiency, non-linear behaviour, frequency dependence.
3. **Power Management Circuit Challenges** — Impedance mismatch, MPPT complexity, cold-start.
4. **Storage & System-Level Challenges** — Battery leakage, self-discharge, ultra-low-power design, scalability.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Rainwater Harvesting" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: Harvesting Energy is Like Catching Rain in a Bucket**

Imagine your IoT node is a **village in a semi-arid region** trying to become water-self-sufficient. The energy harvester is your **roof**, the transducer is the **gutter**, the power management circuit is the **filter & pump**, and the battery is the **underground tank**.

| Real-World Analogy | Energy Harvesting Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Rainfall (unpredictable, seasonal) | Ambient energy source (solar/vibration/RF) |
| Bucket capacity | Power density of the harvester (µW–mW/cm²) |
| Evaporation loss | Conversion inefficiency & leakage |
| Pipe leakage | Quiescent current of power management IC |
| Underground tank cracks | Battery self-discharge & aging |
| Drought years | Source intermittency & dark periods |

The village can never store more rain than falls — just as an IoT node can never generate more energy than its ambient source provides. **Every challenge is essentially a "leak" in this energy pipeline.**

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU

- It is a **direct Module 2 syllabus heading**, frequently asked in Part A (3 marks).
- Examiners often frame a 14-mark Part B question as *"Discuss the major challenges in energy harvesting for IoT with suitable examples"* or *"Explain the power management challenges in battery-less IoT systems."*
- The topic tests the student's **system-level thinking** — a KTU 2024 OBE (Outcome-Based Education) priority under **CO2** (*"Analyse the challenges in designing energy-autonomous IoT systems"*).

---

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for KTU Recall)

- **Power Density of ambient energy:** typically **10 µW/cm² – 10 mW/cm²**
- **Conversion Efficiency of harvesters:** **5% – 40%** (depending on source)
- **Quiescent current of PMICs:** typically **< 1 µA**
- **Battery self-discharge rate:** **1% – 5% per month** (Li-ion), higher for thin-film
- **Cold-start voltage of typical boost converters:** **~20 mV – 300 mV**

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Systematic Classification of Challenges

The challenges are best understood by walking **along the energy flow path** of a typical IoT harvester node:

### 2.1.1 Source-Side Challenges (The "Rainfall" Problem)

1. **Intermittency & Unpredictability**
   - Solar irradiance varies with time-of-day, weather, season, and latitude.
   - Vibration energy depends on machinery duty cycles, which are non-stationary.
   - RF energy depends on transmitter proximity, which changes with mobility.

2. **Low Power Density**
   - Indoor light: ~10–100 µW/cm²
   - Outdoor light: ~10–15 mW/cm²
   - Human body motion: ~0.5–10 µW/cm³
   - Ambient RF (Wi-Fi/GSM): ~0.01–1 µW/cm²

3. **Source Availability Mismatch**
   - The **energy generation profile** rarely matches the **IoT load profile**. A vibration sensor may produce energy at noon when no data is needed, but require energy at 3 AM when vibrations occur.

4. **Environmental Dependency**
   - Temperature, humidity, dust, and corrosion degrade harvester performance over time.

---

### 2.1.2 Transducer / Conversion Challenges

1. **Low Conversion Efficiency**
   - Photovoltaic cells: 15–25% (single junction)
   - Piezoelectric transducers: 5–30%
   - Thermoelectric generators: 3–8%
   - RF rectennas: 10–40% (highly source dependent)

2. **Non-Linear & Source-Dependent Behaviour**
   - PV cells have a current-voltage (I–V) curve that shifts with irradiance and temperature.
   - Piezoelectric output voltage can swing from mV to tens of volts.

3. **Impedance Mismatch**
   - The Thevenin equivalent resistance of the source rarely matches the load. Power transfer is maximised only at the **Maximum Power Point (MPP)**, which drifts with operating conditions.

4. **Frequency Dependence (Vibration Harvesters)**
   - Resonant piezoelectric harvesters work only near their natural frequency (~50–200 Hz). Real-world vibrations are broadband.

---

### 2.1.3 Power Management Circuit Challenges

1. **Cold-Start Problem**
   - Many boost converters require an input of ~300–600 mV to start. Sub-100 mV sources cannot bootstrap.

2. **MPPT Complexity**
   - Perturb & Observe (P&O) and Incremental Conductance algorithms require continuous ADC sampling — which itself consumes power.

3. **Quiescent Current Overhead**
   - Even when the load is off, the PMIC draws µA. Over months, this can exceed the harvested energy.

4. **Dynamic Load Management**
   - IoT loads (radio transmission, sensor read) have peak currents 100–1000× the average. The energy buffer must supply these bursts.

5. **Voltage Regulation & Threshold Issues**
   - Storage elements have a minimum operating voltage. Deep discharge degrades batteries; supercapacitors allow deep discharge but have high leakage.

---

### 2.1.4 Storage & System-Level Challenges

1. **Battery Limitations**
   - Finite cycle life (~500–2000 cycles for Li-ion).
   - Temperature sensitivity.
   - Replacement is impractical in remote deployments.

2. **Supercapacitor Trade-offs**
   - High leakage current (10–100 µA).
   - Lower energy density than batteries.

3. **No Universal Standard**
   - Unlike USB-PD or Qi, no standard governs energy-harvesting power management.

4. **Security vs. Energy Trade-off**
   - Encryption (AES-128) for IoT security can consume more energy than sensing.

5. **Scalability & Cost**
   - Millions of nodes — even $0.10 extra per node is a $100k cost.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are KTU-exam-recommended. Memorise the symbols, units, and boundary conditions.

| **Symbol** | **Quantity** | **Unit** | **Governing Formula** | **Notes / Boundary Condition** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $P_{harv}$ | Harvested power | W (or µW) | $P_{harv} = \eta \cdot P_{source}$ | $\eta$ = conversion efficiency |
| $P_{dens}$ | Power density | W/cm² | $P_{dens} = \dfrac{P_{harv}}{A_{cell}}$ | $A_{cell}$ = active transducer area |
| $E_{daily}$ | Daily harvested energy | J | $E_{daily} = \int_{0}^{24h} P_{harv}(t)\, dt$ | Must exceed $E_{load}$ |
| $\eta_{MPPT}$ | MPPT tracking efficiency | % | $\eta_{MPPT} = \dfrac{P_{actual}}{P_{MPP}} \times 100$ | Target: $> 90\%$ |
| $R_{opt}$ | Optimal load resistance | $\Omega$ | $R_{opt} = R_{Thev}$ (max power transfer) | For linear sources |
| $V_{MPP}$ | Voltage at max power point | V | $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$ at MPP | For PV: ~70–80% of $V_{oc}$ |
| $E_{bat}$ | Battery energy capacity | Wh | $E_{bat} = V_{nom} \cdot C_{Ah}$ | Nominal × capacity |
| $T_{autonomy}$ | Days of autonomy | days | $T_{autonomy} = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{P_{load} \cdot 24}$ | Must exceed worst dark period |
| $\eta_{overall}$ | System efficiency | % | $\eta_{overall} = \eta_{src} \cdot \eta_{MPPT} \cdot \eta_{reg} \cdot \eta_{stor}$ | Multiplicative chain |
| $P_{cons,total}$ | Total node consumption | W | $P_{cons,total} = P_{sleep} + P_{active} \cdot d$ | $d$ = duty cycle (0–1) |
| $L_{self}$ | Self-discharge rate | %/month | $E_{loss} = E_{stored} \cdot L_{self}$ | Critical for multi-year deployment |

---

## 2.3 Engineering Utility of These Formulas

> [!TIP]
> In the KTU exam, a common question is: *"Show that for a self-powered IoT node to operate indefinitely, the daily harvested energy must exceed the daily consumed energy."* You can derive the **Energy Neutral Operation (ENO)** condition:

$$
E_{harvested} \geq E_{consumed}
$$

$$
\int P_{harv}(t)\, dt \geq \int P_{load}(t)\, dt
$$

This single inequality is the **foundation of all energy-aware IoT design**, and the entire Module 2 syllabus builds toward satisfying it under real-world constraints.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Energy Neutral Operation (ENO) Constraint

**Problem Statement:** Prove that for a battery-assisted energy harvesting IoT node to operate in *perpetual* mode, the average harvested power must at least equal the average load power, and find the maximum allowable duty cycle.

**Step 1 — Define the energy balance equation over one operational cycle of duration $T$.**

$$
E_{harvested}(T) = E_{consumed}(T) + E_{stored}(T)
$$

**Step 2 — For steady-state (long-term operation), the net change in stored energy must be zero.**

$$
\Delta E_{stored} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_{harvested}(T) = E_{consumed}(T)
$$

**Step 3 — Expand both sides. Harvested power $P_{harv}$ is assumed constant in this simplified model.**

$$
\int_{0}^{T} P_{harv}\, dt = \int_{0}^{T} \left[ P_{sleep} + d \cdot P_{active} \right] dt
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integrals.**

$$
P_{harv} \cdot T = P_{sleep} \cdot T + d \cdot P_{active} \cdot T
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel $T$ on both sides.**

$$
P_{harv} = P_{sleep} + d \cdot P_{active}
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for the maximum duty cycle $d_{max}$.**

$$
d_{max} = \dfrac{P_{harv} - P_{sleep}}{P_{active}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical interpretation:** If $P_{harv} \leq P_{sleep}$, the node cannot even remain in sleep mode — the system is **energy-negative** and the battery will drain. This is the most fundamental design constraint.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Conversion Chain Efficiency

**Problem:** A solar cell (15% efficient) drives a boost converter (90% efficient) that charges a battery (charging efficiency 85%). The MPPT tracker achieves 95% efficiency. Find the overall system efficiency.

**Step 1 — Write the overall efficiency as the product of individual stage efficiencies.**

$$
\eta_{overall} = \eta_{cell} \cdot \eta_{MPPT} \cdot \eta_{boost} \cdot \eta_{charge}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
\eta_{overall} = 0.15 \times 0.95 \times 0.90 \times 0.85
$$

**Step 3 — Compute pairwise products.**

$$
0.15 \times 0.95 = 0.1425
$$
$$
0.90 \times 0.85 = 0.765
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply the two partial results.**

$$
\eta_{overall} = 0.1425 \times 0.765 = 0.1090
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to percentage.**

$$
\eta_{overall} \approx 10.9\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Students often forget to include the MPPT efficiency or the battery charging efficiency, and quote the cell efficiency as the "system efficiency". This is the most common mark-losing mistake in derivations.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Maximum Power Point Condition for a PV Cell

**Problem:** Show that the maximum power point of a PV cell occurs where $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$, and relate it to the open-circuit voltage.

**Step 1 — The power delivered to the load is $P = V \cdot I$.**

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $V$ (treating $I$ as a function of $V$).**

$$
\dfrac{dP}{dV} = I + V \cdot \dfrac{dI}{dV}
$$

**Step 3 — Set derivative to zero (maximum condition).**

$$
I + V \cdot \dfrac{dI}{dV} = 0
$$

$$
\dfrac{dI}{dV} = -\dfrac{I}{V}
$$

This is the famous **Incremental Conductance condition** used in MPPT algorithms.

**Step 4 — Empirical relation for silicon PV cells.**

$$
V_{MPP} \approx (0.75 \text{ to } 0.85) \times V_{oc}
$$

**Step 5 — Similarly for current.**

$$
I_{MPP} \approx (0.85 \text{ to } 0.95) \times I_{sc}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Days of Autonomy Calculation

**Problem:** A node has a 1000 mWh battery, consumes 5 mW average, and harvests 4 mW average. Find the days of autonomy assuming no further harvesting.

**Step 1 — Energy stored initially.**

$$
E_{stored} = 1000 \text{ mWh}
$$

**Step 2 — Net discharge rate (consumption exceeds harvesting).**

$$
P_{net} = 5 \text{ mW} - 4 \text{ mW} = 1 \text{ mW}
$$

**Step 3 — Time to deplete.**

$$
t = \dfrac{E_{stored}}{P_{net}} = \dfrac{1000 \text{ mWh}}{1 \text{ mW}} = 1000 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to days.**

$$
T_{autonomy} = \dfrac{1000}{24} \approx 41.7 \text{ days}
$$

---

| **Parameter** | **Solar (Outdoor)** | **Vibration (Piezo)** | **Thermal (TEG)** | **Ambient RF** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Power density | **10–15 mW/cm²** | **50–300 µW/cm³** | **10–50 µW/cm²** | **0.01–1 µW/cm²** |
| Conversion efficiency | **15–25%** | **5–30%** | **3–8%** | **10–40%** |
| Predictability | High (day cycle) | Medium | Medium | Low |
| Indoor viability | Poor | Good | Good | Good |
| Key challenge | Intermittency | Frequency match | Small $\Delta T$ | Extremely low power |
| Typical use | Outdoor sensor | Industrial monitor | HVAC monitor | Smart badge |

---

## 3.6 Mathematical Model of Source Intermittency

A simple model for solar irradiance $G(t)$ is:

$$
G(t) = G_{max} \cdot \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi t}{T_{day}}\right) \quad \text{for } 0 \leq t \leq T_{day}
$$

$$
G(t) = 0 \quad \text{for } T_{day} < t \leq 24 \text{ h}
$$

**Average daily power:**

$$
\bar{G} = \dfrac{1}{T_{day}} \int_{0}^{T_{day}} G_{max} \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi t}{T_{day}}\right) dt
$$

**Evaluating:**

$$
\bar{G} = \dfrac{G_{max}}{T_{day}} \cdot \left[ -\dfrac{T_{day}}{\pi} \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi t}{T_{day}}\right) \right]_{0}^{T_{day}}
$$

$$
\bar{G} = \dfrac{G_{max}}{\pi} \cdot \left[ -\cos(\pi) + \cos(0) \right]
$$

$$
\bar{G} = \dfrac{G_{max}}{\pi} \cdot (1 + 1) = \dfrac{2 G_{max}}{\pi}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Even under ideal sinusoidal irradiance, the **average harvested power is only $\dfrac{2}{\pi} \approx 63.7\%$** of the peak. This single result tells students why energy storage is **mandatory** for solar IoT — the source alone cannot drive the load continuously.
