# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: List any three major challenges in energy harvesting for IoT systems and briefly explain each in 2–3 lines.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Source Intermittency (1 mark):** Ambient energy sources such as solar, vibration, and RF are not continuously available. The harvested power varies with time, weather, and operating conditions, making continuous power delivery to the IoT node impossible without storage.

2. **Low Power Density (1 mark):** Most ambient sources provide power in the range of **µW/cm²** to a few **mW/cm²**, which is often insufficient to directly power conventional electronics. This forces designers to use ultra-low-power components and aggressive duty cycling.

3. **Impedance Mismatch (1 mark):** The Thevenin impedance of the transducer rarely matches the load, leading to significant power loss. MPPT circuits are required, but they add complexity, quiescent current, and cost.

---

### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: What is the cold-start problem in energy harvesting? How is it mitigated?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **cold-start problem** (1 mark) refers to the inability of a power management IC (PMIC) to begin operation when the input voltage from the harvester is below its minimum start-up threshold (typically **300–600 mV** for conventional boost converters).

**Mitigation techniques (2 marks):**
- Use of **JFET-based self-startup** circuits that can operate from as low as **20 mV**.
- Use of **mechanical or piezoelectric pre-charging** of a start-up capacitor.
- Use of **auxiliary energy buffers** (e.g., a small pre-charged supercapacitor).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Following the KTU 2024 scheme, students answer **one full question** from this module. Each question below is presented as an **internal-choice pair** — answer either **Question A** OR **Question B**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: Discuss in detail the various challenges encountered in energy harvesting and power management for IoT systems. Use suitable examples and block diagrams to support your answer.**

#### **Part (a) — Source-Side and Conversion-Side Challenges (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Source Intermittency (2 marks):**
Ambient sources such as sunlight, vibration, thermal gradients, and RF are inherently variable. For example, a solar-powered IoT node deployed outdoors experiences zero harvest during night. The harvested power $P(t)$ is a stochastic function of time, and the designer must guarantee operation during worst-case dark periods. This mandates **energy storage** sized for the maximum expected discharge interval.

**2. Low Power Density (2 marks):**
Typical power densities are:
- Indoor light: ~10 µW/cm²
- Human motion: ~5 µW/cm³
- Ambient RF: ~0.1 µW/cm²

These are orders of magnitude below what standard microcontrollers require (tens of mW). Designers must therefore select **sub-mW MCUs** (e.g., MSP430 at 100 µA/MHz) and use **extreme duty cycling** (e.g., 0.1% active time).

**3. Impedance Mismatch and Low Conversion Efficiency (3 marks):**
A PV cell has a strongly non-linear I–V curve. Without MPPT, up to **30–40%** of available power can be lost. Piezoelectric harvesters are highly resonant, meaning their output drops sharply when vibration frequency deviates from the natural frequency. RF rectennas suffer from diode threshold losses at low input power. The **overall system efficiency** is the product of all stage efficiencies (cell, MPPT, regulator, storage), and typically ends up around **5–15%**.

---

#### **Part (b) — Power Management and Storage Challenges (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Cold-Start Problem (2 marks):**
Boost converters like the LTC3108 require ~20 mV, but many require 300 mV or more. Sources such as TEGs at small temperature differentials produce only tens of millivolts. Special cold-start circuits using JFETs, transformers, or mechanical pre-charge are required, adding cost and complexity.

**2. Quiescent Current Overhead (1.5 marks):**
A PMIC with even 1 µA quiescent current drains **8.6 mAh per year** from a battery. Over a 10-year deployment, that is 86 mAh — comparable to the entire battery capacity of a coin cell. The PMIC's $I_Q$ must be << the average harvested current.

**3. Load Peak Currents (1.5 marks):**
Radio transmission (e.g., LoRa, BLE) requires **50–150 mA peak** for short bursts, but average consumption is < 1 mA. The storage element must have very low internal resistance and high peak-power capability. Supercapacitors handle this better than batteries but have higher leakage.

**4. Battery Aging and Self-Discharge (1 mark):**
Li-ion batteries lose capacity over charge cycles and are sensitive to temperature. In a 5-year deployment without maintenance, the node may have only 60–70% of original capacity.

**5. Absence of Standards (1 mark):**
Unlike USB-PD or Qi wireless charging, there is no universal standard for energy-harvesting PMICs, leading to fragmented designs and high BOM cost.

**Conclusion:** Energy harvesting for IoT is fundamentally a **multi-objective optimisation problem** balancing power, cost, size, lifetime, and reliability. Modern solutions such as Texas Instruments' BQ25570, Analog Devices' ADP5090, and academic systems like **AMBIENT++** use adaptive MPPT, nano-power regulators, and hybrid supercapacitor–battery buffers to address these challenges.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Q: Explain the power management challenges in battery-less IoT systems. Discuss Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) techniques and the role of energy storage elements in addressing these challenges.**

#### **Part (a) — Power Management Challenges in Battery-Less IoT (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definition of Battery-Less IoT (1 mark):**
Battery-less IoT nodes rely entirely on harvested energy, often buffered by **supercapacitors** or **thin-film batteries**, to operate intermittently. They must function under strict energy budgets, often in the **µW range**.

**2. Energy Budgeting Challenge (2 marks):**
The node must accumulate sufficient energy in a buffer before "waking up" to perform sensing, computation, and transmission. This requires a **wake-up threshold voltage** design. The challenge is sizing the buffer to minimise charging time while supporting peak loads.

**3. Intermittent Computing (2 marks):**
When energy runs out mid-computation, the system must checkpoint state to non-volatile memory and resume later. This is computationally expensive and energy-intensive. Solutions include **MEMENTOS**, **DINO**, and **Hibernus** frameworks, but they add design complexity.

**4. Predictability vs Adaptability (2 marks):**
Fixed-threshold wake-up is simple but wastes energy. Predictive wake-up (e.g., based on learned energy-harvesting patterns) is efficient but requires machine-learning, which itself consumes energy. The trade-off is a fundamental challenge.

---

#### **Part (b) — MPPT and Energy Storage Role (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**1. MPPT — Perturb and Observe (P&O) Algorithm (2 marks):**
The controller perturbs the operating voltage by a small step $\Delta V$ and observes the resulting power change $\Delta P$.

- If $\Delta P > 0$, continue in the same direction.
- If $\Delta P < 0$, reverse direction.

The algorithm oscillates around the MPP, with tracking efficiency typically **90–98%**. Drawback: slow convergence under rapidly changing conditions.

**2. MPPT — Incremental Conductance (2 marks):**
Based on the condition $\dfrac{dI}{dV} = -\dfrac{I}{V}$, this method responds faster to irradiance changes and is preferred for PV applications with frequent cloud cover. Requires more computation than P&O.

**3. Fractional Open-Circuit Voltage (1 mark):**
A simpler method that periodically measures $V_{oc}$ and sets $V_{MPP} = k \cdot V_{oc}$ where $k \approx 0.75$–$0.85$ for silicon cells. Lower cost, lower accuracy (~80%).

**4. Role of Energy Storage (2 marks):**
- **Supercapacitors:** High power density, millions of cycles, low voltage per cell. Used for short-term buffering of peak loads. Leakage is the main drawback.
- **Thin-film batteries (e.g., solid-state Li):** Higher energy density, lower leakage, but limited cycle life.
- **Hybrid systems:** Supercapacitor for peaks + battery for long-term storage is the emerging solution used in products like the **TI BQ25570EVM**.

**Conclusion:** Battery-less IoT is achievable but requires **co-design of hardware, firmware, and energy-aware algorithms** — a hallmark of modern KTU 2024 NEP-aligned curriculum.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Losing Mistakes in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "power" with "energy"** — Power is instantaneous (W), energy is integrated over time (J or Wh). Many students use them interchangeably, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Quoting cell efficiency as system efficiency** — Always multiply **all** stages in the conversion chain.
> 3. **Forgetting units** — A formula with µW and mW mixed up will yield a wrong answer even if the algebra is correct.
> 4. **Writing generic answers like "low power"** — Be specific: cite numbers like **10 µW/cm²** for indoor light, **~300 mV** for cold-start threshold.
> 5. **Ignoring the energy neutral condition** — Always frame the answer around $E_{harvested} \geq E_{consumed}$.
> 6. **Not using a block diagram** — A 14-mark answer without a diagram typically loses 1–2 marks in KTU valuation.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — "Challenges in Energy Harvesting for IoT"**

- ☐ Energy harvesting is constrained by the **fundamental inequality** $E_{harvested} \geq E_{consumed}$.
- ☐ Source challenges: **intermittency**, **low power density**, **source–load mismatch**, **environmental dependence**.
- ☐ Conversion challenges: **low efficiency (5–40%)**, **non-linear I–V**, **impedance mismatch**, **frequency dependence (vibration)**.
- ☐ Power management challenges: **cold start (< 300 mV typical)**, **MPPT tracking loss**, **quiescent current**, **load peak currents**.
- ☐ Storage challenges: **battery cycle life**, **self-discharge (1–5%/month for Li-ion)**, **supercapacitor leakage**, **no universal standard**.
- ☐ System challenges: **security vs energy**, **scalability**, **long-term reliability**.
- ☐ Average solar irradiance (sinusoidal model) = $\dfrac{2 G_{max}}{\pi} \approx 0.637 \times G_{max}$.
- ☐ Maximum duty cycle: $d_{max} = \dfrac{P_{harv} - P_{sleep}}{P_{active}}$.
- ☐ MPPT methods: **P&O**, **Incremental Conductance**, **Fractional $V_{oc}$**.
- ☐ Overall efficiency is the **product** of all stage efficiencies — multiplicative, not additive.
- ☐ Always mention **MPPT, quiescent current, cold start** in any 14-mark answer on this topic.
- ☐ Real-world example products: **TI BQ25570**, **ADI ADP5090**, **LTC3108**, **Cypress S6AE101A**.
- ☐ Always draw a **block diagram** in 14-mark answers.
- ☐ KTU 2024 OBE: this topic maps to **CO2** — *"Analyse the design challenges of energy-autonomous IoT systems."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fog Protocols
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fog Protocols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
**Fog Protocols** are a class of lightweight, constrained, and energy-aware communication protocols engineered to facilitate data exchange across the **Fog Computing Continuum** — the distributed intermediate layer that resides between the resource-constrained IoT/edge devices at the bottom of the stack and the centralized Cloud infrastructure at the top. These protocols govern message formatting, addressing, session management, publish–subscribe semantics, request–response transactions, and quality-of-service (QoS) negotiation among **Fog Nodes, Edge Gateways, Cloud Brokers, and End Devices**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECIT746 – Module 2):**
> Fog protocols refer to the standardized communication mechanisms (MQTT, CoAP, AMQP, DDS, HTTP/CoAPs, WebSocket, OPC-UA, XMPP, LWM2M) that enable interoperable, low-latency, energy-efficient, and context-aware data transfer in the fog computing layer of an IoT architecture.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a **national postal system**:
- **Cloud = Central Post Office in Delhi** — handles massive bulk sorting, archives everything, but is geographically far from your village.
- **Fog Node = District Post Office** — receives mail from villages, performs local sorting, forwards only essential items to the central office, and sometimes delivers locally without involving Delhi at all.
- **Edge Device (Sensor) = Your Letterbox** — can only push a small envelope (limited data, limited power).

**Fog Protocols** are the *standardized envelopes, address formats, and dispatch rules* (registered post, speed post, ordinary mail) used by these three tiers to talk to each other efficiently. A village letterbox cannot use a heavy diplomatic pouch; it needs a *lightweight protocol envelope* — that is exactly what fog protocols provide.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Design Drivers for Fog Protocols:**
> - **Low Bandwidth Footprint** — Headers often 2–8 bytes (vs. HTTP's hundreds of bytes).
> - **Energy Economy** — Optimized for battery and energy-harvested nodes.
> - **Latency Sensitivity** — Support for sub-10 ms round-trip times for actuation loops.
> - **Intermittent Connectivity** — Built-in support for store-and-forward, last-will, and offline buffering.
> - **Heterogeneity** — Must work across TCP, UDP, IPv6, 6LoWPAN, BLE, LoRaWAN.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics in Fog Protocol Design

| Metric | Typical Target | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Header Overhead** | 2 – 8 bytes | Direct impact on transmit energy |
| **Round-Trip Time (RTT)** | < 50 ms | Real-time actuation capability |
| **Energy per Byte** | ~ 0.5 – 5 µJ/byte (at 0 dBm, short range) | Battery life determinant |
| **Code Footprint** | < 100 KB RAM | Runs on Class-1 constrained devices |
| **MTTR (Mean Time To Reconnect)** | < 5 s | Resilient to link drops |

> [!TIP]
> In a **Energy Harvesting IoT** context, every transmitted byte costs joules harvested from ambient sources (solar, vibration, RF). A protocol with even **10 bytes extra header** at 1 transmission/sec wastes **~3.15 × 10⁸ bytes/year (≈ 300 MB)** and significant harvested energy.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Protocol Stack Position in Fog Architecture
The fog protocol stack typically spans **Application → Transport → Network → Data-Link → Physical** layers. The most common cross-layer implementations are:

1. **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** — OASIS Standard, ISO/IEC 20922.
2. **CoAP (Constrained Application Protocol)** — IETF RFC 7252.
3. **AMQP (Advanced Message Queuing Protocol)** — OASIS Standard, ISO/IEC 19464.
4. **DDS (Data Distribution Service)** — OMG Standard, real-time publish–subscribe.
5. **LWM2M (Lightweight M2M)** — OMA Specworks, device management focused.
6. **HTTP/REST & WebSocket** — Legacy web compatibility layer.
7. **OPC-UA** — Industrial automation, IEC 62541.
8. **XMPP (Extensible Messaging and Presence Protocol)** — Presence and messaging.
9. **ZeroMQ / Nanomsg** — Brokerless messaging patterns.

### 2.2 Functional Taxonomy of Fog Protocols
- **Request–Response (Client–Server):** CoAP, HTTP.
- **Publish–Subscribe (Brokered):** MQTT, AMQP, DDS.
- **Presence / Instant Messaging:** XMPP.
- **Device Management:** LWM2M, CoAP with DTLS.
- **Industrial / Real-Time:** DDS, OPC-UA over TSN.

### 2.3 Why These Protocols? The "Why" Behind the Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" for Energy-Aware Fog Protocols:**
> 1. **Energy cost of transmit is 100× to 1000× that of compute.** Therefore, minimizing *bits-on-air* via compact headers (e.g., CoAP's 4-byte header vs. HTTP's hundreds) is the single most effective energy-saving design choice in fog nodes.
> 2. **QoS vs. Energy Trade-off:** MQTT's three QoS levels (0, 1, 2) directly trade off reliability against retransmission energy. QoS-2 guarantees exactly-once delivery at the cost of a 4-step handshake.
> 3. **Connection vs. Connectionless:** UDP-based CoAP saves the TCP connection-state energy and SYN/ACK overhead, ideal for sleep–duty-cycled energy-harvested nodes.
> 4. **Security in Fog:** DTLS for CoAP, TLS for MQTT — both add 20–50 bytes of overhead per handshake, but can be amortized over session-based reuse to minimize energy penalty.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Metric | Units / Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| **Total Message Size** | $M_{total} = M_{header} + M_{payload}$ | Bytes |
| **Protocol Overhead %** | $\eta_{oh} = \dfrac{M_{header}}{M_{header} + M_{payload}} \times 100$ | % |
| **Net Payload Efficiency** | $\eta_{pl} = 1 - \eta_{oh}$ | Dimensionless, [0, 1] |
| **Transmit Energy (First-Order Radio Model)** | $E_{tx}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}$ | Joules |
| **Receive Energy** | $E_{rx}(k) = k \cdot E_{elec}$ | Joules |
| **Throughput** | $\Theta = \dfrac{M_{payload}}{T_{round-trip}}$ | Bytes/s |
| **Energy per Useful Bit** | $E_{bit} = \dfrac{E_{tx}(M_{total}, d)}{8 \cdot M_{payload}}$ | Joules/bit |
| **Annual Data Volume (duty-cycled node)** | $V_{yr} = M_{total} \cdot f_{tx} \cdot 3.1536 \times 10^{7}$ | Bytes/year |
| **MQTT QoS-2 Handshake Steps** | 4 steps: PUBLISH, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP | — |
| **CoAP Message Format** | Ver (2b) $\vert$ Type (2b) $\vert$ TKL (4b) $\vert$ Code (8b) $\vert$ MsgID (16b) $\vert$ Token (0–8B) $\vert$ Options $\vert$ Payload | Bits shown |

Where:
- $k$ = packet size in bits
- $d$ = distance in meters
- $E_{elec}$ = electronics energy ≈ **50 nJ/bit**
- $\varepsilon_{amp}$ = amplifier energy ≈ **10 pJ/bit/m²** (free space, n=2) or **0.0013 pJ/bit/m⁴** (multipath, n=4)
- $f_{tx}$ = transmissions per second
- $n$ = path loss exponent (2 to 4)

### 2.5 Engineering Utility
- **Smart Agriculture:** LoRaWAN + CoAP with low header overhead for 10-year battery life on solar-harvested soil sensors.
- **Industrial IoT:** DDS over TSN for deterministic, sub-millisecond machine-to-machine communication.
- **Smart Grid:** MQTT-SN (Sensor Networks) variant for sub-100-byte packets over Zigbee with energy-aware QoS.
- **Healthcare Wearables:** CoAP + DTLS over BLE — minimal energy, patient monitoring at 1 Hz.

> [!NOTE]
> KTU board questions often frame fog-protocol selection problems around an **energy budget** (e.g., "Given a 100 mAh coin cell and 1 transmission/min, recommend a protocol"). The selection is driven by $E_{bit}$ minimization.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Protocol Overhead Comparison Across Fog Protocols

**Problem Statement:**
A fog node transmits temperature data using three different protocols. Compute the overhead percentage for each, given payload = 20 bytes.

**Given Data:**

| Protocol | Header Size (bytes) | Payload (bytes) |
|---|---|---|
| HTTP/1.1 (over TCP) | 200 | 20 |
| MQTT (QoS-1) | 10 | 20 |
| CoAP (Confirmable) | 4 | 20 |

**Step 1 — Compute Total Message Size for HTTP:**
Using $M_{total} = M_{header} + M_{payload}$, we evaluate the contribution of each component.

$$
M_{total,HTTP} = 200 + 20 = 220 \text{ bytes}
$$

The HTTP message has 200 bytes of headers, which include request line, Host, User-Agent, Accept, Content-Type, Content-Length, Connection, etc. (minimum realistic low-end estimate).

**Step 2 — Apply Overhead Formula for HTTP:**
Using $\eta_{oh} = \dfrac{M_{header}}{M_{header} + M_{payload}} \times 100$, we substitute the totals.

$$
\eta_{oh,HTTP} = \frac{200}{200 + 20} \times 100 = \frac{200}{220} \times 100 = 90.91\%
$$

This is the dominant energy cost. Almost 91% of the bytes transmitted carry no useful information.

**Step 3 — Compute Overhead for MQTT QoS-1:**
MQTT control packet (PUBLISH) with QoS=1 has a fixed 2-byte header + variable-length header (topic, packet ID) ≈ 10 bytes for a short topic.

$$
M_{total,MQTT} = 10 + 20 = 30 \text{ bytes}
$$

Substituting into the overhead formula:

$$
\eta_{oh,MQTT} = \frac{10}{10 + 20} \times 100 = \frac{10}{30} \times 100 = 33.33\%
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Overhead for CoAP (Confirmable):**
CoAP header is a fixed 4 bytes (Version, Type, Token Length, Code, Message ID) + optional Token (0–8 bytes) + Options (when present). For a minimal message: header = 4 bytes.

$$
M_{total,CoAP} = 4 + 20 = 24 \text{ bytes}
$$

Applying the formula:

$$
\eta_{oh,CoAP} = \frac{4}{4 + 20} \times 100 = \frac{4}{24} \times 100 = 16.67\%
$$

**Step 5 — Compare and Conclude:**
We tabulate the results:

| Protocol | $M_{total}$ (bytes) | $\eta_{oh}$ (%) | $\eta_{pl}$ (%) |
|---|---|---|---|
| HTTP/1.1 | 220 | 90.91 | 9.09 |
| MQTT QoS-1 | 30 | 33.33 | 66.67 |
| CoAP | 24 | 16.67 | 83.33 |

**Conclusion:** CoAP is the most payload-efficient among the three — its compact header makes it the preferred choice for energy-harvested fog nodes.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Energy-per-Bit Analysis for Energy-Harvested Fog Node

**Problem Statement:**
A solar-harvested fog node transmits 24-byte CoAP messages every 60 s to a gateway 50 m away. Compute the **transmit energy per useful bit** and the **annual energy consumption**, given $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{amp} = 10$ pJ/bit/m² (free space, n = 2).

**Step 1 — Convert Bytes to Bits:**
For $M_{total} = 24$ bytes:

$$
k = 24 \text{ bytes} \times 8 = 192 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply First-Order Radio Energy Model:**
Using $E_{tx}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}$, with $d = 50$ m, $n = 2$:

$$
E_{tx}(192, 50) = 192 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} + 192 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 50^{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Electronics Term:**
The electronics term $k \cdot E_{elec}$ is the energy consumed by the radio's baseband circuitry:

$$
E_{elec-term} = 192 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} = 9600 \times 10^{-9} = 9.6 \, \mu\text{J}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Amplifier Term:**
The amplifier term $k \cdot \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}$ represents the radiated signal power for path-loss compensation:

$$
E_{amp-term} = 192 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 2500 = 4800 \times 10^{-9} = 4.8 \, \mu\text{J}
$$

**Step 5 — Sum the Energy:**
Adding both contributions:

$$
E_{tx,total} = 9.6 \, \mu\text{J} + 4.8 \, \mu\text{J} = 14.4 \, \mu\text{J} \text{ per transmission}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute Energy per Useful Bit:**
Using $E_{bit} = \dfrac{E_{tx}(M_{total}, d)}{8 \cdot M_{payload}}$, with $M_{payload} = 20$ bytes = 160 bits:

$$
E_{bit} = \frac{14.4 \, \mu\text{J}}{8 \times 20} = \frac{14.4}{160} = 0.09 \, \mu\text{J/bit} = 90 \, \text{nJ/bit}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute Annual Energy Consumption:**
Number of transmissions per year:

$$
N_{tx} = \frac{3.1536 \times 10^{7} \text{ s/year}}{60 \text{ s}} = 5.256 \times 10^{5} \text{ transmissions/year}
$$

Annual energy consumed by transmit:

$$
E_{annual} = 14.4 \times 10^{-6} \times 5.256 \times 10^{5} = 7.5686 \, \text{J/year}
$$

**Step 8 — Compare with HTTP for Same Scenario:**
For HTTP, $k = 220 \times 8 = 1760$ bits, $M_{payload} = 20$ bytes = 160 bits.

$$
E_{tx,HTTP} = 1760 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} + 1760 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 2500
$$

$$
E_{tx,HTTP} = 88 \, \mu\text{J} + 44 \, \mu\text{J} = 132 \, \mu\text{J}
$$

Annual HTTP energy: $132 \times 10^{-6} \times 5.256 \times 10^{5} \approx 69.38$ J/year.

**Energy Savings from CoAP vs. HTTP:**

$$
\Delta E = \frac{69.38 - 7.57}{69.38} \times 100 = 89.09\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Choosing CoAP over HTTP saves ~89% annual transmit energy — a decisive factor for energy-harvested fog nodes.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — MQTT QoS Energy Trade-off

**Problem Statement:**
Compare energy consumed per PUBLISH message for MQTT QoS-0, QoS-1, and QoS-2. Assume payload = 20 bytes, all control packets have a 2-byte fixed header + 4 bytes variable, and each ACK/PUBREC/PUBREL/PUBCOMP is 6 bytes.

**Step 1 — QoS-0 (At Most Once):**
One-way PUBLISH only, no acknowledgment.

$$
M_{QoS0} = 2 + 4 + 20 = 26 \text{ bytes} = 208 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 2 — QoS-1 (At Least Once):**
PUBLISH + PUBACK (acknowledgment) round-trip.

$$
M_{QoS1} = (2+4+20) + (2+4) = 32 \text{ bytes} = 256 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — QoS-2 (Exactly Once):**
4-step handshake: PUBLISH + PUBREC + PUBREL + PUBCOMP.

$$
M_{QoS2} = (2+4+20) + (2+4) + (2+4) + (2+4) = 46 \text{ bytes} = 368 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply First-Order Radio Model (d = 20 m, n = 2):**
For QoS-0:

$$
E_{QoS0} = 208 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} + 208 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 400 = 10.4 + 0.832 = 11.232 \, \mu\text{J}
$$

For QoS-1:

$$
E_{QoS1} = 256 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} + 256 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 400 = 12.8 + 1.024 = 13.824 \, \mu\text{J}
$$

For QoS-2:

$$
E_{QoS2} = 368 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} + 368 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 400 = 18.4 + 1.472 = 19.872 \, \mu\text{J}
$$

**Step 5 — Summary Table:**

| QoS Level | Total Bytes | Energy (µJ) | Reliability | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| QoS-0 | 26 | 11.232 | Fire-and-forget | Sensor telemetry, loss-tolerant |
| QoS-1 | 32 | 13.824 | At-least-once | Control commands |
| QoS-2 | 46 | 19.872 | Exactly-once | Billing, safety-critical actuation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** QoS-2 consumes **~77% more energy** than QoS-0. In energy-harvested fog deployments, **mixed QoS strategies** are recommended — use QoS-0 for routine telemetry, QoS-2 only for actuation commands.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Protocol Selection

**Selection criteria for fog protocol in energy-constrained IoT:**

| Criterion | Weight | HTTP | MQTT | CoAP | DDS | LWM2M |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Efficiency | 0.30 | 0.1 | 0.7 | 0.9 | 0.5 | 0.8 |
| Latency | 0.20 | 0.4 | 0.6 | 0.8 | 0.95 | 0.7 |
| Reliability | 0.15 | 0.6 | 0.9 | 0.7 | 0.95 | 0.8 |
| Interoperability | 0.15 | 0.95 | 0.85 | 0.8 | 0.5 | 0.6 |
| Security (built-in) | 0.10 | 0.7 | 0.8 | 0.7 | 0.7 | 0.85 |
| Code Footprint | 0.10 | 0.2 | 0.8 | 0.9 | 0.4 | 0.85 |
| **Weighted Score** | — | **0.42** | **0.75** | **0.81** | **0.65** | **0.77** |

**CoAP emerges as the most balanced choice for low-energy fog nodes**, MQTT dominates for broker-mediated pub/sub, and DDS excels in real-time industrial settings.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fog Protocol Stack — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A1[APPLICATION LAYER\nUser Apps, Analytics, Dashboards]
    A2[FOG MIDDLEWARE LAYER\nMQTT Broker, CoAP Server, DDS Domain]
    A3[TRANSPORT LAYER\nTCP TLS, UDP DTLS, SCTP]
    A4[NETWORK LAYER\nIPv6, 6LoWPAN, RPL]
    A5[DATA LINK LAYER\nIEEE 802.15.4, BLE, LoRaWAN, Wi-Fi]
    A6[PHYSICAL LAYER\nRF Transceiver, Energy Harvester]

    B1[Sensor Node A\nTemperature]
    B2[Sensor Node B\nHumidity]
    B3[Actuator Node\nRelay Control]
    B4[Edge Gateway\nFog Node 1]
    B5[Fog Node 2\nLocal Aggregator]
    B6[Cloud Broker\nLong-term Storage]

    B1 -->|CoAP over UDP| A4
    B2 -->|MQTT-SN| A4
    B3 -->|MQTT QoS-2| A4
    A4 --> A3
    A3 --> A2
    A2 --> A4
    A2 --> A1
    A1 --> A4
    A4 --> A5
    A5 --> A6
    A6 -.Harvests.-> A5

    B4 -->|MQTT QoS-1| B5
    B5 -->|CoAP over TCP| B6
    B6 -.Backhaul.-> A1
```

### 4.2 MQTT Message Flow — Publish–Subscribe Pattern

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sensor Node
    participant B as MQTT Broker
    participant C1 as Subscriber 1
    participant C2 as Subscriber 2

    Note over S,C2: Connection Establishment
    S->>B: CONNECT (ClientID, KeepAlive)
    B-->>S: CONNACK (Session Present)
    C1->>B: CONNECT
    B-->>C1: CONNACK
    C2->>B: CONNECT
    B-->>C2: CONNACK

    Note over S,C2: Subscription
    C1->>B: SUBSCRIBE (Topic: sensor/temp, QoS-1)
    B-->>C1: SUBACK (Granted QoS-1)
    C2->>B: SUBSCRIBE (Topic: sensor/temp, QoS-0)
    B-->>C2: SUBACK (Granted QoS-0)

    Note over S,C2: QoS-1 Publish
    S->>B: PUBLISH (sensor/temp, QoS-1, PacketID)
    B-->>S: PUBACK (PacketID)
    B->>C1: PUBLISH (sensor/temp, QoS-1)
    C1-->>B: PUBACK
    B->>C2: PUBLISH (sensor/temp, QoS-0)

    Note over S,C2: Disconnect
    S->>B: DISCONNECT
```

### 4.3 CoAP Request–Response with Observe Pattern

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C[Client / Fog App] -->|1. CON GET /sensor/temp| S[CoAP Server / Edge Node]
    S -->|2. ACK 2.05 Content 24.5 C| C
    C -->|3. CON GET Observe /sensor/temp| S
    S -->|4. ACK 2.05 Content 24.5 C| C
    S -.->|5. NOTIFY 24.6 C| C
    S -.->|6. NOTIFY 24.7 C| C
    S -.->|7. NOTIFY 24.8 C| C
    C -->|8. RST Cancel Observe| S
```

### 4.4 Energy-Aware Protocol Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start: New IoT Fog Deployment] --> Q1{Node Battery\nor Energy Harvested?}
    Q1 -->|Battery Only| Q2{Real-time\nRequirements?}
    Q1 -->|Energy Harvested| Q3{Power Budget\nper Message?}
    Q2 -->|Yes, sub-ms| DDS[DDS over TSN\nOPC-UA]
    Q2 -->|No, seconds OK| Q4{Broker Needed?}
    Q3 -->|Below 50 uJ| COAP[CoAP + DTLS]
    Q3 -->|Above 50 uJ| MQTT[MQTT + TLS]
    Q4 -->|Yes, pub/sub| MQTT2[MQTT QoS-0 or 1]
    Q4 -->|No, REST style| HTTP[HTTP only if no constraint]
    COAP --> END[Selected: Low-Energy Stack]
    MQTT --> END
    MQTT2 --> END
    DDS --> END
    HTTP --> END
```

> [!TIP]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** In the sequence diagrams, solid arrows represent synchronous protocol exchanges; dotted arrows represent asynchronous notifications. The block diagram shows layered separation, while the decision flowchart operationalizes the analytical matrix from Section 3.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define fog protocols. List any four fog protocols with their transport-layer binding.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for listing with bindings):**
Fog protocols are standardized communication mechanisms that enable data exchange among IoT edge devices, fog nodes, and the cloud, optimized for low bandwidth, low energy, and intermittent connectivity.

| Protocol | Transport Binding | Pattern |
|---|---|---|
| MQTT | TCP (port 1883/8883 TLS) | Publish–Subscribe (Broker) |
| CoAP | UDP (port 5683/5684 DTLS) | Request–Response |
| AMQP | TCP (port 5672/5671 TLS) | Publish–Subscribe (Broker) |
| DDS | UDP/TCP/RTPS | Publish–Subscribe (Brokerless) |
| LWM2M | UDP/CoAP | Device Management |

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Sample Paper]**
*Compare HTTP and CoAP in terms of header size, transport protocol, and energy efficiency.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | HTTP/1.1 | CoAP |
|---|---|---|
| Transport | TCP | UDP |
| Header Size | 200 – 800+ bytes | 4 – 10 bytes |
| Energy Efficiency | Low | High |
| Reliability Mechanism | TCP ACK | Confirmable messages + retransmission |
| Security | TLS | DTLS |
| Suitability for Energy-Harvested Nodes | Poor | Excellent |

**Verdict:** CoAP is the protocol of choice for energy-harvested, constrained fog nodes because of its **~95% smaller header** and **connectionless UDP transport**, which avoids TCP state-maintenance energy.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **layered architecture of fog computing** and identify the role of fog protocols at each layer. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An energy-harvested fog node transmits a 30-byte payload every 30 seconds to a gateway 40 m away using MQTT QoS-1. MQTT PUBLISH has a 12-byte header, PUBACK has 4 bytes. Compute the **total bytes transmitted per hour, total transmit energy per hour**, and **annual energy consumption** using the first-order radio model ($E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{amp} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², n = 2). Comment on whether a 100 mAh, 3 V coin cell (360 J) can sustain this node. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Layered Fog Architecture and Protocol Role (7 marks):**

| Layer | Function | Role of Fog Protocols |
|---|---|---|
| **Edge / Device Layer** | Sensors, actuators, harvesters | CoAP, MQTT-SN, LWM2M run here |
| **Fog Node Layer** | Local compute, storage, filtering | MQTT broker, CoAP server, DDS domain |
| **Network Layer** | 6LoWPAN, IPv6, RPL, routing | Header compression, fragmentation |
| **Cloud Layer** | Long-term storage, ML analytics | AMQP, HTTPS, gRPC |

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[EDGE DEVICE LAYER\nSensors, Actuators]
    L2[FOG NODE LAYER\nGateways, Brokers, Local Analytics]
    L3[CLOUD LAYER\nData Lake, ML, Visualization]
    P1[Lightweight Protocols:\nMQTT-SN, CoAP, LWM2M]
    P2[Broker Protocols:\nMQTT, AMQP, DDS]
    P3[Web Protocols:\nHTTPS, gRPC]
    L1 -->|Lightweight Protocols| P1
    P1 --> L2
    L2 -->|Broker Protocols| P2
    P2 --> L3
    L3 -->|Web Protocols| P3
```

**Protocol roles:**
1. **At the edge:** Lightweight protocols (MQTT-SN, CoAP) handle power-constrained devices.
2. **At the fog node:** Broker protocols (MQTT, DDS) aggregate and route messages.
3. **At the cloud-to-fog link:** Secure protocols (AMQP over TLS, HTTPS) provide reliable backhaul.
4. **Cross-layer:** Identity (X.509 in DTLS), device management (LWM2M), security (TLS/DTLS).
5. **QoS negotiation:** MQTT's QoS 0/1/2 maps to latency/energy trade-off.
6. **Interoperability:** Protocol translation at fog gateways (e.g., CoAP-to-MQTT bridge).
7. **Reliability:** ACK/retransmission at each tier ensures end-to-end robustness.

---

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute total bytes per MQTT QoS-1 message:**
PUBLISH = 12 + 30 = 42 bytes, PUBACK = 4 bytes, total per cycle = 46 bytes = 368 bits.

**Step 2 — Compute transmissions per hour:**
$f_{hr} = 3600 / 30 = 120$ transmissions/hour.

**Step 3 — Compute total bytes per hour:**
$M_{hr} = 120 \times 46 = 5520$ bytes/hour.

**Step 4 — Compute transmit energy per message (d = 40 m, n = 2):**

$$
E_{tx} = 368 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} + 368 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 40^{2}
$$

$$
E_{tx} = 18400 \times 10^{-9} + 368 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 1600 = 18.4 + 5.888 = 24.288 \, \mu\text{J}
$$

**Step 5 — Total energy per hour:**

$$
E_{hr} = 120 \times 24.288 \, \mu\text{J} = 2914.56 \, \mu\text{J} \approx 2.915 \text{ mJ/hour}
$$

**Step 6 — Annual energy consumption:**

$$
E_{annual} = 2.915 \times 10^{-3} \times 24 \times 365 = 25.53 \text{ J/year}
$$

**Step 7 — Battery feasibility check:**

$$
\text{Battery energy} = Q \times V = 0.1 \times 3600 \times 3 = 1080 \text{ J (theoretical)}
$$

Effective usable energy (after derating, DC-DC losses, self-discharge, typically 30% usable):

$$
E_{usable} = 0.3 \times 1080 = 324 \text{ J}
$$

**Verdict:** The node consumes only 25.53 J/year out of 324 J usable, so the battery can last **> 12 years** (324/25.53 ≈ 12.69 years) — but only the transmit side is counted. With energy harvesting supplementing, the node becomes **energy-autonomous**.

---

**Question B. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **MQTT protocol architecture** in detail. With a diagram, describe the **three QoS levels** and their message exchanges. Discuss which QoS level is most suitable for **energy-harvested fog nodes** and why. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A smart agriculture deployment uses **CoAP over 6LoWPAN**. A soil moisture sensor transmits a **15-byte payload** every 5 minutes. The CoAP header is **4 bytes**, and a DTLS handshake is required every 24 hours (DTLS adds **50 bytes** of overhead per handshake, exchanged in 4 flights). Compute:
(i) the protocol overhead percentage, (ii) the **average energy per day** for transmit only, using $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/bit, $\varepsilon_{amp} = 10$ pJ/bit/m², n = 2, d = 100 m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — MQTT Architecture and QoS (7 marks):**

**MQTT Architecture Components:**
- **Client:** Any device (sensor, actuator, app) running the MQTT library.
- **Broker:** Central server (e.g., Mosquitto, HiveMQ) that decouples publishers and subscribers.
- **Topic:** Hierarchical string (e.g., `farm/sector3/soil/moisture`) used for message routing.
- **Session:** Persistent state of subscriptions and QoS message flow.

**MQTT Control Packet Format:**
Fixed Header (2 bytes) + Variable Header + Payload.
Fixed Header: Control Packet Type (4 bits) + Flags (4 bits) + Remaining Length (1–4 bytes).

**Three QoS Levels — Diagram and Exchange:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph QoS0
        P0[PUBLISH] --> S0[Broker]
    end
    subgraph QoS1
        P1[PUBLISH] --> S1[Broker]
        S1 --> A1[PUBACK]
        A1 --> P1
    end
    subgraph QoS2
        P2[PUBLISH] --> S2[Broker]
        S2 --> R2[PUBREC]
        R2 --> P2
        P2 --> L2[PUBREL]
        L2 --> S2
        S2 --> C2[PUBCOMP]
        C2 --> P2
    end
```

| QoS Level | Steps | Reliability | Energy Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| QoS-0 | 1 (PUBLISH) | At most once | **Lowest** |
| QoS-1 | 2 (PUBLISH + PUBACK) | At least once | Medium |
| QoS-2 | 4 (handshake) | Exactly once | **Highest** |

**Recommendation for Energy-Harvested Fog Nodes:**
**QoS-0 is preferred** for routine sensor telemetry because:
1. It avoids retransmission energy in lossy 6LoWPAN/LoRa networks.
2. Most sensor data is **time-sensitive but loss-tolerant** — newer readings supersede older ones.
3. Duty-cycling naturally discards old messages.
4. QoS-1/2 should be reserved for **actuation commands** and **firmware updates** where data integrity is non-negotiable.

---

**(b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Total CoAP message size per transmission:**
$M_{total} = 4 + 15 = 19$ bytes = 152 bits.

**Step 2 — Compute overhead percentage:**

$$
\eta_{oh} = \frac{4}{4 + 15} \times 100 = \frac{4}{19} \times 100 = 21.05\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute transmissions per day (excluding DTLS):**

$$
N_{tx,day} = \frac{24 \times 3600}{300} = \frac{86400}{300} = 288 \text{ transmissions/day}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute energy per CoAP transmission (d = 100 m, n = 2):**

$$
E_{tx} = 152 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} + 152 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 100^{2}
$$

$$
E_{tx} = 7600 \times 10^{-9} + 152 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 10000
$$

$$
E_{tx} = 7.6 \, \mu\text{J} + 15.2 \, \mu\text{J} = 22.8 \, \mu\text{J per CoAP message}
$$

**Step 5 — Daily energy for CoAP data transmissions:**

$$
E_{CoAP,day} = 288 \times 22.8 \, \mu\text{J} = 6566.4 \, \mu\text{J} = 6.566 \text{ mJ/day}
$$

**Step 6 — Daily energy for DTLS handshake (4 flights × 50 bytes per flight = 200 bytes per handshake):**

$$
M_{DTLS} = 200 \text{ bytes} = 1600 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
E_{DTLS} = 1600 \times 50 \times 10^{-9} + 1600 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 10000
$$

$$
E_{DTLS} = 80 \, \mu\text{J} + 160 \, \mu\text{J} = 240 \, \mu\text{J per handshake}
$$

(Only 1 handshake per day, so $E_{DTLS,day} = 240$ µJ.)

**Step 7 — Total daily energy for transmit:**

$$
E_{total,day} = 6.566 \text{ mJ} + 0.240 \text{ mJ} = 6.806 \text{ mJ/day}
$$

**Step 8 — Convert to annual for context:**

$$
E_{annual} = 6.806 \times 10^{-3} \times 365 = 2.484 \text{ J/year}
$$

**Conclusion:** The node consumes only 2.484 J/year for transmit — easily sustainable by a small solar panel (~1 cm²) or even a thermal harvester in temperate climates. The CoAP + DTLS stack is highly energy-efficient for fog deployments.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make on Fog Protocol Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing MQTT QoS with CoAP Confirmable/Non-confirmable:** MQTT QoS-0 is NOT equivalent to CoAP NON. CoAP NON has *no* retransmission; CoAP CON is similar to MQTT QoS-1.
> 2. **Forgetting to include PUBACK/PUBCOMP in QoS-1/2 byte counts:** Always add the ACK packet size to the PUBLISH size when computing protocol overhead and energy.
> 3. **Using TCP energy model for CoAP:** CoAP uses UDP — there is no TCP connection-state energy, but there is *application-layer* retransmission energy for CON messages.
> 4. **Mixing up DTLS flights vs. bytes:** A 4-flight DTLS handshake does NOT equal 4 bytes; each flight carries ~30–100 bytes depending on cipher suite.
> 5. **Not converting bytes to bits:** The energy model $E_{elec} = 50$ nJ/**bit** is in bits, not bytes. Always multiply bytes × 8.
> 6. **Ignoring path-loss exponent n:** For indoor (n ≈ 2) vs. outdoor (n ≈ 3–4) deployments, amplifier energy changes by 10–100×.
> 7. **Forgetting the broker's energy in fog calculations:** MQTT broker is a *server* and runs on mains power — do not include broker energy in node energy budget.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Fog Protocols (KTU PECIT746 Module 2)**
>
> - [x] **Fog Protocols** = communication mechanisms for fog/edge/cloud tier interaction; optimized for low energy, low bandwidth, intermittent connectivity.
> - [x] **MQTT** — TCP-based, broker-mediated pub/sub, 3 QoS levels, ISO/IEC 20922.
> - [x] **CoAP** — UDP-based, request–response, 4-byte header, RFC 7252, observe pattern.
> - [x] **AMQP** — TCP-based, broker-mediated, enterprise-grade pub/sub, ISO/IEC 19464.
> - [x] **DDS** — Brokerless, real-time pub/sub, OMG standard, suitable for industrial IoT.
> - [x] **LWM2M** — Device management over CoAP, OMA Specworks.
> - [x] **HTTP** — Heavy headers (~200+ bytes), high energy, avoid in constrained nodes.
> - [x] **WebSocket** — Full-duplex over TCP, useful for browser-based dashboards.
> - [x] **OPC-UA** — Industrial automation, IEC 62541, TSN integration.
> - [x] **XMPP** — Presence and messaging, federated, XML-based.
> - [x] **MQTT-SN** — MQTT for Sensor Networks (UDP), designed for sleeping nodes.
> - [x] **Overhead Formula:** $\eta_{oh} = M_{header} / (M_{header} + M_{payload}) \times 100$.
> - [x] **Energy Formula:** $E_{tx}(k,d) = k \cdot E_{elec} + k \cdot \varepsilon_{amp} \cdot d^{n}$.
> - [x] **Energy per Bit:** $E_{bit} = E_{tx} / (8 \cdot M_{payload})$.
> - [x] **CoAP is most energy-efficient** for constrained fog nodes (16–21% overhead).
> - [x] **MQTT QoS-0 is most energy-efficient** pub/sub mode (fire-and-forget).
> - [x] **DTLS** secures CoAP; **TLS** secures MQTT — both add 20–50 bytes per handshake.
> - [x] **Annual energy savings from CoAP vs. HTTP ≈ 89%** for a typical sensor workload.
> - [x] **Path-loss exponent n = 2** (free space) vs. n = 3–4 (multipath/outdoor).
> - [x] **Always include control packets** (PUBACK, PUBREC, PUBREL, PUBCOMP) in QoS-1/2 calculations.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fog Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fog Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Fog Protocol: Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fog Protocol Definition:**
> A **Fog Protocol** is a standardized set of communication rules, message formats, and interaction semantics that govern the exchange of data between heterogeneous devices, gateways, fog nodes, and cloud back-ends within a **Fog Computing Architecture**. These protocols operate across the *device-to-fog* and *fog-to-cloud* tiers of the IoT stack, providing lightweight, reliable, and often energy-aware message transport optimized for constrained hardware and intermittent connectivity.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, **PECIT746** situates fog protocols as the *nervous system* of the fog layer. They are responsible for:

1. **Device Discovery & Registration** — onboarding sensors/actuators to fog nodes.
2. **Data Ingestion & Telemetry** — moving sensor readings up the hierarchy.
3. **Command Dispatch** — pushing actuation commands downward.
4. **State Synchronization** — keeping distributed digital twins consistent.
5. **QoS Enforcement** — guaranteeing message delivery under energy/latency budgets.

The four protocols canonically studied in this module are:

| Acronym | Full Form | Primary Paradigm |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **MQTT** | Message Queuing Telemetry Transport | Publish / Subscribe (Broker-centric) |
| **CoAP** | Constrained Application Protocol | Request / Response (RESTful over UDP) |
| **AMQP** | Advanced Message Queuing Protocol | Publish / Subscribe + Queuing |
| **DDS** | Data Distribution Service | Publish / Subscribe (Broker-less, real-time) |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Smart City Traffic Control Room:**
> Imagine a **busy city** where thousands of vehicles (IoT sensors) continuously report their position, speed, and fuel levels. Sending every single report directly to a **distant national data center** (the cloud) would be slow, expensive, and cause radio congestion. Instead, the city sets up **local district control rooms** (Fog Nodes) at every neighborhood.
> - **MQTT** acts like a *walkie-talkie dispatcher* — every vehicle (publisher) shouts its status into a channel, and a *dispatcher* (broker) at the district control room relays it to dashboards (subscribers).
> - **CoAP** acts like a *postal courier* — when a dashboard needs specific data, it sends a *registered letter* (GET request) to a specific vehicle, and the vehicle replies with a postcard.
> - **AMQP** acts like a *banking clearing house* — every transaction is queued, acknowledged, and guaranteed to settle, even if the recipient is offline.
> - **DDS** acts like a *military radio net* — every vehicle broadcasts, and any other vehicle tuned to the same frequency hears it *instantly*, with no central dispatcher.
> The **Fog Protocol** is the *language and etiquette* all these district rooms use to talk to each other and to vehicles — ensuring speed, reliability, and low energy cost.

## 1.3 Why Fog Protocols Matter in Energy-Harvested IoT

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Energy Coupling:**
> Fog protocols directly impact the **energy budget** of a node. A protocol with a **1400-byte header** (like HTTPS) drains a coin-cell sensor in days, whereas a **2-byte header** (like CoAP) can extend lifetime to years. Therefore, the choice of fog protocol is a **first-order design decision** in any energy-harvested IoT deployment.

**Standard reference metrics** used throughout this module:
- **Message Overhead (Bytes)** — bytes added per packet beyond payload.
- **Minimum PDU Size (Bytes)** — protocol-imposed minimum datagram.
- **Default Port** — `1883` (MQTT), `5683` (CoAP), `5672` (AMQP), varies (DDS).
- **Transport Layer** — TCP, UDP, or both.
- **QoS Levels** — `0`, `1`, `2` (MQTT); Confirmable/Non-confirmable (CoAP).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Protocol-by-Protocol Architectural Breakdown

### 2.1.1 MQTT — Message Queuing Telemetry Transport

MQTT is an **OASIS-standardized**, TCP-based, **publish/subscribe** protocol designed by IBM (1999) for low-bandwidth, high-latency, or unreliable networks. It is the de-facto lingua franca of fog computing.

**Core Components:**
- **Publisher** — the sensor node sending data.
- **Subscriber** — the application (cloud dashboard, fog analytics engine) wanting data.
- **Broker** — the central fog-node entity that filters and forwards messages.
- **Topic** — a hierarchical UTF-8 string label (e.g., `home/livingroom/temperature`).
- **QoS Level** — `0` (at most once), `1` (at least once), `2` (exactly once).

**Why it is energy-frugal in fog contexts:**
1. **Fixed 2-byte header** (smallest possible) — minimizes radio-on time.
2. **Persistent sessions** — reuses connection, avoiding TLS/handshake energy.
3. **Asynchronous** — the node can sleep until publish, drastically reducing duty cycle.

### 2.1.2 CoAP — Constrained Application Protocol

CoAP is an **IETF RFC 7252** standardized protocol that mimics HTTP semantics but runs over **UDP** with minimal overhead. It is engineered for **machine-to-machine (M2M)** communication in constrained nodes (≤ 10 KB RAM, ≤ 100 kbps links).

**Core Components:**
- **CoAP Client / Server** — peer roles in RESTful exchange.
- **Message Types** — `CON` (Confirmable), `NON` (Non-confirmable), `ACK`, `RST`.
- **Resource URI** — analogous to HTTP path (e.g., `coap://[fd00::1]/sensors/temp`).
- **Observe Option** (RFC 7641) — server pushes updates to subscribed client (a lightweight pub/sub emulation).

**Why it is energy-frugal in fog contexts:**
1. **4-byte base header** over UDP — no TCP handshake cost.
2. **Stateless interaction** — perfect for sleeping endpoints awakened by interrupts.
3. **Block-wise transfer** (RFC 7959) — fragments large payloads to fit energy budgets.
4. **DTLS handshake amortization** — reuses security sessions.

### 2.1.3 AMQP — Advanced Message Queuing Protocol

AMQP is an **OASIS-standardized**, binary, **messaging middleware** protocol originally designed for financial trading. In fog contexts, it is used when **transactional reliability** outweighs energy frugality.

**Core Components:**
- **Exchange** — receives messages from publishers, routes to queues.
- **Queue** — buffers messages for consumers.
- **Binding** — rules linking exchanges to queues.
- **Channel** — multiplexed logical stream over a single TCP connection.

**Why it is *less* energy-frugal but valuable:**
1. **8-byte minimum frame** — heavier than MQTT.
2. **Transactional semantics** — guarantees exactly-once delivery with persistent queues.
3. **Used at the fog-to-cloud boundary** where reliability outweighs energy cost.

### 2.1.4 DDS — Data Distribution Service

DDS is an **OMG-standardized**, **broker-less**, **real-time**, **publish/subscribe** protocol. It is the highest-performance option, used in industrial IoT, autonomous vehicles, and defense fog networks.

**Core Components:**
- **DomainParticipant** — entry point for nodes.
- **Topic, Publisher, Subscriber, DataWriter, DataReader** — RTPS endpoints.
- **QoS Policies** — over 20 parameters (latency budget, reliability, durability, lifespan).

**Why it is energy-intensive but mission-critical:**
1. **No broker** — eliminates a single point of failure.
2. **Discovery protocol** — built-in, but consumes bandwidth.
3. **Used in fog deployments where sub-millisecond latency is mandatory**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Parameter Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All quantities below are testable directly under the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 syllabus.

| Parameter | MQTT | CoAP | AMQP | DDS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Transport** | TCP | UDP | TCP | UDP / TCP (RTPS) |
| **Min Header (Bytes)** | **2** | **4** | **8** | ~**12** (RTPS header) |
| **Default Port** | 1883 / 8883 (TLS) | 5683 / 5684 (DTLS) | 5672 / 5671 (TLS) | User-defined |
| **Paradigm** | Pub/Sub | Req/Res + Observe | Pub/Sub + Queue | Broker-less Pub/Sub |
| **Security** | TLS | DTLS | TLS / SASL | DTLS / TLS |
| **QoS Options** | 3 levels | 4 message types | 4 settle modes | 20+ QoS policies |
| **Best Use Case** | Telemetry, sleep nodes | RESTful actuator control | Mission-critical queues | Real-time control loops |
| **Typical Energy Rank** | **Lowest** | **Lowest** | **Medium** | **Medium-High** |

## 2.3 Energy Cost of Protocol Operation (Foundational Equation)

For a node transmitting a packet of $k$ bits over distance $d$:

$$E_{tx}(k,d) \;=\; k \cdot E_{elec} \;+\; k \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2} \quad \text{(free-space model, } d < d_{0}\text{)}$$

$$E_{tx}(k,d) \;=\; k \cdot E_{elec} \;+\; k \cdot \epsilon_{mp} \cdot d^{4} \quad \text{(multi-path model, } d \geq d_{0}\text{)}$$

The crossover distance $d_{0}$ is:

$$d_{0} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}}$$

**Where:**
- $E_{elec}$ = **50 nJ/bit** (typical radio electronics energy).
- $\epsilon_{fs}$ = **10 pJ/bit/m²** (free-space amplifier coefficient).
- $\epsilon_{mp}$ = **0.0013 pJ/bit/m⁴** (multi-path amplifier coefficient).
- $k$ = total bits = payload bits + **protocol header bits**.

**The protocol's energy impact** enters through $k$. Switching from MQTT (2-byte header) to HTTPS (≈ 500-byte header) on a 100-bit payload increases $k$ by **5×**, ballooning $E_{tx}$ proportionally.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Smart Agriculture:** MQTT over LoRaWAN for soil-moisture telemetry — minimizes transmission energy in fields without grid power.
- **Industrial 4.0:** DDS over TSN (Time-Sensitive Networking) for robotic arm coordination — sub-microsecond determinism.
- **Healthcare Wearables:** CoAP over BLE for heart-rate monitors — enables weeks-long battery life.
- **Smart Grid:** AMQP for billing-critical consumption records — exactly-once delivery is non-negotiable.
- **Autonomous Drones:** DDS for swarm telemetry — broker-less, peer-to-peer, robust to node loss.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Problems

> [!NOTE]
> The derivations below are **fully worked out** with every algebraic transition visible. They map directly to KTU Part A and Part B numerical problem patterns.

## 3.1 Problem 1: Computing Total Energy Cost of MQTT vs CoAP Transmission

> **Problem Statement (modeled on KTU 2024 Scheme style):**
> A temperature sensor must transmit a 16-bit payload over a distance of 20 m. Two fog protocols are under consideration:
> - **Protocol X (MQTT):** adds a 2-byte header.
> - **Protocol Y (CoAP):** adds a 4-byte header.
>
> Given $E_{elec} = 50 \text{ nJ/bit}$, $\epsilon_{fs} = 10 \text{ pJ/bit/m}^{2}$, compute the total transmit energy for both, and the percentage overhead.

### Step 1 — Convert all quantities to SI base units

Payload: $k_{payload} = 16 \text{ bits}$.

MQTT header: $k_{hdr,MQTT} = 2 \text{ bytes} = 2 \times 8 = 16 \text{ bits}$.

CoAP header: $k_{hdr,CoAP} = 4 \text{ bytes} = 4 \times 8 = 32 \text{ bits}$.

Total MQTT packet: $k_{MQTT} = 16 + 16 = 32 \text{ bits}$.

Total CoAP packet: $k_{CoAP} = 16 + 32 = 48 \text{ bits}$.

Constants:
- $E_{elec} = 50 \times 10^{-9} \text{ J/bit}$.
- $\epsilon_{fs} = 10 \times 10^{-12} \text{ J/bit/m}^{2}$.
- $d = 20 \text{ m}$.

### Step 2 — Determine the propagation regime

Compute $d_0$:

$$d_{0} = \sqrt{\frac{\epsilon_{fs}}{\epsilon_{mp}}}$$

We are not given $\epsilon_{mp}$ explicitly; assume free-space (typical for $d = 20$ m indoor).

### Step 3 — Compute $E_{tx}$ for MQTT

$$E_{tx}^{MQTT} = k_{MQTT} \cdot E_{elec} + k_{MQTT} \cdot \epsilon_{fs} \cdot d^{2}$$

Substituting:

$$E_{tx}^{MQTT} = 32 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-9}) + 32 \cdot (10 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (20)^{2}$$

$$E_{tx}^{MQTT} = 1.6 \times 10^{-6} + 32 \cdot (10 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 400$$

$$E_{tx}^{MQTT} = 1.6 \times 10^{-6} + 1.28 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\boxed{E_{tx}^{MQTT} = 1.728 \, \mu\text{J}}$$

### Step 4 — Compute $E_{tx}$ for CoAP

$$E_{tx}^{CoAP} = 48 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-9}) + 48 \cdot (10 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (20)^{2}$$

$$E_{tx}^{CoAP} = 2.4 \times 10^{-6} + 48 \cdot (10 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 400$$

$$E_{tx}^{CoAP} = 2.4 \times 10^{-6} + 1.92 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$\boxed{E_{tx}^{CoAP} = 2.592 \, \mu\text{J}}$$

### Step 5 — Percentage overhead of CoAP over MQTT

$$\Delta\% = \frac{E_{tx}^{CoAP} - E_{tx}^{MQTT}}{E_{tx}^{MQTT}} \times 100$$

$$\Delta\% = \frac{2.592 - 1.728}{1.728} \times 100 = \frac{0.864}{1.728} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\Delta\% = 50\%}$$

**Interpretation:** The 2-byte difference in header (16 bits) propagates as a **50% energy penalty** on this small payload. As payloads grow, the percentage overhead shrinks (law of large numbers), but absolute energy still rises.

## 3.2 Problem 2: Duty-Cycle Analysis for Sleep-Optimized MQTT Node

> **Problem Statement:** A sensor wakes once every $T_{cycle} = 60$ s, performs a measurement, and publishes one MQTT packet of $k = 200$ bits over $d = 30$ m. Compute the **average power** and **battery life** on a **2400 mAh, 3.0 V** coin cell. Use the parameters from Problem 1 and assume $T_{tx} = 10$ ms per packet.

### Step 1 — Compute transmit energy per cycle

$$k = 200 \text{ bits}, \quad d = 30 \text{ m}$$

$$E_{tx} = 200 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-9}) + 200 \cdot (10 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (30)^{2}$$

$$E_{tx} = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} + 200 \cdot (10 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 900$$

$$E_{tx} = 1.0 \times 10^{-5} + 1.8 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$E_{tx} = 1.18 \times 10^{-5} \text{ J} = 11.8 \, \mu\text{J}$$

### Step 2 — Compute average power

$$P_{avg} = \frac{E_{tx}}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{1.18 \times 10^{-5}}{60}$$

$$P_{avg} = 1.967 \times 10^{-7} \text{ W} \approx 196.7 \, \text{nW}$$

### Step 3 — Compute battery energy budget

Battery energy: $E_{bat} = V \cdot Q = 3.0 \cdot (2400 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 3600)$

$$E_{bat} = 3.0 \cdot 8640 = 25{,}920 \text{ J}$$

### Step 4 — Compute battery lifetime

$$L = \frac{E_{bat}}{P_{avg}} = \frac{25920}{1.967 \times 10^{-7}}$$

$$L = 1.318 \times 10^{11} \text{ s}$$

Converting to years:

$$L = \frac{1.318 \times 10^{11}}{365.25 \cdot 86400} \approx 4.18 \text{ years}$$

$$\boxed{L \approx 4.18 \text{ years}}$$

**Interpretation:** The MQTT header (2 bytes) keeps the duty cycle so low that a single coin cell **outlasts** the typical product lifecycle, validating the design choice.

## 3.3 Problem 3: Throughput vs Energy Trade-off for AMQP

> **Problem Statement:** A fog node aggregates data from 100 sensors, each publishing 256-bit payloads every 5 s over CoAP (CON mode). Compute the **aggregate bit-rate** arriving at the fog node and the **AMQP relay energy** if each AMQP frame carries a 64-bit header overhead and is forwarded 50 m.

### Step 1 — Bit rate per sensor

$$R_{sensor} = \frac{256 \text{ bits}}{5 \text{ s}} = 51.2 \text{ bps}$$

### Step 2 — Aggregate bit rate from 100 sensors

$$R_{agg} = 100 \cdot 51.2 = 5120 \text{ bps} = 5.12 \text{ kbps}$$

### Step 3 — AMQP frame energy per relay

Total AMQP frame: $k_{AMQP} = 256 + 64 = 320$ bits, $d = 50$ m.

$$E_{tx}^{AMQP} = 320 \cdot (50 \times 10^{-9}) + 320 \cdot (10 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (50)^{2}$$

$$E_{tx}^{AMQP} = 1.6 \times 10^{-5} + 320 \cdot (10 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot 2500$$

$$E_{tx}^{AMQP} = 1.6 \times 10^{-5} + 8.0 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$E_{tx}^{AMQP} = 2.4 \times 10^{-5} \text{ J} = 24 \, \mu\text{J}$$

### Step 4 — AMQP relay power

$$P_{AMQP} = R_{agg} \cdot \frac{E_{tx}^{AMQP}}{256} = 5120 \cdot \frac{2.4 \times 10^{-5}}{256}$$

$$P_{AMQP} = 5120 \cdot 9.375 \times 10^{-8} = 4.8 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W} = 480 \, \mu\text{W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{AMQP} \approx 480 \, \mu\text{W}}$$

**Interpretation:** The fog node consumes 480 µW of transmit power just for AMQP relaying. This is the energy cost of reliable, queued, exactly-once delivery — a justified expense for billing or medical-grade data.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix for Protocol Selection

| Criterion | MQTT | CoAP | AMQP | DDS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Min Energy per Packet** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |
| **Latency** | Medium | Low | Medium-High | **Lowest** |
| **Reliability** | High (with QoS 1/2) | High (CON mode) | **Highest** | High (with QoS) |
| **Constrained-Node Friendliness** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★ | ★ |
| **Cloud Compatibility** | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ |
| **Real-Time Guarantee** | ★ | ★ | ★ | ★★★★★ |
| **Security Maturity** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Fog Protocol Stack and Layered Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    appLayer[APPLICATION LAYER\nMQTT Topics, CoAP Resources, AMQP Queues]
    presLayer[PRESENTATION LAYER\nTLS / DTLS, Payload Encoding]
    sessLayer[SESSION LAYER\nMQTT Session, CoAP Token, AMQP Channel]
    transLayer[TRANSPORT LAYER\nTCP for MQTT and AMQP, UDP for CoAP and DDS]
    netLayer[NETWORK LAYER\nIPv6, 6LoWPAN, RPL]
    macLayer[MAC LAYER\nIEEE 802.15.4, BLE, LoRaWAN]

    appLayer --> presLayer
    presLayer --> sessLayer
    sessLayer --> transLayer
    transLayer --> netLayer
    netLayer --> macLayer

    mqtt[MQTT BROKER\nRuns on Fog Node]
    coap[COAP SERVER\nRuns on Sensor or Fog]
    amqp[AMQP EXCHANGE\nRuns on Fog to Cloud]
    dds[DDS DOMAIN\nPeer to Peer between Fog Nodes]

    transLayer --> mqtt
    transLayer --> coap
    transLayer --> amqp
    transLayer --> dds
```

## 4.2 MQTT Publish/Subscribe Interaction with Fog Broker

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S1 as SENSOR NODE 1\nPUBLISHER
    participant B as FOG BROKER\nON FOG NODE
    participant S2 as SENSOR NODE 2\nSUBSCRIBER
    participant C as CLOUD DASHBOARD\nSUBSCRIBER

    S1->>B: CONNECT packet\nClientID equals sensor01
    B-->>S1: CONNACK packet
    S2->>B: CONNECT packet\nClientID equals sensor02
    B-->>S2: CONNACK packet
    C->>B: CONNECT packet\nClientID equals cloud01
    B-->>C: CONNACK packet

    S2->>B: SUBSCRIBE topic home slash livingroom slash temp QoS 1
    B-->>S2: SUBACK packet
    C->>B: SUBSCRIBE topic home slash livingroom slash temp QoS 2
    B-->>C: SUBACK packet

    S1->>B: PUBLISH topic home slash livingroom slash temp payload 25 6 QoS 1
    B-->>S1: PUBACK packet
    B->>S2: Forward PUBLISH QoS 1
    S2-->>B: PUBACK
    B->>C: Forward PUBLISH QoS 1
    C-->>B: PUBREC then PUBREL then PUBCOMP chain for QoS 2

    Note over S1,B: Persistent session allows S1 to sleep between publishes
```

## 4.3 CoAP Request/Response Flow with Observe Extension

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    dev[CONSTRAINED SENSOR\nCoAP SERVER on UDP 5683]
    fog[FOG NODE\nCoAP CLIENT and PROXY]
    cloud[CLOUD APPLICATION\nCoAP CLIENT over HTTP gateway]

    dev -->|NON message\n4 byte header\nToken A| fog
    fog -->|ACK with Token A| dev

    fog -->|GET coap colon slash slash resource temp\nCON message| dev
    dev -->|ACK with payload 25 6| fog

    cloud -->|Observe Register coap colon slash slash resource temp| fog
    fog -->|Observe Ack| cloud
    dev -.->|Notify on change\n4 byte header| fog
    fog -.->|Forward to cloud| cloud

    note1[NON best for low power telemetry\nCON best for actuator commands]
    dev -.-> note1
```

## 4.4 AMQP Exchange-Queue-Binding Topology for Fog-to-Cloud

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    pub1[FOG NODE 1\nPUBLISHER] -->|Publish| exch[AMQP EXCHANGE\nTopic type]
    pub2[FOG NODE 2\nPUBLISHER] -->|Publish| exch
    pub3[FOG NODE 3\nPUBLISHER] -->|Publish| exch

    exch -->|Binding key telemetry slash star| q1[QUEUE Telemetry Lake]
    exch -->|Binding key alerts hash hash| q2[QUEUE Critical Alerts]
    exch -->|Binding key billing hash hash| q3[QUEUE Billing Audit]

    q1 --> cons1[CONSUMER Cloud Data Lake]
    q2 --> cons2[CONSUMER On Call Engineer]
    q3 --> cons3[CONSUMER Billing System]

    note2[Queues persist messages\nConsumer can disconnect\nMessages await delivery]
    q1 -.-> note2
```

## 4.5 DDS Broker-Less Real-Time Domain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    n1[FOG NODE A\nDomainParticipant 7\nDataWriter on topic LIDAR]
    n2[FOG NODE B\nDomainParticipant 7\nDataReader on topic LIDAR]
    n3[DRONE C\nDomainParticipant 7\nDataWriter on topic LIDAR]
    n4[DRONE D\nDomainParticipant 7\nDataReader on topic LIDAR]

    n1 <-->|RTPS multicast UDP\nDiscovery built in| n2
    n3 <-->|RTPS multicast UDP| n4
    n1 <-->|RTPS multicast UDP| n3
    n2 <-->|RTPS multicast UDP| n4

    note3[No broker\nPeer discovery via built in protocol\nQoS policy: latency budget 1 ms]
    n1 -.-> note3
```

## 4.6 End-to-End Fog Protocol Reference Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph DEV[DEVICE TIER]
        d1[Temp Sensor]
        d2[Pressure Sensor]
        d3[Camera Module]
    end

    subgraph FOG[FOG TIER]
        f1[Fog Node Alpha\nMQTT Broker\nCoAP Proxy\nLocal Analytics]
        f2[Fog Node Beta\nAMQP Exchange\nDDS Domain\nStorage Cache]
    end

    subgraph CLOUD[CLOUD TIER]
        c1[Data Lake]
        c2[AI Engine]
        c3[Dashboard]
    end

    d1 -->|CoAP NON 4 byte header| f1
    d2 -->|MQTT QoS 0 2 byte header| f1
    d3 -->|DDS RTPS 12 byte header| f2

    f1 -->|AMQP reliable queue 8 byte frame| f2
    f2 -->|AMQP TLS to cloud| c1
    c1 --> c2 --> c3
    c3 -->|CoAP PUT command| f1
    f1 -->|CoAP CON command| d1
```
