\mathcal{T}_{eff,kbps} \approx 34.13 \;\text{kbps}
$$

---

### ⭐ **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative to Q.A*

#### Q.B.(a) Explain the RTPS discovery mechanism in detail. With a sequence diagram, describe the **Participant Discovery Protocol (PDP)** and **Endpoint Discovery Protocol (EDP)** phases. What is the role of the well-known multicast address `239.255.0.1`? `(7 marks)` `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Solution:**

1. **Why discovery is needed (≈1.5 marks):** A new IoT node joining the network has no prior knowledge of other participants. The discovery mechanism enables participants to find each other **dynamically** without any static configuration.

2. **PDP — Participant Discovery Protocol (≈2.5 marks):** Uses **Simple Participant Discovery Protocol (SPDP)**. Each participant periodically (default $T_{PDP} = 3$ s) multicasts an SPDP `DATA(p)` sub-message to the well-known multicast address `239.255.0.1` (for IPv4). The sub-message contains the participant's GUID, lease duration, and other metadata. On receipt, each participant updates its local database of known participants. In response, the receiver also unicast-publishes its own SPDP `DATA(p)` to the new participant.

   **Role of `239.255.0.1`:** This is the **administratively scoped IPv4 multicast address** reserved by the OMG for DDS discovery. It allows participants to find each other without unicast address knowledge, supports automatic reconfiguration, and limits discovery traffic to a single TTL/domain.

3. **EDP — Endpoint Discovery Protocol (≈2.5 marks):** After PDP, participants exchange their **publication and subscription information** using the **Simple Endpoint Discovery Protocol (SEDP)**. Each participant sends a unicast `DATA` sub-message describing each of its `DataWriter` and `DataReader` objects (topic, type, QoS, GUID). The remote participant processes this and creates matching local handles if the topic and QoS are compatible.

4. **Sequence (≈0.5 marks):** The expected sequence is `SPDP announce → SPDP reply → SEDP publication → SEDP subscription → topic matching → data flow`.

---

#### Q.B.(b) A battery-less IoT node runs DDS-RTPS over a sub-GHz radio. Active mode power $P_{active} = 180$ mW, sleep power $P_{sleep} = 0.015$ mW, deadline $T_d = 200$ ms, active slot $t_{active} = 20$ ms. (i) Find the maximum duty cycle. (ii) Compute the average power. (iii) Calculate the percentage power saved compared to always-on. (iv) If the node must support `RELIABLE` QoS, what is the *additional* power overhead per `HEARTBEAT`–`ACKNACK` cycle if the radio stays active for an extra 8 ms? `(7 marks)` `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Maximum duty cycle (≈1.5 marks):**

$$
t_{sleep} \leq T_d - t_{active}
$$

$$
t_{sleep} \leq 200 - 20
$$

$$
t_{sleep} \leq 180 \;\text{ms}
$$

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{20}{20 + 180}
$$

$$
\delta_{max} = \dfrac{20}{200}
$$

$$
\delta_{max} = 0.10 \;\text{or}\; 10\%
$$

**(ii) Average power (≈1.5 marks):**

$$
P_{avg} = \delta \cdot P_{active} + (1 - \delta) \cdot P_{sleep}
$$

$$
P_{avg} = 0.10 \times 180 + 0.90 \times 0.015
$$

$$
P_{avg} = 18 + 0.0135
$$

$$
P_{avg} = 18.0135 \;\text{mW}
$$

**(iii) Percentage power saved (≈1.5 marks):**

$$
P_{saved} = P_{active} - P_{avg}
$$

$$
P_{saved} = 180 - 18.0135
$$

$$
P_{saved} = 161.9865 \;\text{mW}
$$

$$
\%_{saved} = \dfrac{161.9865}{180} \times 100
$$

$$
\%_{saved} \approx 90.0\%
$$

**(iv) Additional overhead per `HEARTBEAT`–`ACKNACK` cycle (≈2.5 marks):**

The extra active time is $\Delta t = 8$ ms out of the original cycle of $t_{cycle} = 200$ ms. The **additional duty-cycle fraction** is:

$$
\Delta\delta = \dfrac{\Delta t}{t_{cycle}}
$$

$$
\Delta\delta = \dfrac{8}{200}
$$

$$
\Delta\delta = 0.04
$$

The **additional average power** is:

$$
\Delta P_{avg} = \Delta\delta \cdot P_{active}
$$

$$
\Delta P_{avg} = 0.04 \times 180
$$

$$
\Delta P_{avg} = 7.2 \;\text{mW}
$$

As a percentage of the original $P_{avg}$:

$$
\%_{overhead} = \dfrac{\Delta P_{avg}}{P_{avg}} \times 100
$$

$$
\%_{overhead} = \dfrac{7.2}{18.0135} \times 100
$$

$$
\%_{overhead} \approx 39.97\%
$$

Hence, switching from `BEST_EFFORT` to `RELIABLE` QoS incurs a **~40% increase in average power**, a critical trade-off for energy-harvested nodes.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **DDS = OMG middleware standard** for real-time, decentralized Pub/Sub.
- **RTPS = wire protocol** that carries DDS data over UDP, TCP, or shared memory.
- **Core entities:** `DomainParticipant` → `Publisher`/`Subscriber` → `DataWriter`/`DataReader` ↔ `Topic`.
- **Decentralized** = no broker, no single point of failure; ideal for IoT.
- **Two discovery phases:** PDP (SPDP over multicast `239.255.0.1`) → EDP (SEDP unicast).
- **Header overhead:** $H_{RTPS} = 20$ bytes + $4n$ bytes for $n$ sub-messages.
- **Framing efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{P_{size}}{P_{size} + H_{total}}$.
- **Top-6 QoS for exams:** `RELIABILITY`, `DURABILITY`, `DEADLINE`, `LATENCY_BUDGET`, `LIFESPAN`, `HISTORY`.
- **`RELIABLE` costs more power** than `BEST_EFFORT` due to `HEARTBEAT`–`ACKNACK` round-trips.
- **Duty-cycling** can yield **~90% power savings** for typical IoT duty cycles ($\delta \approx 10\%$).
- **Discovery latency** is upper-bounded by $\dfrac{T_{PDP}}{2}$ assuming uniform random join.
- **DDS-RTPS powers ROS 2**, OPC UA, IEC 61850, and NASA's cFS.
- **Default RTPS ports:** well-known discovery at `PB` = 7400 + domain ID (UDP).
- **Durability ≠ Reliability.** They are orthogonal QoS axes.
- **Topic matching** requires both *topic name* match AND *QoS compatibility* (strictest interpretation wins for `RELIABILITY`).
- **Late-joining subscribers** rely on `DURABILITY > VOLATILE` to obtain cached samples.
- **Energy-harvested nodes** benefit from `LATENCY_BUDGET > 0` to enable sample batching and reduce radio-on time.

topics.title: Power Sources for WSN
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Sources for WSN

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Power Source for a Wireless Sensor Network (WSN)** is defined as the autonomous energy provisioning subsystem that delivers the regulated electrical energy required to sustain the sensing, computation, communication, and actuation operations of a distributed wireless sensor node over its intended operational lifetime. In the context of KTU's 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PECIT746**, power sources for WSNs are classified along two dominant axes:

1. **Stored Energy Sources** – Conventional electrochemical reservoirs such as primary (non-rechargeable) and secondary (rechargeable) batteries, and electrochemical double-layer capacitors (supercapacitors).
2. **Ambient Energy Sources (Harvesters)** – Transducers that convert ambient physical phenomena (solar irradiance, mechanical vibration, thermal gradients, and RF electromagnetic waves) into usable electrical energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** Module 3 specifically requires the student to evaluate the *energy density, lifetime characteristics, and feasibility trade-offs* of each power source class for low-power duty-cycled WSN nodes operating in IoT environments.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **remote forest** where a tiny weather station (a sensor node) must measure temperature and humidity for 5 years **without ever being touched by a human**. The power source is its **food and water supply**.

- A **battery** is like a **packed lunchbox** — it contains a finite amount of energy, measured in "calories" (Joules or mAh). Once eaten, it's gone (or needs recharging).
- A **supercapacitor** is like a **sports drink bottle** — it refills extremely fast (charges in seconds) but holds very little.
- A **solar harvester** is like a **farmer who grows food daily from sunlight** — the supply is theoretically infinite, but it depends on weather (intermittent).
- A **vibration harvester** is like a **fisherman who catches fish from passing currents** — opportunistic, depends on environmental motion.

The WSN designer's job is to **size, select, and orchestrate** these energy sources so the node's average daily energy budget **never exceeds** the daily available energy — a strict conservation law.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics for WSN Power Sources:**
> - **Energy Density** (Wh/kg) — how much energy per unit mass
> - **Power Density** (W/kg) — how quickly energy can be delivered
> - **Self-Discharge Rate** (%/month) — parasitic energy loss at rest
> - **Cycle Life** (number of charge/discharge cycles)
> - **Form Factor & Cost** — physical and economic feasibility
> - **Operating Temperature Range** — environmental robustness

## 1.3 Classification of Power Sources for WSN

| Class | Sub-Class | Examples | Rechargeable? |
|-------|-----------|----------|---------------|
| **Electrochemical** | Primary Batteries | Alkaline, Lithium-Thionyl Chloride (Li-SOCl₂), Zinc-Air | No |
| **Electrochemical** | Secondary Batteries | Li-Ion, LiPo, NiMH, LiFePO₄ | Yes |
| **Electrochemical** | Supercapacitors | EDLCs, Hybrid Capacitors | Yes (10⁵–10⁶ cycles) |
| **Ambient Harvesters** | Photovoltaic | a-Si, c-Si, CIGS solar cells | Continuous (replenished) |
| **Ambient Harvesters** | Piezoelectric / Electromagnetic | Vibration harvesters | Continuous |
| **Ambient Harvesters** | Thermoelectric | TEGs (Peltier modules) | Continuous |
| **Ambient Harvesters** | RF / Inductive | RFID rectennas, NFC coils | Continuous |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Battery Fundamentals for WSN Nodes

A WSN node's battery must satisfy three competing constraints: **high energy density, low self-discharge, and stable voltage under pulsed loads** (because radios draw current in short bursts).

### 2.1.1 Battery Capacity and Energy

The **theoretical capacity** $Q$ of a battery (in Coulombs) is determined by Faraday's laws of electrolysis:

$$Q = n \cdot F \cdot m / M$$

where:
- $n$ = number of electrons transferred per reaction
- $F$ = Faraday's constant = **96485 C/mol**
- $m$ = active mass of electrode material
- $M$ = molar mass of active material

The **practical capacity** is expressed in **mAh** (milliampere-hours), and the **stored energy** is:

$$E_{stored} = V_{nom} \cdot Q = V_{nom} \cdot I_{avg} \cdot t$$

### 2.1.2 Battery Lifetime Equation (Core KTU Formula)

The simplest closed-form lifetime estimate for a WSN node is:

$$L_{batt} = \frac{C_{batt} \cdot V_{nom}}{P_{avg}} \quad \text{[hours]}$$

For a current-driven analysis:

$$L_{batt} = \frac{C_{batt}}{I_{avg}} \quad \text{[hours]}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **$I_{avg}$ is NOT the peak radio current** — it is the **time-averaged current** including sleep current, wake-up transients, and active duty-cycled phases.

### 2.1.3 Peukert's Law (Capacity Fade under High Discharge)

Real batteries lose effective capacity when discharged at high rates:

$$t = \frac{C_{rated}}{I^{k}}$$

where $k$ is the **Peukert exponent** (typically $k = 1.05$ to $1.4$; ideally $k = 1$). For WSNs with low duty cycles, $k$ is close to 1.

### 2.1.4 Self-Discharge and Shelf Life

The actual charge retained after storage time $t$ is:

$$Q(t) = Q_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$

where $\lambda$ is the self-discharge rate constant. For **Li-SOCl₂**, $\lambda \approx 0.5\%$/year — making it the **gold standard for long-life WSN deployments**.

## 2.2 Energy Density Hierarchy

The following table presents the **practical specific energy** of common WSN power sources:

| Source | Specific Energy (Wh/kg) | Energy Density (Wh/L) | Self-Discharge |
|--------|------------------------:|----------------------:|---------------:|
| Alkaline (AA) | 100–150 | 250–400 | ~5%/year |
| Li-SOCl₂ (Primary) | 500–700 | 900–1100 | <1%/year |
| Li-Ion (Secondary) | 150–250 | 400–650 | ~2–3%/month |
| LiPo | 150–200 | 300–450 | ~5%/month |
| NiMH | 60–120 | 200–350 | ~20–30%/month |
| EDLC Supercapacitor | 5–10 | 5–10 | ~5–10%/day |
| Hybrid Li-Cap | 20–50 | 30–80 | ~1–2%/month |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** The energy density of an EDLC supercapacitor is **two orders of magnitude lower** than a Li-SOCl₂ primary cell. This is why supercapacitors are used as **peak-power buffers** (for radio bursts), not as the primary energy reservoir.

## 2.3 Energy Harvesting Power Density

The **available power per unit area/volume** for ambient energy harvesters determines whether the source can sustain a WSN node (typically needing **10 μW – 10 mW** average).

| Ambient Source | Typical Power Density | Availability |
|---------------|----------------------|-------------|
| Outdoor Solar (direct sun) | 10–15 mW/cm² | Daylight only |
| Indoor Solar (office light) | 10–100 μW/cm² | Daylight only |
| Human Vibration (walking) | 1–10 μW/cm³ | Intermittent |
| Machine Vibration (motor) | 100–1000 μW/cm³ | Continuous |
| Thermal Gradient (10 K) | 10–50 μW/cm² | Continuous |
| RF (GSM base station, 1 km) | 0.01–0.1 μW/cm² | Continuous |
| Wind (3 m/s) | 1–5 μW/cm² | Intermittent |

## 2.4 WSN Power Budget Equation

For a duty-cycled WSN node, the **average power consumption** is:

$$P_{avg} = \frac{P_{sleep} \cdot T_{sleep} + P_{sense} \cdot T_{sense} + P_{radio} \cdot T_{radio} + P_{proc} \cdot T_{proc}}{T_{cycle}}$$

where $T_{cycle} = T_{sleep} + T_{sense} + T_{radio} + T_{proc}$ is the total period.

For the node to be **perpetually operational** (Energy Neutral Operation, ENO):

$$E_{harvested} \geq E_{consumed} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{harv,avg} \cdot \eta_{conv} \geq P_{avg}$$

where $\eta_{conv}$ is the conversion efficiency of the power management circuit.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $L = C/I$ | Battery lifetime | hours |
| 2 | $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$ | Energy stored | Joules (or Wh) |
| 3 | $E = Q \cdot V$ | Energy from charge × voltage | Wh |
| 4 | $Q = nFm/M$ | Theoretical capacity (Faraday) | Coulombs |
| 5 | $t = C/I^{k}$ | Peukert's law | hours |
| 6 | $Q(t) = Q_0 e^{-\lambda t}$ | Self-discharge decay | Coulombs |
| 7 | $P_{avg} = \sum P_i T_i / T_{cycle}$ | Duty-cycled average power | W |
| 8 | $E_{neutral}: P_{harv} \cdot \eta \geq P_{avg}$ | ENO condition | W |
| 9 | $C_{eff} = I^k \cdot t$ | Effective capacity at rate | Ah |
| 10 | $E_{node,daily} = 24 \cdot P_{avg}$ | Daily energy budget | Wh/day |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT confuse Wh with Ah.** A 3.7 V, 1000 mAh Li-Ion cell stores **3.7 Wh**, not 1 Wh. Always multiply by nominal voltage.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Precision Agriculture:** Soil moisture sensor nodes use **Li-SOCl₂ primary cells** with a tiny solar backup because the 5–10 year deployment must be maintenance-free.
- **Structural Health Monitoring (SHM):** Bridge-mounted WSNs use **piezoelectric vibration harvesters** powered by passing traffic.
- **Wearable Healthcare:** Body-area networks (BAN) use **LiPo batteries** recharged by **body-heat TEGs** or **kinetic motion harvesters**.
- **Smart Meters:** Indoor deployments use **indoor photovoltaic cells** with **supercapacitor buffers** to ride out RF transmission peaks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: WSN Node Lifetime from a Primary Battery

**Problem:** A WSN mote uses 2 × AA Alkaline cells in series. Each cell has $C = 2500$ mAh at $V_{nom} = 1.5$ V. The mote operates with a duty cycle where the average current draw is $I_{avg} = 250$ μA. Estimate the operational lifetime in years.

### Step 1: Establish the Series Configuration

For two cells in series, the capacity in mAh remains the same, but the voltage doubles:

$$V_{pack} = 2 \times 1.5\,\text{V} = 3.0\,\text{V}$$
$$C_{pack} = 2500\,\text{mAh}$$

### Step 2: Apply the Battery Lifetime Equation

$$L = \frac{C_{pack}}{I_{avg}}$$

$$L = \frac{2500 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Ah}}{250 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{A}}$$

$$L = \frac{2.5}{0.00025} = 10000\,\text{hours}$$

### Step 3: Convert Hours to Years

Using 1 year = 8760 hours:

$$L = \frac{10000}{8760} \approx 1.14\,\text{years}$$

### Step 4: Account for Self-Discharge

For Alkaline cells, self-discharge is approximately 5% per year. Over the 1.14-year period, the effective capacity is:

$$C_{eff} = C_{pack} \cdot (1 - 0.05 \cdot L_{years})$$

$$C_{eff} = 2500 \cdot (1 - 0.05 \cdot 1.14) = 2500 \cdot 0.943 = 2357.5\,\text{mAh}$$

### Step 5: Recompute Lifetime with Self-Discharge

$$L_{corrected} = \frac{2357.5 \times 10^{-3}}{250 \times 10^{-6}} = 9430\,\text{hours}$$

$$L_{corrected} = \frac{9430}{8760} \approx 1.077\,\text{years}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The node will last approximately **1.08 years (≈ 13 months)** on 2 × AA Alkaline cells. If a 5-year lifetime is required, switch to **Li-SOCl₂** with $C = 3500$ mAh and self-discharge < 1%/year, giving $L \approx 1.6$ years per pair — still not 5 years. Two pairs in parallel would suffice.

## 3.2 Derivation: Energy-Neutral Operation (ENO) Condition with Solar Harvester

**Problem:** A WSN node consumes $P_{avg} = 100$ μW average. The node is powered by a small solar cell of area $A = 4\,\text{cm}^2$ with efficiency $\eta_{pv} = 15\%$. The average daily solar irradiance is $G_{avg} = 200\,\text{W/m}^2$ for $t_{sun} = 8$ hours/day. The power management circuit has conversion efficiency $\eta_{pm} = 80\%$. A Li-Ion buffer battery has $C = 200$ mAh at $V = 3.7$ V. Determine if ENO is satisfied and compute the required buffer size for 24-hour autonomy.

### Step 1: Compute Harvested Energy Per Day

$$P_{harv} = G_{avg} \cdot A \cdot \eta_{pv}$$

Converting area: $A = 4\,\text{cm}^2 = 4 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2$

$$P_{harv} = 200 \cdot 4 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 0.15 = 0.012\,\text{W} = 12\,\text{mW}$$

### Step 2: Compute Daily Harvested Energy After Conversion

$$E_{harv,day} = P_{harv} \cdot t_{sun} \cdot \eta_{pm}$$

$$E_{harv,day} = 0.012 \cdot 8 \cdot 0.80 = 0.0768\,\text{Wh/day} = 76.8\,\text{mWh/day}$$

### Step 3: Compute Daily Energy Consumption

$$E_{cons,day} = P_{avg} \cdot 24\,\text{h}$$

$$E_{cons,day} = 100 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 24 = 2.4 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Wh/day} = 2.4\,\text{mWh/day}$$

### Step 4: Compare — Energy Surplus

$$\Delta E = E_{harv,day} - E_{cons,day} = 76.8 - 2.4 = 74.4\,\text{mWh/day}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** The harvester delivers **32× more energy** than the node consumes. ENO is **easily satisfied** with a massive surplus. The Li-Ion buffer's role is to ride out the **16-hour night** when $P_{harv} = 0$.

### Step 5: Required Buffer Capacity for 16-Hour Autonomy

$$E_{night} = P_{avg} \cdot t_{night} = 100\,\mu\text{W} \cdot 16\,\text{h} = 1.6\,\text{mWh}$$

In mAh at 3.7 V:

$$C_{buf} = \frac{E_{night}}{V_{nom}} = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{3.7} = 0.432\,\text{mAh}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Insight:** The 200 mAh Li-Ion battery is **grossly oversized** for this load. Even with a 10× safety margin and accounting for self-discharge, only ~5 mAh is needed. The remaining 195 mAh is wasted capacity and cost.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Battery Selection Decision for WSN

| Criterion | Li-SOCl₂ Primary | Li-Ion Secondary | Alkaline | Supercapacitor |
|-----------|:----------------:|:----------------:|:--------:|:--------------:|
| **Lifetime Goal** | Excellent (5–10 yr) | Good (3–5 yr with recharge) | Fair (1–2 yr) | Poor (days–weeks) |
| **Energy Density** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★ |
| **Power Density (Burst)** | ★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★★★★ |
| **Temperature Range** | −55 °C to +85 °C | −20 °C to +60 °C | −20 °C to +55 °C | −40 °C to +70 °C |
| **Self-Discharge** | ★★★★★ (very low) | ★★ (moderate) | ★★★ (low) | ★ (high leakage) |
| **Rechargeable** | No | Yes | No | Yes |
| **Cost per Wh** | High | Medium | Very Low | Low |
| **Best Application** | Remote/Harsh | Indoor/Body-worn | Consumer toys | Peak-power buffer |

## 3.4 Energy Budget Verification (Sample WSN Node)

A typical TelosB-class mote:

| Phase | Current | Duration | Energy per Cycle |
|-------|--------:|---------:|-----------------:|
| Sleep | 5 μA | 999 ms | 4.995 μC |
| MCU Active | 1.5 mA | 4 ms | 6.0 μC |
| Sensor Read | 2.0 mA | 2 ms | 4.0 μC |
| Radio TX | 20 mA | 1 ms | 20.0 μC |
| **Total per cycle** | — | 1006 ms | **35.0 μC** |
| **Average current** | $35\,\mu\text{C} / 1.006\,\text{s}$ | — | **34.8 μA** |

At $V = 3$ V: $P_{avg} = 3 \times 34.8\,\mu\text{A} = 104.4\,\mu\text{W}$.

A 2000 mAh, 3.6 V Li-SOCl₂ D-cell would last:

$$L = \frac{2000\,\text{mAh}}{0.0348\,\text{mA}} = 57{,}471\,\text{h} \approx 6.56\,\text{years}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Reducing the radio duty cycle from 1% to 0.1% (smart MAC protocols like B-MAC) extends lifetime by **10×**, demonstrating that **protocol design** matters as much as **battery chemistry**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power Source Classification Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram provides a block-level functional architecture of the **power source taxonomy for WSN** as mandated by the KTU 2024 Module 3 syllabus.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Power Sources for WSN] --> nodeB[Stored Energy]
    nodeA[Power Sources for WSN] --> nodeC[Ambient Energy Harvesters]

    nodeB[Stored Energy] --> nodeD[Primary Batteries]
    nodeB[Stored Energy] --> nodeE[Secondary Batteries]
    nodeB[Stored Energy] --> nodeF[Supercapacitors]

    nodeD[Primary Batteries] --> nodeD1[Alkaline]
    nodeD[Primary Batteries] --> nodeD2[Li-SOCl2 Thionyl Chloride]
    nodeD[Primary Batteries] --> nodeD3[Zinc Air]

    nodeE[Secondary Batteries] --> nodeE1[Li-Ion]
    nodeE[Secondary Batteries] --> nodeE2[LiPo Polymer]
    nodeE[Secondary Batteries] --> nodeE3[NiMH]

    nodeF[Supercapacitors] --> nodeF1[EDLC Electric Double Layer]
    nodeF[Supercapacitors] --> nodeF2[Hybrid Li-Ion Capacitor]

    nodeC[Ambient Energy Harvesters] --> nodeG[Solar Photovoltaic]
    nodeC[Ambient Energy Harvesters] --> nodeH[Mechanical Vibration]
    nodeC[Ambient Energy Harvesters] --> nodeI[Thermal TEG]
    nodeC[Ambient Energy Harvesters] --> nodeJ[RF Electromagnetic]

    nodeG[Solar Photovoltaic] --> nodeG1[Outdoor a-Si c-Si]
    nodeG[Solar Photovoltaic] --> nodeG2[Indoor Low Light DSSC]

    nodeH[Mechanical Vibration] --> nodeH1[Piezoelectric]
    nodeH[Mechanical Vibration] --> nodeH2[Electromagnetic]
    nodeH[Mechanical Vibration] --> nodeH3[Electrostatic]

    nodeI[Thermal TEG] --> nodeI1[Body Heat Wristband]
    nodeI[Thermal TEG] --> nodeI2[Industrial Boiler Pipe]

    nodeJ[RF Electromagnetic] --> nodeJ1[Ambient RF GSM WiFi]
    nodeJ[RF Electromagnetic] --> nodeJ2[Deductive RF RFID NFC]
```

## 4.2 WSN Node Power Management Architecture

The following diagram shows the **functional power management flow** of a typical WSN node with hybrid (battery + harvester) power supply.

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[Ambient Source] --> nodeB[Transducer Harvester]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Rectifier AC to DC]
    nodeC --> nodeD[MPPT and Regulator]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Power Management Unit PMU]

    nodeF[Primary Battery] --> nodeE[Power Management Unit PMU]
    nodeG[Supercapacitor Buffer] --> nodeE[Power Management Unit PMU]

    nodeE[Power Management Unit PMU] --> nodeH[Voltage Regulator LDO or Buck]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Sensor Subsystem]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[MCU and DSP]
    nodeH --> nodeK[Radio Transceiver TX RX]
    nodeH --> nodeL[Actuator Optional]

    nodeE[Power Management Unit PMU] --> nodeM[Charge Controller]
    nodeM --> nodeF[Primary Battery]
    nodeM --> nodeG[Supercapacitor Buffer]
```

## 4.3 Battery Selection Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeStart[Start Battery Selection] --> nodeQ1{Deployment Lifetime Required}

    nodeQ1 --> nodeGT5[More than 5 Years]
    nodeQ1 --> nodeLT5[Less than 5 Years]

    nodeGT5 --> nodeQ2{Rechargeable Access Available}
    nodeQ2 --> nodeNo[No Remote Harsh]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeYes[Yes Indoor]

    nodeNo --> nodeAns1[Use Li-SOCl2 Primary D Cell]
    nodeYes --> nodeAns2[Use Li-Ion with Solar Harvester]

    nodeLT5 --> nodeQ3{Power Burst Requirement}
    nodeQ3 --> nodeHighB[High Burst mA to A]
    nodeQ3 --> nodeLowB[Low Burst Less than 50 mA]

    nodeHighB --> nodeAns3[Use Supercapacitor in Parallel with Li-Ion]
    nodeLowB --> nodeQ4{Form Factor}

    nodeQ4 --> nodeThin[Thin Flexible Wearable]
    nodeQ4 --> nodeStd[Standard Coin Cell or AA]

    nodeThin --> nodeAns4[Use LiPo Pouch Cell]
    nodeStd --> nodeAns5[Use Alkaline AA or Li-SOCl2 AA]
```

## 4.4 Hybrid Energy Storage Topology

A common architecture pairs a **high-energy-density battery** with a **high-power-density supercapacitor** to handle radio bursts:

| Stage | Component | Role |
|-------|-----------|------|
| 1 | Harvester (e.g., solar) | Continuous low-rate energy input |
| 2 | Supercapacitor | Absorbs high-frequency peaks and pulses |
| 3 | Primary/Secondary Battery | Long-term energy reservoir |
| 4 | PMU Switch | Routes current based on load demand |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Hybrid Topology?** Radio TX draws **20–100 mA for milliseconds**, while sleep mode draws **5 μA continuously**. A single Li-Ion cell suffers voltage sag under such pulses; the supercapacitor acts as a **decoupling buffer** that supplies the pulse and is recharged slowly by the battery/harvester.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]

**Q: Compare primary and secondary batteries for WSN applications. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**
Primary batteries are non-rechargeable electrochemical cells with high energy density and very low self-discharge, making them suitable for **long-term, maintenance-free, remote WSN deployments**. Examples: **Alkaline, Lithium-Thionyl Chloride (Li-SOCl₂), Zinc-Air**.

Secondary batteries are rechargeable cells with lower energy density but can be replenished via energy harvesters. They are suitable for **indoor or body-worn IoT nodes**. Examples: **Lithium-Ion (Li-Ion), Lithium-Polymer (LiPo), Nickel-Metal Hydride (NiMH)**.

Primary cells offer **5–10 year** lifetimes, while secondaries require **active energy harvesting** or manual recharging.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]

**Q: Define "Energy Neutral Operation" (ENO) of a WSN node. State the governing equation.**

**Model Answer:**
**Energy Neutral Operation (ENO)** is the design paradigm in which a WSN node harvests **at least as much ambient energy as it consumes** over each operational cycle, making its operational lifetime **theoretically infinite** without battery replacement.

The governing equation is:

$$E_{harvested}(t) \geq E_{consumed}(t) \quad \forall t$$

In power form:

$$P_{harv,avg} \cdot \eta_{conv} \geq P_{node,avg}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** Always mention the role of the **power management unit (PMU)** and the **buffer element** (supercapacitor or rechargeable battery) that bridges energy deficits during low-harvest periods.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]

**Q: A WSN node operates with the following duty cycle:**
- Sleep: $I_{sleep} = 5\,\mu\text{A}$ for $T_{sleep} = 950\,\text{ms}$
- Active (sensing + processing): $I_{act} = 2\,\text{mA}$ for $T_{act} = 8\,\text{ms}$
- Radio TX: $I_{tx} = 20\,\text{mA}$ for $T_{tx} = 2\,\text{ms}$

The node is powered by a 3 V, 2500 mAh battery.

**(a)** Compute the average current draw and the total energy consumed per duty cycle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Estimate the battery lifetime in years and suggest two design changes to extend it by at least 5×. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a): Average Current and Energy per Cycle (7 marks)

**Step 1: Compute the Total Period**

$$T_{cycle} = T_{sleep} + T_{act} + T_{tx} = 950 + 8 + 2 = 960\,\text{ms}$$

> Note: We assume negligible ramp times; this is standard for KTU problems.

**Step 2: Compute the Charge per Cycle**

The charge consumed in each phase is $Q_i = I_i \times T_i$:

$$Q_{sleep} = 5\,\mu\text{A} \times 950\,\text{ms} = 4750\,\text{nC}$$

$$Q_{act} = 2\,\text{mA} \times 8\,\text{ms} = 16{,}000\,\text{nC}$$

$$Q_{tx} = 20\,\text{mA} \times 2\,\text{ms} = 40{,}000\,\text{nC}$$

$$Q_{total} = 4750 + 16{,}000 + 40{,}000 = 60{,}750\,\text{nC} = 60.75\,\mu\text{C}$$

**Step 3: Compute Average Current**

$$I_{avg} = \frac{Q_{total}}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{60.75\,\mu\text{C}}{0.960\,\text{s}} = 63.28\,\mu\text{A}$$

**Step 4: Compute Energy per Cycle**

$$E_{cycle} = V \cdot Q_{total} = 3\,\text{V} \times 60.75\,\mu\text{C} = 182.25\,\mu\text{J}$$

**Step 5: Compute Energy per Hour**

$$\text{Cycles per hour} = \frac{3600}{0.960} = 3750\,\text{cycles/hour}$$

$$E_{hour} = 3750 \times 182.25\,\mu\text{J} = 683{,}437.5\,\mu\text{J} = 0.683\,\text{J/hour}$$

$$\boxed{I_{avg} \approx 63.3\,\mu\text{A}, \quad E_{cycle} \approx 182.3\,\mu\text{J}}$$

---

#### Part (b): Battery Lifetime and Extension Strategies (7 marks)

**Step 1: Apply Battery Lifetime Equation**

$$L = \frac{C_{batt}}{I_{avg}} = \frac{2500\,\text{mAh}}{63.28\,\mu\text{A}} = \frac{2.5}{0.00006328}\,\text{h}$$

$$L = 39{,}507\,\text{hours} \approx 4.51\,\text{years}$$

**Step 2: Account for Self-Discharge**

For a generic Lithium primary, assume 2%/year:

$$C_{eff} = C_{rated} \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$$

At $t = 4.51$ years:

$$C_{eff} = 2500 \cdot e^{-0.02 \cdot 4.51} \approx 2500 \cdot 0.914 = 2285\,\text{mAh}$$

$$L_{corrected} = \frac{2285}{0.00006328} \approx 36{,}112\,\text{h} \approx 4.12\,\text{years}$$

$$\boxed{L \approx 4.1\,\text{years}}$$

**Step 3: Two Design Strategies for 5× Extension (to ~20 years)**

**Strategy 1: Reduce Radio Duty Cycle by 10×**
- Implement **synchronized wake-up receivers (WuR)** or **low-power listening (LPL)** MAC protocols.
- The radio TX time becomes $T_{tx}' = 0.2\,\text{ms}$ instead of 2 ms.
- New $Q_{tx}' = 20\,\text{mA} \times 0.2\,\text{ms} = 4000\,\text{nC}$.
- New $I_{avg}' = (4750 + 16000 + 4000) / 960 \approx 25.78\,\mu\text{A}$.
- New lifetime: $L' = 2500 / 0.00002578 \approx 96{,}974\,\text{h} \approx 11$ years (≈ 2.7× improvement).

**Strategy 2: Add Energy Harvester + Rechargeable Backup**
- Add a small indoor solar cell (1 cm², 5% η) producing ~50 μW under 200 lux office lighting.
- Use an MSP430 + CC2420 radio (typical WSN mote) with a 100 mAh Li-Ion secondary cell.
- Combined effective lifetime: theoretically **unlimited** (ENO), practically >15 years.
- Combined improvement: **>5×** through complementary strategies.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write the radio current directly as $I_{avg}$ without computing the **time-weighted average** across the duty cycle. This leads to a lifetime answer that is **10–1000× too short**. **Always compute $I_{avg}$ explicitly** from $Q_{total}/T_{cycle}$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Sample Style]

**Q:**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Solar Energy Harvesting System for a WSN node**. List the typical power density of outdoor and indoor solar sources. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A WSN node is powered by a Li-Ion cell (3.7 V, 600 mAh) and a 2 cm² amorphous silicon solar cell with conversion efficiency 8%. The node requires an average power of 200 μW continuously.
- (i) Compute the daily energy consumption of the node. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) If the average daily effective sunlight (accounting for cloudy periods) is 4 hours at 300 W/m², compute the daily harvested energy. **(3 marks)**
- (iii) Does the system achieve Energy Neutral Operation? If not, compute the number of days until the battery is fully depleted. **(2 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a): Solar Harvesting System Architecture (7 marks)

**Block Diagram Description:**

The solar energy harvesting system for a WSN node consists of the following stages:

1. **Photovoltaic (PV) Cell**: Converts incident solar irradiance into DC electrical energy. Typical cells are amorphous silicon (a-Si) for indoor, or crystalline silicon (c-Si) for outdoor.
2. **Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT) Circuit**: Continuously adjusts the operating point of the PV cell to extract maximum available power despite varying light intensity and temperature.
3. **Rectifier and Input Filter**: Smooths the DC output and protects against reverse current at night.
4. **DC-DC Converter (Boost or Buck-Boost)**: Steps the variable PV voltage up or down to match the battery/load voltage.
5. **Charge Controller**: Regulates current and voltage flowing into the rechargeable battery to prevent overcharge and over-discharge.
6. **Energy Storage Element**: A rechargeable Li-Ion or LiPo battery stores excess harvested energy for night-time operation.
7. **Supercapacitor (Optional)**: A short-term buffer for handling high-current radio bursts.
8. **Voltage Regulator (LDO or Buck)**: Provides a stable 3.3 V or 1.8 V supply rail to the sensor, MCU, and radio.

**Power Density Values:**

| Source | Power Density |
|--------|--------------:|
| Outdoor Solar (direct sun) | 10–15 mW/cm² |
| Outdoor Solar (cloudy) | 1–3 mW/cm² |
| Indoor Solar (office, 500 lux) | 30–100 μW/cm² |
| Indoor Solar (low light, 100 lux) | 5–20 μW/cm² |

---

#### Part (b): Numerical Analysis (7 marks)

**(i) Daily Energy Consumption (2 marks)**

$$E_{cons,day} = P_{avg} \times 24\,\text{h} = 200\,\mu\text{W} \times 24\,\text{h} = 4.8\,\text{mWh/day}$$

**(ii) Daily Harvested Energy (3 marks)**

First, compute the harvested power:

$$P_{harv} = G \times A \times \eta_{pv}$$

Convert area: $A = 2\,\text{cm}^2 = 2 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2$

$$P_{harv} = 300\,\text{W/m}^2 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{m}^2 \times 0.08 = 0.0048\,\text{W} = 4.8\,\text{mW}$$

Then compute daily energy (assuming 4 effective sun-hours/day):

$$E_{harv,day} = P_{harv} \times t_{sun} = 4.8\,\text{mW} \times 4\,\text{h} = 19.2\,\text{mWh/day}$$

**(iii) ENO Check and Depletion Time (2 marks)**

$$\Delta E = E_{harv,day} - E_{cons,day} = 19.2 - 4.8 = 14.4\,\text{mWh/day}$$

Since $\Delta E > 0$, **Energy Neutral Operation IS achieved** with a surplus of 14.4 mWh/day.

**Energy Stored in Battery:**

$$E_{batt} = V \times C = 3.7\,\text{V} \times 600\,\text{mAh} = 2220\,\text{mWh}$$

**Time for Full Charge (from 0) under surplus:**

$$t_{charge} = \frac{E_{batt}}{\Delta E} = \frac{2220}{14.4} \approx 154.2\,\text{days} \approx 5\,\text{months}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The node's battery will reach full charge in ~154 days. After that, the surplus is curtailed by the MPPT controller (the PV is "throttled" to prevent overcharge). The node operates **perpetually** thereafter.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Trap:** Students often forget to convert the solar cell area from cm² to m². The factor of **10⁴ difference** between these units is the most frequent source of incorrect answers in this type of KTU problem. Always double-check unit conversions.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Power Sources for WSN (Module 3)**

### 1. Core Classification
- **Stored Energy** → Primary, Secondary Batteries, Supercapacitors
- **Harvested Energy** → Solar, Vibration, Thermal, RF

### 2. The "Big Three" Battery Equations
- $L = C/I_{avg}$ (lifetime, hours)
- $E = V \cdot I \cdot t$ (energy, Wh)
- $P_{avg} = \sum P_i T_i / T_{cycle}$ (duty-cycled average power)

### 3. The ENO Condition
- $P_{harv} \cdot \eta_{conv} \geq P_{node,avg}$ — perpetual operation
- Buffer storage (battery or supercapacitor) is **mandatory** to bridge harvest gaps

### 4. Battery Selection Cheat Codes
- **5+ year remote** → **Li-SOCl₂** (highest energy density, lowest self-discharge)
- **Rechargeable, indoor** → **Li-Ion / LiPo + solar harvester**
- **Wearable, thin** → **LiPo pouch cell**
- **Peak power buffer** → **EDLC supercapacitor** (10⁶ cycles, charges in seconds)

### 5. Key Numerical Values to Memorize
- **Li-SOCl₂:** 500–700 Wh/kg, <1% self-discharge/year
- **Alkaline:** 100–150 Wh/kg, ~5% self-discharge/year
- **Li-Ion:** 150–250 Wh/kg, ~2–3% self-discharge/month
- **EDLC:** 5–10 Wh/kg, **not** a primary storage — only a buffer
- **Outdoor solar:** ~10 mW/cm²; **Indoor solar:** ~50 μW/cm²
- **Vibration:** ~1–10 μW/cm³
- **Body-heat TEG:** ~10–50 μW/cm² (per 10 K gradient)

### 6. Design Levers for Lifetime Extension
- **Duty cycle reduction** (MAC protocols): 10–100× improvement
- **Sleep current minimization** (sub-μA MCUs like MSP430)
- **Radio protocol efficiency** (WuR, LPL, preamble sampling)
- **Energy harvesting + ENO design**: theoretically infinite lifetime
- **Hybrid battery-supercapacitor topology**: handles peak currents

### 7. Common KTU Exam Traps
- Confusing **mAh** with **Wh** (must multiply by V)
- Using **peak current** instead of **time-averaged current** in $L = C/I$
- Forgetting to convert **cm² to m²** in solar power calculations
- Forgetting to apply **self-discharge** correction
- Treating supercapacitors as primary energy storage (they are buffers only)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Generation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Generation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Energy Harvesting & Power Management in IoT (PECIT746)**, **Power Generation** refers to the systematic process of converting ambient energy available in the environment into usable electrical energy that can power autonomous, low-power IoT sensor nodes, wearables, and edge devices. Under the KTU 2024 Scheme (Module 3), Power Generation specifically encompasses the study of transducers, energy conversion principles, and the underlying physics governing each harvesting mechanism.

The fundamental principle is governed by the **Energy Conservation Law** where ambient sources (solar radiation, thermal gradients, mechanical vibrations, radio-frequency waves, and airflow) are converted via specific transducers into DC electrical energy. The harvested power is typically in the **microwatt (μW) to milliwatt (mW)** range, which is sufficient to operate duty-cycled IoT transceivers (e.g., BLE, Zigbee, LoRa).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Power Generation):**
> This module typically covers Solar (Photovoltaic) Cells, Thermoelectric Generators (TEG), Piezoelectric Transducers, Electromagnetic Induction harvesters, and RF energy harvesting — along with their equivalent circuit models, governing equations, and practical figures of merit.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Self-Filling Water Bottle"**
> Imagine a smart water bottle that refills itself from the moisture in the air, or a wristwatch that never needs a battery change because it draws energy from your body heat and arm movement. **Power Generation in IoT is the engineering of these "self-filling" mechanisms.** Instead of relying on a wall socket (the grid) or a finite battery (a limited reservoir), the device "scavenges" tiny amounts of energy from its surroundings and continuously trickle-charges its storage element (capacitor or thin-film battery).

Every form of power generation discussed in this module answers a single question:
**"What physical phenomenon converts an environmental stimulus into moving charges (current)?"**

| Energy Source | Transducer | Output Type |
|---------------|------------|-------------|
| Sunlight | Photovoltaic Cell | DC Voltage |
| Temperature Difference | Thermoelectric Generator (Peltier/Seebeck) | DC Voltage |
| Vibration / Pressure | Piezoelectric Crystal | AC → Rectified DC |
| Magnetic Flux Change | Electromagnetic Coil (Faraday's Law) | AC → Rectified DC |
| RF / Wi-Fi Signals | Rectenna (Antenna + Rectifier) | DC Voltage |

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants are **frequently referenced** in Module 3 derivations and must be memorized:

- **Boltzmann Constant (k) = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- **Electron Charge (q) = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- **Vacuum Permittivity (ε₀) = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m**
- **Stephan-Boltzmann Constant (σ) = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/m²K⁴**
- **Seebeck Coefficient Unit: V/K** (typically μV/K for practical materials)
- **Standard Solar Irradiance (AM 1.5) = 1000 W/m²**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Power Generation Methods

For KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation purposes, the five principal power generation mechanisms studied under Module 3 are classified as follows:

### 2.1.1 Photovoltaic (Solar) Power Generation
- **Operating Principle:** Photon absorption excites electron-hole pairs across a semiconductor p-n junction (photovoltaic effect).
- **Why it matters:** Highest power density among harvesters (~15 mW/cm² under direct sunlight, ~0.1–0.5 mW/cm² indoors).
- **How it works:** Photons with energy $E = hf \geq E_g$ (bandgap) liberate carriers, which are separated by the junction's built-in electric field, producing photocurrent $I_{ph}$.

### 2.1.2 Thermoelectric Power Generation
- **Operating Principle:** Direct conversion of temperature gradients into voltage via the **Seebeck Effect**.
- **Why it matters:** Ideal for industrial IoT (boiler surfaces, exhaust pipes) and wearable devices (body heat vs. ambient).
- **How it works:** Charge carriers diffuse from the hot side to the cold side, generating an open-circuit voltage proportional to $\Delta T$.

### 2.1.3 Piezoelectric Power Generation
- **Operating Principle:** Mechanical strain applied to non-centrosymmetric crystals (Quartz, PZT, BaTiO₃, PVDF) produces charge displacement (direct piezoelectric effect).
- **Why it matters:** Dominant choice for vibration-based IoT nodes mounted on machinery, bridges, and human body.
- **How it works:** Applied stress $\sigma$ generates an electric displacement $D = d \cdot \sigma$, where $d$ is the piezoelectric charge coefficient.

### 2.1.4 Electromagnetic Power Generation
- **Operating Principle:** Relative motion between a magnetic field and a conductor induces EMF via **Faraday's Law of Induction**.
- **Why it matters:** Long operational life (no capacitor degradation), robust for large-amplitude/low-frequency vibrations.
- **How it works:** A magnet oscillating through a coil (or coil moving through a fixed field) changes flux linkage, generating voltage $V = -N \frac{d\Phi}{dt}$.

### 2.1.5 RF Energy Harvesting
- **Operating Principle:** Captures ambient electromagnetic radiation (TV, Wi-Fi, GSM, LTE) and rectifies it via a Schottky diode-based rectenna.
- **Why it matters:** Continuous availability indoors; powers RFID and ultra-low-power sensors.
- **How it works:** Antenna captures RF → impedance matching network → RF-to-DC rectifier → DC output.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **examination-critical**. Memorize the formula, the governing law, and the SI unit. KTU examiners frequently test these in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

| # | Formula | Governing Law / Concept | Unit / Standard |
|---|---------|------------------------|------------------|
| 1 | $V_{oc} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Seebeck Effect (Thermoelectric) | Volts |
| 2 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{\alpha^2 \cdot \Delta T^2}{4R}$ | TEG Maximum Power Transfer | Watts |
| 3 | $I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ e^{\dfrac{qV}{kT}} - 1 \right]$ | Photovoltaic Cell I-V Equation | Amperes |
| 4 | $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Fill Factor of Solar Cell | Dimensionless (0 < FF < 1) |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{P_{in}}$ | Solar Cell Conversion Efficiency | Percentage |
| 6 | $V = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ | Faraday's Law (Electromagnetic) | Volts |
| 7 | $D = d \cdot \sigma + \varepsilon^E \cdot E$ | Piezoelectric Constitutive Eqn. | C/m² |
| 8 | $g = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{t}$ | Piezoelectric Voltage Coefficient | V·m/N |
| 9 | $V_{RF} = \sqrt{P_{rx} \cdot R_a}$ | RF Harvested Voltage | Volts |
| 10 | $P_{rx} = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | Friis Transmission Equation | Watts |
| 11 | $E_{photon} = hf = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon Energy | Joules / eV |
| 12 | $P_d = \dfrac{1}{2} m \zeta^2 \omega^3 Y_0^2$ | Vibration Harvester Power Density | W/m³ |

> Where:
> - $\alpha$ = Seebeck coefficient (V/K)
> - $I_{ph}$ = Photocurrent; $I_0$ = Diode saturation current
> - $q$ = Electron charge, $k$ = Boltzmann constant
> - $\Phi$ = Magnetic flux per turn; $N$ = Number of coil turns
> - $d$ = Piezoelectric strain coefficient; $\sigma$ = Mechanical stress
> - $G_t, G_r$ = Transmit/Receive antenna gains
> - $m$ = Mass, $\zeta$ = damping ratio, $\omega$ = angular frequency, $Y_0$ = base acceleration amplitude

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Power generation modules are the **front-end** of every energy-autonomous IoT system. Without a robust generation stage, no power management circuit (boost converter, MPPT, battery management) can function. The choice of generation technology is determined by:

- **Ambient Energy Profile** (continuous vs. intermittent)
- **Form Factor Constraints** (wearable, implantable, industrial)
- **Required Average Power** (μW for BLE, mW for LoRa, W for edge AI)
- **Geographic and Indoor/Outdoor Deployment**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Power Point of a Photovoltaic Cell

### Step 1: Write the Equivalent Circuit Equation
A practical PV cell is modeled as a current source $I_{ph}$ in parallel with a diode (ideality factor $n$), a series resistance $R_s$, and a shunt resistance $R_{sh}$. The terminal current is:

$$
I = I_{ph} - I_D - I_{sh}
$$

where the diode current follows the Shockley equation:

$$
I_D = I_0 \left[ e^{\dfrac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}} - 1 \right]
$$

### Step 2: Apply the Open-Circuit and Short-Circuit Conditions
At **open-circuit** ($I = 0$): The total current is zero, so:

$$
0 = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ e^{\dfrac{q V_{oc}}{nkT}} - 1 \right] - \dfrac{V_{oc}}{R_{sh}}
$$

Assuming $R_{sh} \to \infty$ (ideal cell):

$$
V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln \left( \dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1 \right)
$$

At **short-circuit** ($V = 0$): $I_{sc} = I_{ph}$ (approximately).

### Step 3: Formulate Output Power
Power delivered to an external load $R_L$ is:

$$
P = V \cdot I
$$

### Step 4: Apply Maximum Power Transfer Theorem
The maximum power point is found by setting $\dfrac{dP}{dV} = 0$. Substituting the I-V equation and simplifying yields the transcendental condition:

$$
\dfrac{dI}{dV} = -\dfrac{I}{V} \bigg\vert_{V = V_{mp}}
$$

This condition is satisfied at a unique operating point $(V_{mp}, I_{mp})$ on the I-V curve, which defines the **Fill Factor (FF)**:

$$
FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}
$$

### Step 5: Conversion Efficiency
The overall conversion efficiency of the cell is:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{FF \cdot V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{G \cdot A}
$$

where $G$ is the incident irradiance (W/m²) and $A$ is the active cell area.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** For a typical silicon cell under AM 1.5, $FF \approx 0.7$ to $0.85$. The empirical formula for FF (Green's approximation) often tested is $FF_0 = \dfrac{v_{oc} - \ln(v_{oc} + 0.72)}{v_{oc} + 1}$, where $v_{oc} = \dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Thermoelectric Generator (TEG) Output Voltage and Power

### Step 1: Establish the Seebeck Relationship
A TEG consists of $N$ pairs of p-type and n-type semiconductor legs connected electrically in series and thermally in parallel. The open-circuit voltage is directly proportional to the temperature difference $\Delta T = T_h - T_c$:

$$
V_{oc} = N \cdot \alpha_{pn} \cdot (T_h - T_c)
$$

where $\alpha_{pn} = \alpha_p - \alpha_n$ is the **net Seebeck coefficient** of the couple (typically 100–250 μV/K for Bi₂Te₃).

### Step 2: Model Internal Electrical Resistance
Each leg has an electrical resistance $R = \dfrac{\rho L}{A}$, where $\rho$ is electrical resistivity, $L$ is leg length, and $A$ is cross-sectional area. For a thermocouple, internal resistance is:

$$
R_{int} = \dfrac{\rho_p L_p}{A_p} + \dfrac{\rho_n L_n}{A_n}
$$

### Step 3: Apply Thevenin's Equivalent
The TEG acts as a voltage source $V_{oc}$ in series with $R_{int}$. When connected to a load $R_L$, the current is:

$$
I = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{R_{int} + R_L}
$$

### Step 4: Power Delivered to the Load
The instantaneous power at the load is:

$$
P_L = I^2 R_L = \dfrac{V_{oc}^2 \cdot R_L}{(R_{int} + R_L)^2}
$$

### Step 5: Maximize Power Transfer
Differentiate $P_L$ with respect to $R_L$ and set $\dfrac{dP_L}{dR_L} = 0$:

$$
\dfrac{dP_L}{dR_L} = \dfrac{V_{oc}^2 (R_{int} + R_L)^2 - V_{oc}^2 R_L \cdot 2(R_{int} + R_L)}{(R_{int} + R_L)^4} = 0
$$

Simplifying the numerator:

$$
V_{oc}^2 (R_{int} + R_L) \left[ (R_{int} + R_L) - 2R_L \right] = 0
$$

This yields the classic result:

$$
R_L = R_{int}
$$

### Step 6: Maximum Power Expression
Substituting $R_L = R_{int}$ back:

$$
P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_{int}} = \dfrac{N^2 \alpha_{pn}^2 (T_h - T_c)^2}{4 R_{int}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common Part B question is: *"A TEG with $\alpha_{pn} = 200 \text{ μV/K}$ and 50 couples is subjected to $\Delta T = 40$ K. If $R_{int} = 5 \text{ Ω}$, find the maximum power delivered to a matched load."*
> Solution: $V_{oc} = 50 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 40 = 0.4$ V, then $P_{max} = 0.4^2 / (4 \times 5) = 8$ mW.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Electromagnetic Harvester Voltage (Faraday's Law)

### Step 1: Define Magnetic Flux
For a coil of $N$ turns enclosing a magnetic flux $\Phi$ through one turn:

$$
\Phi = \vec{B} \cdot \vec{A} = B A \cos \theta
$$

### Step 2: Apply Faraday's Law
The induced EMF in the coil is the time derivative of the total flux linkage $\lambda = N\Phi$:

$$
V_{emf} = -\dfrac{d\lambda}{dt} = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}
$$

The negative sign follows Lenz's law (opposes the change in flux).

### Step 3: Consider a Sinusoidal Motion
For a magnet oscillating with displacement $x(t) = X_0 \sin(\omega t)$ near a coil with field gradient $\dfrac{dB}{dx}$, the flux varies as:

$$
\Phi(t) = B(x) \cdot A \quad \Rightarrow \quad \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt} = A \cdot \dfrac{dB}{dx} \cdot \dfrac{dx}{dt}
$$

Substituting $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = X_0 \omega \cos(\omega t)$:

$$
V_{emf}(t) = -N A \dfrac{dB}{dx} \cdot X_0 \omega \cos(\omega t)
$$

### Step 4: Peak Voltage
The peak (amplitude) of the induced voltage is:

$$
V_{peak} = N A \left\vert \dfrac{dB}{dx} \right\vert X_0 \omega
$$

### Step 5: RMS Voltage and Power
For a sinusoidal waveform, $V_{rms} = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{\sqrt{2}}$. Power delivered to load $R_L$:

$$
P = \dfrac{V_{rms}^2}{R_L + R_{int}} = \dfrac{N^2 A^2 \left( \dfrac{dB}{dx} \right)^2 X_0^2 \omega^2}{2 (R_L + R_{int})}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Insight:** Power scales with the **square of frequency**. This is why electromagnetic harvesters excel at high-frequency vibration sources (motors, turbines) but underperform at low-frequency human motion (< 5 Hz) — a domain where piezoelectric harvesters dominate.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Piezoelectric Voltage Generation

### Step 1: The Direct Piezoelectric Effect
When mechanical stress $\sigma$ (N/m²) is applied to a piezoelectric material, an electric displacement $D$ (C/m²) is produced:

$$
D = d \cdot \sigma
$$

where $d$ (C/N) is the **piezoelectric charge coefficient**.

### Step 2: Open-Circuit Voltage
For a piezoelectric patch of thickness $t$, the open-circuit voltage is:

$$
V_{oc} = g \cdot \sigma \cdot t
$$

where $g$ (V·m/N) is the **piezoelectric voltage coefficient**, related to $d$ by $g = d / (\varepsilon^T)$ and $\varepsilon^T$ is the permittivity at constant stress.

### Step 3: Equivalent Circuit Model
A piezoelectric harvester is modeled as a current source $I_p$ in parallel with the clamped capacitance $C_p$, with the mechanical domain represented by mass-spring-damper.

### Step 4: Power at Resonance
At mechanical resonance $\omega = \omega_n$, the harvested power reaches its maximum:

$$
P_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2} \zeta \omega_n m Y_0^2
$$

where $\zeta$ is the damping ratio introduced by the piezoelectric coupling, $m$ is the seismic mass, and $Y_0$ is the base acceleration amplitude.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Power Generation Block Diagram — Full IoT Energy Harvesting Chain

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the entire power generation pipeline, from ambient energy source to regulated DC output suitable for IoT electronics.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Ambient Sunlight] --> B1[Photovoltaic Cell Array]
    A2[Thermal Gradient] --> B2[Thermoelectric Generator TEG]
    A3[Mechanical Vibration] --> B3[Piezoelectric Transducer]
    A4[Kinetic Motion] --> B4[Electromagnetic Coil and Magnet]
    A5[Ambient RF Signals] --> B5[Rectenna Antenna plus Diode]

    B1 --> C1[DC Output V1]
    B2 --> C2[DC Output V2]
    B3 --> C6[Bridge Rectifier AC to DC]
    B4 --> C6
    C6 --> C3[DC Output V3]
    B5 --> C5[Impedance Matching Network]
    C5 --> C4[DC Output V4]

    C1 --> D1[MPPT and DC DC Boost Converter]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Energy Storage Supercap or Li Ion]
    E1 --> F1[Voltage Regulator LDO or Buck]
    F1 --> G1[IoT Sensor and MCU Load]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The five parallel branches on the left represent the five power generation methods. After rectification and conditioning, all sources feed a common **Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT)** stage, which dynamically extracts peak power before charging the storage element.

## 4.2 Piezoelectric Harvester — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M1[Mechanical Vibration Source] --> M2[Seismic Mass m]
    M2 --> M3[Piezoelectric Cantilever Beam]
    M3 --> M4{AC Output Voltage}
    M4 --> M5[Full Wave Bridge Rectifier]
    M5 --> M6[Smoothing Capacitor C filter]
    M6 --> M7[Regulated DC Output Vdc]
    M7 --> M8[IoT Sensor Node]
    M7 -.feedback.-> M5
```

## 4.3 Thermoelectric Generator (TEG) — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T1[Heat Source T hot] --> T2[Hot Side Ceramic Plate]
    T2 --> T3[P type and N type Semiconductor Legs]
    T3 --> T4[Cold Side Ceramic Plate]
    T4 --> T5[Heat Sink Ambient T cold]
    T3 --> T6[Series Electrical Interconnects]
    T6 --> T7[Output Terminals V oc equals N alpha delta T]
    T7 --> T8[Matched Load Resistor R load]
```

## 4.4 Energy Flow Comparison Matrix

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph Sources
        S1[Solar PV]
        S2[Thermoelectric]
        S3[Piezo]
        S4[Electromagnetic]
        S5[RF Harvesting]
    end
    subgraph Conversion
        K1[Photovoltaic Effect]
        K2[Seebeck Effect]
        K3[Direct Piezoelectric Effect]
        K4[Faraday Induction]
        K5[Rectification plus Rectenna]
    end
    S1 --> K1
    S2 --> K2
    S3 --> K3
    S4 --> K4
    S5 --> K5
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Seebeck Effect. Mention its significance in IoT power generation. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Similar Pattern]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Seebeck Effect** is the direct conversion of a temperature difference into an electric voltage at the junction of two dissimilar conductors. The generated EMF is given by:

$$
V = \alpha \cdot \Delta T
$$

where $\alpha$ is the Seebeck coefficient (V/K) and $\Delta T$ is the temperature gradient across the junction.

**Significance in IoT:**
- Enables battery-less operation of wireless sensor nodes in industrial environments (boilers, pipelines, exhaust systems).
- Provides continuous, maintenance-free power from waste heat.
- Solid-state construction ensures long operational life (>10 years) with no moving parts.

---

### Question 2: What is a rectenna? List its two main functional blocks. `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Similar Pattern]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **rectenna** (rectifying antenna) is a specialized antenna system used in RF energy harvesting that converts electromagnetic waves into usable DC electricity. Its two main functional blocks are:

1. **Receiving Antenna:** Captures ambient RF signals (Wi-Fi, GSM, TV bands) and converts electromagnetic waves into a high-frequency AC voltage.
2. **Rectifier Circuit:** Typically a Schottky diode-based full-wave rectifier (or voltage multiplier) followed by a smoothing capacitor, which converts the AC signal into a stable DC output voltage.

**Key advantage:** Enables powering of RFID tags and ultra-low-power IoT devices in environments where other ambient sources are absent.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks)

**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Module 3, Question Pattern]`**

**Question A(a):** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a solar photovoltaic (PV) energy harvesting system for IoT applications. Derive the expression for the open-circuit voltage of a PV cell. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Working Principle (4 marks):**
A solar PV system converts incident sunlight directly into electricity using semiconductor p-n junctions. The process involves:

1. **Photon Absorption:** Photons with energy $E = hf \geq E_g$ strike the semiconductor surface, generating electron-hole pairs in the depletion region.
2. **Charge Separation:** The built-in electric field at the junction sweeps electrons toward the n-side and holes toward the p-side, producing a photocurrent $I_{ph}$.
3. **External Load Connection:** The generated DC current flows through an external load, with the cell behaving as a constant current source in parallel with a forward-biased diode.

The block diagram for an IoT solar harvesting system is:
**PV Cell → MPPT Controller → DC-DC Boost Converter → Battery/Supercapacitor → Voltage Regulator → IoT Load**

**Derivation of Open-Circuit Voltage (3 marks):**

The PV cell current equation is:

$$
I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ e^{\dfrac{qV}{nkT}} - 1 \right]
$$

At open circuit, $I = 0$, so:

$$
I_{ph} = I_0 \left[ e^{\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}} - 1 \right]
$$

Rearranging:

$$
e^{\dfrac{qV_{oc}}{nkT}} = \dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1
$$

Taking natural logarithm:

$$
V_{oc} = \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln \left( \dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} + 1 \right)
$$

Since $I_{ph} \gg I_0$, this is often approximated as $V_{oc} \approx \dfrac{nkT}{q} \ln \left( \dfrac{I_{ph}}{I_0} \right)$.

---

**Question A(b):** A thermoelectric generator has 100 p-n couples, each with a Seebeck coefficient of 220 μV/K. The hot side is maintained at 200°C and the cold side at 30°C. If the internal resistance of the TEG is 8 Ω, calculate: (i) the open-circuit voltage, (ii) the maximum power delivered to a matched load, and (iii) the load current at maximum power. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Given Data:**
- Number of couples, $N = 100$
- Seebeck coefficient, $\alpha = 220 \times 10^{-6}$ V/K
- $T_h = 200°C = 473$ K, $T_c = 30°C = 303$ K
- Internal resistance, $R_{int} = 8$ Ω

**Step 1: Calculate temperature difference**

$$
\Delta T = T_h - T_c = 473 - 303 = 170 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2: Open-circuit voltage**

$$
V_{oc} = N \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 100 \times 220 \times 10^{-6} \times 170
$$

$$
V_{oc} = 3.74 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3: Maximum power (at $R_L = R_{int}$)**

$$
P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_{int}} = \dfrac{(3.74)^2}{4 \times 8} = \dfrac{13.9876}{32}
$$

$$
P_{max} = 0.437 \text{ W} \approx 437 \text{ mW}
$$

**Step 4: Load current at maximum power**

$$
I_{max} = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{2 R_{int}} = \dfrac{3.74}{2 \times 8} = \dfrac{3.74}{16}
$$

$$
I_{max} = 0.234 \text{ A} = 234 \text{ mA}
$$

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**`[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Module 3, Question Pattern]`**

**Question B(a):** Explain the principle of operation of a piezoelectric energy harvester. Derive the expression for the open-circuit voltage generated by a piezoelectric crystal under applied stress. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Principle of Operation (4 marks):**
A piezoelectric energy harvester converts ambient mechanical vibration into electrical energy using the **direct piezoelectric effect** discovered by Jacques and Pierre Curie. The key components are:

1. A **piezoelectric material** (PZT, BaTiO₃, or PVDF polymer) shaped as a cantilever beam.
2. A **seismic mass** attached to the free end of the beam to amplify strain.
3. When external vibration matches the beam's natural frequency, the beam oscillates, subjecting the piezo layer to alternating tensile and compressive stress.
4. This stress generates alternating charge on the electrode surfaces, producing an AC voltage that is rectified and stored.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

The direct piezoelectric effect is described by:

$$
D = d \cdot \sigma
$$

where $D$ is electric displacement (C/m²), $d$ is the piezoelectric charge coefficient (C/N), and $\sigma$ is the applied stress (N/m²).

For a crystal of thickness $t$ with electrodes on opposite faces, the electric field is:

$$
E = \dfrac{V_{oc}}{t} = \dfrac{g \cdot \sigma}{1}
$$

Therefore, the open-circuit voltage is:

$$
V_{oc} = g \cdot \sigma \cdot t
$$

where $g = d / \varepsilon^T$ is the **piezoelectric voltage coefficient** (V·m/N).

---

**Question B(b):** Compare electromagnetic and piezoelectric vibration energy harvesters in terms of operating principle, output voltage, frequency range, and typical applications. Mention the figure of merit used for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| Comparison Parameter | Electromagnetic Harvester | Piezoelectric Harvester |
|----------------------|--------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Operating Principle** | Faraday's Law of Induction (magnetic flux change) | Direct Piezoelectric Effect (strain-induced charge) |
| **Output Voltage** | Low (typically 0.1–1 V) | High (typically 1–10 V) |
| **Output Type** | AC (requires rectification) | AC (requires rectification) |
| **Frequency Range** | Low to moderate (1–200 Hz) | Moderate to high (50–1000 Hz) |
| **Power Density** | 0.1–1 mW/cm³ | 0.5–10 mW/cm³ |
| **Lifetime** | Very long (no fatigue) | Limited (material fatigue, ~10⁷ cycles) |
| **Figure of Merit** | Coupling coefficient $k = N \cdot \dfrac{d\Phi}{dx}$ | Coupling coefficient $k_{31}^2$ or $k_{33}^2$ |
| **Typical Applications** | Large-amplitude slow motion, wind turbines, human motion | Machine vibrations, structural health monitoring |
| **Magnet Required** | Yes (NdFeB rare-earth) | No |

**Conclusion (for full 7 marks):**
Electromagnetic harvesters are preferable for low-frequency, high-amplitude, long-life applications, while piezoelectric harvesters dominate in compact, high-frequency, high-power-density IoT scenarios.

---

## 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Module 3 Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion in TEG Problems:** Students frequently forget to convert Celsius to Kelvin when computing $\Delta T$. KTU evaluators specifically check for this — using $\Delta T = 170°C$ instead of $\Delta T = 170$ K (numerically equal but technically wrong; the formula is derived for absolute temperature).
> 2. **Missing the Factor of N in TEG Voltage:** The Seebeck voltage is $V = N \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$, NOT $V = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$. Forgetting the number of couples loses 2 marks immediately.
> 3. **Piezo vs. Electromagnetic AC Output:** Both produce AC, but students often write "piezoelectric produces DC" — this is a fundamental conceptual error worth 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Rectenna Confusion:** A rectenna is NOT just a rectifier; it is antenna + rectifier. Naming only the diode circuit loses 1 mark.
> 5. **FF (Fill Factor) Definition:** Students sometimes write $FF = \dfrac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}$ (inverted). Memorize the correct form: $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$.
> 6. **Faraday's Law Sign:** The negative sign in $V = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ represents Lenz's law. Dropping it loses ½ mark in derivations.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module 3 — Power Generation: 60-Second Revision**

- ✅ **Five Power Generation Methods** to memorize: **PV, TEG, Piezo, Electromagnetic, RF (Rectenna)**
- ✅ **Seebeck Voltage:** $V_{oc} = N \alpha \Delta T$ — remember the factor of **N** (number of couples)
- ✅ **Maximum TEG Power:** $P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_{int}}$ — occurs at **impedance matching** ($R_L = R_{int}$)
- ✅ **PV I-V Equation:** $I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left[ e^{\dfrac{qV}{nkT}} - 1 \right]$ — shockley diode equation with photocurrent
- ✅ **Fill Factor (FF):** Always between **0.7 and 0.85** for a good silicon cell; defined as $FF = \dfrac{V_{mp} I_{mp}}{V_{oc} I_{sc}}$
- ✅ **Faraday's Law:** $V = -N \dfrac{d\Phi}{dt}$ — power scales as $\omega^2$ (frequency squared)
- ✅ **Piezoelectric Voltage:** $V_{oc} = g \sigma t$ — where $g$ is voltage coefficient, $t$ is thickness
- ✅ **Rectenna = Antenna + Schottky Rectifier** — used for RF energy harvesting
- ✅ **Photon Energy Condition:** $E = hf \geq E_g$ — photons with energy below the bandgap do NOT generate carriers
- ✅ **KTU-Favorite Constants:** $k = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K; $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C; AM 1.5 = 1000 W/m²
- ✅ **Power Density Ranking (highest to lowest):** Solar > Piezo > Electromagnetic > TEG > RF
- ✅ **Selection Rule:** High frequency vibration → Piezo; Low frequency large motion → Electromagnetic; Continuous heat source → TEG; Outdoor sunlight → PV; Indoor RF only → Rectenna
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conversion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Conversion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 3 — Conversion Mechanisms in Energy Harvesting

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> In the context of energy harvesting for IoT, **Conversion** refers to the physical transduction process by which ambient (waste) energy available in the surrounding environment — such as mechanical vibration, thermal gradients, solar radiation, or ambient RF signals — is transformed into a usable form of electrical energy (voltage/current) that can be rectified, conditioned, regulated, and stored to power autonomous low-power IoT sensor nodes.

The conversion stage sits at the **front-end of every energy harvesting system**, immediately after the energy source is identified. It is the *first functional block* in the canonical energy harvesting signal chain:

$$\text{Ambient Source} \;\longrightarrow\; \boxed{\text{Conversion / Transducer}} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Rectification} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Regulation} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Storage} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Load (IoT Node)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Universal Charger:**
> Think of a Swiss Army knife with four different charging heads: solar panel (light→electric), hand-crank generator (mechanical→electric via Faraday's law), thermocouple on a stove (heat→electric via Seebeck effect), and a radio antenna + rectifier (RF→electric). The **Conversion** block in an energy harvester is exactly that "smart charging head" — it picks up whatever free energy nature offers and converts it into the universal "language" the IoT node understands: *electricity*. No matter the source — vibration, heat, light, or radio waves — the job of the transducer is the same: **harvested physical domain energy → regulated electrical energy**.

The standard physical metrics used to grade any conversion mechanism are:

- **Power Density ($P_d$)** — $\mu W/cm^2$ or $mW/cm^3$
- **Conversion Efficiency ($\eta$)** — dimensionless ratio of output electrical power to input ambient power
- **Open-Circuit Voltage ($V_{oc}$)** — Volts
- **Internal (Source) Impedance ($Z_s$)** — Ohms ($\Omega$)
- **Bandwidth ($f_{bw}$)** — Hz
- **Form Factor / Scalability** — miniaturization metric

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:**
> KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to compare conversion techniques on the basis of *output voltage level, source impedance, and harvested power density*, because these three parameters dictate the choice of the *downstream* power management circuit (rectifier, booster, MPPT stage).

---

## 2. Classification of Conversion Mechanisms

The conversion block can be classified along the **type of ambient energy domain** it harvests. The four principal families covered in Module 3 are:

| # | Domain | Conversion Mechanism | Governing Physical Principle |
|---|--------|----------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | **Mechanical (Vibration / Strain)** | **Piezoelectric** | Direct piezoelectric effect ($d_{33}$, $d_{31}$ coefficients) |
| 2 | **Mechanical (Vibration / Motion)** | **Electromagnetic** | Faraday's Law of Induction ($\varepsilon = -d\Phi/dt$) |
| 3 | **Mechanical (Vibration / Pressure)** | **Electrostatic** | Variable-capacitance charge pump ($W = \tfrac{1}{2}CV^2$) |
| 4 | **Thermal (Heat / Temperature Gradients)** | **Thermoelectric** | Seebeck Effect ($V = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$) |
| 5 | **Light (Solar / Indoor Photons)** | **Photovoltaic** | Photovoltaic effect at p–n junction |
| 6 | **RF / EM Waves (Ambient Wi-Fi, GSM)** | **Rectenna** | Schottky diode rectification of induced RF voltage |

> [!NOTE]
> The first three are the **transduction triplet** for mechanical vibration energy harvesters. The remaining three cover thermal, optical, and electromagnetic radiation harvesting — together, these six mechanisms form the *complete conversion toolbox* for IoT power autonomy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Piezoelectric Conversion

A piezoelectric material (e.g., PZT, BaTiO₃, PVDF) generates an internal charge separation when subjected to mechanical stress. The constitutive equations (in Voigt notation, IEEE 1987 standard) are:

$$D_i = d_{ik}\,T_k + \varepsilon^{T}_{ij}\,E_j \quad \text{(Direct piezoelectric effect — sensor mode)}$$

$$S_j = s^{E}_{jk}\,T_k + d_{ik}\,E_i \quad \text{(Converse piezoelectric effect — actuator mode)}$$

where:
- $D_i$ = electric displacement (C/m²)
- $T_k$ = mechanical stress (N/m²)
- $E_j$ = electric field (V/m)
- $d_{ik}$ = piezoelectric charge coefficient (C/N or m/V)
- $\varepsilon^{T}_{ij}$ = permittivity at constant stress
- $S_j$ = mechanical strain
- $s^{E}_{jk}$ = elastic compliance at constant electric field

**Why it works in IoT:** PZT patches bonded to a cantilever beam generate AC voltage in response to ambient vibrations (e.g., HVAC ducts, machinery, human motion). The beam resonates at a specific natural frequency $f_n$, maximizing harvested power when the ambient vibration frequency matches $f_n$.

**How to design it:** Tune the cantilever's natural frequency:

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m_{eff}}} = \frac{\lambda_n^2}{2\pi L^2}\sqrt{\frac{EI}{\rho A}}$$

where $k$ is the effective stiffness, $m_{eff}$ is the effective mass, $EI$ is flexural rigidity, $\rho A$ is mass per unit length, $L$ is beam length, and $\lambda_n$ is the eigenvalue (1.875 for first mode of cantilever).

**Harvested Power (single-degree-of-freedom, sinusoidal base excitation):**

$$P_{harv} = \frac{1}{2}\,\zeta_e\,\omega_n\,m_{eff}\,Y^2 \cdot \frac{\Omega^2}{\left(1-\Omega^2\right)^2 + \left(2\zeta\Omega\right)^2}$$

where $\Omega = \omega/\omega_n$, $Y$ is base displacement amplitude, $\zeta$ is total damping ratio, $\zeta_e$ is the *electrical* damping ratio (the only portion that does electrical work).

## 2.2 Electromagnetic Conversion

A coil moving through a magnetic field (or a magnet moving through a coil) produces an EMF by Faraday's Law:

$$\varepsilon(t) = -N\frac{d\Phi_B}{dt} = -N\frac{d}{dt}\!\int_{S} \mathbf{B}\cdot d\mathbf{A}$$

For a coil of $N$ turns, length $\ell$, moving with velocity $v$ through a uniform field $B$:

$$\varepsilon = -N\,B\,\ell\,v$$

For sinusoidal motion $x(t) = X_0 \sin(\omega t)$, the induced EMF is:

$$\varepsilon(t) = -N\,B\,\ell\,\omega\,X_0 \cos(\omega t)$$

Peak harvested power into matched load $R_L$:

$$P_{max} = \frac{(N B \ell \,\omega X_0)^2}{8 R_L}$$

**Why it works in IoT:** Rotational micro-generators in wearable devices, vibration harvesters using moving magnets, and kinetic watch-style harvesters all exploit Faraday's law. The *big advantage*: no need for active material — permanent magnets and copper coils scale beautifully at the macro scale.

**Limitation:** Poor scaling at MEMS scale because induced voltage $V \propto B\,\ell\,v$ collapses as device size shrinks (inductance and magnetic flux fall).

## 2.3 Electrostatic (Capacitive) Conversion

A variable-capacitance structure with pre-charge $Q$ does work as the capacitance changes. The energy stored in a charged capacitor:

$$W = \frac{Q^2}{2C} = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$$

As the capacitance varies from $C_{min}$ to $C_{max}$:

$$W_{out} = \frac{1}{2}Q^2\!\left(\frac{1}{C_{min}} - \frac{1}{C_{max}}\right) = \frac{1}{2}C_{max}V_{max}^2 - \frac{1}{2}C_{min}V_{min}^2$$

Two operating modes exist:

- **Charge-constrained cycle:** $Q$ = constant → $V = Q/C$ changes with $C$
- **Voltage-constrained cycle:** $V$ = constant → $Q = CV$ changes with $C$

> [!NOTE]
> Charge-constrained mode is more common in vibration harvesters because the pre-charge can be conveniently stored on the variable capacitor itself or supplied by an electret (a quasi-permanent charged dielectric — e.g., Teflon, CYTOP).

## 2.4 Thermoelectric Conversion (Seebeck Effect)

A temperature gradient $\Delta T$ across a thermoelectric (TE) material of Seebeck coefficient $\alpha$ produces an open-circuit voltage:

$$V_{oc} = \alpha\,\Delta T$$

For a thermocouple made of *n* and *p* legs:

$$V_{oc} = (\alpha_p - \alpha_n)\,\Delta T = \alpha_{pn}\,\Delta T$$

Harvested power at matched load ($R_L = R_{TE}$):

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{oc}^2}{4R_{TE}} = \frac{(\alpha_{pn}\Delta T)^2}{4R_{TE}}$$

The figure of merit governing TE efficiency:

$$ZT = \frac{\alpha^2 \sigma T}{\kappa} = \frac{\alpha^2 T}{\rho \kappa}$$

where $\sigma$ is electrical conductivity, $\kappa$ is thermal conductivity, $\rho$ is resistivity.

**Carnot-limited conversion efficiency:**

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{\Delta T}{T_h}\cdot\frac{\sqrt{1+ZT_{avg}} - 1}{\sqrt{1+ZT_{avg}} + T_c/T_h}$$

## 2.5 Photovoltaic Conversion

A p–n junction illuminated by photons of energy $h\nu \geq E_g$ generates electron–hole pairs separated by the built-in field, producing photocurrent. The Shockley diode equation with illumination:

$$I = I_L - I_0\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{qV}{nkT}\right) - 1\right]$$

where:
- $I_L$ = light-generated current (≈ $I_{sc}$)
- $I_0$ = diode reverse saturation current
- $n$ = ideality factor (1 ≤ n ≤ 2)
- $q = 1.602\times10^{-19}$ C
- $k = 1.381\times10^{-23}$ J/K (Boltzmann constant)

Maximum Power Point (MPP): the operating point where the product $P = IV$ is maximized. Solving $dP/dV = 0$ gives the implicit MPP condition:

$$V_{MPP} = V_{oc} - \frac{nkT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{V_{MPP}}{nkT/q} + 1\right)$$

Fill Factor:

$$FF = \frac{V_{MPP}\,I_{MPP}}{V_{oc}\,I_{sc}}$$

Conversion efficiency:

$$\eta_{PV} = \frac{P_{max}}{P_{in}} = \frac{FF\cdot V_{oc}\cdot I_{sc}}{P_{in}}$$

## 2.6 RF-to-DC Conversion (Rectenna)

An antenna intercepts ambient RF power density $S$ (W/m²), inducing an open-circuit RMS voltage at its terminals. The received power available from a perfectly matched antenna of effective aperture $A_e$ is:

$$P_{r} = S \cdot A_e = S\cdot\frac{G\,\lambda^2}{4\pi}$$

A Schottky diode (low forward voltage, fast switching) rectifies this AC RF voltage into DC, followed by a low-pass filter. The RF-to-DC conversion efficiency:

$$\eta_{RF-DC} = \frac{P_{DC}}{P_{RF,in}} = f(V_{oc},\,P_{in},\,R_s,\,R_j)$$

where $R_s$ is diode series resistance and $R_j$ is junction resistance.

---

## 📋 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Mechanism | Key Equation | Critical Parameter | Typical Power Density |
|---|-----------|--------------|--------------------|----------------------|
| 1 | Piezoelectric | $V_{oc} = g_{33}\,T\,\ell = d_{33}\,Y/\varepsilon$ | $d_{33}$, $g_{33}$ | 200 $\mu$W/cm³ (PZT) |
| 2 | Electromagnetic | $\varepsilon = -N\,B\,\ell\,v$ | $N$, $B$, $\ell$ | 1–10 mW/cm³ (macro) |
| 3 | Electrostatic | $W = \tfrac{1}{2}Q^2(1/C_{min} - 1/C_{max})$ | $C_{max}/C_{min}$ ratio | 0.1–1 mW/cm³ |
| 4 | Thermoelectric | $V_{oc} = \alpha_{pn}\,\Delta T$ | $ZT$, $\alpha$ | 10–100 $\mu$W/cm² (human body) |
| 5 | Photovoltaic | $I = I_L - I_0[\exp(qV/nkT) - 1]$ | $FF$, $V_{oc}$, $I_{sc}$ | 10–20 mW/cm² (outdoor), 100 $\mu$W/cm² (indoor) |
| 6 | RF Rectenna | $P_r = S\cdot G\lambda^2/(4\pi)$ | $G$, $\lambda$, $S$ | 0.1–10 $\mu$W/cm² (ambient) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality Check:**
> The **source impedance** of each mechanism differs by orders of magnitude — piezoelectric: 10s of kΩ to MΩ (high voltage, low current); electromagnetic: a few Ω to 100s of Ω (low voltage, high current); photovoltaic: nonlinear I–V; thermoelectric: low Ω to 10s of Ω. This dictates whether the next stage needs a **step-up transformer**, a **voltage multiplier**, or a **boost converter** — a *must-know* for the power management module.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Harvested Power of a Piezoelectric Cantilever Under Base Excitation

We derive the standard closed-form expression for power extracted by a piezoelectric cantilever beam under harmonic base acceleration $a(t) = A\cos(\omega t)$. This is the most frequently tested derivation in KTU ESE for Module 3.

**Step 1 — Define the Single-Degree-of-Freedom (SDOF) model.**
Let the cantilever tip mass $m$ undergo displacement $z(t)$ relative to a base that moves by $y(t)$. Define the relative displacement $x(t) = z(t) - y(t)$. Newton's second law yields:

$$m\ddot{z} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m\ddot{y}(t)$$

**Step 2 — Total damping $c$ is the sum of three physical loss mechanisms.**

$$c = c_m + c_e + c_a$$

where $c_m$ = mechanical (structural) damping, $c_a$ = aero/acoustic damping, $c_e$ = electrical damping from the piezoelectric coupling. Define total damping ratio $\zeta = c/(2\sqrt{km})$ and electrical damping ratio $\zeta_e = c_e/(2\sqrt{km})$.

**Step 3 — Sinusoidal forcing.** With $\ddot{y}(t) = -A\omega\sin(\omega t)$ and steady-state harmonic assumption $x(t) = X\cos(\omega t - \phi)$:

$$X = \frac{mA\omega^2}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}} = \frac{A\,\Omega^2}{\sqrt{(1-\Omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\Omega)^2}}$$

where $\Omega = \omega/\omega_n$ and $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$. The phase shift is $\phi = \tan^{-1}\!\left[\frac{2\zeta\Omega}{1-\Omega^2}\right]$.
