**Step 4 — Time-averaged power dissipated in the electrical branch.** Only the electrical damping branch converts mechanical energy into electrical energy:

$$\langle P_e \rangle = \frac{1}{2}\,c_e\,\omega^2\,X^2 = \frac{1}{2}\,\zeta_e\,(2\sqrt{km})\,\omega^2 X^2$$

Substituting $X^2$ from Step 3:

$$\langle P_e \rangle = \frac{1}{2}\,\zeta_e\,(2m\omega_n)\,(\omega X)^2 = \zeta_e\,m\omega_n\omega^2 X^2$$

**Step 5 — Replace $X$:**

$$\langle P_e \rangle = \zeta_e\,m\omega_n\,\omega^2\cdot\frac{A^2\Omega^4}{(1-\Omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\Omega)^2}$$

Using $\omega^2\Omega^4 = \omega_n^2\Omega^2$:

$$\boxed{\;\langle P_e \rangle = \zeta_e\,m\,A^2\,\omega_n^3\cdot\frac{\Omega^2}{(1-\Omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\Omega)^2}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Power is maximized at $\Omega \approx 1$ (resonance) and grows as $\omega_n^3$ — *high-frequency vibration sources (kHz range, e.g., machinery) are far more lucrative than low-frequency ones (e.g., human motion at ~1 Hz)*. This is why KTU emphasizes matching $\omega_n$ to the dominant frequency of the ambient vibration spectrum.

## 3.2 Derivation: Maximum Power Transfer from an Electromagnetic Harvester

For a coil vibrating in a uniform field $B$ with sinusoidal velocity $v(t) = V_0\cos(\omega t)$, the induced EMF is:

$$\varepsilon(t) = N\,B\,\ell\,v(t) = N\,B\,\ell\,V_0\cos(\omega t) \equiv \varepsilon_0\cos(\omega t)$$

The Thévenin equivalent source has EMF $\varepsilon_0$ and internal impedance $Z_s = R_c + j\omega L$. At resonance / matched load ($R_L = R_s + R_c$ where $R_s$ is the source resistance seen by the coil):

$$i(t) = \frac{\varepsilon_0\cos(\omega t)}{R_c + R_L} = \frac{\varepsilon_0\cos(\omega t)}{2R_c}$$

Instantaneous power:

$$p(t) = \varepsilon(t)\,i(t) = \frac{\varepsilon_0^2\cos^2(\omega t)}{2R_c}$$

Time-averaged (over one cycle):

$$\langle P \rangle = \frac{1}{2}\cdot\frac{\varepsilon_0^2}{2R_c} = \frac{\varepsilon_0^2}{4R_c} = \frac{(N\,B\,\ell\,V_0)^2}{4R_c}$$

$$\boxed{\;P_{max,\,EM} = \frac{(N\,B\,\ell\,V_0)^2}{4R_c}\;}$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Thermoelectric Power into Matched Load

A TE module with Seebeck coefficient $\alpha_{pn}$, source resistance $R_{TE}$ and open-circuit voltage $V_{oc} = \alpha_{pn}\Delta T$ is connected to load $R_L$.

By **Maximum Power Transfer Theorem**, $P_L$ is maximized when $R_L = R_{TE}$:

$$P_{max} = \frac{V_{oc}^2}{4R_{TE}} = \frac{\alpha_{pn}^2(\Delta T)^2}{4R_{TE}}$$

Substituting $R_{TE} = \rho\,\ell/A$ where $\rho$ is electrical resistivity, $\ell$ the leg length, $A$ the cross-section:

$$\boxed{\;P_{max,\,TE} = \frac{\alpha_{pn}^2\,A\,(\Delta T)^2}{4\rho\,\ell}\;}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Trade-off Insight:** Power scales as $A/\ell$ — *short, fat legs* maximize power density. However, the *efficiency* $\eta$ is independent of geometry and depends only on $ZT$. Thus, power-optimized harvesters (IoT sensors) and efficiency-optimized generators (spacecraft RTGs) use very different geometries.

## 3.4 Derivation: Photovoltaic MPP Condition

The PV cell delivers power $P = IV$. Using the diode equation $I = I_L - I_0[\exp(qV/nkT) - 1]$:

$$P(V) = V\left[I_L - I_0\!\left(\exp\!\frac{qV}{nkT} - 1\right)\right]$$

Differentiate and set to zero:

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = I_L - I_0\!\left(\exp\!\frac{qV}{nkT} - 1\right) - V\,I_0\,\frac{q}{nkT}\exp\!\frac{qV}{nkT} = 0$$

Substituting $I = I_L - I_0[\exp(qV/nkT) - 1]$:

$$I + V\cdot\frac{q}{nkT}I_0\exp\!\frac{qV}{nkT} - I_0\exp\!\frac{qV}{nkT} + I_0 = 0$$

After algebraic rearrangement, the **implicit MPP voltage** is:

$$\boxed{\;V_{MPP} = V_{oc} - \frac{nkT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{V_{MPP}}{nkT/q} + 1\right)\;}$$

This transcendental equation is solved numerically (Newton–Raphson) in MPPT controllers. The fill factor $FF$ is then $FF = (V_{MPP}\cdot I_{MPP})/(V_{oc}\cdot I_{sc})$, and conversion efficiency follows.

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Piezoelectric | Electromagnetic | Electrostatic | Thermoelectric | Photovoltaic | RF Rectenna |
|----------|---------------|-----------------|---------------|----------------|---------------|-------------|
| Output $V_{oc}$ | High (10–50 V) | Low (mV–V) | Requires bias | Low (mV–V) | Medium (0.5–1 V) | Very low (mV) |
| Source Impedance | High (kΩ–MΩ) | Low (Ω) | High (GΩ) | Low (Ω) | Nonlinear | 50 Ω (antenna) |
| Output Nature | AC | AC | Charge pulse | DC | DC | Pulsed DC |
| Needs External Bias? | No | No | **Yes (or electret)** | No | No | No |
| Power Density | High @ resonance | High (macro) | Moderate | Low (small $\Delta T$) | Highest (outdoor) | Very low |
| MEMS-Scalable? | **Yes** | Poor | **Yes** | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| Frequency Dependence | Strong (resonant) | Moderate | Strong | None | None | Band-limited |
| Best IoT Use Case | Vibration-rich machines | Human motion, wearables | MEMS vibration | Body heat, industrial waste heat | Indoor light, outdoor solar | Ambient Wi-Fi/GSM |
| Maturity (TRL) | TRL 6–8 | TRL 9 | TRL 4–6 | TRL 9 | TRL 9 | TRL 5–7 |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]**
*Compare piezoelectric and electromagnetic energy conversion mechanisms in vibration energy harvesters. Highlight any two key differences.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Comparison Parameter | Piezoelectric | Electromagnetic |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Direct piezoelectric effect (stress → charge) | Faraday's Law of Induction (motion → EMF) |
| Output Voltage | High (10s of V) | Low (mV to a few V) |
| Source Impedance | High (kΩ – MΩ) | Low (Ω – 100s of Ω) |
| MEMS Compatibility | Excellent (PZT thin films) | Poor (magnets do not scale well) |
| Power Density | ~200 $\mu$W/cm³ at resonance | High at macro scale, low at micro scale |

Two key differences for the answer: **(i) Piezoelectric produces high-voltage, high-impedance output suited to diode-bridge rectifiers; electromagnetic produces low-voltage, low-impedance output suited to transformer-boosted conditioning; (ii) piezoelectric is MEMS-friendly, electromagnetic is not.**

(3 marks awarded for: definition 1 M, comparison 1 M, conclusion 1 M)

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*State and explain the Seebeck effect. Write the expression for the open-circuit voltage generated by a thermoelectric generator.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Seebeck effect** is the direct conversion of a temperature difference into a DC voltage at the junction of two dissimilar conductors. When the junctions of a thermocouple made of materials with Seebeck coefficients $\alpha_p$ and $\alpha_n$ are held at temperatures $T_h$ (hot) and $T_c$ (cold), an open-circuit voltage is developed:

$$V_{oc} = (\alpha_p - \alpha_n)\,(T_h - T_c) = \alpha_{pn}\,\Delta T$$

For typical Bi₂Te₃ thermoelectric modules, $\alpha_{pn} \approx 200\;\mu V/K$, so a 10 K temperature gradient produces $\approx 2$ mV per thermocouple pair.

(3 marks: statement 1 M, equation 1 M, numerical example 1 M)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a piezoelectric vibration energy harvester. Derive the expression for the harvested power in single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) form under sinusoidal base excitation.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cantilever beam harvester has effective mass $m = 5$ g, base acceleration amplitude $A = 1\;\text{m/s}^2$, and natural frequency $f_n = 100$ Hz. The electrical damping ratio is $\zeta_e = 0.02$ and total damping ratio is $\zeta = 0.05$. Calculate the maximum harvested power and the frequency at which it occurs.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Part (a) — 7 marks**

*Block diagram & working (3 marks):*
The ambient vibration drives a cantilever beam with a tip mass. The piezoelectric layer (bonded on the beam) experiences alternating tensile/compressive strain as the beam flexes. By the direct piezoelectric effect, this strain produces a sinusoidal AC voltage across the electrodes. The AC voltage is rectified by a bridge rectifier, smoothed, and stored.

*Derivation (4 marks):* The SDOF equation of motion with base displacement $y(t) = Y\cos(\omega t)$ and relative displacement $x = z - y$ is:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = -m\ddot{y}$$

In steady state, with $x(t) = X\cos(\omega t - \phi)$:

$$X = \frac{mA\omega^2}{\sqrt{(k-m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}} = \frac{A\,\Omega^2}{\sqrt{(1-\Omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\Omega)^2}}$$

Time-averaged harvested power in the electrical branch:

$$\boxed{\;\langle P_e \rangle = \zeta_e\,m\,A^2\,\omega_n^3\cdot\frac{\Omega^2}{(1-\Omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\Omega)^2}\;}$$

Power is maximum near resonance ($\Omega \approx 1$), confirming the necessity of tuning $\omega_n$ to the dominant ambient vibration frequency.

**(b) Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given:
- $m = 5$ g $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ kg
- $A = 1$ m/s²
- $f_n = 100$ Hz $\Rightarrow \omega_n = 2\pi f_n = 200\pi$ rad/s $\approx 628.32$ rad/s
- $\zeta_e = 0.02$, $\zeta = 0.05$

**Step 1 — Find $\Omega$ that maximizes $P_e$.** Differentiate the bracketed term with respect to $\Omega$ and set to zero. Let $g(\Omega) = \Omega^2 / [(1-\Omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\Omega)^2]$.

$$g'(\Omega) = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; \Omega_{opt} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 2\zeta}} \quad \text{(for lightly damped)}$$

$$\Omega_{opt} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + 2(0.05)}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.10}} = 0.9535$$

**Step 2 — Compute $g(\Omega_{opt})$.**

Denominator: $(1 - 0.9535^2)^2 + (2 \times 0.05 \times 0.9535)^2$
$$= (1 - 0.9092)^2 + (0.09535)^2 = (0.0908)^2 + (0.09535)^2$$
$$= 0.008244 + 0.009092 = 0.017336$$

Numerator: $\Omega^2 = 0.9092$

$$g(\Omega_{opt}) = \frac{0.9092}{0.017336} = 52.45$$

**Step 3 — Compute peak harvested power.**

$$\langle P_e \rangle = \zeta_e\,m\,A^2\,\omega_n^3\cdot g(\Omega_{opt})$$

$$= (0.02)(5\times10^{-3})(1)^2\,(628.32)^3\,(52.45)$$

$$= (0.02)(5\times10^{-3})(2.481\times10^8)(52.45)$$

$$= (0.02)(5\times10^{-3})(1.301\times10^{10})$$

$$\boxed{\;\langle P_e \rangle \approx 1.301 \times 10^6 \;\mu W \approx 1.30 \text{ W}\;}$$

*Check arithmetic:*
- $\omega_n^3 = 628.32^3 = 2.481 \times 10^8$ rad³/s³
- $\zeta_e \cdot m = 0.02 \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}$
- $A^2 = 1$
- Multiplied: $1.0 \times 10^{-4} \times 2.481 \times 10^8 = 2.481 \times 10^4$
- Times $g$: $2.481 \times 10^4 \times 52.45 = 1.301 \times 10^6$ μW

Frequency of max power: $f_{opt} = \Omega_{opt}\cdot f_n = 0.9535 \times 100 = 95.35$ Hz.

**Answer:** Maximum harvested power ≈ **1.30 W** at $f \approx$ **95.35 Hz**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Students frequently confuse the location of the power peak (which is at $\Omega_{opt} = 1/\sqrt{1+2\zeta}$, *not* exactly at $\Omega = 1$ — the resonance of the *displacement*, not the *power*). Writing $f = f_n = 100$ Hz instead of the correct 95.35 Hz will cost 2 marks. Also, watch unit conversions: $m$ in **kg**, $A$ in **m/s²**, and the result will be in **watts**.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the photovoltaic conversion mechanism. Derive the implicit expression for the maximum power point voltage $V_{MPP}$ in terms of the open-circuit voltage $V_{oc}$ and the ideality factor $n$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A small amorphous-silicon PV cell intended for indoor IoT sensor nodes is rated as follows under 200 lux indoor lighting: $I_{sc} = 50\;\mu A$, $V_{oc} = 0.45$ V, $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ A, ideality factor $n = 1.3$, and temperature $T = 300$ K. Compute the maximum power point voltage $V_{MPP}$ and the maximum deliverable power.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Part (a) — 7 marks**

*Mechanism (3 marks):* When a p–n junction is illuminated by photons of energy $h\nu \geq E_g$, electron–hole pairs are generated. The built-in electric field at the depletion region sweeps the minority carriers across, producing a photocurrent $I_L$ that opposes the diode's dark current. The illuminated diode characteristic is:

$$I = I_L - I_0\!\left[\exp\!\frac{qV}{nkT} - 1\right]$$

The power delivered is $P = V\cdot I$. Setting $dP/dV = 0$ at the MPP:

**Derivation (4 marks):**

$$P(V) = V\left[I_L - I_0\!\left(\exp\!\frac{qV}{nkT} - 1\right)\right]$$

$$\frac{dP}{dV} = I_L - I_0\!\left(\exp\!\frac{qV}{nkT} - 1\right) - V\cdot I_0\,\frac{q}{nkT}\exp\!\frac{qV}{nkT} = 0$$

At MPP, $I = I_L - I_0[\exp(qV_{MPP}/nkT) - 1]$, so substituting back:

$$I_{MPP} + V_{MPP}\cdot\frac{q}{nkT}\!\left(I_L - I_{MPP}\right) - \left(I_L - I_{MPP}\right) + I_0 = 0$$

Recognizing $I_L \approx I_{sc}$ and using the open-circuit condition $0 = I_{sc} - I_0[\exp(qV_{oc}/nkT) - 1]$, i.e. $I_0\exp(qV_{oc}/nkT) = I_{sc} + I_0 \approx I_{sc}$:

$$\boxed{\;V_{MPP} = V_{oc} - \frac{nkT}{q}\ln\!\left(\frac{V_{MPP}}{nkT/q} + 1\right)\;}$$

**(b) Part (b) — 7 marks**

Given: $I_{sc} = 50\;\mu A$, $V_{oc} = 0.45$ V, $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ A, $n = 1.3$, $T = 300$ K.

**Step 1 — Compute thermal voltage scaled by ideality factor.**

$$V_T = \frac{nkT}{q} = \frac{1.3 \times (1.381\times10^{-23})\times 300}{1.602\times10^{-19}}$$

$$V_T = \frac{1.3 \times 4.143\times10^{-21}}{1.602\times10^{-19}} = \frac{5.386\times10^{-21}}{1.602\times10^{-19}} = 0.03363 \text{ V}$$

So $V_T \approx 33.63$ mV.

**Step 2 — Solve the transcendental equation for $V_{MPP}$ by iteration.**

$$V_{MPP} = V_{oc} - V_T\ln\!\left(\frac{V_{MPP}}{V_T} + 1\right) = 0.45 - 0.03363\ln\!\left(\frac{V_{MPP}}{0.03363} + 1\right)$$

*Iteration 1:* Start with $V_{MPP}^{(0)} = 0.8\,V_{oc} = 0.36$ V.

$$V_{MPP}^{(1)} = 0.45 - 0.03363\ln(0.36/0.03363 + 1) = 0.45 - 0.03363\ln(11.706) = 0.45 - 0.03363(2.4599) = 0.45 - 0.0827 = 0.3673 \text{ V}$$

*Iteration 2:*

$$V_{MPP}^{(2)} = 0.45 - 0.03363\ln(0.3673/0.03363 + 1) = 0.45 - 0.03363\ln(11.923) = 0.45 - 0.03363(2.4793) = 0.45 - 0.0834 = 0.3666 \text{ V}$$

*Iteration 3:*

$$V_{MPP}^{(3)} = 0.45 - 0.03363\ln(0.3666/0.03363 + 1) = 0.45 - 0.03363\ln(11.901) = 0.45 - 0.0834 = 0.3666 \text{ V}$$

Converged: $\boxed{V_{MPP} \approx 0.367 \text{ V}}$

**Step 3 — Compute the corresponding $I_{MPP}$.**

$$I_{MPP} = I_{sc} - I_0\!\left[\exp\!\frac{V_{MPP}}{V_T} - 1\right]$$

$$\frac{V_{MPP}}{V_T} = \frac{0.3666}{0.03363} = 10.902$$

$$\exp(10.902) = 5.41 \times 10^4$$

$$I_0\cdot\exp(10.902) = 10^{-12}\times 5.41\times10^4 = 5.41\times10^{-8}\text{ A} = 0.0541\;\mu A$$

$$I_{MPP} = 50\;\mu A - 0.0541\;\mu A \approx 49.95\;\mu A$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Power.**

$$P_{max} = V_{MPP}\cdot I_{MPP} = 0.3666 \times 49.95\times10^{-6}$$

$$\boxed{\;P_{max} \approx 18.31\;\mu W\;}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> Three common errors:
> **(1)** Forgetting to multiply $kT/q$ by the ideality factor $n$ — using just the thermal voltage (25.85 mV) instead of $nV_T$ (33.63 mV) is worth 1 mark.
> **(2)** Not iterating the transcendental equation — students sometimes stop at the first estimate, which costs 1 mark.
> **(3)** Forgetting the "$-1$" inside the exponential bracket in $I_{MPP}$ calculation, leading to a wildly wrong $I_{MPP}$ (e.g., $5.41 \times 10^4\;\mu A$ instead of $0.0541\;\mu A$) — this costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Conversion**

- ⭐ **Conversion is the front-end transducer** that turns ambient energy (mechanical / thermal / optical / RF) into electrical energy.
- ⭐ **Six canonical mechanisms:** Piezoelectric, Electromagnetic, Electrostatic, Thermoelectric (Seebeck), Photovoltaic, RF Rectenna.
- ⭐ **Piezoelectric:** $V_{oc} = g_{33}\,T\,\ell$; governed by $d_{ij}$ coefficients; high $V_{oc}$, high source impedance; MEMS-friendly.
- ⭐ **Electromagnetic:** $\varepsilon = -N\,d\Phi/dt = -N\,B\,\ell\,v$; low $V_{oc}$, low impedance; not MEMS-friendly; strong at macro scale.
- ⭐ **Electrostatic:** $W = \tfrac{1}{2}Q^2(1/C_{min} - 1/C_{max})$; requires electret or external pre-charge; charge-constrained vs voltage-constrained cycles.
- ⭐ **Thermoelectric:** $V_{oc} = \alpha_{pn}\,\Delta T$; $P_{max} = \alpha_{pn}^2(\Delta T)^2/(4R_{TE})$; efficiency governed by figure of merit $ZT$.
- ⭐ **Photovoltaic:** $I = I_L - I_0[\exp(qV/nkT) - 1]$; $V_{MPP}$ satisfies implicit equation; Fill Factor $FF \approx 0.6$–$0.85$ for good cells.
- ⭐ **RF Rectenna:** $P_r = S\,G\,\lambda^2/(4\pi)$; uses Schottky diode (low $V_F$); efficiency is $P_{DC}/P_{RF,in}$.
- ⭐ **Source Impedance dictates the next stage:** high-Z → voltage multiplier / active rectifier; low-Z → transformer / boost converter.
- ⭐ **Power Peak ≠ Displacement Peak:** $P_{max}$ occurs at $\Omega_{opt} = 1/\sqrt{1+2\zeta}$, not at $\Omega = 1$.
- ⭐ **Implicit MPP equations must be iterated (Newton–Raphson or fixed-point).**
- ⭐ **Material selection (KTU frequently asks):** PZT for piezo, NdFeB for magnets, Bi₂Te₃ for TE, amorphous-Si or CIGS for indoor PV.
- ⭐ **Maturity ranking (TRL):** PV (9) > EM (9) > TE (9) > Piezo (6–8) > Rectenna (5–7) > Electrostatic (4–6).
- ⭐ **Trade-off principle:** Power-optimized (IoT) vs Efficiency-optimized (RTG) harvesters use *opposite* TE leg geometries.

> **End of Module 3 — Conversion Notes | KTU 2024 Scheme | PECIT746**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Examples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

**Worked Examples in Energy Harvesting & Power Management for IoT** are numerical and design-based problems that consolidate the theoretical concepts of Module 3, which typically encompasses **solar (photovoltaic) energy harvesting, piezoelectric/vibration harvesting, thermal (TEG) harvesting, RF harvesting, and the associated power management circuits (DC-DC converters, MPPT algorithms, and energy storage sizing)**. These examples train the student to translate physical parameters (irradiance, temperature, acceleration, heat flux) into **electrical quantities (open-circuit voltage, short-circuit current, harvested power, duty cycle, conversion efficiency)** that drive an IoT node.

### 1.1 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Think of an energy harvester like a **rainwater collection system on a rooftop**. The size of the roof (area), the rain intensity (irradiance/vibration amplitude), and the downpipe diameter (transducer coupling) decide how much water (electrical energy) you collect. The **storage tank** is your battery/supercapacitor, and the **smart valve** that opens/closes to keep the tank optimally filled is your **MPPT controller**.

| Physical Quantity | IoT Engineering Equivalent | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Rainfall rate | Solar irradiance $G$ | $\text{W/m}^2$ |
| Roof area | Active transducer area $A$ | $\text{m}^2$ |
| Pipe diameter | Coupling coefficient $k$ | dimensionless |
| Collected water | Harvested electrical power $P_h$ | $\text{W}$ or $\text{mW}$ |
| Storage tank | Battery / Supercapacitor | $\text{Ah}$ or $\text{F}$ |
| Smart valve | MPPT controller duty cycle $D$ | dimensionless ($0 < D < 1$) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Constants used throughout this module:**
> - Standard Test Conditions (STC) irradiance: $G_{STC} = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$
> - Standard cell temperature: $T_{STC} = 25^\circ\text{C} = 298.15 \text{ K}$
> - Boltzmann constant: $k_B = 1.381 \times 10^{-23} \text{ J/K}$
> - Electron charge: $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$
> - Seebeck coefficient (typical Bi$_2$Te$_3$): $S \approx 200 \text{ }\mu\text{V/K}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Logic of Typical Module-3 Example Problems

Every worked example in this module follows a **four-step logical skeleton**:

1. **Identify the Energy Source Class** → Solar / Piezo / Thermal / RF. This decides the governing transducer equation.
2. **Extract Physical Input Parameters** → $G$, $T$, $a$ (acceleration), $\Delta T$, $P_{RF}$, etc.
3. **Apply the Transducer / Converter Equation** → Convert physical input to electrical output ($V_{oc}$, $I_{sc}$, $P_{out}$, $D$).
4. **Cross-Verify with Power Budget of the IoT Node** → Compare harvested power with the load's average power consumption $P_{load}$.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The table below is the **must-memorize formula bank** for solving any Module-3 example. Pay close attention to units — board examiners frequently award (or deduct) marks strictly on unit consistency.

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Typical Range / Boundary |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | PV Cell Current | $I = I_{ph} - I_0 \left(e^{\frac{q(V + IR_s)}{nkT}} - 1\right) - \frac{V + IR_s}{R_{sh}}$ | $I$ in A, $V$ in V, $I_{ph}$ photocurrent (A), $I_0$ reverse saturation (A), $n$ ideality factor (1–2), $R_s$ series $\Omega$, $R_{sh}$ shunt $\Omega$ | At STC: $I_{ph} \approx I_{sc}$ |
| 2 | Photocurrent vs. Irradiance | $I_{ph} = I_{ph,STC} \cdot \frac{G}{G_{STC}}$ | $G$ in W/m² | Linear scaling |
| 3 | Open-Circuit Voltage vs. Temperature | $V_{oc} = V_{oc,STC} + \alpha \cdot (T - T_{STC})$ | $\alpha$ in V/°C (negative, typically $-0.3$ %/°C) | Decreases with $T$ |
| 4 | Fill Factor | $FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$ | Dimensionless | $0.5 \leq FF \leq 0.85$ |
| 5 | Cell Efficiency | $\eta = \frac{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF}{G \cdot A}$ | $\eta$ dimensionless, $A$ in m² | Si: 15–22 % |
| 6 | Piezoelectric Harvested Power | $P_h = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot m \cdot \omega_n^3 \cdot Y^2$ | $m$ mass (kg), $\omega_n$ natural freq (rad/s), $Y$ vibration amplitude (m), $\zeta$ damping ratio | Peaks at $\omega = \omega_n$ |
| 7 | Thermoelectric (Seebeck) Voltage | $V_{th} = N \cdot S \cdot \Delta T$ | $N$ thermocouples, $S$ Seebeck coeff (V/K), $\Delta T$ in K | Bi$_2$Te$_3$: $S \approx 200 \text{ }\mu$V/K |
| 8 | Thermoelectric Harvested Power | $P_{th} = \frac{V_{th}^2}{4 R_{load}}$ | Maximum at $R_{load} = R_{internal}$ | Impedance matching critical |
| 9 | Boost Converter Voltage Gain | $V_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{1 - D}$ | $D$ duty cycle ($0 < D < 1$) | Step-up only |
| 10 | Buck-Boost Gain | $V_{out} = -V_{in} \cdot \frac{D}{1 - D}$ | Sign inversion | Step-up or down |
| 11 | MPPT Perturb & Observe Rule | $\Delta P / \Delta V > 0 \Rightarrow$ decrease $D$ | $\Delta P = P_{new} - P_{old}$ | Local MPP tracking |
| 12 | Supercapacitor Energy Storage | $E_C = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ | $C$ in F, $V$ in V | Quadratic in $V$ |
| 13 | Battery Self-Discharge Budget | $t_{life} = \frac{C_{bat} \cdot \eta_{DSCH}}{P_{load} + P_{leak}}$ | $C_{bat}$ in Ah, $P$ in W | Autonomy in hours |
| 14 | RF Received Power (Friis) | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\frac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | $d$ distance, $\lambda$ wavelength | Falls as $1/d^2$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are not academic exercises — they directly drive the design of **self-powered wireless sensor nodes (WSNs)**, **wearable biomedical implants**, **structural health monitoring systems on bridges**, and **smart agriculture IoT devices** deployed in Kerala's humid, monsoonal climate where battery replacement is economically and physically impractical.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Example 1 — Solar PV Output Power Under Non-STC Conditions

**Problem Statement:**
A small monocrystalline silicon PV panel used for an outdoor IoT weather station has the following STC ratings from its datasheet:
- Open-circuit voltage: $V_{oc,STC} = 22.0 \text{ V}$
- Short-circuit current: $I_{sc,STC} = 3.50 \text{ A}$
- Voltage at MPP: $V_{mp,STC} = 17.5 \text{ V}$
- Current at MPP: $I_{mp,STC} = 3.20 \text{ A}$
- Temperature coefficient of $V_{oc}$: $\alpha = -0.32 \text{ \%/°C}$ (per °C above 25 °C)
- Short-circuit current temperature coefficient: $\beta = +0.05 \text{ \%/°C}$

The panel is operating in **Kochi** at an ambient cell temperature of $T = 45\text{°C}$ and an average solar irradiance of $G = 600 \text{ W/m}^2$. The IoT load draws $P_{load} = 5.0 \text{ W}$ continuously. Determine:
(a) The new open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current at operating conditions.
(b) The approximate new MPP power output.
(c) Whether the panel can sustain the load (state clearly with a margin calculation).

---

#### Part (a) — New $V_{oc}$ and $I_{sc}$ at $T = 45\text{°C}$ and $G = 600 \text{ W/m}^2$

**Step 1 — Temperature difference from STC**

$$\Delta T = T - T_{STC} = 45 - 25 = 20 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Correct the temperature coefficient to absolute units**

$$\alpha_{abs} = \frac{-0.32}{100} \times V_{oc,STC} = \frac{-0.32}{100} \times 22.0$$

$$\alpha_{abs} = -0.0704 \text{ V/°C}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the new open-circuit voltage**

$$V_{oc} = V_{oc,STC} + \alpha_{abs} \cdot \Delta T$$

$$V_{oc} = 22.0 + (-0.0704)(20)$$

$$V_{oc} = 22.0 - 1.408 = 20.592 \text{ V}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the new short-circuit current (irradiance scaling)**

$$I_{sc} = I_{sc,STC} \cdot \frac{G}{G_{STC}}$$

$$I_{sc} = 3.50 \times \frac{600}{1000} = 3.50 \times 0.6 = 2.10 \text{ A}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the small temperature correction to $I_{sc}$**

$$\beta_{abs} = \frac{+0.05}{100} \times I_{sc,STC} = \frac{0.05}{100} \times 3.50 = +0.00175 \text{ A/°C}$$

$$I_{sc,corrected} = I_{sc} \cdot (1 + \beta_{abs} \cdot \Delta T / I_{sc,STC})$$

A more direct path often used in board solutions:

$$I_{sc,corrected} = I_{sc,STC} \cdot \frac{G}{G_{STC}} \cdot \left[1 + \frac{\beta}{100} \cdot \Delta T\right]$$

$$I_{sc,corrected} = 3.50 \times 0.6 \times \left[1 + 0.0005 \times 20\right]$$

$$I_{sc,corrected} = 2.10 \times 1.010 = 2.121 \text{ A}$$

> **Result (a):** $V_{oc} = 20.59 \text{ V}$, $I_{sc} = 2.12 \text{ A}$

---

#### Part (b) — Approximate New MPP Power

**Step 1 — Estimate Fill Factor at STC**

$$FF_{STC} = \frac{V_{mp,STC} \cdot I_{mp,STC}}{V_{oc,STC} \cdot I_{sc,STC}}$$

$$FF_{STC} = \frac{17.5 \times 3.20}{22.0 \times 3.50} = \frac{56.00}{77.00} = 0.7273$$

**Step 2 — Approximate $FF$ at operating point**

For small temperature and irradiance deviations, $FF$ changes only marginally (typically 1–3 % drop). Assume:

$$FF \approx 0.7273 \times 0.97 \approx 0.7055$$

**Step 3 — Compute new MPP power**

$$P_{mp} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF$$

$$P_{mp} = 20.592 \times 2.121 \times 0.7055$$

$$P_{mp} = 43.68 \times 0.7055 \approx 30.82 \text{ W}$$

> **Result (b):** $P_{mp} \approx 30.8 \text{ W}$

**Alternative direct method using STC $P_{mp}$:**

$$P_{mp,STC} = V_{mp,STC} \times I_{mp,STC} = 17.5 \times 3.20 = 56.0 \text{ W}$$

$$P_{mp} = P_{mp,STC} \cdot \frac{G}{G_{STC}} \cdot \left[1 - 0.004 \cdot \Delta T\right]$$

$$P_{mp} = 56.0 \times 0.6 \times \left[1 - 0.004 \times 20\right]$$

$$P_{mp} = 33.6 \times 0.92 = 30.91 \text{ W}$$

Both methods converge to **$\approx 30.8$–$30.9$ W**.

---

#### Part (c) — Power Sustainability Check

$$P_{margin} = P_{mp} - P_{load}$$

$$P_{margin} = 30.82 - 5.0 = 25.82 \text{ W}$$

$$\text{Margin \%} = \frac{25.82}{5.0} \times 100 = 516.4 \text{ \%}$$

> **Result (c):** Yes — the panel delivers **$\approx 30.8 \text{ W}$**, vastly exceeding the 5.0 W load. The system can sustain the load with a **$\approx 516$ % margin**, with surplus power available for battery charging and night-time operation.

---

### 3.2 Example 2 — Piezoelectric Vibration Energy Harvester Sizing

**Problem Statement:**
A piezoelectric cantilever harvester is mounted on a factory motor housing. Given:
- Tip mass: $m = 12 \text{ g} = 0.012 \text{ kg}$
- Natural frequency: $f_n = 50 \text{ Hz} \Rightarrow \omega_n = 2\pi f_n = 314.159 \text{ rad/s}$
- Damping ratio: $\zeta = 0.035$
- Vibration displacement amplitude: $Y = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}$

Compute:
(a) The maximum harvested power (at resonance).
(b) The power delivered to an optimally matched resistive load.
(c) The average daily energy harvested if the motor runs 16 hours per day.

---

**Step 1 — Compute maximum harvested power at resonance**

$$P_{h,max} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot \zeta \cdot m \cdot \omega_n^3 \cdot Y^2$$

$$\omega_n^3 = (314.159)^3 = 3.101 \times 10^{7} \text{ rad}^3/\text{s}^3$$

$$P_{h,max} = 0.5 \times 0.035 \times 0.012 \times 3.101 \times 10^{7} \times (5 \times 10^{-4})^2$$

$$P_{h,max} = 0.5 \times 0.035 \times 0.012 \times 3.101 \times 10^{7} \times 2.5 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$P_{h,max} = 1.628 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} = 1.628 \text{ mW}$$

> **Result (a):** $P_{h,max} \approx 1.63 \text{ mW}$

---

**Step 2 — Power to matched load**

At optimal impedance matching, the load absorbs **50 %** of the available mechanical power (the other half is dissipated as damping loss).

$$P_{load} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot P_{h,max} = \frac{1.628}{2} = 0.814 \text{ mW}$$

> **Result (b):** $P_{load} \approx 0.814 \text{ mW}$

---

**Step 3 — Daily energy harvested**

$$E_{daily} = P_{load} \times t_{operational}$$

$$E_{daily} = 0.814 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W} \times 16 \times 3600 \text{ s}$$

$$E_{daily} = 0.814 \times 10^{-3} \times 57600$$

$$E_{daily} = 46.886 \text{ J} \approx 46.9 \text{ J}$$

> **Result (c):** $E_{daily} \approx 46.9 \text{ J}$ per day

> [!NOTE]
> This is sufficient to power a low-duty-cycle IoT node (e.g., a temperature sensor transmitting once per minute) drawing $\approx 50 \text{ }\mu\text{W}$ average — a classic fit for **Industrial IoT (IIoT) machine-condition monitoring**.

---

### 3.3 Example 3 — DC-DC Boost Converter for MPPT

**Problem Statement:**
A thermoelectric generator (TEG) produces an open-circuit voltage of $V_{th} = 0.6 \text{ V}$ with an internal resistance of $R_{int} = 2.0 \text{ }\Omega$. It feeds a boost converter that charges a 3.3 V IoT node battery. Assuming ideal converter operation and that the boost converter's input is matched to the TEG's maximum power point:
(a) Find the source current at MPP.
(b) Determine the duty cycle $D$ that the MPPT controller must command (assume continuous conduction mode).
(c) Find the average input current drawn from the TEG and the average output current delivered to the battery.

---

**Step 1 — Voltage at MPP (impedance match)**

At maximum power transfer, the load seen by the TEG equals its internal resistance, and the terminal voltage is half of the open-circuit voltage:

$$V_{mp} = \frac{V_{th}}{2} = \frac{0.6}{2} = 0.3 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Source current at MPP**

$$I_{mp} = \frac{V_{mp}}{R_{int}} = \frac{0.3}{2.0} = 0.15 \text{ A}$$

> **Result (a):** $I_{mp} = 0.15 \text{ A} = 150 \text{ mA}$

**Step 3 — Power at MPP**

$$P_{mp} = V_{mp} \times I_{mp} = 0.3 \times 0.15 = 0.045 \text{ W} = 45 \text{ mW}$$

---

**Step 4 — Duty cycle for boost conversion**

Boost converter input-to-output relation (ideal):

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{1 - D}$$

$$3.3 = \frac{0.3}{1 - D}$$

$$1 - D = \frac{0.3}{3.3} = \frac{1}{11} = 0.0909$$

$$D = 1 - 0.0909 = 0.9091$$

> **Result (b):** $D \approx 0.909$ (i.e., the switch is ON 90.9 % of the period)

---

**Step 5 — Average output current (power balance, ideal converter)**

For an ideal lossless converter, input power = output power:

$$P_{in} = P_{out}$$

$$V_{in} \cdot I_{in,avg} = V_{out} \cdot I_{out,avg}$$

$$I_{out,avg} = \frac{V_{in} \cdot I_{in,avg}}{V_{out}}$$

For a boost converter, the average inductor (input) current equals the input current of the source:

$$I_{in,avg} = I_{mp} = 0.15 \text{ A}$$

$$I_{out,avg} = \frac{0.3 \times 0.15}{3.3} = \frac{0.045}{3.3} = 0.01364 \text{ A} \approx 13.64 \text{ mA}$$

> **Result (c):** $I_{in,avg} = 150 \text{ mA}$, $I_{out,avg} \approx 13.6 \text{ mA}$

---

### 3.4 Example 4 — MPPT Perturb & Observe (P&O) Trace

**Problem Statement:**
A P&O MPPT algorithm perturbs the boost-converter duty cycle by $\Delta D = 0.02$ at each step. From the PV panel of Example 1, the following successive $(D, P_{mp})$ measurements are recorded:

| Step | $D$ | $P_{mp}$ (W) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.50 | 28.0 |
| 2 | 0.52 | 30.5 |
| 3 | 0.54 | 31.8 |
| 4 | 0.56 | 31.2 |

Identify the MPP and justify the next perturbation direction.

---

**Step-by-step P&O Decision Logic**

| Step | $D$ | $P_{mp}$ | $\Delta D$ | $\Delta P$ | $\Delta P / \Delta D$ | Decision |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 → 2 | 0.50 → 0.52 | 28.0 → 30.5 | +0.02 | +2.5 | Positive | Keep increasing $D$ |
| 2 → 3 | 0.52 → 0.54 | 30.5 → 31.8 | +0.02 | +1.3 | Positive | Keep increasing $D$ |
| 3 → 4 | 0.54 → 0.56 | 31.8 → 31.2 | +0.02 | $-0.6$ | **Negative** | **Reverse — decrease $D$** |

**Conclusion:**
- The MPP lies between $D = 0.54$ and $D = 0.56$ at approximately $P_{mp}^{max} \approx 31.8 \text{ W}$.
- Next perturbation: **$\Delta D = -0.02$** (duty cycle reduced to 0.54 again, then 0.52, oscillating around the true MPP).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This oscillation around the MPP is the **inherent limitation of P&O** — it never perfectly settles. More advanced algorithms like **Incremental Conductance (IncCond)** or **Fuzzy-Logic MPPT** eliminate this steady-state error.

---

### 3.5 Example 5 — Supercapacitor Storage Sizing

**Problem Statement:**
An IoT node consumes $P_{load} = 200 \text{ mW}$ average. It is supported by a supercapacitor of $C = 10 \text{ F}$ rated $V_{max} = 5.4 \text{ V}$, with a brown-out cutoff at $V_{min} = 3.0 \text{ V}$. The harvester is offline (e.g., night-time for solar). How long can the node survive?

---

**Step 1 — Usable energy stored**

$$E_{usable} = \frac{1}{2} C \left(V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2\right)$$

$$E_{usable} = 0.5 \times 10 \times \left(5.4^2 - 3.0^2\right)$$

$$E_{usable} = 5.0 \times \left(29.16 - 9.00\right)$$

$$E_{usable} = 5.0 \times 20.16 = 100.8 \text{ J}$$

**Step 2 — Survivable duration**

$$t_{survive} = \frac{E_{usable}}{P_{load}} = \frac{100.8 \text{ J}}{0.200 \text{ W}} = 504 \text{ s} \approx 8.4 \text{ min}$$

> **Result:** The supercapacitor can power the node for **$\approx 8.4$ minutes** — adequate for ride-through of brief energy-harvester outages but not full night-time autonomy. A **larger supercapacitor ($C = 50$–$100$ F) or a hybrid Li-ion + supercap topology** is required for 24×7 operation.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Solar-Powered IoT Node

The diagram below maps the **signal and power flow** from the photovoltaic source through the MPPT-controlled converter to the storage and load — this is the canonical architecture that underlies **all five examples** in this module.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SrcPV[PV Panel] -->|Vth Iph| MPPTin
    MPPTin[MPPT Input Filter] -->|Vin Cin| BoostStage
    BoostStage[Boost Converter Switch Q] -->|Vout L D| RectStage
    RectStage[Rectifier and Output Cap] -->|Vbat| BattBank
    BattBank[Battery or Supercap Bank] -->|Vdd| IoTNode
    IoTNode[IoT Sensor MCU Radio]
    MPPTctrl[MPPT Controller P and O] -->|Gate Pulse D| BoostStage
    Vsen[Voltage and Current Sensors] -->|V I feedback| MPPTctrl
    SrcPV -.-> Vsen
    IoTNode -.->|Sleep Wake telemetry| MPPTctrl
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The PV panel (top-left) is the source. The **MPPT controller** senses voltage and current, computes the perturbation, and drives the boost switch. The boost stage steps up the (typically low and variable) PV voltage to a regulated level. The **battery bank** acts as the energy buffer, and the **IoT node** is the load. Dotted lines represent **control / feedback signals**; solid lines represent **power flow**.

### 4.2 Decision Flow of a P&O MPPT Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Start new MPPT cycle]) --> MeasV[Measure Vpv and Ipv]
    MeasV --> CalcP[Compute Ppv = Vpv x Ipv]
    CalcP --> Comp{Delta P greater than 0}
    Comp -- Yes and Delta D positive --> KeepD[Keep increasing D]
    Comp -- Yes and Delta D negative --> KeepD2[Keep decreasing D]
    Comp -- No and Delta D positive --> DecD[Reverse: decrease D]
    Comp -- No and Delta D negative --> IncD[Reverse: increase D]
    KeepD --> Apply[Apply new D to PWM]
    KeepD2 --> Apply
    DecD --> Apply
    IncD --> Apply
    Apply --> Wait[Wait one perturbation period]
    Wait --> Start
```

### 4.3 Energy Source Selection Map for IoT Applications

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Choose Energy Source] --> B{Is sunlight available?}
    B -- Yes --> S[Photovoltaic Harvester]
    B -- No --> C{Are vibrations present?}
    C -- Yes --> P[Piezoelectric Harvester]
    C -- No --> D{Is heat gradient available?}
    D -- Yes --> T[Thermoelectric TEG Harvester]
    D -- No --> E{Is ambient RF strong?}
    E -- Yes --> R[RF Energy Harvester]
    E -- No --> F[Use hybrid source or replace battery]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagrams above replace physical drawings (PV cell cross-sections, cantilever schematics) with **block-architecture flow** as mandated by the system constraints, while preserving the full functional and decision-flow information.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**
*Define Fill Factor (FF) of a solar cell. What is its typical range for a crystalline silicon cell, and how does temperature affect it?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Fill Factor is the ratio of the maximum power delivered by a solar cell to the product of its open-circuit voltage and short-circuit current:
> $$FF = \frac{V_{mp} \cdot I_{mp}}{V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc}}$$
>
> **Typical range (1 mark):** For crystalline silicon cells, $FF$ lies in the range **0.70 – 0.85**.
>
> **Temperature effect (1 mark):** As cell temperature increases, $V_{oc}$ decreases (by $\approx -0.3$ %/°C) and $I_{sc}$ increases marginally (+0.05 %/°C), causing the **Fill Factor to drop** at a rate of approximately $-0.1$ % to $-0.2$ % per °C. Hence, hot-climate operation in Kerala reduces $FF$ and overall conversion efficiency.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**
*State the governing equation of a boost converter in continuous conduction mode. Why is a boost (and not a buck) converter preferred in most energy-harvesting IoT front-ends?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Equation (1 mark):** In CCM, the ideal boost converter obeys
> $$V_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{1 - D}, \quad 0 < D < 1$$
>
> **Why boost is preferred — Reason 1 (1 mark):** Most transducers (PV cells, TEGs, piezoelectric stacks) produce **low open-circuit voltages** (0.3–1.5 V), far below the 1.8–5 V needed by IoT MCUs and radios. A boost converter **steps up** this voltage efficiently.
>
> **Why boost is preferred — Reason 2 (1 mark):** The boost topology inherently allows the MPPT controller to **modulate a single switch duty cycle** while presenting a continuously variable input impedance to the source — this is essential for impedance-matched maximum power point tracking.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Module 3 Examples):**
> - **(−2 marks)** Forgetting to convert °C to K or to STC baseline before applying temperature coefficients.
> - **(−2 marks)** Mixing up irradiance scaling of $I_{sc}$ (linear) with that of $V_{oc}$ (logarithmic / near-constant).
> - **(−1 mark)** Reporting duty cycle > 1 or < 0 — physically impossible, indicates calculation error.
> - **(−1 mark)** Missing units in the final answer. KTU answer scripts MUST carry units at every numerical result.
> - **(−2 marks)** Confusing **average** and **RMS** currents in DC-DC converter analysis.
> - **(−1 mark)** Failing to state assumptions (ideal converter, CCM, matched impedance) explicitly.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style]*

**(a)** A wireless IoT temperature sensor deployed on a Kerala rubber plantation is powered by a small PV panel with the following STC specifications: $V_{oc,STC} = 10.5 \text{ V}$, $I_{sc,STC} = 1.20 \text{ A}$, $V_{mp,STC} = 8.4 \text{ V}$, $I_{mp,STC} = 1.08 \text{ A}$, temperature coefficient of $V_{oc}$: $\alpha = -0.30 \text{ \%/°C}$. At an operating cell temperature of $T = 40 \text{ °C}$ and irradiance $G = 800 \text{ W/m}^2$, compute **(i)** the new $V_{oc}$ and $I_{sc}$, **(ii)** the new MPP power, and **(iii)** the conversion efficiency of the panel, given the panel's active area is $A = 0.06 \text{ m}^2$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A thermoelectric generator (TEG) with $N = 200$ thermocouples of Seebeck coefficient $S = 200 \text{ }\mu\text{V/K}$ is subjected to a temperature gradient $\Delta T = 30 \text{ K}$ across its hot and cold faces. Its internal resistance is $R_{int} = 5 \text{ }\Omega$. The TEG output is fed to a DC-DC boost converter charging a 3.7 V Li-ion cell. Compute **(i)** the open-circuit TEG voltage, **(ii)** the maximum power available at the source, and **(iii)** the duty cycle $D$ required to deliver this power at the converter output (assume ideal CCM operation). **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**A(a)(i) New $V_{oc}$ and $I_{sc}$:**

$\Delta T = 40 - 25 = 15 \text{ °C}$

$\alpha_{abs} = \frac{-0.30}{100} \times 10.5 = -0.0315 \text{ V/°C}$

$$V_{oc} = 10.5 + (-0.0315)(15) = 10.5 - 0.4725 = 10.028 \text{ V}$$

$$I_{sc} = 1.20 \times \frac{800}{1000} = 0.96 \text{ A}$$

(1 mark each for the two quantities, **2 marks total**)

**A(a)(ii) New MPP power:**

$$FF_{STC} = \frac{8.4 \times 1.08}{10.5 \times 1.20} = \frac{9.072}{12.60} = 0.720$$

Assume $FF$ drops by 3 %: $FF \approx 0.6984$.

$$P_{mp} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF = 10.028 \times 0.96 \times 0.6984 \approx 6.72 \text{ W}$$

Alternative path:
$$P_{mp} = P_{mp,STC} \times \frac{G}{G_{STC}} = (8.4 \times 1.08) \times 0.8 = 9.072 \times 0.8 = 7.258 \text{ W}$$

(adjusted for temperature drop: $7.258 \times [1 - 0.0035 \times 15] = 7.258 \times 0.9475 \approx 6.88 \text{ W}$)

Acceptable range: **6.5 – 7.0 W** — **2 marks**

**A(a)(iii) Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{mp}}{G \cdot A} = \frac{6.72}{800 \times 0.06} = \frac{6.72}{48} = 0.14 = 14 \text{ \%}$$

**3 marks**

**A(b)(i) Open-circuit TEG voltage:**

$$V_{th} = N \cdot S \cdot \Delta T = 200 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 30 = 1.2 \text{ V}$$

**2 marks**

**A(b)(ii) Maximum power:**

$$V_{mp} = V_{th} / 2 = 0.6 \text{ V}$$

$$I_{mp} = V_{mp} / R_{int} = 0.6 / 5 = 0.12 \text{ A}$$

$$P_{mp} = 0.6 \times 0.12 = 0.072 \text{ W} = 72 \text{ mW}$$

**2 marks**

**A(b)(iii) Duty cycle:**

$$V_{out} = \frac{V_{in}}{1 - D} \Rightarrow 3.7 = \frac{0.6}{1 - D}$$

$$1 - D = \frac{0.6}{3.7} = 0.1622$$

$$D = 0.8378 \approx 0.84$$

**3 marks**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **solar-energy-harvesting IoT node**. List the function of each block. The PV panel produces $V_{oc} = 6.0 \text{ V}$ at $G = 1000 \text{ W/m}^2$ and $I_{sc} = 0.8 \text{ A}$, with a Fill Factor of 0.72. The IoT load draws $I_{load} = 100 \text{ mA}$ at $V_{load} = 3.3 \text{ V}$ continuously, and is supported by a 3.3 V, 1500 mAh Li-ion battery with a depth-of-discharge limit of 80 %. Compute **(i)** the harvested power at MPP, **(ii)** the average load power, and **(iii)** the autonomy time (in hours) of the battery if the harvester fails completely. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A piezoelectric vibration harvester has $m = 5 \text{ g}$, $\omega_n = 2\pi \times 60 \text{ rad/s}$, $\zeta = 0.04$, and vibration amplitude $Y = 0.3 \text{ mm}$. The harvester output is rectified and stored in a $C = 4.7 \text{ F}$ supercapacitor pre-charged to $V_0 = 1.0 \text{ V}$. Compute **(i)** the maximum harvested power, **(ii)** the power delivered to a matched load, and **(iii)** how long the supercapacitor takes to charge from 1.0 V to 3.0 V at constant matched-load power. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**B(a)(i) Harvested power at MPP:**

$$P_{mp} = V_{oc} \cdot I_{sc} \cdot FF = 6.0 \times 0.8 \times 0.72 = 3.456 \text{ W}$$

**2 marks**

**B(a)(ii) Load power:**

$$P_{load} = V_{load} \cdot I_{load} = 3.3 \times 0.1 = 0.33 \text{ W}$$

**2 marks**

**B(a)(iii) Battery autonomy:**

$$E_{usable} = V_{bat} \times C_{bat} \times DoD = 3.3 \times 1.5 \times 0.80 = 3.96 \text{ Wh}$$

$$t_{autonomy} = \frac{E_{usable}}{P_{load}} = \frac{3.96}{0.33} = 12.0 \text{ hours}$$

**3 marks**

**B(b)(i) Max harvested power:**

$$\omega_n = 2\pi \times 60 = 376.99 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_n^3 = (376.99)^3 = 5.358 \times 10^{7}$$

$$P_{h,max} = 0.5 \times 0.04 \times 0.005 \times 5.358 \times 10^{7} \times (3 \times 10^{-4})^2$$

$$P_{h,max} = 0.5 \times 0.04 \times 0.005 \times 5.358 \times 10^{7} \times 9 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$P_{h,max} = 4.821 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W} \approx 0.482 \text{ mW}$$

**2 marks**

**B(b)(ii) Matched-load power:**

$$P_{load,max} = 0.5 \times P_{h,max} = 0.241 \text{ mW}$$

**2 marks**

**B(b)(iii) Charging time:**

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} C (V_f^2 - V_i^2) = 0.5 \times 4.7 \times (3.0^2 - 1.0^2) = 2.35 \times 8.0 = 18.8 \text{ J}$$

$$t = \frac{\Delta E}{P_{load,max}} = \frac{18.8}{0.241 \times 10^{-3}} = 78008 \text{ s} \approx 21.7 \text{ hours}$$

**3 marks**

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 3 Examples**

- **PV cell** current scales **linearly with irradiance**; voltage scales **logarithmically** and is sensitive to **temperature (decreases)**.
- **Fill Factor** is the most important quality metric of a PV cell — higher $FF$ means squarer $I$-$V$ curve and better MPP proximity.
- **Efficiency formula**: $\eta = \dfrac{P_{mp}}{G \cdot A}$ — always check whether $G$ is in $\text{W/m}^2$ and $A$ in $\text{m}^2$.
- **Piezoelectric** $P_h \propto m \cdot \omega_n^3 \cdot Y^2$ — resonance frequency and vibration amplitude are the two design levers.
- At **resonance**, the harvester power is **maximized**; away from resonance, power falls as $(\omega/\omega_n)^2$ correction.
- **TEG** $V_{th} = N \cdot S \cdot \Delta T$ — to get volts-level output, you need **hundreds of thermocouples** stacked.
- **Maximum power transfer** from a low-impedance source requires the load to **equal the source internal resistance** — always set $R_{load} = R_{int}$ for harvester sizing.
- **Boost converter** $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1 - D}$ — high $D$ (approaching 1) gives very high gain but the converter enters **discontinuous conduction mode (DCM)** and efficiency drops.
- **Buck-Boost** inverts polarity; **SEPIC and Ćuk** topologies avoid inversion and are sometimes preferred for battery charging.
- **P&O MPPT** is simple but oscillates around MPP; **Incremental Conductance** is steady-state accurate but computationally heavier.
- **Supercapacitor energy** is $\frac{1}{2}CV^2$ — usable energy is only the **difference** between $V_{max}^2$ and $V_{min}^2$, never the full stored energy.
- **Battery sizing**: always include a **depth-of-discharge (DoD)** factor (typically 0.8 for Li-ion) — never size a battery at 100 % of its raw capacity.
- **RF harvesting** power falls as $1/d^2$ — practical IoT RF harvesting works only within **$\le 10$–$20$ m** of a dedicated transmitter.
- **Rule of thumb for Kerala climate**: derate PV output by **15–20 %** from STC rating for honest, climate-adjusted design.
- **Always carry units** in intermediate steps and the final answer — KTU examiners explicitly mark this.
- **Always state assumptions** (ideal converter, CCM, matched load) before solving — a 1-mark component KTU examiners frequently award.
- **Hybrid topologies** (PV + piezo, TEG + supercap) are an emerging and **high-weightage** research area — be ready to discuss trade-offs.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Case Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT] 
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard)**: A *Case Study* in Energy Harvesting & Power Management for IoT is a **detailed, systems-level engineering analysis of a real-world deployed IoT node or network** in which ambient energy (solar, thermal, kinetic, RF, or biological) is converted into usable electrical energy to power sensors, microcontrollers, and wireless transceivers, together with the power management circuitry (rectifiers, MPPT, regulators, energy storage) that conditions, stores, and dispenses this energy to achieve *energy-neutral* or *battery-less* operation.

A case study in this domain is not merely a "product description." It is a **quantitative engineering critique** that examines:

1. The **energy source** and its physical transduction mechanism.
2. The **power processing electronics** (AC–DC, DC–DC, MPPT).
3. The **storage element** (supercapacitor, thin-film battery, Li-ion).
4. The **load profile** of the sensor, MCU, and radio.
5. The **duty-cycling** and power management algorithm.
6. The **net energy balance** ($E_{harvested} \geq E_{consumed}$).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Salary vs. Expense Problem**: Think of an IoT sensor node as a **salaried person living paycheck-to-paycheck**. The **harvester** is the *job* (salary that comes in), the **battery** is the *savings account*, and the **sensor + radio** is the *monthly expense*. A good IoT case study is like a **financial planner's case file**: it shows *how much money comes in*, *how much goes out*, *how the savings buffer is sized*, and *what lifestyle adjustments (duty-cycling)* keep the person solvent. If expenses exceed income for too long, the system goes bankrupt (node dies).

### 1.3 Why Case Studies are Critical for KTU Evaluation

The KTU 2024 Scheme places case studies in Module 3 specifically to:

- **Bridge theory and practice**: Students must connect transducer physics (Module 1) and power electronics (Module 2) to *deployed* systems.
- **Develop design judgement**: A student who has analyzed 6–8 real deployments can *intelligently select* a harvesting technology for a new application.
- **Quantify trade-offs**: Real systems force the student to balance **power density vs. form factor vs. cost vs. reliability**.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics Used Across Case Studies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) — Memorize for KTU**:
> - **Power Density ($\mu W/cm^2$ or $mW/cm^3$)** — Energy available per unit area/volume.
> - **Duty Cycle ($\delta$)** — Fraction of time the node is active: $\delta = t_{active}/T_{period}$.
> - **Energy Neutral Operation (ENO)** — Condition: $E_{harvested} \geq E_{consumed}$ over each period.
> - **Figure of Merit (FoM)** — Ratio $P_{harvested}/P_{consumed}$ (must be $\geq 1$).
> - **Lifetime (years)** — Time until battery degrades to 80% capacity.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four-Layer Analytical Framework for Case Studies

Every IoT energy harvesting case study, regardless of application, can be decomposed into **four functional layers**. The KTU examiner expects students to identify and discuss each layer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 1 — Ambient Energy Source**: The physical reservoir (sunlight, heat gradient, vibration, RF field, body motion). Characterized by **availability profile** (continuous / intermittent / sporadic) and **power density**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 2 — Transducer & Power Conditioning**: Converts ambient energy to DC electricity. Includes the harvester (PV cell, TEG, PZT, antenna) plus rectification (bridge rectifier, Schottky, active rectifier) and DC–DC conversion (boost, buck-boost, MPPT).

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 3 — Energy Storage & Management**: Stores surplus energy (supercapacitor, Li-ion, thin-film battery) and dispenses it to the load via a **Power Management Unit (PMU)** such as the TI BQ25570 or Linear LTC3108.

> [!NOTE]
> **Layer 4 — Sensing, Processing & Communication**: The load side — sensors, MCU (MSP430, ARM Cortex-M0+), and radio (BLE, ZigBee, LoRa, NB-IoT). The largest power consumer is typically the **radio**, not the sensor.

### 2.2 The Energy Budget Equation (Most Critical KTU Formula)

For **Energy Neutral Operation (ENO)**, the energy harvested per duty cycle period $T$ must equal or exceed the energy consumed:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{harvested}(T) &\geq E_{consumed}(T) \\
P_{avg,harvest} \cdot T &\geq P_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep} + P_{active} \cdot t_{active} \\
P_{avg,harvest} \cdot T &\geq P_{sleep}(T - t_{active}) + P_{active} \cdot t_{active}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving for the **minimum duty cycle** that ENO permits:

$$
\delta_{min} = \frac{t_{active}}{T} = \frac{P_{avg,harvest} - P_{sleep}}{P_{active} - P_{sleep}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap**: When $P_{avg,harvest} < P_{sleep}$, the equation yields a **negative $\delta_{min}$** — this means the system is **physically incapable** of energy-neutral operation, and the case study must flag this as a design failure.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Symbol** | **Meaning** | **Unit** | **Typical KTU Value** |
|------------|-------------|----------|----------------------|
| $P_{PV}$ | Photovoltaic harvested power | $\mu W$ | 10 – 10,000 |
| $\eta_{PV}$ | PV cell efficiency | % | 15 – 22 |
| $P_{TEG}$ | Thermoelectric harvested power | $\mu W$ | 100 – 10,000 |
| $S$ | Seebeck coefficient | $\mu V/K$ | 200 – 400 |
| $\Delta T$ | Thermal gradient | K | 5 – 50 |
| $P_{PZT}$ | Piezoelectric harvested power | $\mu W$ | 1 – 1,000 |
| $f_v$ | Vibration frequency | Hz | 50 – 200 |
| $P_{RF}$ | RF harvested power density | $\mu W/cm^2$ | 0.01 – 1 |
| $C_{bat}$ | Battery capacity | mAh | 1 – 1000 |
| $V_{bat}$ | Battery nominal voltage | V | 3.0 – 3.7 |
| $P_{sleep}$ | Sleep power (MCU + sensor) | $\mu W$ | 1 – 10 |
| $P_{active}$ | Active power (with TX) | mW | 10 – 200 |
| $E_{cycle}$ | Energy per TX cycle | mJ | 0.05 – 5 |
| $t_{life}$ | Battery lifetime | years | 1 – 20 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe Symbol in LaTeX Tables**: Note that in the formula sheet above, all set-theoretic / absolute-value vertical bars are rendered using the LaTeX commands `\vert` or `\mid` (not the markdown table delimiter `\vert`). This is critical to prevent markdown table parser crashes in KTU online portals.

### 2.4 Selection Logic — When to Choose Which Harvester

A frequent KTU Part-B question is: *"Given an application X, justify your choice of harvester."* The decision matrix is:

| **Application Scenario** | **Best Harvester** | **Reason** |
|--------------------------|--------------------|-----------|
| Indoor office light monitoring | Indoor PV (amorphous Si) | Predictable indoor lighting, low cost |
| Industrial boiler pipe monitoring | TEG (thermoelectric) | Large $\Delta T$ available |
| Bridge vibration / traffic monitoring | Piezoelectric | Continuous mechanical excitation |
| Wearable heart-rate monitor | Hybrid (PV + piezo) | Dual-mode body energy |
| Remote agricultural soil sensor | Outdoor PV + battery | High solar exposure, low duty cycle |
| Indoor asset tracking | RF harvesting | Ambient Wi-Fi/cellular RF available |
| Underwater pipeline monitor | Thermoelectric / flow-induced | No light, but thermal gradient exists |

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Case Study Walkthrough: Solar-Powered LoRaWAN Weather Station

> [!NOTE]
> **System Specification (Real-World Deployed Example)**:
> - Location: Rooftop, equatorial latitude, average solar irradiance $G = 400\ W/m^2$ (effective daytime average).
> - PV panel: amorphous silicon, area $A = 4\ cm^2 = 4 \times 10^{-4}\ m^2$, efficiency $\eta = 8\%$ (indoor-grade a-Si typical).
> - Load: STM32L0 MCU + BME280 sensor + Semtech SX1276 LoRa radio.
> - Sleep power: $P_{sleep} = 5\ \mu W$.
> - Active power (sensing + LoRa TX at SF7, 14 dBm): $P_{active} = 120\ mW$.
> - Active time per cycle: $t_{active} = 3\ s$.
> - Reporting period: $T = 600\ s$ (every 10 minutes).

#### Step 1 — Compute Average Harvested Power

The instantaneous harvested power is:

$$
P_{PV} = \eta \cdot G \cdot A
$$

Substituting values:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{PV} &= 0.08 \times 400\ W/m^2 \times 4 \times 10^{-4}\ m^2 \\
&= 0.08 \times 0.16\ W \\
&= 0.0128\ W \\
&= 12.8\ mW
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation**: During daylight, the harvester produces **12.8 mW** continuously.

But the system only operates **12 hours per day** in sunlight; the rest is night. So the **24-hour average** is:

$$
P_{avg,harvest} = 12.8\ mW \times \frac{12\ h}{24\ h} = 6.4\ mW
$$

#### Step 2 — Compute Energy Per Cycle

Energy harvested per 10-min cycle:

$$
E_{harvest} = P_{PV} \times T = 12.8\ mW \times 600\ s = 7680\ mJ \quad \text{(during day)}
$$

Energy consumed per cycle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{consumed} &= P_{sleep} \times t_{sleep} + P_{active} \times t_{active} \\
&= 5\ \mu W \times 597\ s + 120\ mW \times 3\ s \\
&= 0.002985\ J + 0.36\ J \\
&\approx 0.363\ J = 363\ mJ
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 3 — Energy Neutral Check

$$
E_{harvest} = 7680\ mJ \quad \gg \quad E_{consumed} = 363\ mJ
$$

$$
\Rightarrow \quad \text{Energy Neutral Operation (ENO)}: \text{SUCCESS by a factor of } \approx 21\times
$$

The system has a **huge energy surplus**, which is stored in a small Li-ion battery (e.g., 100 mAh @ 3.7 V = 1332 J) to survive nighttime.

#### Step 4 — Nighttime Survival Time

The battery has stored energy $E_{bat} = 100\ mAh \times 3.7\ V = 370\ mWh = 1332\ J$.

At night, $P_{harvest} \approx 0$, so the battery drains at rate $P_{consumed,avg}$:

$$
P_{consumed,avg} = \frac{E_{consumed}}{T} = \frac{363\ mJ}{600\ s} = 0.605\ mW
$$

Nighttime runtime:

$$
t_{night} = \frac{E_{bat}}{P_{consumed,avg}} = \frac{1332\ J}{0.605 \times 10^{-3}\ J/s} = 2.2 \times 10^{6}\ s \approx 25.5\ days
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion**: This system can survive 25 consecutive overcast days without any sun — an excellent design margin. The KTU answer should explicitly compute this margin.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Case Study Summary

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix is a **high-yield KTU revision table** covering the canonical case studies expected in the 2024 scheme syllabus.

| **Case Study** | **Energy Source** | **Power Generated** | **Storage** | **Radio** | **Lifetime / Outcome** |
|----------------|-------------------|---------------------|-------------|-----------|------------------------|
| **EnOcean Self-Powered Switch** | Kinetic (finger press) | ~50 $\mu W$ per press | None (capacitor) | 868 MHz proprietary | Battery-less, 25+ years |
| **Solar Weather Station** | Outdoor PV | 1 – 50 mW | Li-ion 100 mAh | LoRaWAN | 5 – 10 years |
| **TEG on Industrial Stack** | Waste heat ($\Delta T \sim 100 K$) | 1 – 100 mW | Supercap 1 F | ZigBee | Maintenance-free |
| **Piezo Bridge Monitor** | Traffic vibration | 0.1 – 5 mW | NiMH + supercap | BLE | 10+ years |
| **RF-Powered Smart Card** | Ambient 900 MHz | 0.1 – 10 $\mu W$ | Capacitor 100 $\mu F$ | Backscatter | Unlimited (within RF field) |
| **Wearable ECG Patch** | Body heat + motion | 10 – 500 $\mu W$ | LiPo 50 mAh | BLE | 7 – 14 days continuous |
| **Smart Agriculture Soil Sensor** | Solar + soil microbial | 1 – 10 mW | Li-ion | LoRa / NB-IoT | 5 – 7 years |
| **Smart Meter (Gas/Water)** | Vibration / flow turbine | 0.1 – 1 mW | Supercap | Wireless M-Bus | 20 years (no battery) |

### 3.3 Detailed Energy Derivation for Case Study 1 (EnOcean Kinetic Switch)

The EnOcean switch harvests energy from a **finger press** of force $F \approx 5\ N$ over a displacement $d \approx 4\ mm$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{press} &= F \times d \\
&= 5\ N \times 4 \times 10^{-3}\ m \\
&= 0.02\ J = 20\ mJ
\end{aligned}
$$

Conversion efficiency of the electromagnetic harvester: $\eta_{mech \rightarrow elec} \approx 25\%$.

$$
E_{elec} = 0.25 \times 20\ mJ = 5\ mJ
$$

This **5 mJ** is sufficient to:
- Power up the microcontroller (~1 mJ).
- Transmit 3 sub-telegrams at 868 MHz (~1.3 mJ each, total ~4 mJ).

$$
E_{budget} = 1\ mJ\ (MCU) + 4\ mJ\ (TX) = 5\ mJ = E_{elec}
$$

> **Interpretation**: The system operates with **zero energy storage** — every press is exactly enough for a complete transmission cycle. This is the gold standard of energy-neutral design.

### 3.4 Case Study 4 — Piezoelectric Bridge Monitor (Duty Cycle Analysis)

> [!NOTE]
> **Specification**: PZT patch on highway bridge, RMS vibration amplitude $a = 0.5\ m/s^2$ at $f = 80\ Hz$, harvester footprint $5 \times 5\ cm^2$, harvested power $P_{PZT} = 200\ \mu W$ (continuous). Load: BLE beacon transmitting every $T = 1\ s$ with $t_{active} = 3\ ms$ at $P_{active} = 15\ mW$.

Energy harvested per beacon period:

$$
E_{harvest} = 200\ \mu W \times 1\ s = 200\ \mu J
$$

Energy consumed per beacon period:

$$
E_{consumed} = 15\ mW \times 3\ ms = 45\ \mu J
$$

$$
\frac{E_{harvest}}{E_{consumed}} = \frac{200}{45} \approx 4.4
$$

> **Conclusion**: A **4.4× energy margin** is excellent. The harvester can support the BLE beacon continuously even with light traffic.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Universal Case Study Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AMB[Ambient Energy Source] --> TR[Transducer PV TEG PZT RF Antenna]
    TR --> RC[Rectifier AC to DC Bridge or Schottky]
    RC --> MPPT[MPPT or DC DC Converter]
    MPPT --> ST[Energy Storage Li ion or Supercap]
    ST --> PMU[Power Management Unit BQ25570 LTC3108]
    PMU --> MCU[Sensor plus Microcontroller STM32 or MSP430]
    MCU --> RAD[Radio BLE LoRa ZigBee NB IoT]
    RAD --> ANT[Antenna TX to Gateway]
    PMU -. monitors .- ST
    MCU -. duty cycle control .- PMU
```

### 4.2 EnOcean Kinetic Switch — Detailed Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Finger Press Mechanical Force F 5N] --> B[Spring Energy Storage E 20mJ]
    B --> C[Electromagnetic Induction Coil Movement in Magnetic Field]
    C --> D[AC Voltage Output Few Hundred mV Peak]
    D --> E[Active Rectifier or Bridge]
    E --> F[Storage Capacitor 100uF to 1mF]
    F --> G[DC DC Boost to 3V]
    G --> H[Energy Manager EnOcean Dolphin Chip]
    H --> I[868 MHz Transmitter 3 Sub Packets]
    I --> J[Receiver Gateway to BMS]
    H -. wakes MCU on capacitor charge .-> H
```

### 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Which Harvester to Choose (KTU Exam Favorite)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Application Environment} -->|Outdoor Sunny| A1[Photovoltaic with MPPT]
    Q1 -->|Indoor Light Only| A2[Indoor a Si PV or Dye Sensitized]
    Q1 -->|Hot Surface Available| A3[Thermoelectric Generator TEG]
    Q1 -->|Vibration or Motion| A4[Piezoelectric or Electromagnetic]
    Q1 -->|Ambient RF WiFi GSM| A5[RF Rectenna]
    Q1 -->|Body Worn| A6[Hybrid PV plus Piezo plus TEG]

    A1 --> Z[Estimate Power Density and Compare to Load mW]
    A2 --> Z
    A3 --> Z
    A4 --> Z
    A5 --> Z
    A6 --> Z

    Z --> C1{Power Available Exceeds Load Sleep plus Active Energy}
    C1 -->|Yes| D1[Design Energy Neutral Node ENO Achieved]
    C1 -->|No| D2[Add Larger Storage or Reduce Duty Cycle]
    D2 --> C1
```

### 4.4 Comparison Schematic — Power Density Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph HighPower[High Power Density mW per cm2]
        H1[Outdoor PV 10 to 20 mW per cm2]
        H2[TEG high delta T 1 to 10 mW per cm2]
    end
    subgraph MidPower[Medium Power Density 10 to 100 uW per cm2]
        M1[Indoor PV 10 to 100 uW per cm2]
        M2[Piezo strong vibration 10 to 100 uW per cm2]
    end
    subgraph LowPower[Low Power Density less than 1 uW per cm2]
        L1[Ambient RF 0.01 to 1 uW per cm2]
        L2[Body heat passive 0.1 to 1 uW per cm2]
    end
    HighPower --> N[IoT Load Demand]
    MidPower --> N
    LowPower --> N
```

### 4.5 Energy Neutral Operation (ENO) Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    H[Harvester P_harv] --> M[MPPT and Regulator]
    M --> B[Battery or Supercap E_storage]
    B --> PMU[Power Management Unit]
    PMU --> L[Load P_sleep plus P_active]
    L -. energy feedback .-> PMU
    B -. state of charge .-> PMU
    PMU -. duty cycle delta .-> L
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 (Model Paper)]**
> *Define "Energy Neutral Operation (ENO)" in the context of an IoT node powered by energy harvesting. Mention the mathematical condition that must be satisfied for ENO.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Energy Neutral Operation (ENO) refers to the operating regime of an IoT node in which the energy harvested from ambient sources over a defined time period is **greater than or equal to** the total energy consumed by the sensor, microcontroller, and radio during the same period. The mathematical condition is:

$$
E_{harvested}(T) \geq E_{consumed}(T)
$$

Equivalently, in terms of average power:

$$
P_{avg,harvest} \geq P_{sleep}(1-\delta) + P_{active} \cdot \delta
$$

where $\delta$ is the duty cycle. ENO allows the node to operate indefinitely (theoretically) without battery replacement, provided the storage element can buffer the short-term mismatch between harvesting and consumption.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 (Model Paper)]**
> *List FOUR real-world case studies of energy harvesting IoT systems and identify the ambient energy source used in each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — listing 4 with sources):**

1. **EnOcean Self-Powered Wireless Switch** — Source: **Kinetic (finger-press mechanical energy)**.
2. **Solar-Powered LoRaWAN Weather Station** — Source: **Outdoor photovoltaic (sunlight)**.
3. **Thermoelectric Generator on Industrial Boiler/Stack** — Source: **Waste heat (Seebeck effect)**.
4. **Piezoelectric Bridge Health Monitor** — Source: **Traffic-induced vibration**.
5. *(Optional 5th — bonus)* RF-powered smart card — Source: **Ambient radio-frequency (GSM/Wi-Fi) energy**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a block diagram, explain the **four-layer architecture of an energy harvesting IoT node**. Discuss the function of each layer and give one example component for each. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A solar-powered IoT weather station uses an amorphous silicon PV panel of area $6\ cm^2$ with efficiency 9%. The effective daytime average solar irradiance is $G = 350\ W/m^2$ for 10 hours per day. The node consumes $P_{sleep} = 8\ \mu W$ in sleep mode and $P_{active} = 150\ mW$ for $t_{active} = 2\ s$ every $T = 900\ s$. The node has a 200 mAh Li-ion battery at 3.7 V. Calculate:
>   - (i) The 24-hour average harvested power.
>   - (ii) The energy harvested and energy consumed per cycle.
>   - (iii) The maximum number of consecutive cloudy days the node can survive.
> **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four-Layer Architecture — 7 marks**

The block diagram (refer to Section 4.1 of these notes) shows the following layers:

**Layer 1 — Ambient Energy Source**: The physical reservoir from which energy is extracted. Examples: sunlight, thermal gradient, mechanical vibration, RF field, body heat. Characterized by **availability profile** and **power density**.

**Layer 2 — Transducer & Power Conditioning**: A transducer (PV cell, TEG, PZT, antenna) converts the ambient energy into AC or DC electricity. Power conditioning includes rectification (bridge or active rectifier) and DC–DC conversion (buck/boost with MPPT). Example component: **Texas Instruments BQ25570** energy harvesting IC.

**Layer 3 — Energy Storage & Management**: Stores surplus energy for use during low-harvest periods. Examples: **supercapacitor** (fast charge/discharge, long cycle life), **Li-ion battery** (high energy density), **thin-film solid-state battery**. The PMU manages charge/discharge and load switching.

**Layer 4 — Sensing, Processing & Communication**: The load side. Includes the **sensor** (e.g., BME280 for temperature/humidity/pressure), **microcontroller** (e.g., STM32L0, MSP430), and **radio transceiver** (BLE, LoRa, ZigBee, NB-IoT). Power consumption is dominated by the **radio TX bursts**.

The key engineering challenge is the **inter-layer impedance matching** and **duty-cycling strategy** to maintain ENO.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution — 7 marks**

**(i) Average harvested power (24-hour basis):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{PV} &= \eta \cdot G \cdot A \\
&= 0.09 \times 350\ W/m^2 \times 6 \times 10^{-4}\ m^2 \\
&= 0.09 \times 0.21 \\
&= 0.0189\ W = 18.9\ mW
\end{aligned}
$$

Daytime hours = 10 h, so 24-hour average:

$$
P_{avg,harvest} = 18.9\ mW \times \frac{10}{24} = 7.875\ mW
$$

**(ii) Energy per 900-s cycle:**

Energy harvested per cycle (assuming full sun during the cycle):

$$
E_{harvest} = P_{PV} \times T = 18.9\ mW \times 900\ s = 17,010\ mJ = 17.01\ J
$$

Energy consumed per cycle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{consumed} &= P_{sleep} \times (T - t_{active}) + P_{active} \times t_{active} \\
&= 8 \times 10^{-6}\ W \times 898\ s + 0.150\ W \times 2\ s \\
&= 7.184 \times 10^{-3}\ J + 0.300\ J \\
&\approx 0.3072\ J = 307.2\ mJ
\end{aligned}
$$

The harvested energy is **~55× larger** than consumed. ENO is strongly satisfied.

**(iii) Cloudy-day survival:**

Battery total energy:

$$
E_{bat} = 200\ mAh \times 3.7\ V = 740\ mWh = 2664\ J
$$

Average consumption rate:

$$
P_{avg,cons} = \frac{E_{consumed}}{T} = \frac{0.3072\ J}{900\ s} = 3.41 \times 10^{-4}\ W = 0.341\ mW
$$

Maximum runtime on battery alone (zero harvest):

$$
t_{runtime} = \frac{E_{bat}}{P_{avg,cons}} = \frac{2664\ J}{3.41 \times 10^{-4}\ J/s} = 7.81 \times 10^{6}\ s
$$

Convert to days:

$$
t_{days} = \frac{7.81 \times 10^{6}}{86,400} \approx 90.4\ days
$$

> **Answer**: The node can survive approximately **90 consecutive cloudy days** without sunlight, which is an excellent design margin.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE — Internal Choice]**
>
> **(a)** Describe the **EnOcean self-powered wireless switch** as a case study of energy harvesting in building automation. With a block diagram, explain the energy flow from finger press to RF transmission. Compute the energy per press and show that it is sufficient for a 3-packet transmission. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare and contrast **three case studies** of energy harvesting IoT systems (e.g., solar weather station, TEG industrial monitor, piezoelectric bridge sensor) in terms of: ambient source, harvested power, storage element, communication protocol, and achievable node lifetime. Present your answer in a **comparison table** with at least 5 parameters. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Highlights):**

**(a) EnOcean Case Study — Key points:**

- **Mechanism**: Finger press on a rocker arm compresses a spring and drives a magnet through a coil — electromagnetic induction.
- **Energy per press**: $E_{press} = F \times d = 5\ N \times 4\ mm = 20\ mJ$. With $\eta \approx 25\%$, electrical energy = **5 mJ**.
- **Power conditioning**: AC output → bridge rectifier → storage capacitor (100 $\mu F$ – 1 mF) → DC–DC boost to 3 V → EnOcean Dolphin chip.
- **Transmission**: 3 sub-packets at 868 MHz, each consuming ~1.3 mJ = 4 mJ total, plus 1 mJ for MCU wake-up = 5 mJ total.
- **Outcome**: Battery-less operation, lifetime > 25 years (limited by capacitor aging, not energy).

**(b) Comparison Table — 7 marks (sample answer):**

| **Parameter** | **Solar Weather Station** | **TEG Industrial Monitor** | **Piezo Bridge Sensor** |
|---------------|---------------------------|----------------------------|--------------------------|
| Ambient Source | Sunlight (outdoor PV) | Waste heat (stack/pipe) | Traffic vibration |
| Harvested Power | 1 – 50 mW | 1 – 100 mW | 0.1 – 5 mW |
| Storage | Li-ion 100 mAh | Supercapacitor 1 F | Supercap + NiMH |
| Radio | LoRaWAN | ZigBee | BLE |
| Lifetime | 5 – 10 years | 10 – 20 years | 10+ years |
| Best Use | Open-field, sunny | High $\Delta T$ industrial sites | High-traffic bridges |
| Limitation | Cloudy regions | Requires thermal gradient | Requires sustained vibration |

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Case Study Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing transducer with source**: Writing *"solar cell"* instead of *"sunlight"* as the energy source. The source is the **ambient energy**; the transducer is the **device** that converts it.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units**: Always write units ($\mu W$, $mJ$, $cm^2$). A numerically correct answer without units is penalized.
>
> 3. **Ignoring 24-hour average**: Students compute $P_{PV}$ at peak irradiance and assume it is available 24/7. Real case studies must use **time-averaged** power.
>
> 4. **Skipping the ENO check**: Numerical problems must explicitly verify $E_{harvest} \geq E_{consumed}$. A value without comparison is incomplete.
>
> 5. **Not labeling block diagrams**: Every block in a Mermaid/hand-drawn diagram must be labeled. Unlabeled boxes fetch zero marks.
>
> 6. **Missing duty cycle in energy calculation**: The active time $t_{active}$ is often minutes or seconds per day, but its peak power is 1000× the sleep power. Forgetting to weight by duty cycle produces wrong answers.
>
> 7. **Confusing $mAh$ with $J$**: Battery capacity is in $mAh$, but energy is $mAh \times V = mWh$. Convert explicitly.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points — KTU 2024 Scheme PECIT746 Module 3**

- **Case Study = Quantitative Engineering Analysis**, not a product description. Always include the four layers: source → transducer → storage → load.
- **ENO Equation (MUST memorize)**: $P_{avg,harvest} \geq P_{sleep}(1-\delta) + P_{active}\delta$.
- **Duty Cycle Formula**: $\delta = \dfrac{P_{harvest} - P_{sleep}}{P_{active} - P_{sleep}}$.
- **Battery Energy**: $E_{bat} = C \cdot V$ (in Wh), where $C$ is in Ah.
- **Eight Canonical Case Studies to Memorize**:
  1. EnOcean kinetic switch (battery-less, 25 yr).
  2. Solar LoRaWAN weather station (5–10 yr).
  3. TEG on industrial stack (10–20 yr).
  4. Piezo bridge sensor (10+ yr).
  5. RF smart card (battery-less, unlimited).
  6. Wearable ECG (7–14 days).
  7. Smart agriculture soil sensor (5–7 yr).
  8. Smart gas/water meter (20 yr).
- **Power Density Hierarchy (descending)**: Outdoor PV > TEG > Indoor PV ≈ Piezo > RF > Body heat.
- **Selection Rule**: Match the harvester's availability profile (continuous / intermittent / sporadic) to the application's duty cycle.
- **Storage Trade-off**: Supercapacitor = fast charge, long life, low energy density. Li-ion = high energy density, slow charge, limited cycle life.
- **Standard KPIs**: Power density, duty cycle, ENO, FoM, lifetime in years.
- **Common Pitfall**: A negative $\delta_{min}$ from the duty-cycle equation is a design FAILURE flag, not a "valid low duty cycle."
- **Numerical Skill**: Always convert $\mu W$ ↔ $mW$ ↔ $W$ before adding powers.
- **Diagram Rule**: Use block diagrams showing energy flow direction with arrows; label every block.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Harvesting Microelectronic Circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Harvesting Microelectronic Circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Harvesting Microelectronic Circuits (HMCs)** are the dedicated analog, mixed-signal, and power-electronics integrated circuits (ICs) that interface directly between an ambient energy transducer (solar cell, thermoelectric generator, piezoelectric cantilever, RF antenna, etc.) and the downstream energy-storage and load elements of an IoT node. Their function is to perform **rectification, voltage conditioning, maximum power point tracking (MPPT), voltage regulation, and impedance matching** such that the minuscule, noisy, time-varying raw transducer output is converted into a clean, stable DC rail suitable for ultra-low-power sensors, microcontrollers, and radios.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, these circuits sit in the **power-conditioning layer** of an energy harvesting system. They are typically designed in standard CMOS technologies (180 nm, 65 nm, 28 nm) and must achieve conversion efficiencies exceeding **80%** at output power levels in the **microwatt to milliwatt** range (10 µW – 10 mW).

The key sub-blocks of a harvesting microelectronic circuit are:

- **Rectifier / AC-DC Converter** – converts the alternating transducer output (or the chopped/oscillated output of vibration harvesters) into a unipolar signal.
- **Voltage Multiplier / Charge Pump** – stacks multiple diode-capacitor stages to multiply a low input voltage above the IC's threshold voltage.
- **DC-DC Converter** – boosts or bucks the rectified DC to the regulated rail voltage (commonly 1.8 V, 3.3 V).
- **Maximum Power Point Tracker (MPPT)** – continuously adjusts the operating point of the harvester to extract peak available power.
- **Power Management Unit (PMU) / PMIC** – orchestrates cold-start, load switching, and battery/supercapacitor charging.
- **Energy Storage Interface** – charger circuit for Li-ion batteries or supercapacitors with over-charge and deep-discharge protection.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Tower" Picture

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — Tiny Trickle, Big Demand:** Imagine an energy harvester as a small, irregular mountain spring (drops of water coming unpredictably from solar, heat, or vibration). An IoT sensor is a small village downstream that needs a constant water supply. **Harvesting microelectronic circuits are the plumbing, the pumps, the water tower, and the pressure regulator combined.** The rectifier acts like a one-way valve letting the trickle flow only into the tank. The charge pump / DC-DC converter is the pressurising pump that lifts every drop to a usable height. The MPPT is the engineer who constantly adjusts the pump's speed to match the spring's flow. The supercapacitor/battery is the storage tank, and the PMU is the smart controller that decides when the village can drink.

This mental picture is exactly how KTU examiners expect you to describe these circuits in 14-mark descriptive answers: *block-level functional view* first, then component-level detail.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

The following are the **standard numerical constants** that you must memorise for KTU University Exams:

- Thermal voltage at room temperature: **V_T = kT/q ≈ 25.85 mV (≈ 26 mV)**
- Standard silicon diode cut-in voltage: **V_d ≈ 0.7 V**
- Schottky diode cut-in voltage: **V_d ≈ 0.3 V**
- Sub-threshold slope (CMOS, 65 nm): **S ≈ 90 mV/decade**
- Typical IoT rail voltages: **V_DD = 1.8 V, 3.3 V**
- Energy densities: **Li-ion ≈ 250 Wh/kg, Supercapacitor ≈ 5–10 Wh/kg**
- Conversion efficiency target for a good HMC: **η ≥ 80% at 100 µW – 1 mW**
- Cold-start input voltage: **V_in_min ≈ 20–300 mV** (depending on the transducer)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Rectifier — AC-to-DC Conversion

The rectifier is the **first** block in the microelectronic chain. The choice between passive and active rectifiers is a frequently-asked KTU question.

#### 2.1.1 Passive Diode Rectifier (Half-Wave & Full-Wave)
