In a **half-wave rectifier**, only one half-cycle of the input sinusoid passes through a single diode; the other half is blocked. The DC component of the output is:

$$V_{DC} = \frac{V_m}{\pi}$$

For a **full-wave bridge rectifier**, both half-cycles are rectified, giving:

$$V_{DC} = \frac{2 V_m}{\pi}$$

A **voltage doubler** (Villard/Dickson stage) stacks two diode-capacitor cells in series at the output, giving:

$$V_{out} = 2 V_{in} - 2 V_{d}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this is a problem for energy harvesting:** Standard silicon diodes drop **0.7 V** each. If a thermoelectric generator (TEG) produces only 50–200 mV, the diode drops 70× the available signal — total loss. This is the central engineering reason that **Schottky diodes** (V_d ≈ 0.3 V) and **active MOSFET rectifiers** (V_d ≈ 30 mV) are mandatory in HMC design.

#### 2.1.2 Active MOSFET Rectifier

A diode-connected NMOS or a comparator-driven synchronous MOSFET replaces the passive diode. The voltage drop becomes:

$$V_{drop} = I_{load} \cdot R_{ds(on)}$$

For modern CMOS, R_ds(on) is in the range of **a few hundred milliohms**, so the drop is typically **20–50 mV** at µA-level load currents — a 10×–20× improvement.

### 2.2 The Charge Pump / Voltage Multiplier

> [!NOTE]
> A **charge pump** is a switched-capacitor circuit that uses clocked MOSFET switches and flying capacitors to "pump" charge from the input to the output, multiplying voltage. It is the workhorse of low-power energy harvesting because it has no inductors, making it fully integrable on-chip.

#### 2.2.1 Dickson Charge Pump

For an N-stage Dickson charge pump, the ideal no-load output is:

$$V_{out} = (N+1) \cdot (V_{in} - V_{th})$$

where **V_th** is the threshold voltage of the switching transistor. The term **(V_in − V_th)** is called the **stage voltage gain** and it is the single most important number for cold-start analysis.

#### 2.2.2 Threshold-Voltage Cancellation Techniques

To overcome the V_th loss, designers use:

- **Static V_th cancellation** – biasing the bulk or gate with a fixed offset.
- **Dynamic V_th cancellation** – using a four-phase clock that pre-charges the gate.
- **Cross-coupled charge pump** – NMOS and PMOS pumps in parallel, halving V_th loss per stage.

The effective gain becomes:

$$V_{out} = (N+1) \cdot V_{in} - 2 V_{th}$$

### 2.3 DC-DC Converters

These are **switch-mode power supplies (SMPS)** that step voltage up or down using an inductor (or capacitor) as the energy-transfer element. For HMC, the most common topologies are:

- **Boost (Step-Up)** – raises V_in to a higher V_out.
- **Buck (Step-Down)** – lowers V_in to a lower V_out.
- **Buck-Boost** – can do either, with inverted output.

The KTU-expected formulas (assuming continuous conduction mode, ideal components) are:

| Topology | Conversion Ratio (M = V_out / V_in) | Boundary Condition | Typical Use in HMC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Boost | $M = \dfrac{1}{1-D}$ | 0 < D < 1 | TEG, low-voltage piezo |
| Buck | $M = D$ | 0 < D < 1 | Solar panel to 1.8 V rail |
| Buck-Boost | $M = -\dfrac{D}{1-D}$ | 0 < D < 1 | Battery charging from variable source |

where **D** is the duty cycle (0 ≤ D ≤ 1) of the switching PWM signal.

### 2.4 Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MPPT is a control algorithm that continuously varies the equivalent load impedance seen by the harvester so that the operating point sits on the "knee" of the source's Power-vs-Voltage curve.** Without MPPT, harvesters can lose **30–60%** of available power.

The three classical MPPT algorithms are:

1. **Perturb & Observe (P&O)** – measure P, perturb V, observe ΔP/ΔV.
2. **Incremental Conductance (IncCond)** – uses dI/dV + I/V = 0 at the MPP.
3. **Fractional Open-Circuit Voltage (FOCV)** – sets V_MPP = k · V_OC where k ≈ 0.7–0.8 for solar, 0.4–0.6 for TEG.

For a solar cell, the maximum power point voltage relation is:

$$V_{MPP} \approx k \cdot V_{OC}, \quad k \in [0.7,\, 0.8]$$

For a thermoelectric generator:

$$V_{MPP} \approx 0.5 \cdot V_{OC}$$

### 2.5 Power Management Unit (PMU) / PMIC

The PMU is the **"brain"** of the HMC. It performs:

- **Cold-start** from a fully discharged storage element using a mechanical/photonic/thermal kick.
- **Load switching** – disconnecting the load when V_out < V_min (under-voltage lockout, UVLO).
- **Sleep/wake duty-cycling** – the IoT node sleeps 99% of the time and wakes briefly to transmit.
- **Battery management** – CC-CV (constant-current, constant-voltage) charging of Li-ion; coulomb counting.

A typical PMU efficiency benchmark is:

$$\eta_{PMU} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \frac{V_{out} \cdot I_{out}}{V_{in} \cdot I_{in}}$$

### 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Half-wave V_DC | $V_{DC} = V_m / \pi$ | V | Single diode |
| 2 | Full-wave V_DC | $V_{DC} = 2 V_m / \pi$ | V | Bridge |
| 3 | Voltage doubler output | $V_{out} = 2V_{in} - 2V_d$ | V | Dickson stage |
| 4 | Dickson charge pump | $V_{out} = (N+1)(V_{in} - V_{th})$ | V | N stages |
| 5 | Boost converter ratio | $M = 1/(1-D)$ | – | CCM ideal |
| 6 | Buck converter ratio | $M = D$ | – | CCM ideal |
| 7 | Buck-Boost ratio | $M = -D/(1-D)$ | – | Inverted output |
| 8 | Conversion efficiency | $\eta = P_{out}/P_{in}$ | % or ratio | Always ≤ 1 |
| 9 | Thermal voltage | $V_T = kT/q$ | V | ≈ 25.85 mV at 300 K |
| 10 | Solar MPP voltage | $V_{MPP} \approx 0.75 V_{OC}$ | V | Empirical k-factor |
| 11 | TEG MPP voltage | $V_{MPP} \approx 0.5 V_{OC}$ | V | Resistive source |
| 12 | Charge per pump cycle | $Q = C \cdot \Delta V$ | C | Flying capacitor |
| 13 | Cold-start energy | $E_{cs} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V_{cs}^2$ | J | Energy stored in cold-start cap |
| 14 | Battery C-rate | $I = C_{rate} \cdot C_{Ah}$ | A | Charging current |
| 15 | Average load current | $I_{avg} = I_{active} \cdot D_{duty}$ | A | Duty-cycled IoT |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

Harvesting microelectronic circuits are the silent enablers of:

- **Wireless Sensor Networks (WSN)** in bridges, pipelines, and tunnels where battery replacement is impossible.
- **Wearable medical devices** (pacemakers, hearing aids, continuous glucose monitors) powered by body heat or motion.
- **Smart agriculture** sensor nodes powered by solar + TEG.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT)** vibration-powered bearing-health monitors on rotating machinery.
- **Smart buildings** RF-powered light, temperature, and occupancy sensors.

> [!NOTE]
> The global energy harvesting market (PMICs + transducers) is projected to cross **USD 1.5 billion** by 2027, with HMCs being a major sub-segment — a strong indicator of the industrial relevance of this topic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations are exhaustive, KTU-board-pattern solutions. Each algebraic transition is explicitly shown. Memorise the **logic** behind each step, not just the formula — that is how KTU examiners award full marks.

### 3.1 Derivation: Output Voltage of an N-Stage Dickson Charge Pump

**Problem statement:** A 4-stage Dickson charge pump is driven by a clock of amplitude V_ck and is fed from a source V_in. Each switching transistor has threshold V_th. The flying and reservoir capacitors all have capacitance C. Find the no-load output voltage V_out.

**Step 1 — Define stage voltages.**
At steady state, the voltage swing at the top plate of the n-th flying capacitor is V_ck (peak-to-peak). The bottom plate is held at the previous stage's output. Therefore each stage pumps a charge equal to:

$$\Delta Q = C \cdot (V_{ck} - V_{th})$$

**Step 2 — Write the recurrence relation.**
If V_{n-1} is the output of stage (n-1), then stage n adds one diode drop worth of charge:

$$V_n = V_{n-1} + (V_{ck} - V_{th}) - V_{d,top}$$

For an ideal diode, V_{d,top} = 0, and for V_{ck} = V_in (clock rail equal to supply), we get:

$$V_n = V_{n-1} + (V_{in} - V_{th})$$

**Step 3 — Unroll the recurrence.**

$$V_1 = V_{in} - V_{th}$$

$$V_2 = V_1 + (V_{in} - V_{th}) = 2(V_{in} - V_{th})$$

$$V_3 = 3(V_{in} - V_{th})$$

$$\vdots$$

$$V_N = N \cdot (V_{in} - V_{th})$$

**Step 4 — Account for the reservoir output.**
The final reservoir stage adds one more (V_in − V_th) to give:

$$V_{out} = (N+1)(V_{in} - V_{th})$$

This is the canonical KTU answer. For N = 4, V_in = 0.3 V, V_th = 0.4 V, the gain is:

$$V_{out} = 5 \cdot (0.3 - 0.4) = 5 \cdot (-0.1) = -0.5 \text{ V}$$

which is **negative** — meaning the pump **fails to start**. This is exactly why threshold-cancellation is needed in any practical HMC.

### 3.2 Derivation: Boost-Converter Voltage Conversion Ratio in CCM

**Problem statement:** A boost converter operates in continuous conduction mode (CCM) with input voltage V_in, duty cycle D, inductor L, switch S, diode D, output capacitor C, and load R. Prove that M = V_out / V_in = 1 / (1 − D).

**Step 1 — Switch ON interval (0 < t < D·T).**
When S is closed, the diode is reverse-biased and the inductor is connected across the input. The voltage across the inductor is:

$$V_L = V_{in} = L \cdot \frac{di_L}{dt}$$

The inductor current rises by:

$$\Delta i_L^{ON} = \frac{V_{in}}{L} \cdot D \cdot T$$

**Step 2 — Switch OFF interval (D·T < t < T).**
When S is open, the diode conducts and the inductor is in series with the input and the output. The voltage across the inductor becomes:

$$V_L = V_{in} - V_{out}$$

The current falls by:

$$\Delta i_L^{OFF} = \frac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{L} \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T$$

**Step 3 — Apply volt-second balance.**
In steady state, the net change in inductor current over one cycle must be zero (otherwise the current would ramp to infinity):

$$\Delta i_L^{ON} + \Delta i_L^{OFF} = 0$$

$$\frac{V_{in} \cdot D \cdot T}{L} + \frac{(V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T}{L} = 0$$

**Step 4 — Simplify algebraically.**

$$V_{in} \cdot D + (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot (1 - D) = 0$$

$$V_{in} \cdot D + V_{in}(1 - D) - V_{out}(1 - D) = 0$$

$$V_{in} \cdot (D + 1 - D) = V_{out} \cdot (1 - D)$$

$$V_{in} = V_{out} \cdot (1 - D)$$

**Step 5 — Solve for M.**

$$M = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{1}{1 - D}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **core** boost-converter relation. The same volt-second-balance approach (Step 3) is what KTU examiners expect you to use for the buck and buck-boost derivations as well. Always start from the inductor volt-second balance — it is the universal trick.

### 3.3 Derivation: Conversion Efficiency of a Rectifier with Diode Drop

**Problem statement:** A half-wave rectifier is fed by a sinusoid v(t) = V_m sin(ωt) through a diode with forward drop V_d. The load is R_L. Find the average DC output voltage and the rectification efficiency.

**Step 1 — Identify the conduction interval.**
The diode conducts only when v(t) > V_d, i.e., for the interval where sin(ωt) > V_d/V_m. Define:

$$\sin \theta_1 = \frac{V_d}{V_m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \theta_1 = \sin^{-1}\!\left(\frac{V_d}{V_m}\right)$$

The diode conducts from θ_1 to (π − θ_1).

**Step 2 — Compute the average output voltage.**

$$V_{DC} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{\theta_1}^{\pi - \theta_1} (V_m \sin\theta - V_d)\, d\theta$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral.**

$$V_{DC} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ -V_m \cos\theta \Big|_{\theta_1}^{\pi - \theta_1} - V_d \theta \Big|_{\theta_1}^{\pi - \theta_1} \right]$$

$$V_{DC} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \left[ V_m (\cos\theta_1 + \cos\theta_1) - V_d (\pi - 2\theta_1) \right]$$

$$V_{DC} = \frac{V_m \cos\theta_1}{\pi} - \frac{V_d (\pi - 2\theta_1)}{2\pi}$$

**Step 4 — Limiting cases.**
For V_d ≪ V_m, θ_1 → 0 and cos θ_1 → 1, so we recover the ideal:

$$V_{DC} \rightarrow \frac{V_m}{\pi}$$

For the limiting case V_d → V_m, conduction just barely occurs and V_DC → 0.

**Step 5 — Rectification efficiency.**
The rectification efficiency is defined as:

$$\eta = \frac{P_{DC}}{P_{ac,in}} = \frac{V_{DC}^2 / R_L}{V_{rms,in}^2 / R_L} = \left(\frac{V_{DC}}{V_{rms,in}}\right)^2$$

where V_{rms,in} is the RMS of the input sinusoid. KTU examiners commonly quote the result:

$$\eta_{max} = \frac{4}{\pi^2} \approx 40.6\%$$

for an ideal full-wave rectifier — this is the well-known benchmark students should remember.

### 3.4 Derivation: MPPT Perturb-and-Observe (P&O) Update Rule

**Problem statement:** A solar cell is operating at point (V_k, I_k) with power P_k = V_k · I_k. The controller perturbs the voltage by ΔV. Show the P&O decision rule.

**Step 1 — Measure the current power.**

$$P_k = V_k \cdot I_k$$

**Step 2 — Apply perturbation.**

$$V_{k+1} = V_k + \Delta V$$

$$P_{k+1} = V_{k+1} \cdot I_{k+1}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the power change.**

$$\Delta P = P_{k+1} - P_k$$

**Step 4 — Decision logic.**

$$
\text{If } \Delta P > 0 \text{ and } \Delta V > 0: \text{ continue increasing } V
$$
$$
\text{If } \Delta P > 0 \text{ and } \Delta V < 0: \text{ continue decreasing } V
$$
$$
\text{If } \Delta P < 0 \text{ and } \Delta V > 0: \text{ reverse direction, decrease } V
$$
$$
\text{If } \Delta P < 0 \text{ and } \Delta V < 0: \text{ reverse direction, increase } V
$$

**Step 5 — Oscillation analysis.**
At the true MPP, the algorithm oscillates with amplitude ΔV. The trade-off is well-known:

- Large ΔV → fast tracking, large oscillation loss.
- Small ΔV → slow tracking, small oscillation loss.

A fixed-step P&O has tracking efficiency of **90–96%**; an adaptive-step P&O can reach **99%**.

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Passive vs Active Rectification

| Parameter | Passive (Diode) | Active (MOSFET) | Impact on HMC |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forward drop V_d | 0.3 – 0.7 V | 20 – 50 mV | Determines cold-start V_in |
| Reverse leakage | 1 – 10 µA | < 100 nA | Critical for µW sources |
| Threshold dependence | None | Process-dependent V_th | Affects yield |
| Static power | 0 | µA-level bias | Reduces η at low I_load |
| Integrability | Discrete | Fully on-chip | Lower BOM cost |
| Efficiency at 100 µW | 30 – 50% | 70 – 90% | Defines usable harvester power |

> [!NOTE]
> **The single most important insight:** A passive silicon-diode rectifier cannot cold-start from a TEG or small solar cell that produces < 0.6 V. This is the fundamental reason that **active CMOS rectifiers** are the cornerstone of modern HMC design. KTU examiners love asking this as a "compare and justify" question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following Mermaid diagrams follow the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 **Node Identifier Alpha Rule** (all node IDs are alphanumeric, prefixed with letters) and **Label Formatting Restriction** (no markdown tags inside double-quoted labels). They render the **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** of harvesting microelectronic circuits.

### 4.1 End-to-End Energy Harvesting Microelectronic System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Ambient Energy Source] --> A2[Transducer TEG Solar Piezo RF]
    A2 --> A3[Active Rectifier CMOS Bridge]
    A3 --> A4[Charge Pump N Stage Dickson]
    A4 --> A5[Boost DC DC Converter]
    A5 --> A6[MPPT Controller P and O]
    A6 --> A7[Power Management Unit PMU]
    A7 --> A8[Energy Storage Battery Supercap]
    A7 --> A9[Voltage Regulator LDO]
    A9 --> A10[IoT Load Sensor MCU Radio]
    A6 -. feedback .-> A3
    A7 -. enable signal .-> A9
```

### 4.2 Internal Structure of an Active CMOS Rectifier

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B1[AC Input from Transducer] --> B2[Comparator Threshold Detector]
    B2 --> B3{Polarity Decision}
    B3 -- Positive Half Cycle --> B4[PMOS Switch M1 ON]
    B3 -- Negative Half Cycle --> B5[NMOS Switch M2 ON]
    B4 --> B6[Top Plate of Flying Cap]
    B5 --> B7[Bottom Plate of Flying Cap]
    B6 --> B8[Charge Transfer Switch M3]
    B7 --> B8
    B8 --> B9[Reservoir Capacitor C out]
    B9 --> B10[Rectified DC Output]
    B2 -. gate drive .-> B4
    B2 -. gate drive .-> B5
    B2 -. gate drive .-> B8
```

### 4.3 Closed-Loop MPPT Controller

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Harvester V and I Sensors] --> C2[ADC Sampling]
    C2 --> C3[Power Calculator P equals V times I]
    C3 --> C4{Delta P Decision}
    C4 -- Delta P greater than 0 --> C5[Continue Same Perturbation]
    C4 -- Delta P less than 0 --> C6[Reverse Perturbation Direction]
    C5 --> C7[DAC Update Vref]
    C6 --> C7
    C7 --> C8[DC DC Converter Duty Cycle]
    C8 --> C9[Harvester Load Impedance Adjusted]
    C9 -. feedback to .-> C1
```

### 4.4 PMU State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    s1[Cold Start] --> s2[Harvesting Mode]
    s2 --> s3[Charging Storage]
    s3 --> s4[Load Active Mode]
    s4 --> s5[Load Sleep Mode]
    s5 --> s2
    s2 --> s6[UVLO Triggered]
    s6 --> s1
    s4 --> s7[Over Voltage Cutoff]
    s7 --> s2
```

### 4.5 Complete HMC Block Diagram with Power Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D1[Ambient Source Light Heat Motion RF] --> D2[Transducer Stage]
    D2 --> D3[Rectifier Stage]
    D3 --> D4[Voltage Multiplier Stage]
    D4 --> D5[Switch Mode DC DC Stage]
    D5 --> D6[Energy Storage Interface]
    D6 --> D7[Linear Regulator LDO]
    D7 --> D8[Digital Load MCU Radio]
    D5 <-. MPPT Feedback .-> D4
    D6 <-. Battery Gauge .-> D8
    D8 -. Enable Pin .-> D7
```

### 4.6 Pin-Out of a Typical HMC IC (Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Pin VIN_SRC] --- E2[Rectifier Block]
    E3[Pin GND] --- E4[Common Reference]
    E2 --- E5[Charge Pump Block]
    E5 --- E6[DC DC Block]
    E6 --- E7[Pin VOUT_REG Regulated Rail]
    E6 --- E8[Pin VBAT Battery Terminal]
    E9[Pin MPPT_EN] --- E10[MPPT Engine]
    E10 -. modulates .-> E6
    E11[Pin LOAD_EN] --- E12[Load Switch]
    E12 --- E7
```

> [!NOTE]
> These diagrams collectively cover the **input side** (rectifier, charge pump), the **intermediate stage** (DC-DC converter, MPPT), and the **output side** (storage interface, regulator, load) of a harvesting microelectronic circuit. In KTU 14-mark answers, the **functional block diagram (Diagram 4.1) is mandatory** — it instantly earns 2–3 valuation marks.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
*Define a charge pump. Why is it preferred over an inductive boost converter in micro-scale energy harvesting ICs?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **charge pump** is a switched-capacitor DC-DC converter that uses clocked MOSFET switches and capacitors to transfer charge from a low-voltage input to a higher-voltage output without using inductors. It is preferred in micro-scale energy harvesting ICs for three reasons:

- (1 mark) **Full on-chip integration** — no bulky inductors, so the entire HMC fits in a few mm² of silicon.
- (1 mark) **Low electromagnetic interference (EMI)** — no switching of magnetic fields, suitable for medical/IoT.
- (1 mark) **Low quiescent power** — typically < 1 µA, compatible with µW-level harvested energy.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT)? List any two MPPT algorithms.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **MPPT** is a control technique that dynamically varies the equivalent load impedance seen by an energy harvester so that it always operates at the knee of its Power-vs-Voltage (P-V) curve, extracting maximum available power under varying ambient conditions. (2 marks)

Two classical MPPT algorithms (1 mark):
- Perturb and Observe (P and O)
- Incremental Conductance (IncCond)
- Fractional Open-Circuit Voltage (FOCV)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU End-Semester Examinations under the 2024 Scheme follow the **Module Internal Choice** pattern: a student attempts ONE full 14-mark question from a module. We present TWO alternatives (Q-A and Q-B) so you can practise both pathways.

#### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Pattern]

**Q-A(a).** With a neat functional block diagram, describe the architecture of a complete harvesting microelectronic circuit for a solar/Thermoelectric IoT node. Identify the function of each block. **(7 marks)**

**Q-A(b).** Derive the voltage conversion ratio M = V_out / V_in of a boost converter operating in continuous conduction mode (CCM). Use volt-second balance on the inductor. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(a) — 7 Marks:**

A complete harvesting microelectronic circuit consists of **six functional blocks** in series from the ambient source to the IoT load (2 marks for naming the blocks, 5 marks for explanation):

1. **Transducer Stage** (1 mark) — converts ambient energy (light, heat, vibration) into a low-amplitude electrical signal. Examples: solar cell (0.5–0.6 V per cell), TEG (10–500 mV), piezoelectric cantilever (1–10 V AC).

2. **Rectifier Stage** (1 mark) — converts the transducer's AC (or pulsed DC) output into a unipolar DC signal. Active CMOS rectifiers are preferred over passive diode rectifiers because of their **20–50 mV voltage drop** versus the 0.7 V of a silicon diode.

3. **Charge Pump / Voltage Multiplier Stage** (1 mark) — stacks multiple diode-capacitor stages (Dickson) or uses four-phase clocking with V_th cancellation to multiply the rectified DC up to a usable rail. An N-stage Dickson provides V_out = (N+1)(V_in − V_th).

4. **DC-DC Converter Stage** (1 mark) — boosts the voltage to a regulated rail (1.8 V or 3.3 V) using either an inductive boost converter or a switched-capacitor boost. CCM operation is mandatory for predictable conversion ratio.

5. **Maximum Power Point Tracker (MPPT)** (1 mark) — senses V and I at the transducer, computes P = V·I, and adjusts the converter's duty cycle D so that dP/dV = 0, locking the harvester to its peak-power operating point.

6. **Power Management Unit (PMU) and Energy Storage Interface** (1 mark) — charges a Li-ion battery or supercapacitor using CC-CV profile, performs under-voltage lockout (UVLO), load switching, and sleep/wake duty-cycling of the IoT load.

The block diagram from Section 4.1 of these notes illustrates the architecture. The PMU orchestrates the entire chain via enable signals, MPPT feedback, and a battery-gauge interface.

---

**Model Solution for Q-A(b) — 7 Marks:**

Consider a boost converter with input V_in, output V_out, switch duty cycle D, and switching period T.

**Step 1 (1 mark):** During switch ON, the inductor is connected directly across the input. The voltage across L is V_in, and the inductor current rises.

$$\Delta i_{L,\text{ON}} = \frac{V_{in}}{L} \cdot D \cdot T$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** During switch OFF, the diode conducts, and the inductor sees the difference V_in − V_out. The current falls.

$$\Delta i_{L,\text{OFF}} = \frac{V_{in} - V_{out}}{L} \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** In steady state, the volt-second balance on the inductor requires that the net change over one switching period is zero.

$$\Delta i_{L,\text{ON}} + \Delta i_{L,\text{OFF}} = 0$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Substituting the expressions:

$$\frac{V_{in} \cdot D \cdot T}{L} + \frac{(V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot (1 - D) \cdot T}{L} = 0$$

$$V_{in} \cdot D + (V_{in} - V_{out})(1 - D) = 0$$

$$V_{in}(D + 1 - D) = V_{out}(1 - D)$$

$$V_{in} = V_{out}(1 - D)$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Therefore the conversion ratio is:

$$M = \frac{V_{out}}{V_{in}} = \frac{1}{1 - D}$$

For D = 0.6, M = 1 / 0.4 = 2.5, so a 1 V input produces a 2.5 V output.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Pattern]

**Q-B(a).** Compare passive diode rectifiers and active CMOS rectifiers used in energy harvesting interfaces. Justify why active rectifiers are essential for sub-1 V harvesters. **(7 marks)**

**Q-B(b).** Explain the Perturb and Observe (P&O) MPPT algorithm. Discuss the trade-off between tracking speed and steady-state oscillation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(a) — 7 Marks:**

| Parameter | Passive Diode Rectifier | Active CMOS Rectifier | Marks |
|---|---|---|---|
| Forward voltage drop | 0.3–0.7 V | 20–50 mV | 2 |
| Reverse leakage | 1–10 µA | < 100 nA | 1 |
| Cold-start capability | Fails below 0.6 V | Works down to 20–50 mV | 2 |
| Quiescent power | 0 | µA-level bias current | 1 |
| Integrability | External component | Fully on-chip | 1 |

**Justification (1 mark):** Sub-1 V harvesters such as TEGs (50–200 mV) and small photovoltaic cells (0.3–0.5 V) cannot overcome the 0.6–0.7 V forward drop of a silicon diode. A Schottky diode reduces this to 0.3 V, but the drop is still 50% of the available signal. An active CMOS rectifier replaces the diode with a comparator-driven MOSFET, achieving a drop of I_load · R_ds(on) which can be as low as **20 mV** — a 15×–25× improvement. This drop reduction is the **single most important** reason that active rectifiers are essential in modern sub-1 V harvesting microelectronic circuits.

---

**Model Solution for Q-B(b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1 (2 marks):** The P&O algorithm operates as a closed loop. At every sampling instant k, the controller measures the harvester voltage V_k and current I_k, computes power P_k = V_k · I_k, and compares it with the previous power P_{k−1}.

**Step 2 (2 marks):** The decision rule:
- If ΔP > 0 and ΔV > 0 → keep increasing V (we are on the rising side of the P-V curve, moving towards MPP).
- If ΔP > 0 and ΔV < 0 → keep decreasing V.
- If ΔP < 0 and ΔV > 0 → reverse direction, decrease V (we have crossed the MPP).
- If ΔP < 0 and ΔV < 0 → reverse direction, increase V.

**Step 3 (2 marks):** Trade-off:
- **Large step size ΔV** → fast tracking under rapidly changing ambient conditions, but large steady-state oscillation around MPP, reducing the average extracted power.
- **Small step size ΔV** → slow tracking, but very small oscillation loss. Convergence can take many seconds under fast-changing irradiance.
- **Adaptive step size** (variable ΔV) — reduces step size near MPP and increases it far from MPP — provides the best of both worlds and is the state-of-the-art in commercial PMICs.

**Step 4 (1 mark):** Tracking efficiency: a fixed-step P&O typically achieves 90–96%, while an adaptive P&O can reach **> 99%** of the theoretical maximum available power.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in HMC questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the V_th term** in the Dickson charge pump formula. Students often write V_out = (N+1) V_in, which is **wrong** by the threshold loss. The correct form is V_out = (N+1)(V_in − V_th). This single sign error can cost 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing the half-wave and full-wave rectifier DC formulas.** V_DC = V_m/π is half-wave; V_DC = 2V_m/π is full-wave. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 3. **Using CCM equations for a DCM (discontinuous conduction mode) boost converter.** In DCM, the conversion ratio depends on R, L, D, and T — *not* the simple M = 1/(1−D). Always check the operating mode.
> 4. **Writing MPPT = "maximum power point tracker" without describing the algorithm.** KTU examiners award the algorithmic detail (perturb, observe, decide, update) **2 marks** minimum.
> 5. **Drawing the block diagram without arrows showing power flow direction.** Always annotate arrows as "P_in", "P_out", or "feedback" to earn full marks.
> 6. **Ignoring cold-start.** A harvesting microelectronic circuit that cannot cold-start from < 0.3 V is **useless** for TEGs and indoor solar. Examiners explicitly ask for cold-start design considerations.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Harvesting microelectronic circuits = the analog/power ICs that condition raw transducer output into a regulated DC rail.
- **Six core blocks:** Transducer → Rectifier → Charge Pump → DC-DC → MPPT → PMU + Storage.
- **Half-wave V_DC:** $V_m/\pi$ | **Full-wave V_DC:** $2V_m/\pi$ | **Voltage doubler:** $2V_{in} - 2V_d$.
- **Dickson charge pump:** $V_{out} = (N+1)(V_{in} - V_{th})$ — always include V_th.
- **Boost converter:** $M = 1/(1-D)$ (CCM, ideal) — derived by volt-second balance on L.
- **Buck converter:** $M = D$ | **Buck-boost:** $M = -D/(1-D)$ (inverted output).
- **MPPT k-factors:** Solar ≈ 0.75 V_OC, TEG ≈ 0.5 V_OC.
- **P&O trade-off:** Large step → fast, oscillatory. Small step → slow, accurate. Adaptive step → best.
- **Active vs passive rectifier:** Active MOSFET has **20–50 mV** drop vs **0.3–0.7 V** for diodes — this is the single most important design choice for sub-1 V sources.
- **Cold-start V_in_min:** 20–300 mV is the target range for modern HMCs.
- **Conversion efficiency target:** η ≥ 80% at 100 µW – 1 mW output.
- **Thermal voltage V_T:** ≈ 25.85 mV at 300 K — appears in sub-threshold MOSFET equations.
- **Efficiency benchmark:** Maximum ideal rectification efficiency = $4/\pi^2 \approx 40.6\%$ (full-wave, single-tone).
- **Storage choice:** Supercapacitor for high-cycle, low-energy density; Li-ion for high-energy density, limited cycle life.
- **PMU functions:** Cold-start, UVLO, CC-CV charging, load switching, sleep/wake duty-cycling.
- **Standard rails:** 1.8 V, 3.3 V — most KTU numerical problems assume one of these.
- **Standard CMOS tech nodes for HMC ICs:** 180 nm, 130 nm, 65 nm — finer nodes give better MOSFET R_ds(on) but higher leakage.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** Always start a 14-mark HMC answer with the **functional block diagram**, then proceed block-by-block. This single discipline alone routinely lifts a 9-mark answer to a 13-mark answer in KTU valuation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Conditioning and Losses
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Power Conditioning and Losses

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚡ Power Conditioning and Losses — Core Foundations

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Power Conditioning** is the process of modifying the electrical characteristics of power obtained from an energy harvester (or any source) so that it becomes a regulated, stable, and usable form suitable for the downstream IoT load and energy storage element.

In the context of **Energy Harvesting & Power Management for IoT**, power conditioning forms the critical *intermediate stage* that sits between the **transducer/harvester** (e.g., piezoelectric, photovoltaic, thermoelectric, RF) and the **load / battery / supercapacitor**. Because ambient energy sources are inherently:

- **Intermittent** (not always available),
- **Low-amplitude** (often mV to a few V),
- **Variable impedance** (output Z changes with excitation),
- **Noisy / unregulated** (AC ripple, spikes, frequency drift),

… the raw harvested signal **cannot directly power a microcontroller, sensor, or wireless transceiver**. Power conditioning solves this by performing one or more of the following sub-tasks:

1. **Rectification** (AC → DC conversion for piezoelectric, electromagnetic, or RF harvesters).
2. **Voltage Regulation** (step-up, step-down, or step-up/down conversion).
3. **Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT)** — impedance matching for maximum energy extraction.
4. **Filtering & Ripple Suppression** — smoothing the DC output.
5. **Load Matching** — ensuring the harvester sees its optimal operating point.

**Losses** in this stage represent the energy that is *consumed* within the conditioning circuit itself and never reaches the load. They are the dominant cause of reduced end-to-end system efficiency in IoT energy harvesting nodes.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Water Treatment Plant"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of an energy harvester as a **rainwater collection pond** on a hill. The water (energy) is dirty, arrives in unpredictable spurts, and is collected at varying heights (voltages). The power conditioning circuit is the **water treatment plant + pumping station** that:
> - **Filters** the water (rectification & filtering),
> - **Pumps** it to the right pressure (voltage regulation),
> - **Stores** excess in a tank (battery/capacitor),
> - **Loses** some water through pipe leaks, pump friction, and filter clogging (**conduction, switching, and quiescent losses**).
> 
> The *goal* is to deliver the **maximum clean, pressurized water** to the household (IoT device) per drop of rain harvested.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following **bold-faced parameters** are the benchmark numbers used throughout KTU 2024 Scheme evaluation for this module:

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Significance |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Diode Forward Voltage Drop | $V_F$ | **0.3 V (Schottky) – 0.7 V (Si)** | Causes conduction loss in rectifiers |
| Switching Frequency | $f_{sw}$ | **100 kHz – 10 MHz** | Higher → smaller passives, more switching loss |
| Quiescent Current | $I_Q$ | **nA – µA range** | Critical for nano-power harvesters |
| Conversion Efficiency | $\eta$ | **70 % – 95 %** | Measure of conditioning goodness |
| Load Regulation | $\Delta V_L$ | **< 1 % to 3 %** | Output stability against load changes |
| Line Regulation | $\Delta V_{in}$ | **< 0.1 %/V** | Output stability against input variations |
| Output Voltage Ripple | $V_{ripple}$ | **< 50 mV** for digital ICs | Affects ADC and sensor accuracy |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU exam answers, always quote the **units** of these parameters. Marks are routinely deducted when students write only the symbol without the unit.

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Power Conditioning Chain (Top-Down)

A typical energy harvesting power conditioning chain consists of these sequential blocks:

1. **Harvester (Transducer)** → generates raw electrical signal.
2. **Rectifier** (only for AC sources: piezo, EM, RF, triboelectric).
3. **Pre-Regulator / MPPT Stage** → matches source impedance to load.
4. **DC-DC Converter** (Buck / Boost / Buck-Boost / SEPIC).
5. **Output Filter** (LC network) → suppresses ripple.
6. **Energy Storage Element** (Li-ion battery, supercapacitor, thin-film battery).
7. **Load** (sensor, MCU, radio).

## 2.2 Rectifier Topologies (AC → DC Front-End)

### (a) Half-Wave Rectifier
- Uses **one diode**; conducts only during positive half-cycle.
- Poor efficiency (~40.6 %), high ripple → rarely used in harvesters.

### (b) Full-Wave Center-Tapped Rectifier
- Uses **two diodes + center-tapped transformer**.
- Higher efficiency (~81.2 %), but bulky transformer → **not preferred** for miniature IoT nodes.

### (c) Bridge Rectifier (Full-Wave Bridge)
- Uses **four diodes** in a bridge; no center tap needed.
- Industry standard for **piezoelectric and RF harvesters**.
- Efficiency ~81.2 %, but each current path loses **2 × $V_F$** → important for low-voltage harvesters.

### (d) Active / Synchronous Rectifier
- Replaces diodes with **MOSFET switches** controlled by a timing circuit.
- Reduces $V_F$ loss to $I \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ → can boost efficiency to **> 95 %** at low voltages.
- Used in premium nano-power ICs (e.g., Analog Devices ADP5090, Texas Instruments BQ25504).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For sub-1V AC harvesters, the diode drop itself can be **larger than the available signal** — a hard physical limit. This is precisely why **active rectifiers** were invented.

## 2.3 DC-DC Converter Topologies

### (a) Buck Converter (Step-Down)
- Output voltage **lower** than input.
- Single switch (MOSFET), one diode, one inductor, one capacitor.
- **Continuous Conduction Mode (CCM)** operation.

### (b) Boost Converter (Step-Up)
- Output voltage **higher** than input.
- Essential for **thermoelectric (TEG) and low-voltage PV** harvesters.
- Stores energy in inductor during ON-time, releases to load during OFF-time.

### (c) Buck-Boost Converter
- Output can be **either higher or lower** than input.
- Output is **inverted** (negative with respect to ground) in classic topology.
- Used when harvester voltage can swing above and below the regulated output (e.g., variable-speed vibration harvesters).

### (d) SEPIC (Single-Ended Primary Inductor Converter)
- Non-inverting output, can step up or down.
- **Preferred** for energy harvesting when the input voltage range straddles the output (e.g., solar panels at varying irradiance).

## 2.4 Loss Mechanisms (The "Loss Budget")

Power losses in a conditioning circuit arise from **five primary sources**:

1. **Conduction Losses ($P_{cond}$)** — resistive dissipation in switches, diodes, inductors, PCB traces.
2. **Switching Losses ($P_{sw}$)** — energy lost during MOSFET turn-on and turn-off transitions.
3. **Quiescent / Static Losses ($P_Q$)** — current consumed by control circuitry, voltage reference, gate drivers.
4. **Magnetic Core Losses ($P_{core}$)** — hysteresis + eddy current losses in the inductor core.
5. **Capacitive / ESR Losses** — equivalent series resistance of filter and bypass capacitors.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often quote only the *switching loss* formula and forget the **quiescent current loss**. For ultra-low-power IoT harvesters (µW range), **$P_Q$ is often the dominant loss term**.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Boundary / Notes |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Buck Output Voltage | $V_{out} = D \cdot V_{in}$ | $0 < D < 1$ |
| 2 | Boost Output Voltage | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1-D}$ | $0 < D < 1$ |
| 3 | Buck-Boost Output Voltage | $V_{out} = -\dfrac{D}{1-D} \cdot V_{in}$ | Negative output |
| 4 | SEPIC Output Voltage | $V_{out} = \dfrac{D}{1-D} \cdot V_{in}$ | Non-inverting |
| 5 | Conversion Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{P_{in} - P_{loss}}{P_{in}}$ | Dimensionless, $0 < \eta \le 1$ |
| 6 | Switching Loss (per switch) | $P_{sw} = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot V_{in} \cdot I_{out} \cdot (t_r + t_f) \cdot f_{sw}$ | $t_r, t_f$ = rise/fall times |
| 7 | Conduction Loss (MOSFET) | $P_{cond} = I_{RMS}^{2} \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ | $R_{DS(on)}$ in mΩ |
| 8 | Diode Conduction Loss | $P_{D} = V_F \cdot I_F \cdot (1-D)$ | Average loss per cycle |
| 9 | Quiescent Loss | $P_Q = V_{in} \cdot I_Q$ | Critical at low load |
| 10 | Inductor Copper Loss | $P_{Cu} = I_{L,RMS}^{2} \cdot R_{L,DC}$ | Depends on winding |
| 11 | Core Loss (Steinmetz) | $P_{core} = K \cdot f_{sw}^{\alpha} \cdot B_{max}^{\beta}$ | Empirical constants K, α, β |
| 12 | Ripple Factor (Rectifier) | $\gamma = \dfrac{V_{rms,ac}}{V_{dc}}$ | Ideal: 0; Half-wave: 1.21; Full-wave: 0.482 |
| 13 | Rectifier Efficiency | $\eta_{rect} = \dfrac{P_{dc}}{P_{dc} + P_{ac,ripple} + P_{diode}}$ | $< 100\%$ always |
| 14 | Inductor Current Ripple | $\Delta I_L = \dfrac{V_{in} \cdot D}{L \cdot f_{sw}}$ | Buck: 10–30 % typical |
| 15 | Output Voltage Ripple | $\Delta V_{out} = \dfrac{\Delta I_L}{8 \cdot f_{sw} \cdot C}$ | Buck CCM approximation |

> [!NOTE]
> In the table above, the absolute value notation $\vert x \vert$ has been written as plain $x$ for symbols that are inherently positive (e.g., $V_{in}$, $I_{RMS}$). For expressions such as $\vert V_{out} \vert$ in a row, use $\lvert V_{out} \rvert$ to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Power conditioning and loss minimization is the **backbone** of every commercial IoT energy harvesting product:
- **Self-powered wireless switches** (EnOcean, ZigBee Green Power) — use piezo + active rectifier + buck regulator.
- **Solar-powered IoT sensors** (，农业 IoT, smart agriculture) — use boost MPPT + battery charger.
- **Body-wearable medical devices** — use thermoelectric + boost + supercapacitor storage.
- **Smart building sensors** — use indoor photovoltaic + MPPT + Li-ion charger.

# 📐 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every algebraic transition below is shown explicitly. **No skipped steps.** Each equation block is preceded and followed by a single blank line as per LaTeX isolation rules.

## 3.1 Derivation — Output Voltage of a Boost Converter (CCM)

**Setup:** Consider a boost converter in steady-state, Continuous Conduction Mode. Switch closes for time $DT_s$ (where $D$ is duty cycle, $T_s$ is switching period), and opens for $(1-D)T_s$.

**Step 1 — Volt-Second Balance on Inductor L**

In steady state, the average voltage across the inductor over one switching period must be zero (otherwise the inductor current would grow unboundedly).

During **ON-time** (switch closed, diode reverse-biased):
- Inductor is connected directly across $V_{in}$.
- Voltage across L: $V_L = V_{in}$.

During **OFF-time** (switch open, diode forward-biased):
- Inductor current flows through diode to output.
- Voltage across L: $V_L = V_{in} - V_{out}$.

**Step 2 — Apply Volt-Second Balance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{in} \cdot (D T_s) + (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot (1-D) T_s &= 0 \\[4pt]
V_{in} \cdot D + (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot (1-D) &= 0 \\[4pt]
V_{in} \cdot D + V_{in} \cdot (1-D) - V_{out} \cdot (1-D) &= 0 \\[4pt]
V_{in} \cdot [D + 1 - D] - V_{out} \cdot (1-D) &= 0 \\[4pt]
V_{in} - V_{out} \cdot (1-D) &= 0 \\[4pt]
V_{out} \cdot (1-D) &= V_{in} \\[4pt]
\boxed{\,V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1-D}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** As $D \to 0$, $V_{out} \to V_{in}$ (no boost). As $D \to 1$, $V_{out} \to \infty$ (ideal limit; in practice, losses limit this).

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Output Voltage of a Buck Converter (CCM)

**Step 1 — Volt-Second Balance**

During **ON-time** (switch closed, diode off): $V_L = V_{in} - V_{out}$.

During **OFF-time** (switch open, diode conducting): $V_L = -V_{out}$.

**Step 2 — Apply Volt-Second Balance**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot D T_s + (-V_{out}) \cdot (1-D) T_s &= 0 \\[4pt]
(V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot D - V_{out} \cdot (1-D) &= 0 \\[4pt]
V_{in} \cdot D - V_{out} \cdot D - V_{out} \cdot (1-D) &= 0 \\[4pt]
V_{in} \cdot D - V_{out} \cdot [D + (1-D)] &= 0 \\[4pt]
V_{in} \cdot D - V_{out} &= 0 \\[4pt]
\boxed{\,V_{out} = D \cdot V_{in}\,}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Switching Loss in a MOSFET

When a MOSFET transitions from OFF to ON (or ON to OFF), both the voltage across it ($V_{DS}$) and the current through it ($I_D$) are non-zero during the transition (because they cannot change instantaneously). The instantaneous power dissipation is:

$$
p(t) = v_{DS}(t) \cdot i_D(t)
$$

**Assume linear transitions:**

- During turn-on (rise time $t_r$): current rises linearly from 0 to $I_{out}$, voltage falls linearly from $V_{in}$ to 0.
- During turn-off (fall time $t_f$): the reverse.

**Energy lost per switching transition:**

$$
E_{sw} = \int_0^{t_r} \left( V_{in} \cdot \dfrac{t}{t_r} \right) \left( I_{out} \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{t}{t_r}\right) \right) dt
$$

Let $u = t/t_r$, $dt = t_r \, du$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{sw} &= V_{in} \cdot I_{out} \cdot t_r \int_0^1 u(1-u)\, du \\[4pt]
&= V_{in} \cdot I_{out} \cdot t_r \cdot \left[\dfrac{u^2}{2} - \dfrac{u^3}{3}\right]_0^1 \\[4pt]
&= V_{in} \cdot I_{out} \cdot t_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{2} - \dfrac{1}{3}\right) \\[4pt]
&= \dfrac{1}{6} \cdot V_{in} \cdot I_{out} \cdot t_r
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summing turn-on and turn-off losses (with $t_r$ and $t_f$ included separately):**

$$
E_{sw,total} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot V_{in} \cdot I_{out} \cdot (t_r + t_f)
$$

**Average switching power** (multiply by $f_{sw}$):

$$
\boxed{\,P_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot V_{in} \cdot I_{out} \cdot (t_r + t_f) \cdot f_{sw}\,}
$$

**Key insight:** Switching loss grows **linearly with frequency**. This sets a practical upper bound on $f_{sw}$ for ultra-low-power IoT converters (often capped at ~1 MHz).

---

## 3.4 Derivation — Total Loss Budget of a Conditioning Stage

Consider a complete conditioning stage with all major loss components. The total input power is:

$$
P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{cond} + P_{sw} + P_Q + P_{core} + P_{Cu} + P_{ESR}
$$

Efficiency:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{out} + P_{loss,total}}
$$

**Example numerical evaluation:**

Suppose $P_{out} = 50 \,\text{mW}$, with the following losses:

- $P_{sw} = 5 \,\text{mW}$
- $P_{cond} = 3 \,\text{mW}$
- $P_Q = 2 \,\text{mW}$
- $P_{core} + P_{Cu} + P_{ESR} = 5 \,\text{mW}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{in} &= 50 + 5 + 3 + 2 + 5 = 65 \,\text{mW} \\[4pt]
\eta &= \dfrac{50}{65} = 0.769 = 76.9 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation — Ripple Factor of a Full-Wave Rectifier

The ripple factor is defined as:

$$
\gamma = \dfrac{V_{rms,ac}}{V_{dc}}
$$

For a full-wave rectifier with **capacitor filter**, the ripple voltage (peak-to-peak) is approximated by:

$$
V_{ripple} = \dfrac{I_{load}}{2 \cdot f_{line} \cdot C}
$$

where $f_{line}$ is the line frequency (doubled to $2f$ for full-wave). Substituting and simplifying for the standard full-wave case:

$$
\gamma_{FW} \approx \dfrac{1}{2\sqrt{3}} \cdot \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{1}{2fRC}\right)^2 + \text{harmonic terms}}
$$

For an unfiltered full-wave rectifier, the theoretical value is:

$$
\boxed{\,\gamma_{FW} = 0.482\,}
$$

This is **less than half** the half-wave value (1.21), which is why full-wave is preferred for harvesters.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Loss vs. Topology Trade-off

| Topology | Typical η (Light Load) | Typical η (Full Load) | $V_F$ Sensitivity | Best Use Case |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Half-Wave Rectifier | 35–40 % | 40–50 % | High | Educational / non-critical |
| Bridge Rectifier (Diode) | 60–70 % | 80–85 % | High (2 $V_F$ per path) | Piezo harvesters |
| Synchronous Rectifier | 80–90 % | 92–96 % | Very Low | Premium nano-power ICs |
| Buck Converter | 75–85 % | 90–95 % | Low | PV with $V_{in} > V_{out}$ |
| Boost Converter | 70–80 % | 88–93 % | Medium | TEG, low-voltage PV |
| Buck-Boost / SEPIC | 65–75 % | 85–90 % | Medium | Wide-range harvesters |
| Linear Regulator (LDO) | 40–60 % | 50–70 % | Low | Low-dropout, low-noise supplies |

# 🧭 Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Complete Power Conditioning Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Energy Source] --> srcB[Transducer Harvester]
    srcB --> srcC[Rectifier AC to DC]
    srcC --> srcD[MPPT and Pre-Regulator]
    srcD --> srcE[DC-DC Converter]
    srcE --> srcF[Output LC Filter]
    srcF --> srcG[Energy Storage Battery Cap]
    srcF --> srcH[IoT Load Sensor MCU Radio]

    classDef power fill:#FFF4D6,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cond fill:#D6E4FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef loss fill:#FFD6D6,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef load fill:#D6FFD6,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class srcA,srcB power
    class srcC,srcD,srcE,srcF cond
    class srcG loss
    class srcH load
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Amber blocks** = Energy generation stage.
- **Blue blocks** = Conditioning stage (where losses primarily occur).
- **Red block** = Energy storage (charge/discharge losses).
- **Green block** = Useful load (what the IoT node actually needs).

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Loss Allocation Across a Conditioning Stage

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inA[Input Power Pin] --> lossA[Conduction Loss Pcond]
    inA --> lossB[Switching Loss Psw]
    inA --> lossC[Quiescent Loss PQ]
    inA --> lossD[Core Loss Pcore]
    inA --> lossE[Copper and ESR Loss]
    inA --> outA[Output Power Pout]

    lossA --> sumA[Total Ploss]
    lossB --> sumA
    lossC --> sumA
    lossD --> sumA
    lossE --> sumA
    sumA --> effA[Efficiency eta equals Pout divided by Pin]

    classDef inp fill:#FFF4D6,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ls fill:#FFD6D6,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#D6FFD6,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ef fill:#E6D6FF,stroke:#4B0082,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class inA inp
    class lossA,lossB,lossC,lossD,lossE,sumA ls
    class outA out
    class effA ef
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram — Converter Switch Operation (Buck, CCM)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> switchON
    switchON: SWITCH ON Phase
    switchON: Inductor charges
    switchON: Diode reverse biased
    switchON: VL equals Vin minus Vout
    switchON --> switchOFF: After time DTs
    switchOFF: SWITCH OFF Phase
    switchOFF: Inductor discharges
    switchOFF: Diode conducts
    switchOFF: VL equals minus Vout
    switchOFF --> switchON: After time 1 minus D Ts
    switchOFF --> [*]
```

**Interpretation:** The state diagram shows the two phases of buck converter operation. Energy is **stored** in the inductor during the ON phase and **released** to the load during the OFF phase. The ratio $D = T_{on}/T_s$ directly determines the output voltage.

---

## 4.4 Architecture Mapping — Adaptive Fallback Block Diagram

For topics requiring physical circuit drawings, here is the **functional architecture mapping** of the standard full-wave bridge rectifier with capacitor filter (commonly used as the front-end of energy harvesters):

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    acIn[AC Harvester Output] --> d1[D1 Forward]
    acIn --> d2[D2 Forward]
    d1 --> nodeA[Positive Output Node]
    d2 --> nodeA
    nodeA --> cFilt[Filter Capacitor Cf]
    cFilt --> rLoad[Load Resistor Rload]
    rLoad --> nodeB[Negative Output Node]
    d3[D3 Return Path] --> nodeB
    d4[D4 Return Path] --> nodeB

    classDef src fill:#FFF4D6,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef dio fill:#D6E4FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef cap fill:#FFD6D6,stroke:#8B0000,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef ld fill:#D6FFD6,stroke:#006400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class acIn src
    class d1,d2,d3,d4 dio
    class cFilt cap
    class rLoad,nodeA,nodeB ld
```

**Engineering reading:**
- During the positive half-cycle, **D1 and D4** conduct; during the negative half-cycle, **D2 and D3** conduct.
- Each current path drops **2 × $V_F$** across diodes.
- Capacitor $C_f$ smooths the pulsating DC into a near-constant level.
- The ripple amplitude is inversely proportional to $C_f$ and load current.

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define power conditioning. Why is it essential in an IoT energy harvesting system?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Power conditioning is the process of converting and regulating the raw electrical output of an energy harvester into a stable, usable DC form for the IoT load. (1 mark) It performs rectification, voltage regulation, filtering, and impedance matching. (1 mark) It is essential because ambient energy sources produce **intermittent**, **low-amplitude**, and **variable-impedance** signals that cannot directly power microcontrollers or sensors; power conditioning ensures **stable voltage**, **maximum energy extraction** (via MPPT), and **efficient energy storage** interfacing. (1 mark)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List any four sources of power loss in a DC-DC converter used for energy harvesting.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Conduction loss** — $I_{RMS}^{2} \cdot R_{DS(on)}$ in MOSFETs and inductor windings. (0.75)
2. **Switching loss** — $\frac{1}{2} V_{in} I_{out} (t_r + t_f) f_{sw}$ during transitions. (0.75)
3. **Quiescent loss** — $V_{in} \cdot I_Q$ consumed by the control IC. (0.75)
4. **Magnetic core loss** — hysteresis and eddy current losses in the inductor core. (0.75)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — ESE Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 regulations, Part B questions carry **14 marks** with sub-parts of **7 + 7 marks**, and students must answer **either Option A or Option B** in full.

---

### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the various stages of power conditioning in an IoT energy harvesting system. Discuss the role of each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A boost converter operates in CCM with $V_{in} = 3.3\,\text{V}$, $D = 0.6$, switching frequency $f_{sw} = 500\,\text{kHz}$, and load current $I_{out} = 100\,\text{mA}$. The MOSFET has $R_{DS(on)} = 50\,\text{m}\Omega$ and switching times $t_r + t_f = 20\,\text{ns}$. Calculate:
   (i) The output voltage $V_{out}$.
   (ii) The switching loss $P_{sw}$.
   (iii) The conduction loss $P_{cond}$ (assume $I_{RMS} \approx I_{out}$).
   (iv) The total efficiency if quiescent + core + ESR losses sum to $4\,\text{mW}$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question A

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

The functional block diagram of the power conditioning chain is:

1. **Energy Source / Transducer (1 mark):** Piezo, PV, TEG, RF, or vibration harvester producing a raw electrical signal (often AC, low-amplitude, variable).
2. **Rectifier (1 mark):** Converts AC to DC. For low-voltage harvesters, a **synchronous active rectifier** is used to overcome diode drop losses.
3. **MPPT / Pre-Regulator (1 mark):** Adjusts the operating point of the harvester to its maximum power point via impedance matching (e.g., Fractional Open-Circuit Voltage or Perturb & Observe).
4. **DC-DC Converter (2 marks):** Steps the unregulated DC up or down to the required load voltage. Topologies: Buck, Boost, Buck-Boost, SEPIC.
5. **Output Filter and Storage (1 mark):** LC filter smooths ripple; battery or supercapacitor stores excess energy.
6. **Load (1 mark):** The IoT device — sensor + MCU + radio.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) Output Voltage (2 marks):**

$$
V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1-D} = \dfrac{3.3}{1 - 0.6} = \dfrac{3.3}{0.4} = 8.25\,\text{V}
$$

**(ii) Switching Loss (2 marks):**

$$
P_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot V_{in} \cdot I_{out} \cdot (t_r + t_f) \cdot f_{sw}
$$

$$
P_{sw} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot 3.3 \cdot 0.1 \cdot (20 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (500 \times 10^{3}) = 1.65\,\text{mW}
$$

**(iii) Conduction Loss (1.5 marks):**

$$
P_{cond} = I_{out}^{2} \cdot R_{DS(on)} = (0.1)^{2} \cdot 0.05 = 0.5\,\text{mW}
$$

**(iv) Total Efficiency (1.5 marks):**

$$
P_{out} = V_{out} \cdot I_{out} = 8.25 \cdot 0.1 = 825\,\text{mW}
$$

$$
P_{in} = P_{out} + P_{sw} + P_{cond} + P_{other} = 825 + 1.65 + 0.5 + 4 = 831.15\,\text{mW}
$$

$$
\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = \dfrac{825}{831.15} = 0.9925 = 99.25\,\text{\%}
$$

> [!TIP]
> The high efficiency here is because the load is light relative to the switching transients. In real low-power harvesters, the **quiescent current** of the IC dominates and efficiency drops to 60–80 %.

---

### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Compare linear regulators (LDO) and switching DC-DC converters in the context of energy harvesting. Which is preferred for a battery-less thermoelectric harvester, and why? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A full-wave bridge rectifier supplies a load $R_L = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ from a sinusoidal source of $V_{peak} = 5\,\text{V}$ and $f = 50\,\text{Hz}$. Each diode has a forward drop $V_F = 0.7\,\text{V}$. Calculate:
   (i) The DC output voltage.
   (ii) The peak inverse voltage across each diode.
   (iii) The ripple frequency and fundamental ripple amplitude.
   (iv) The rectifier efficiency (assume second-harmonic ripple is dominant). **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — Question B

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

| Parameter | LDO (Linear) | Switching DC-DC | Mark |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :---: |
| Efficiency | $\eta = V_{out}/V_{in}$ — poor at low $V_{in}$ | 80–95 % across range | 1.5 |
| Noise / EMI | Very low (no switching) | Higher (switching noise) | 1 |
| Component count | Small (no inductor) | Larger (L, C, switches) | 0.5 |
| Quiescent current | Low (µA) | Higher (controller draws current) | 1 |
| Heat dissipation | $P = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I$ | Very low | 1 |
| Input range | Narrow | Wide (especially Boost / SEPIC) | 1 |
| Best for harvesters | **Low-dropout, low-noise** nodes | **Wide-input, high-efficiency** nodes | 1 |

**Preferred for battery-less TEG (1 mark):**
A **switching boost converter** is preferred because:
- TEG output is **sub-1V** (e.g., 0.3–0.8 V) and must be stepped up to 3.3 V.
- An LDO would give $\eta < 50\%$ at $V_{in} = 0.5\,\text{V}$ and $V_{out} = 3.3\,\text{V}$ — most energy is wasted as heat.
- A boost converter with **cold-start** capability (e.g., TI BQ25504) can begin operating from a few hundred millivolts and self-power once running.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

**(i) DC Output Voltage (1.5 marks):**

For a full-wave bridge with capacitor filter (approximated by peak value minus two diode drops):

$$
V_{DC} \approx V_{peak} - 2V_F = 5 - 2(0.7) = 3.6\,\text{V}
$$

**(ii) Peak Inverse Voltage (1.5 marks):**

In a bridge rectifier, each diode sees the peak of the input when reverse-biased:

$$
PIV = V_{peak} = 5\,\text{V}
$$

**(iii) Ripple Frequency and Amplitude (2 marks):**

For a full-wave rectifier, the ripple frequency is twice the line frequency:

$$
f_{ripple} = 2f = 2 \cdot 50 = 100\,\text{Hz}
$$

Ripple amplitude (approximate, with capacitor filter):

$$
V_{ripple} \approx \dfrac{I_{load}}{2 f_{ripple} C} = \dfrac{V_{DC}/R_L}{2 f_{ripple} C} = \dfrac{3.6/1000}{100 \cdot C}
$$

Without C specified, the unfiltered RMS ripple is:

$$
V_{rms,ac} = \dfrac{V_{peak} - 2V_F}{2\sqrt{3}} = \dfrac{3.6}{3.464} \approx 1.04\,\text{V}
$$

**(iv) Rectifier Efficiency (2 marks):**

$$
\eta_{rect} = \dfrac{P_{DC}}{P_{DC} + P_{AC}} = \dfrac{V_{DC}^{2}/R_L}{V_{DC}^{2}/R_L + V_{rms,ac}^{2}/R_L} = \dfrac{V_{DC}^{2}}{V_{DC}^{2} + V_{rms,ac}^{2}}
$$

$$
\eta_{rect} = \dfrac{(3.6)^{2}}{(3.6)^{2} + (1.04)^{2}} = \dfrac{12.96}{12.96 + 1.0816} = \dfrac{12.96}{14.0416} = 0.923 = 92.3\,\text{\%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> If the load were purely resistive without capacitor filtering, the standard textbook formula gives $\eta_{rect} = 81.2\,\%$. The higher value here (92.3 %) is because the capacitor filter reduces AC ripple content dramatically.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks on "Power Conditioning and Losses" questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the diode drop in rectifier calculations** — the answer must subtract $2V_F$ for a bridge rectifier. Many students write $V_{DC} = V_{peak}$ and lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Mixing up Buck and Boost formulas** — Buck: $V_{out} = D V_{in}$; Boost: $V_{out} = V_{in}/(1-D)$. Confusion here costs the entire 7-mark sub-part.
> 3. **Omitting units in numerical answers** — KTU examiners explicitly deduct 0.5 marks per missing unit. Always write V, mW, kHz, etc.
> 4. **Quoting only the switching loss formula and ignoring quiescent loss** — for sub-mW harvesters, $P_Q$ dominates; an answer ignoring it is incomplete.
> 5. **Not specifying CCM vs DCM** — boost converter derivation assumes Continuous Conduction Mode. Always state this assumption.
> 6. **Confusing $f_{sw}$ (switching frequency) with $f_{line}$ (mains/AC frequency)** — these are two very different quantities and using one in place of the other produces wildly wrong results.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Power Conditioning and Losses**

- 🔹 Power conditioning = **Rectification + Regulation + Filtering + MPPT** between harvester and load.
- 🔹 **Half-wave** rectifiers: $\eta \approx 40.6\,\%$, ripple factor 1.21 → rarely used.
- 🔹 **Full-wave bridge** rectifiers: $\eta \approx 81.2\,\%$, ripple factor 0.48 → industry standard.
- 🔹 **Synchronous / active rectifiers** replace diodes with MOSFETs to eliminate $V_F$ loss — essential for sub-1V harvesters.
- 🔹 **Buck**: $V_{out} = D V_{in}$ (step-down); **Boost**: $V_{out} = V_{in}/(1-D)$ (step-up); **Buck-Boost**: $V_{out} = -D V_{in}/(1-D)$.
- 🔹 **SEPIC** = non-inverting buck-boost; preferred when input range straddles output.
- 🔹 Five loss categories: **Conduction, Switching, Quiescent, Core, Capacitor ESR**.
- 🔹 Switching loss: $P_{sw} = \frac{1}{2} V_{in} I_{out} (t_r + t_f) f_{sw}$ — grows **linearly** with $f_{sw}$.
- 🔹 Conduction loss: $P_{cond} = I_{RMS}^{2} R_{DS(on)}$ — minimize by choosing low-$R_{DS(on)}$ MOSFETs.
- 🔹 Quiescent loss: $P_Q = V_{in} I_Q$ — **dominant** in µW-range harvesters; choose nano-power ICs.
- 🔹 LDO is **simple but inefficient** for large $V_{in}/V_{out}$ ratios; switching converters preferred for energy harvesting.
- 🔹 For **thermoelectric (TEG)** harvesters, a **boost converter with cold-start** is the standard choice.
- 🔹 For **photovoltaic (PV)** harvesters, an **MPPT + boost/buck** stage is used.
- 🔹 For **piezoelectric/vibration** harvesters, a **bridge or active rectifier + buck/SEPIC** is the typical chain.
- 🔹 Always state **CCM assumption** in converter derivations.
- 🔹 Always include **units** in numerical answers — KTU examiners deduct for missing units.
- 🔹 **PIV** of a bridge diode = $V_{peak}$ of the AC source.
- 🔹 Total efficiency $\eta = P_{out} / (P_{out} + \sum P_{loss})$ — never exceeds 100 %.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies for Implanted Medical Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies for Implanted Medical Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Implanted Medical Devices (IMDs)** are active electronic systems that are surgically placed inside the human body (subcutaneously, intravascularly, or within specific organs) to perform continuous diagnostic, monitoring, or therapeutic functions over extended periods — typically **5 to 15 years** without physical intervention. From an energy perspective, IMDs are the most demanding class of IoT edge nodes because they operate under strict **power budgets (10 µW – 10 mW)**, severe **size constraints (sub-cm³ to few cm³)**, and absolute **biocompatibility and safety constraints**.

**Case Studies for Implanted Medical Devices** in the context of Energy Harvesting & Power Management refers to the structured engineering analysis of representative IMDs — such as cardiac pacemakers, cochlear implants, retinal prostheses, deep brain stimulators (DBS), and ingestible capsule endoscopes — by examining their **load power profile, available harvested energy source, energy storage architecture, and the power management integrated circuit (PMIC) topology** that conditions the harvested energy to a usable form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECIT746 Module 4):**
> *Study of representative case studies illustrating how energy harvesting (vibration, thermal, RF, bioelectric) and ultra-low-power PMIC design principles are applied in real-world implanted medical devices, with emphasis on power budget, lifetime, biocompatibility, and wireless power transfer strategies.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Bicycle Light" Analogy:**
> Think of an implanted medical device as a **bicycle light** that must work perfectly while buried deep inside a sealed metal can (the human body), with **no access to swap batteries for 10 years**.

| Bicycle Light Concept | Implanted Medical Device (IMD) |
|---|---|
| Bulb (Load) | Sensor / Pulse generator / Stimulator electrode |
| Battery | Lithium-iodine primary cell or rechargeable Li-ion |
| Dynamo (energy harvester) | Piezoelectric vibration / Thermoelectric (TEG) / RF coil |
| Voltage regulator | Ultra-low-power PMIC (Boost/Buck converter) |
| Light switch | Wireless telemetry / Inductive link |
| Waterproof casing | Hermetic titanium/ceramic encapsulation |
| Darkness (battery dies) | Device failure (potentially fatal for pacemakers!) |

The device must **harvest every scrap of available energy** (body heat, heartbeat vibration, glucose oxidation, external RF) and **spend it as efficiently as possible** — because the "battery" cannot be replaced without another surgery.

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters for KTU

KTU examiners consistently test whether students can:
1. Map an **energy source** to a **specific anatomical energy budget**.
2. Calculate **endurance / lifetime** of an implant.
3. Justify the choice of **PMIC topology** (LDO vs Buck vs Boost vs Charge Pump).
4. Discuss **wireless power transfer (WPT)** as a recharge strategy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metrics to Memorize (Bold = examiner-favourite):**
> - **Power budget of modern pacemakers ≈ 10 – 30 µW (average)**
> - **Target lifetime: 7 – 10 years (primary cell) / 15+ years (with energy harvesting)**
> - **Thermoelectric gradient available subcutaneously: ΔT ≈ 1 – 5 K**
> - **Heartbeat vibration frequency: 0.5 – 5 Hz, acceleration 0.5 – 2 m/s²**
> - **Specific energy of Li-I₂ cell: ≈ 200 – 300 Wh/kg**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Architecture of Any IMD

Every implanted medical device, regardless of clinical function, consists of **five functional blocks** interacting with the energy supply chain:

1. **Energy Source (ES):** Primary battery / Secondary battery / Energy Harvester
2. **Power Conditioning & PMIC:** Rectifier, DC-DC converter (LDO / Buck / Boost / Charge Pump), Voltage Reference
3. **Energy Storage (ES2):** Supercapacitor / Thin-film Li-ion / Hybrid capacitor
4. **Load / Application Circuit:** Sensors, DSP, RF telemetry, stimulation electrodes
5. **Wireless Interface:** Inductive link (WPT) + Telemetry (ASK/FSK backscatter or BLE)

### The Three Operating Modes

| Mode | Function | Typical Power |
|---|---|---|
| **Sleep / Monitoring** | Watch for threshold / event | **1 – 10 µW** |
| **Active / Stimulation** | Deliver therapy pulse | **1 – 10 mW (pulsed)** |
| **Communication / Telemetry** | Wireless data exchange | **10 – 50 mW (pulsed)** |

The **average power** is what dictates battery life — so the device spends **>99% of its time in sleep mode**.

## 2.2 Energy Source Decision Matrix

| Energy Source | Power Density | Best Suited For | KTU Favourite Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary Li-I₂ battery** | 200–300 Wh/kg | Pacemakers (hermetic, 10 yr) | Cardiac Pacemaker |
| **Inductive WPT (RF coil)** | 10–50 mW @ cm distance | Rechargeable implants | Cochlear implant, DBS |
| **Thermoelectric (TEG)** | 10–100 µW/cm² @ ΔT=5K | Subcutaneous location | Pacemaker, glucose sensor |
| **Piezoelectric (vibration)** | 1–100 µW | Heartbeat / lung motion | Pacemaker, artificial heart |
| **Biofuel cell (glucose)** | 1–10 µW/cm² | Continuous metabolite | Subcutaneous sensor |
| **Photovoltaic** | 10–100 µW | Subcutaneous near NIR window | Retinal implant |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{total} = P_{avg} \times t_{life}$ | Total energy consumed over device life | Joules or Wh |
| 2 | $t_{life} = \dfrac{C_{batt} \times V_{nom}}{P_{avg}}$ | Battery lifetime | Hours / Years |
| 3 | $P_{TEG} = \dfrac{\alpha^2 \cdot \Delta T^2}{4 R_{load} \cdot (1 + \alpha \bar{T})}$ | Thermoelectric generator output (Seebeck) | Watts |
| 4 | $P_{piezo} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot C_p \cdot V_{oc}^2 \cdot f$ | Piezo harvestable power (rectified) | Watts |
| 5 | $P_{RF,rect} = \eta_{rect} \cdot P_{inc} \cdot G_t \cdot G_r \cdot \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi d}\right)^2$ | Friis-equation RF harvesting | Watts |
| 6 | $P_{ind} = \dfrac{V_1^2 \cdot \omega^2 \cdot M^2}{(R_2^2 + (\omega L_2)^2) \cdot R_L}$ | Inductive link power transfer | Watts |
| 7 | $\eta_{PMIC} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} = 1 - \dfrac{P_{loss}}{P_{in}}$ | Converter efficiency | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 8 | $V_{out} = V_{in} \cdot D$ | Buck converter (D = duty) | Volts |
| 9 | $V_{out} = \dfrac{V_{in}}{1-D}$ | Boost converter | Volts |
| 10 | $C_{storage} \ge \dfrac{I_{load} \cdot \Delta t}{\Delta V_{drop}}$ | Hold-up capacitor sizing | Farads |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** In the table above, vertical bars have been replaced with `\vert` or parenthetical expressions to prevent markdown table breakage. KTU examiners will deduct marks for sloppy notation!

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The same PMIC design decisions made for pacemakers directly translate to:
- **Structural health monitoring** sensors in bridges (vibration harvesting)
- **Livestock tracking tags** (primary Li cells, ultra-low duty cycle)
- **Smart agriculture soil sensors** (biofuel-cell inspired)
- **Wearable health patches** (TEG harvesting from skin)

This makes IMDs a **canonical benchmark** for any ultra-low-power IoT design.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Case Study 1: Cardiac Pacemaker — Lifetime Derivation

**Problem Statement (KTU typical):** A pacemaker uses a **Li-I₂ primary cell** with capacity **C = 2.5 Ah** at nominal voltage **V = 2.8 V**. The circuit consumes:
- Sleep current: **I_sleep = 8 µA @ 2.8 V**
- Stimulation pulse (1 ms duration, once per second): **I_pulse = 5 mA @ 2.8 V**
- Telemetry burst (10 ms, once per hour): **I_telem = 20 mA @ 2.8 V**

Calculate the **average power consumption** and **expected lifetime in years**.

### Step 1: Compute Average Current

The average current is the time-weighted mean of the three operating modes.

$$
I_{avg} = \dfrac{(I_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep}) + (I_{pulse} \cdot N_{pulse} \cdot t_{pulse}) + (I_{telem} \cdot N_{telem} \cdot t_{telem})}{T_{total}}
$$

Where $T_{total}$ = **1 hour** (convenient base period covering all events).

### Step 2: Substitute Known Values

$$
I_{sleep} = 8~\mu A \quad ; \quad t_{sleep} = 3600~s - 3600~pulses \times 1~ms - 1~burst \times 0.01~s
$$

Let us first compute the times in 1 hour:
- Total pulses: $N_{pulse} = 3600$ (one per second)
- Total telemetry: $N_{telem} = 1$

$$
t_{sleep} = 3600 - (3600 \times 10^{-3}) - (1 \times 10 \times 10^{-3}) = 3600 - 3.6 - 0.01 = 3596.39~s
$$

### Step 3: Compute Charge Consumed per Hour

$$
Q_{hour} = I_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep} + I_{pulse} \cdot t_{pulse,total} + I_{telem} \cdot t_{telem,total}
$$

$$
Q_{hour} = (8 \times 10^{-6})(3596.39) + (5 \times 10^{-3})(3.6) + (20 \times 10^{-3})(0.01)
$$

$$
Q_{hour} = 0.02877 + 0.018 + 0.0002 = 0.04697~C
$$

But current is charge per unit time, so:

$$
I_{avg} = \dfrac{Q_{hour}}{3600} = \dfrac{0.04697}{3600} = 1.305 \times 10^{-5}~A = 13.05~\mu A
$$

### Step 4: Average Power

$$
P_{avg} = V_{nom} \cdot I_{avg} = 2.8 \times 13.05 \times 10^{-6} = 36.5~\mu W
$$

### Step 5: Battery Lifetime

$$
t_{life} = \dfrac{C_{batt}}{I_{avg}} = \dfrac{2.5~Ah}{13.05~\mu A} = \dfrac{2.5}{13.05 \times 10^{-6}}~h
$$

$$
t_{life} = 191{,}571~h = 21.87~years
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** Although the *math* suggests 21+ years, **derating factors** (cell self-discharge ≈ 1%/year, temperature-induced capacity fade, and a safety margin to avoid end-of-life sudden failure) reduce the **clinically approved lifetime to 7–10 years**. KTU expects you to mention this derating!

## 3.2 Case Study 2: Cochlear Implant — Inductive WPT Link Analysis

**Problem:** A cochlear implant receives power via an external coil (TX) worn behind the ear coupled to an internal coil (RX) under the skin. Parameters:
- Coil separation: **d = 8 mm** (skin + scalp)
- Mutual inductance: **M = 2.5 µH**
- TX frequency: **f = 5 MHz**, $\omega = 2\pi f$
- RX coil inductance: **L₂ = 1.5 µH**, internal R₂ = 0.4 Ω
- Load: **R_L = 200 Ω** (rectifier + implant electronics)

**Find:** Power delivered to load if TX current I₁ produces a primary voltage V₁ = 5 V across its self-inductance at resonance.

### Step 1: Compute ω²M² Term

$$
\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 5 \times 10^6 = 3.142 \times 10^7~rad/s
$$

$$
\omega^2 M^2 = (3.142 \times 10^7)^2 \times (2.5 \times 10^{-6})^2
$$

$$
\omega^2 M^2 = (9.87 \times 10^{14}) \times (6.25 \times 10^{-12}) = 6.17 \times 10^3 = 6170
$$

### Step 2: Compute Denominator

At resonance, $\omega L_2$ dominates the secondary branch:

$$
\omega L_2 = 3.142 \times 10^7 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-6} = 47.13~\Omega
$$

$$
R_2 + R_L = 0.4 + 200 = 200.4~\Omega
$$

So $\omega L_2^2 + (R_2 + R_L)^2 \approx 200.4^2 + 47.13^2$

Wait — the standard formula for inductive link power delivered to load $R_L$ when both sides are series-resonated is:

$$
P_L = \dfrac{V_1^2 \cdot \omega^2 M^2}{(R_2 + R_L)^2 \cdot R_L}
$$

(after resonance cancellation of the imaginary parts). The total secondary resistance is $R_2 + R_L$ when reflected.

Actually, for **series-series compensation**, with the TX driven at the primary resonant angular frequency $\omega$, the secondary induced voltage is $V_{ind} = j\omega M I_1$. After resonance, the current in the secondary loop is:

$$
I_2 = \dfrac{V_{ind}}{R_2 + R_L} = \dfrac{\omega M I_1}{R_2 + R_L}
$$

We are given $V_1$ (not $I_1$). At primary resonance, $V_1 = \omega L_1 I_1$, so $I_1 = V_1 / (\omega L_1)$. But we are not given $L_1$ explicitly.

**Simpler interpretation:** Treat the secondary side power directly. The induced EMF in the secondary is $V_2 = \omega M I_1$. The fraction of $V_1$ that appears as $V_2$ in a well-coupled link is governed by $k$ (coupling coefficient). For $k \approx 0.1$ at d = 8 mm (typical for cochlear), and assuming $V_1 = 5~V$ is on the primary and that the standard model gives:

$$
V_2 = k \sqrt{L_1/L_2} \cdot V_1
$$

For this problem we will use the **standard simplified formula** directly:

$$
P_L = \dfrac{(V_1 \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{L_1/L_2})^2 \cdot \omega^2 M^2}{...}
$$

**To keep the KTU derivation tractable**, let us adopt the **reflected impedance model**. The reflected resistance from the secondary into the primary is:

$$
R_{ref} = \dfrac{\omega^2 M^2}{R_2 + R_L}
$$

### Step 3: Compute Reflected Resistance

$$
R_{ref} = \dfrac{6170}{200.4} = 30.79~\Omega
$$

### Step 4: Compute Secondary Current

If primary is driven with $V_1 = 5~V$ across $L_1$ at resonance, and the secondary is in series with $R_L + R_2$, the secondary current is:

$$
I_2 = \dfrac{\omega M I_1}{R_2 + R_L} = \dfrac{V_1 \cdot k \cdot \sqrt{L_1/L_2}}{R_2 + R_L}
$$

This requires $L_1$. **For KTU exam simplicity**, the standard form often given is:

$$
P_L = \dfrac{V_1^2 \cdot \omega^2 M^2}{(R_2 + R_L)^2 \cdot R_{primary,total}}
$$

But the cleanest form is to use the **secondary-side analysis**:

Given the TX current $I_1$ such that $\omega L_1 I_1 = V_1 = 5~V$ at resonance, the secondary induced voltage is $V_2 = j\omega M I_1$. Assuming $L_1 = 10~\mu H$ (typical for a cochlear TX coil):

$$
I_1 = \dfrac{V_1}{\omega L_1} = \dfrac{5}{3.142 \times 10^7 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}} = \dfrac{5}{314.2} = 0.01592~A
$$

$$
V_2 = \omega M I_1 = 3.142 \times 10^7 \times 2.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.01592 = 1.250~V
$$

### Step 5: Power Delivered to Load

$$
I_2 = \dfrac{V_2}{R_2 + R_L} = \dfrac{1.250}{200.4} = 6.24~mA
$$

$$
P_L = I_2^2 \cdot R_L = (6.24 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 200 = 7.78 \times 10^{-3}~W = 7.78~mW
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result Interpretation:** 7.78 mW is **comfortably above** the typical cochlear implant operating power of 1–5 mW. This is why inductive WPT is the **industry standard** for cochlear implants. The external speech processor's battery (typically 3–4 days runtime) is rechargeable, while the internal electronics never need to be replaced.

## 3.3 Case Study 3: Thermoelectric-Powered Pacemaker — TEG Power Estimate

A subcutaneous TEG is placed between the muscle (≈ 37 °C) and the skin surface (≈ 33 °C). 
- Number of thermocouples: N = 200
- Seebeck coefficient per junction: α = 200 µV/K
- Internal TEG resistance: R_in = 5 Ω
- Load resistance (matched): R_L = 5 Ω
- Hot/cold side temp: T_h = 310 K, T_c = 306 K → ΔT = 4 K

### Step 1: Open-Circuit Voltage

$$
V_{oc} = N \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 200 \times 200 \times 10^{-6} \times 4 = 0.16~V
$$

### Step 2: Matched-Load Power

With $R_L = R_{in}$ (matched):

$$
P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_{in}} = \dfrac{(0.16)^2}{4 \times 5} = \dfrac{0.0256}{20} = 1.28~mW
$$

### Step 3: Available Energy in 1 Day

$$
E_{day} = P_{max} \times 86{,}400 = 1.28 \times 10^{-3} \times 86{,}400 = 110.6~J
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy vs Power Trade-off:** 1.28 mW is *more than 30× the average pacemaker demand* (≈ 36.5 µW from §3.1). However, the harvested energy must pass through a **boost converter + energy buffer** because the TEG voltage (0.16 V) is far below the battery's 2.8 V. Efficiency of this boost stage (typically 70–85%) determines the **net usable energy**, which is closer to 80–100 J/day — still ample, but it does NOT eliminate the battery, it only **recharges** it.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which PMIC for Which Case?

| Case Study | Voltage Source | PMIC Choice | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pacemaker (Li-I₂)** | 2.8 V stable | **LDO** | Lowest noise for sensing, very low I_q (<1 µA) |
| **Pacemaker + TEG** | 0.1 – 0.3 V | **Boost + MPPT** | Step-up with cold-start circuit |
| **Cochlear Implant (WPT)** | 4 – 6 V rectified | **Buck + LDO cascade** | Efficient step-down + clean analog rails |
| **Retinal Prosthesis** | 3.7 V Li-ion | **Buck-Boost** | Battery varies 3.0 – 4.2 V across SoC |
| **Capsule Endoscopy** | 1.5 V Zn-air | **Boost** | Single-cell step-up to 3.3 V for camera/RF |
| **DBS** | 3.7 V Li-ion (recharge) | **Buck + wireless charger IC** | Pulse-mode load needs high efficiency |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Generic IMD Power Management Architecture

The block-level functional architecture flow for an implanted medical device with **hybrid energy harvesting + battery backup + WPT telemetry** is shown below.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Inductive TX Coil External] --> B[Inductive RX Coil Implanted]
    C[TEG Subcutaneous] --> D[Rectifier TEG]
    E[Piezo Vibration] --> F[Rectifier Piezo]
    G[Biofuel Cell Glucose] --> H[Rectifier Bio]

    B --> I[Bridge Rectifier and Clamp]
    D --> I
    F --> I
    H --> I

    I --> J[PMIC Cold Start Circuit]
    J --> K{Primary or Harvester}

    K -- Harvester Path --> L[MPPT Controller]
    L --> M[Buck Boost DC DC Converter]

    K -- WPT Path --> N[Synchronous Rectifier]
    N --> M

    M --> O[Energy Storage Bank]
    O --> P[Thin Film Li ion Cell]
    O --> Q[Supercapacitor Buffer]

    M --> R[Voltage Regulation LDO]
    R --> S[DSP and DSP core]
    R --> T[Stimulation Pulse Generator]
    R --> U[RF Telemetry BLE MICS]

    V[Implanted Sensor Array] --> S
    W[Therapy Electrodes] --> T
```

**Explanation of Flow:**
- **Energy In (Left):** Four harvested/RF sources feed into a unified rectifier network.
- **Power Conditioning (Center):** Cold-start + MPPT + DC-DC conversion provides a regulated intermediate rail.
- **Storage (Right-Center):** Hybrid Li-ion + supercapacitor buffer handles pulse loads.
- **Load (Right):** Three independent rails: DSP, Stimulation, and Telemetry — each with its own LDO for noise isolation.

## 4.2 Pacemaker Power State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> SleepMode
    SleepMode --> BradyDetect : Heartbeat below threshold
    BradyDetect --> StimulationActive : Pacing required
    BradyDetect --> SleepMode : Normal rhythm restored
    StimulationActive --> SleepMode : Pulse delivered
    SleepMode --> TelemetryBurst : Scheduled data upload
    TelemetryBurst --> SleepMode : Data sent

    note right of SleepMode : Power 8 microamp
    note right of StimulationActive : Power 5 milliamp pulsed
    note right of TelemetryBurst : Power 20 milliamp short burst
```

## 4.3 Cochlear Implant — Inductive Link Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Sound Microphone] --> B[Speech Processor DSP]
    B --> C[Encoding and Compression]
    C --> D[TX Coil Driver Class E Amp]
    D --> E[External Coil 5 MHz]
    E --> F[Internal Coil Subcutaneous]
    F --> G[Differential Rectifier]
    G --> H[Capacitor Bank Reservoir]
    H --> I[Buck Regulator 3.3 V]
    I --> J[Cochlear Electrode Array Stimulation]
    I --> K[Signal Decoder ASIC]
    K --> J
```

## 4.4 Retinal Prosthesis Energy Path

```mermaid
graph TD
```
