```mermaid
    A[External Glasses Mounted Camera] --> B[Image Processor Video]
    B --> C[NIR Photodiode Array on Glasses]
    C --> D[Inductive TX Coil Near Infrared]
    D --> E[Photovoltaic Cells on Retinal Implant]
    E --> F[Rectifier on Retina]
    F --> G[Buck Boost Converter]
    G --> H[Stimulator ASIC 60 Electrode Array]
    G --> I[Telemetry Return Link FSK]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading Tip for KTU Exam:** When asked "draw the block diagram", examiners expect *all four* layers: Energy Source → PMIC → Storage → Load. Missing any one of these layers costs **2 marks minimum**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**List the three primary energy harvesting modalities used in implanted medical devices and state the typical power density available from each.**

**Model Answer:**

The three primary energy harvesting modalities for IMDs are:

1. **Thermoelectric (TEG / Seebeck):** Exploits the temperature gradient between core body (~37 °C) and subcutaneous tissue / skin surface (~33 °C). Typical power density is **10 – 100 µW/cm²** at a gradient of 1–5 K, which is the most useful range for subcutaneous pacemakers and continuous glucose monitors.

2. **Piezoelectric (Vibration):** Converts mechanical vibration of the heartbeat, lung motion, or muscle contraction into electrical energy. Available power is **1 – 100 µW** depending on coupling, but only **0.5 – 5 Hz** dominant frequencies from cardiac motion — very low compared to industrial vibration harvesters.

3. **Inductive / RF (Wireless Power Transfer):** External coil coupled to an implanted coil delivers **10 – 50 mW** over a few cm — by far the highest power. Used in cochlear implants, DBS, and retinal prostheses where external powering is acceptable.

> [!NOTE]
> Other modalities include **biofuel cells** (glucose oxidation, 1–10 µW/cm²) and **photovoltaic** (subcutaneous NIR, 10–100 µW).

**Marks distribution:** 1 mark for each modality with correct modality name, 0.5 mark extra for mentioning the typical power range with units.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]
**Why is a Buck-Boost converter preferred over a pure Buck or pure Boost in a battery-powered retinal prosthesis?**

**Model Answer:**

A retinal prosthesis uses a **rechargeable Li-ion cell** whose voltage varies from **4.2 V (full charge) to 3.0 V (end-of-discharge)**. The stimulator ASIC and electrode drivers require a **stable 3.3 V rail** for current-controlled biphasic pulses.

- A **pure Buck** can only step down — it would fail when $V_{bat} < 3.3~V$ (i.e., most of the discharge curve), causing the prosthesis to shut down prematurely.
- A **pure Boost** can only step up — it would be inefficient when $V_{bat} > 3.3~V$ and the converter would not regulate properly.
- A **Buck-Boost** (or 4-switch SEPIC / flyback topology) provides **regulation across the full battery SoC range**, ensuring the implant operates until the battery is fully drained.

Additionally, the Buck-Boost topology allows seamless transition between battery-powered operation and WPT-powered operation, critical for an implant that may be recharged via glasses-mounted NIR coils.

**Marks distribution:** 1 mark for explaining Li-ion voltage range, 1 mark for explaining why pure Buck/Boost fails, 1 mark for the Buck-Boost advantage.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam Pattern]

**(a)** [7 Marks] With the aid of a block diagram, describe the power management architecture of a modern **cardiac pacemaker** powered by a Li-I₂ primary cell. Include the role of each block and the typical power consumption in sleep, stimulation, and telemetry modes.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A pacemaker Li-I₂ cell has a capacity of **1.8 Ah at 2.8 V**. The circuit consumes **I_sleep = 5 µA** continuously, **I_pulse = 4 mA** for 0.5 ms once per second, and **I_telem = 15 mA** for 5 ms once every 4 hours. Calculate the **average current, average power, and the expected lifetime in years**, accounting for a 1%/year self-discharge derating.

---

### QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** [7 Marks] Compare **inductive wireless power transfer (WPT)** and **thermoelectric harvesting (TEG)** as energy sources for a deep brain stimulator (DBS). Discuss the trade-offs in power level, surgical convenience, patient compliance, and PMIC complexity.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A TEG designed for a subcutaneous implant has **N = 250** thermocouples, each with Seebeck coefficient **α = 180 µV/K**. The hot-side (muscle) is at **T_h = 37 °C** and the cold-side (skin) at **T_c = 33 °C**. Internal resistance is **R_TEG = 6 Ω**. Calculate the **open-circuit voltage, the maximum power delivered to a matched load, and the daily energy harvested**.

---

## 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks Model Solution

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes for the standard PMIC architecture. Specifically, the pacemaker block diagram includes:)

| Block | Role | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Li-I₂ Primary Cell** | Energy source, hermetic, biocompatible | 2.8 V nominal, 1.0 – 3.0 Ah |
| **Input Protection (Zener + Schottky)** | Reverse polarity and ESD | Clamp @ 3.2 V |
| **LDO Regulator (Primary)** | Step-down to clean analog rail | 2.0 V output, I_q < 1 µA |
| **Boost Converter (Auxiliary)** | Step-up to 3.3 V for telemetry RF | 90% efficiency |
| **Microcontroller / DSP** | Bradycardia detection, pacing logic | Active: 500 µA @ 1 MHz |
| **Stimulation Pulse Gen** | Charge capacitor to 5 V, discharge into heart | 5 mA peak, 0.5 ms |
| **RF Telemetry (MICS band)** | 402 – 405 MHz data uplink | 20 mA peak, 5 ms burst |
| **Ceramic Feedthrough Filter** | EMI suppression on the hermetic seal | – |

**Sleep mode power:** $\approx 8–15~\mu W$ (LDO + RTC + sense amplifier)
**Stimulation mode power:** $\approx 5–10~mW$ (pulsed, duty cycle < 1%)
**Telemetry mode power:** $\approx 50–80~mW$ (pulsed, duty cycle < 0.01%)

The PMIC must isolate the sensitive analog sensing circuits from digital/RF noise — hence the LDO (not buck) is used for the primary rail because LDO produces **lower output ripple** (10 – 50 µV) critical for ECG signal detection.

*(Examiner's note: 4 marks for block diagram with 6+ blocks correctly labelled, 2 marks for power values with units, 1 mark for explaining LDO choice.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks Model Solution

**Step 1: Time per hour analysis**

In 1 hour (= 3600 s):
- Sleep time: $t_{sleep} = 3600 - (3600 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-3}) - (1 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}) = 3600 - 1.8 - 0.005 = 3598.195~s$
- Pulse active time: $t_{pulse} = 1.8~s$
- Telemetry active time: $t_{telem} = 0.005~s$

**Step 2: Average current**

$$
I_{avg} = \dfrac{I_{sleep} \cdot t_{sleep} + I_{pulse} \cdot t_{pulse} + I_{telem} \cdot t_{telem}}{3600}
$$

$$
I_{avg} = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-6})(3598.195) + (4 \times 10^{-3})(1.8) + (15 \times 10^{-3})(0.005)}{3600}
$$

$$
I_{avg} = \dfrac{0.017991 + 0.0072 + 0.000075}{3600} = \dfrac{0.025266}{3600} = 7.018~\mu A
$$

**Step 3: Average power**

$$
P_{avg} = V \cdot I_{avg} = 2.8 \times 7.018 \times 10^{-6} = 19.65~\mu W
$$

**Step 4: Raw lifetime (no derating)**

$$
t_{raw} = \dfrac{1.8~Ah}{7.018~\mu A} = \dfrac{1.8}{7.018 \times 10^{-6}} = 256{,}484~h = 29.28~years
$$

**Step 5: Apply 1% per year self-discharge derating**

Effective capacity reduces each year. For a constant-load application, the **effective lifetime** with linear self-discharge rate $r = 0.01$/year is given by:

$$
t_{eff} = t_{raw} \times (1 - r \cdot t_{eff})
$$

This is an implicit equation. Solving iteratively (or using the formula $t_{eff} \approx t_{raw}(1 - r \cdot t_{raw}/2)$ for small $r$):

$$
t_{eff} \approx 29.28 \times \left(1 - 0.01 \times \dfrac{29.28}{2}\right) = 29.28 \times (1 - 0.1464) = 29.28 \times 0.8536 = 24.99~years
$$

For a more rigorous approach, solving $t = 29.28 (1 - 0.01 t)$ gives $t = 29.28 - 0.2928 t$ → $1.2928 t = 29.28$ → $t = 22.65~years$.

**Step 6: Apply 50% safety margin (clinically)**

$$
t_{clinical} = 0.5 \times 22.65 = 11.3~years
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** Average current = **7.02 µA**, Average power = **19.65 µW**, Clinically expected lifetime = **11.3 years** — which matches real-world pacemaker specifications (7–12 years) published by Medtronic, Boston Scientific, and Abbott.

**Marks distribution:** Step 1: 1 mark, Step 2: 2 marks, Step 3: 1 mark, Step 4: 1 mark, Step 5: 1 mark, Step 6 with interpretation: 1 mark.

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks Model Solution

| Parameter | Inductive WPT | Thermoelectric (TEG) |
|---|---|---|
| **Power delivered** | 10 – 50 mW (high) | 50 – 500 µW (low) |
| **Distance / Penetration** | 1 – 15 mm through skin | 0 (subcutaneous, ΔT) |
| **Patient compliance** | Must wear external coil on head (stigma, sleep impact) | No external hardware (fully implanted) |
| **Surgical convenience** | Receiver coil subcutaneously, simple | TEG between muscle & skin, more invasive |
| **PMIC complexity** | Rectifier + Buck + telemetry | Boost + MPPT + cold-start |
| **Lifestyle impact** | Must recharge / wear coil 1–2 hr/day | Truly invisible, lifetime 10+ years |
| **EMI / Safety** | Stricter SAR limits (head) | Essentially zero EMI |
| **Best fit** | High-power DBS, BCI | Pacemaker, glucose sensor |

**Conclusion:** Inductive WPT is chosen for **high-power DBS** where stimulus pulses need mA-level currents many times per second. TEG is chosen for **always-on monitoring** where patient comfort outweighs the need for high power.

*(2 marks for the comparison table, 2 marks for trade-off discussion, 2 marks for "best fit" reasoning, 1 mark for conclusion.)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks Model Solution

**Step 1: Open-Circuit Voltage**

$$
V_{oc} = N \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T = 250 \times 180 \times 10^{-6} \times (37 - 33)
$$

$$
V_{oc} = 250 \times 180 \times 10^{-6} \times 4 = 0.180~V
$$

**Step 2: Matched-Load Maximum Power**

With $R_L = R_{TEG} = 6~\Omega$:

$$
P_{max} = \dfrac{V_{oc}^2}{4 R_{TEG}} = \dfrac{(0.180)^2}{4 \times 6} = \dfrac{0.0324}{24} = 1.35~mW
$$

**Step 3: Daily Energy Harvested**

$$
E_{day} = P_{max} \times 86{,}400 = 1.35 \times 10^{-3} \times 86{,}400 = 116.64~J/day
$$

$$
E_{day} = \dfrac{116.64}{3600} = 0.0324~Wh/day
$$

**Step 4: Practical Accounting**

With realistic MPPT + boost efficiency of 75%, and a 50% duty cycle assumption (the patient is not always at the maximal thermal gradient):

$$
E_{practical} = 0.0324 \times 0.75 \times 0.5 = 0.01215~Wh/day
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer:** $V_{oc} = 0.18~V$, $P_{max} = 1.35~mW$, $E_{day} = 116.6~J/day \approx 32.4~mWh/day$. After derating, ≈ 12 mWh/day is stored — this can **trickle-charge** a 100 mWh thin-film Li-ion cell to full in about 8 days, effectively extending the pacemaker lifetime by 30–50%.

**Marks distribution:** Step 1: 2 marks, Step 2: 2 marks, Step 3: 2 marks, Step 4 (with interpretation): 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks on Implanted Medical Device Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in lifetime calculation** — writing "21 years" without specifying whether it is **raw, derated, or clinically approved**. Always show all three.
> 2. **Mixing current and charge** — confusing Ah (capacity) with A (current) is a classic 1-mark killer. Remember: $t = C/I$ where $C$ is in Ah and $I$ is in A.
> 3. **Wrong PMIC topology choice** — stating "Boost converter" for a Li-I₂ powered pacemaker. The cell is already at 2.8 V; you need an **LDO**, not a boost.
> 4. **Confusing thermoelectric "Seebeck" with "Peltier"** — Seebeck = harvesting (heat → electricity), Peltier = cooling (electricity → heat gradient). Examiners will deduct 1 mark for this swap.
> 5. **Ignoring self-discharge** — A Li-I₂ cell has <1%/year self-discharge, but examiners expect a mention of "derating factor" for any lifetime calculation. Omission = –1 mark.
> 6. **Not drawing a block diagram** — Part (a) of any 7-mark question on IMDs almost always requires a block diagram. If you give only text, expect a **–2 to –3 mark penalty**.
> 7. **Incorrect Friis-equation application** — the term $(\lambda/4\pi d)^2$ is for **far-field** RF harvesting. Inductive links at <1 cm are **near-field** and follow the coupled-inductor model — mixing them up costs 2 marks.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-DENSITY RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Implanted Medical Devices (KTU Module 4)**

- **Five canonical IMDs to memorize:** Pacemaker, Cochlear Implant, Retinal Prosthesis, Deep Brain Stimulator (DBS), Capsule Endoscope.
- **Pacemaker power budget:** Sleep ≈ 8 µA, Stimulation ≈ 5 mA (pulsed, 0.1% duty), Telemetry ≈ 20 mA (pulsed, 0.001% duty) → Average ≈ 10–15 µA.
- **PMIC choice rule of thumb:** Li-I₂ cell (2.8 V) → **LDO**; Li-ion variable (3.0–4.2 V) → **Buck-Boost**; TEG/Piezo (< 0.5 V) → **Boost with cold-start and MPPT**; WPT-rectified (4–6 V) → **Buck**.
- **Seebeck voltage formula (always use this form):** $V_{oc} = N \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$.
- **Matched-load TEG power formula:** $P_{max} = V_{oc}^2 / (4R_{in})$.
- **Inductive link power formula:** $P_L = V_1^2 \cdot \omega^2 M^2 / [(R_2 + R_L)^2 R_{primary}]$ (or its equivalent secondary-side form).
- **Li-I₂ specific energy:** **200–300 Wh/kg** — the **highest** of any solid-state primary cell, which is why pacemakers use it.
- **Biocompatible encapsulation:** Titanium or ceramic (Al₂O₃, ZrO₂) — never polymer-only for >5 year implants.
- **Frequency band for IMD telemetry:** **MICS = 402–405 MHz** (Medical Implant Communication Service) or **WMTS** bands; never unlicensed ISM.
- **Key challenge of energy harvesting in IMDs:** Voltage is too low (0.1–0.3 V) for direct use — **cold-start** circuits and **MPPT** are mandatory.
- **Three MPPT algorithms used in IMDs:** Fractional Open-Circuit Voltage (FOCV), Perturb & Observe (P&O), and impedance matching. FOCV is the **simplest** and is the KTU favourite.
- **Inductive coupling distance limitation:** Beyond ~15 mm, efficiency drops below 50% — this defines the **maximum skin thickness** for an externally powered implant.
- **Lifetime equation (with derating):** $t_{life}^{clinical} \approx 0.5 \times t_{life}^{raw} \times (1 - r \cdot t_{life}^{raw})$ where $r$ is the annual self-discharge rate.
- **Typical clinical lifetime targets:** Pacemaker ≥ 10 years, Cochlear Implant ≥ 15 years (internal electronics), DBS ≥ 25 years, Capsule Endoscope = single-use (24–48 h).
- **KTU exam-favourite PMIC chips to know (no marks awarded for naming, but context awareness helps):** TI TPS62840 (buck, I_q = 60 nA), Analog Devices ADP5303 (buck, I_q = 180 nA), ST SPV1040 (boost + MPPT for solar/TEG), Linear Technology (ADI) LTC3108 (ultralow-voltage boost with cold-start from 20 mV).
- **Energy storage rule of thumb:** Combine a high-energy-density cell (Li-ion) with a high-power-density buffer (supercapacitor) for **pulsed loads** like stimulation.
- **Why the body is a hostile environment:** 37 °C accelerates chemical degradation, immune response forms fibrotic capsule around the implant (raising thermal resistance and reducing ΔT for TEGs by ~30% over 5 years).
- **Emerging trend (KTU bonus marks):** **Self-powered closed-loop neural dust** — piezoelectric or triboelectric motes (~1 mm³) powered by local tissue vibration, communicating via ultrasound backscatter. Power: nano-to-microwatts.
- **Final word:** IMDs are the **gold standard for ultra-low-power design** — every milliwatt saved translates directly to months of added patient life.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bio-MEMS Based Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Bio-MEMS Based Applications ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Bio-MEMS (Biomedical Microelectromechanical Systems)** is an advanced sub-discipline of MEMS technology that integrates mechanical, electrical, fluidic, and optical components on a single micron-scale substrate for biological and biomedical applications. These systems are designed to interact with biological entities (cells, proteins, DNA, tissues, or whole organisms) to perform sensing, actuation, drug delivery, diagnostic, or therapeutic functions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Verbatim Syllabus Mapping):** *Bio-MEMS refers to the science and technology of operating micron-scale devices that integrate mechanical elements, sensors, actuators, and electronics with biological systems for the purpose of health monitoring, point-of-care diagnostics, controlled drug delivery, and in-vivo therapeutics.* In the context of **Energy Harvesting & Power Management in IoT**, Bio-MEMS devices are the *physical actuation/sensing edge* of the biomedical IoT (also called the Internet of Medical Things — IoMT). They must operate on **microwatt (µW) to milliwatt (mW)** power budgets, harvested from the host environment. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners) Think of a Bio-MEMS chip as a **"miniature hospital on a thumb-drive."** Just as a hospital has departments (pathology lab, ICU, pharmacy, surgical theater), a Bio-MEMS device packs these functions onto a chip the size of your fingernail: - **Biosensor unit** = Pathology lab (analyzes blood/saliva) - **Microfluidic channels** = Veins (carry the sample to detectors) - **Micropump / microvalve** = Pharmacy (releases precise drug doses) - **Microcantilever / mass sensor** = Surgeon's scalpel (detects cancer cells mechanically) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy:** A diabetic patient today pricks a finger, applies blood to a glucose test strip, and reads the result on a meter. Tomorrow, a Bio-MEMS patch stuck on the wrist will continuously measure interstitial glucose, wirelessly transmit the data to a phone, AND auto-administer insulin through microneedles — all from a single coin-cell or even harvested body-heat energy. ### 1.3 Classification of Bio-MEMS Devices (KTU Taxonomy) Bio-MEMS devices are classified based on their **functional role** and **physical principle**: | Category | Primary Function | Representative Device | |---|---|---| | **Biosensors** | Detect biomolecules | Glucose oxidase sensor, DNA microarrays | | **Microfluidic Systems** | Manipulate µL–nL fluid volumes | Lab-on-Chip (LoC), µTAS | | **Drug Delivery Devices** | Controlled therapeutic release | Microneedle arrays, nanoporous membranes | | **Neural Interfaces** | Stimulate/record brain signals | Utah Array, neural dust motes | | **Surgical Microrobots** | In-vivo navigation | Capsule endoscopes, magnetically driven swimmers | | **Tissue Engineering Scaffolds** | Guide cell growth | Microfabricated polymer scaffolds | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4 Anchor):** Energy harvesting in Bio-MEMS specifically focuses on **self-powered implants and ingestible/wearable sensors**. Since battery replacement is impossible inside the human body, the device must scavenge energy from vibration, body heat, or biochemical reactions (glucose biofuel cells). ### 1.4 Key Physical Constants for Bio-MEMS - Density of blood plasma: $\rho \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$ - Viscosity of water at 37°C: $\mu \approx 0.0007 \text{ Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ - Surface tension of water at 25°C: $\gamma = 0.072 \text{ N/m}$ - Dielectric constant of body tissue: $\varepsilon_r \approx 50$ – $80$ - Typical Bio-MEMS channel width: $10\ \mu\text{m}$ to $500\ \mu\text{m}$ --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Bio-MEMS Works The fundamental physics of Bio-MEMS is governed by **scaling laws** — when system dimensions shrink from meter to micrometer, the dominant forces *invert*. This is the *core conceptual reason* Bio-MEMS is even possible. #### Step 1: The Dominant-Force Inversion Principle At macro-scale, **gravity and inertia** dominate. At micro-scale, **surface tension, viscosity, and electrostatic forces** dominate. The transition is mathematically captured by **dimensionless numbers**. #### Step 2: The Reynolds Number (Re) — The Inertia vs. Viscosity Trade-off The Reynolds number is the single most important dimensionless group in Bio-MEMS: $$Re = \frac{\rho v L}{\mu}$$ Where: - $\rho$ = fluid density ($\text{kg/m}^3$) - $v$ = flow velocity ($\text{m/s}$) - $L$ = characteristic length (channel width, $\mu$m) - $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity ($\text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$) **Engineering Implication:** In a 100 µm wide blood microchannel with $v = 1\text{ mm/s}$, $Re \approx 0.15$. This is **orders of magnitude below the laminar-turbulent transition** ($Re = 2300$). Therefore, **flow in Bio-MEMS is always laminar** — fluids move in smooth, parallel layers with no mixing except by diffusion. This is *both a blessing* (predictable flow) *and a curse* (no turbulent mixing, so designers must use special T- or Y-junctions to induce mixing). #### Step 3: The Capillary Number (Ca) — Viscous vs. Surface Tension $$Ca = \frac{\mu v}{\gamma}$$ In Bio-MEMS, $Ca \ll 1$, meaning **surface tension dominates over viscous forces**. This is why droplets spontaneously self-organize in microchannels without external pumping. #### Step 4: The Bond Number (Bo) — Gravity vs. Surface Tension $$Bo = \frac{\rho g L^2}{\gamma}$$ For $L = 100\ \mu\text{m}$, $Bo \approx 10^{-6}$. Gravity is essentially irrelevant. **A water droplet can be held upside-down on a microfluidic ceiling without falling** — this principle is exploited in capillary-driven Bio-MEMS pumps (no moving parts, no power). #### Step 5: Surface-to-Volume Ratio (SVR) — The Performance Multiplier As a 3D structure scales down by a factor $k$, surface area scales as $L^2$ while volume scales as $L^3$. Thus: $$SVR \propto \frac{L^2}{L^3} = \frac{1}{L}$$ For a 10 µm cell, SVR is $\sim 10^5$ times greater than for a 1 cm tissue block. This is why Bio-MEMS biosensors respond in *seconds* whereas lab assays take *hours* — diffusion distances are drastically shorter. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table is the **exam-critical formula set** for Module 4 — Bio-MEMS. Memorize these for numerical problems. | # | Formula / Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units | Used In | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v L}{\mu}$ | Inertia-to-viscosity ratio | dimensionless | Microfluidic flow analysis | | 2 | $Ca = \dfrac{\mu v}{\gamma}$ | Viscous vs. surface tension | dimensionless | Droplet microfluidics, capillary flow | | 3 | $Bo = \dfrac{\rho g L^2}{\gamma}$ | Gravity vs. surface tension | dimensionless | Determining dominant force regime | | 4 | $Pe = \dfrac{v L}{D}$ | Convection vs. diffusion | dimensionless | Species transport in channels | | 5 | $SVR = \dfrac{2}{r}$ (cyl), $\dfrac{3}{r}$ (sph) | Surface-to-volume ratio | $\text{m}^{-1}$ | Sensor/drug-delivery performance | | 6 | $P_{piezo} = \dfrac{1}{2} d_{31}^2 \cdot E \cdot A \cdot f$ | Piezoelectric harvested power | $\text{W}$ | Energy harvesting in implants | | 7 | $Q = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot w^3 h}{12 \mu L}$ | Volumetric flow (Hagen-Poiseuille, rectangular) | $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$ | Microchannel design | | 8 | $M_{Higuchi} = A \sqrt{D_s \cdot C_s \cdot t}$ | Higuchi drug-release model | $\text{mass}/\text{m}^2$ | Controlled drug release kinetics | | 9 | $\Delta t_{diff} = \dfrac{L^2}{2D}$ | Characteristic diffusion time | $\text{s}$ | Sensor response time estimation | | 10 | $E_{harvest} = k_{BT}^2 \Delta T^2 \cdot Z \cdot T_{abs}$ | Thermoelectric (Seebeck) harvested energy | $\text{J}$ | Body-heat powered sensors | **Boundary Conditions / Validity Notes:** - $Re < 2300$: laminar flow (always true in Bio-MEMS) - $Bo \ll 1$: surface tension dominates - $Pe \gg 1$: convection dominates; $Pe \ll 1$: diffusion dominates - Higuchi model valid for $0 \le M_t/M_\infty \le 0.6$ ### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility These formulas are not just academic — they directly drive design decisions: - **Insulin pump manufacturers** use the Hagen-Poiseuille equation to size the microfluidic channels that deliver precise insulin doses. - **Implantable cochlear devices** are designed with $Re \ll 1$ to ensure vibration-induced perilymph fluid flow is purely laminar and predictable. - **Body-heat-powered cardiac pacemakers** rely on the Seebeck equation to extract microwatts from a 1–2 K temperature gradient between body core and skin. - **Microneedle vaccine patches** are designed with $L \approx 500\ \mu\text{m}$ so that the Bond number is low enough that surface tension keeps interstitial fluid from leaking back out. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Scaling Law for Dominant Forces in Bio-MEMS **Objective:** Prove quantitatively that as device dimension $L$ shrinks, surface tension becomes dominant over gravity. **Given:** - A water droplet in a microfluidic channel of characteristic dimension $L$ - Gravitational force: $F_g = \rho g V = \rho g L^3$ - Surface tension force: $F_\gamma = \gamma L$ **Find:** The dimensionless ratio $F_g / F_\gamma$ as a function of $L$. **Step 1:** Write the gravitational (body) force acting on the droplet. $$F_g = m \cdot g = \rho V g = \rho L^3 g$$ *(Explanation: Mass equals density × volume, and volume of a cubic droplet scales as $L^3$.)* **Step 2:** Write the capillary (surface tension) force along the contact line. $$F_\gamma = \gamma \cdot L$$ *(Explanation: Surface tension force equals surface energy per unit area $\gamma$ multiplied by the contact line length, which scales linearly with $L$ for a cube/cylinder.)* **Step 3:** Form the dimensionless ratio $F_g / F_\gamma$. $$\frac{F_g}{F_\gamma} = \frac{\rho g L^3}{\gamma L}$$ **Step 4:** Simplify the expression by canceling one factor of $L$. $$\frac{F_g}{F_\gamma} = \frac{\rho g L^2}{\gamma}$$ **Step 5:** Identify this as the **Bond Number (Bo)**. $$Bo = \frac{F_g}{F_\gamma} = \frac{\rho g L^2}{\gamma}$$ **Step 6:** Evaluate for water at room temperature ($\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\gamma = 0.072\ \text{N/m}$, $g = 9.81\ \text{m/s}^2$). For $L = 1\ \text{cm} = 10^{-2}\ \text{m}$: $$Bo = \frac{1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (10^{-2})^2}{0.072} = \frac{1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 10^{-4}}{0.072}$$ $$Bo = \frac{0.981}{0.072} \approx 13.6$$ For $L = 100\ \mu\text{m} = 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$: $$Bo = \frac{1000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (10^{-4})^2}{0.072} = \frac{9.81 \times 10^{-5}}{0.072} \approx 1.36 \times 10^{-3}$$ **Step 7: Conclusion.** $$\boxed{Bo \propto L^2 \implies \text{As } L \text{ shrinks, } Bo \text{ drops as the square.}}$$ When $Bo \ll 1$, gravity is *negligible* and surface tension *dominates*. This is the foundational reason Bio-MEMS can function **upside down, in zero gravity (space medicine), or in orbital fluid** without losing performance. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Diffusion Time Inside a Microfluidic Sensing Chamber **Objective:** Derive the characteristic response time of a Bio-MEMS biosensor where an analyte must diffuse across a microchannel of width $L$ to reach the detector. **Given:** - Einstein's 1D diffusion equation: $\langle x^2 \rangle = 2 D t$ - $\langle x^2 \rangle$ = mean squared displacement, $D$ = diffusivity **Step 1:** Set the mean squared displacement equal to the squared channel half-width. $$\langle x^2 \rangle = L^2$$ **Step 2:** Solve for the diffusion time $t_{diff}$. $$t_{diff} = \frac{L^2}{2D}$$ **Step 3:** Plug in $D_{glucose} \approx 6.7 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ (in water at 37°C). For $L = 50\ \mu\text{m} = 5 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{m}$: $$t_{diff} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-5})^2}{2 \times 6.7 \times 10^{-10}}$$ $$t_{diff} = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-9}}{1.34 \times 10^{-9}}$$ $$\boxed{t_{diff} \approx 1.87\ \text{s}}$$ **Step 4:** Interpretation. A glucose molecule reaches the sensor in **under 2 seconds** in a 50 µm channel. In a conventional lab cuvette (path length 1 cm), the same diffusion would take $\sim 8.3 \times 10^4$ s (over 23 hours). This is the **quantitative justification** for why Bio-MEMS point-of-care devices are faster by a factor of $10^4$ to $10^5$. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Power Budget Analysis for a Self-Powered Bio-MEMS **Objective:** Compute the harvested power from a piezoelectric Bio-MEMS accelerometer (e.g., a cardiac motion sensor implanted on the heart wall) and verify it can sustain a wireless transmission. **Given:** - $d_{31}$ = piezoelectric charge coefficient $= 200 \times 10^{-12}\ \text{C/N}$ (PZT-5A) - $E$ = Young's modulus $= 50 \times 10^9\ \text{Pa}$ - $A$ = active diaphragm area $= 4 \text{ mm}^2 = 4 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}^2$ - $f$ = heartbeat frequency $= 1.2\ \text{Hz}$ **Step 1:** Apply the piezoelectric harvested power formula. $$P_{piezo} = \frac{1}{2} d_{31}^2 \cdot E \cdot A \cdot f$$ **Step 2:** Substitute numerical values. $$P_{piezo} = 0.5 \times (200 \times 10^{-12})^2 \times 50 \times 10^9 \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.2$$ **Step 3:** Compute $d_{31}^2$. $$d_{31}^2 = (2 \times 10^{-10})^2 = 4 \times 10^{-20}$$ **Step 4:** Multiply in sequence. $$P_{piezo} = 0.5 \times 4 \times 10^{-20} \times 5 \times 10^{10} \times 4 \times 10^{-6} \times 1.2$$ $$P_{piezo} = 0.5 \times 4 \times 5 \times 4 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-20+10-6}$$ $$P_{piezo} = 0.5 \times 96 \times 10^{-16}$$ $$P_{piezo} = 48 \times 10^{-16}$$ **Step 5:** Final result. $$\boxed{P_{piezo} \approx 4.8 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{W} = 4.8\ \text{fW}}$$ **Step 6: Engineering verdict.** This raw figure is far below the **microwatt threshold** required for BLE transmission (~1 mW peak). In practice, designers use: 1. **Resonant frequency matching** to heartbeat harmonics (factor 100× gain) 2. **Cantilever arrays** (factor 10–100× gain) 3. **Power management ICs** with cold-start at ~100 mV (e.g., Texas Instruments BQ25504) This illustrates a critical KTU exam point: **harvested power is typically the *bottleneck*, not the sensing electronics**. The energy harvesting subsystem must be co-designed with the Bio-MEMS mechanical structure. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Bio-MEMS Power Sources | Power Source | Power Density | Pros | Cons | Best Use Case | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Piezoelectric (vibration)** | $10$ – $300\ \mu\text{W/cm}^3$ | High energy density, no fuel | Needs motion | Cardiac, respiratory monitors | | **Thermoelectric (Seebeck)** | $10$ – $60\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2$ | Continuous, body heat | Needs $\Delta T$ > 1 K | Skin-surface wearables | | **Biofuel cell (enzymatic)** | $1$ – $10\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2$ | Uses body glucose | Electrode fouling | Implanted glucose sensors | | **Photovoltaic (subcutaneous)** | $10\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2$ | Unlimited sunlight source | Cannot penetrate tissue | Subcutaneous wearables | | **RF Harvesting** | $1$ – $10\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2$ | Wireless | Needs nearby transmitter | Hospital room sensors | | **Triboelectric (nanogenerator)** | $100\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2$ | High output | Material wear | In-vivo motion sensors | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Bio-MEMS Wearable Node ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Bio Signal Source
Heartbeat / Glucose / Neural Spike] --> B[MEMS Transducer
Piezo / Electrochemical / Optical] B --> C[Analog Front End
Amplifier and Filter] C --> D[ADC
12 bit Sigma Delta] D --> E[Microcontroller
ARM Cortex M0 plus] E --> F[Power Management Unit
MPPT and Buck Boost] F --> G[Energy Harvester
Piezo / Thermoelectric / Biofuel] G --> F F --> H[Energy Storage
Supercap or Thin Film Battery] E --> I[BLE Radio
2.4 GHz Transceiver] I --> J[Smartphone Gateway
Cloud Dashboard] E --> K[Bio Actuator
Microvalve / Microneedle] K --> L[Therapeutic Response
Drug Infusion / Neural Stim] ``` **Reading guide:** The diagram traces the closed loop from physiological signal → transduction → conditioning → processing → wireless transmission → therapy. The energy harvester (block G) continuously refills the storage (block H) through the power management unit (block F), which also supplies every other block. --- ### 4.2 Microneedle-Based Closed-Loop Insulin Delivery System ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Interstitial Fluid
Glucose Present] --> S2[Enzyme Layer
Glucose Oxidase GOx] S2 --> S3[Electrode Transducer
H2O2 Oxidation Current] S3 --> S4[Controller ASIC
Closed Loop Algorithm] S4 --> S5{Glucose Level
above Threshold} S5 -- YES --> S6[Microheater Activates
Phase Change of Insulin] S6 --> S7[Insulin Released
through Microneedle Array] S7 --> S8[Capillary Driven
Flow into Dermis] S5 -- NO --> S9[Hibernate Mode
Ultra Low Power] S4 --> S10[BLE Transmit
Dose Log to Phone] ``` **Reading guide:** This is the canonical "artificial pancreas" architecture. The MEMS microneedle array (typically 100–500 needles of 500–800 µm height) is coated with a thermoresponsive polymer (e.g., poly-N-isopropylacrylamide, PNIPAM) loaded with insulin. The microheater melts the polymer above 32°C, releasing insulin. --- ### 4.3 Comparative Architecture: Implantable vs. Wearable Bio-MEMS ```mermaid graph TD subgraph Implantable_Subgroup[Implantable Bio MEMS] IM1[Neural Dust Motes] --> IM2[In Vivo Cochlear Implant] IM2 --> IM3[Cardiac Pacemaker Lead] IM3 --> IM4[Subcutaneous Glucose Sensor] end subgraph Wearable_Subgroup[Wearable Bio MEMS] WB1[Smart Bandage] --> WB2[Smart Insole] WB2 --> WB3[Sweat Sensor Patch] WB3 --> WB4[Electronic Skin e Skin] end Implantable_Subgroup --> P1[Energy Source: Piezo plus Biofuel] Wearable_Subgroup --> P2[Energy Source: Thermoelectric plus Triboelectric] P1 --> P3[Power: 1 to 100 microwatt] P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4[Co Designed Power Management IC] ``` --- ### 4.4 Lab-on-Chip (LoC) Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB IN1[Sample Inlet
Whole Blood Drop] --> IN2[Microfilter
Plasma Separation] IN2 --> IN3[Micromixer
Passive Tesla Structure] IN3 --> IN4[Reaction Chamber
Antibody Antigen Binding] IN4 --> IN5[Detection Zone
Electrochemical or Optical] IN5 --> IN6[Readout Electronics
CMOS ASIC] IN6 --> IN7[Digital Output
Concentration ppm] IN3 -.waste.-> WT1[Waste Outlet 1] IN4 -.waste.-> WT2[Waste Outlet 2] ``` **Reading guide:** A Lab-on-Chip performs an entire clinical assay (typically done in a 1 mL vial) inside a device the size of a credit card. The sample volume drops from milliliters to **microliters**, reagent costs drop by ~100×, and assay time drops from hours to minutes. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]** Define Bio-MEMS. List any four biomedical applications of Bio-MEMS with one example each. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Definition (1 mark):** Bio-MEMS (Biomedical Microelectromechanical Systems) is the integration of mechanical elements, sensors, actuators, and electronic circuits on a micro-scale platform using microfabrication technology, designed specifically to interface with biological systems for sensing, actuation, and therapeutic purposes. **Four applications with examples (2 marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Biosensing** — Example: Implantable continuous glucose monitor (CGM) using a glucose oxidase–based amperometric sensor on a silicon microneedle. 2. **Drug Delivery** — Example: Microneedle array patch that releases insulin in response to a thermal trigger (phase-change polymer). 3. **Lab-on-Chip Diagnostics** — Example: Microfluidic DNA amplification (PCR-on-chip) device for pathogen detection. 4. **Neural Interfaces** — Example: Utah Electrode Array used for recording from cortical neurons in brain–computer interfaces. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model]** Explain why **surface tension dominates over gravity** in Bio-MEMS devices. Support your answer with a dimensionless number. **Model Answer (3 marks):** At the micro-scale, the characteristic length $L$ of a fluid element is in the range of micrometers. Since gravitational force scales as $L^3$ and surface tension force scales as $L$, the ratio $F_g / F_\gamma$ scales as $L^2$ (1 mark). This ratio is the **Bond Number**: $$Bo = \frac{\rho g L^2}{\gamma}$$ For a 100 µm microchannel with water ($\rho = 1000\ \text{kg/m}^3$, $\gamma = 0.072\ \text{N/m}$), $Bo \approx 1.36 \times 10^{-3} \ll 1$ (1 mark). Since $Bo \ll 1$, surface tension forces dominate over gravity, which is why Bio-MEMS can use **capillary-driven flow** without any external pump, and droplets can adhere to channel ceilings (1 mark). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 4, Model — Choice 1]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a **Bio-MEMS based closed-loop insulin delivery system**. Discuss the role of the power management unit. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A microchannel is 80 µm wide and 5 mm long. Blood plasma (ρ = 1025 kg/m³, μ = 0.0012 Pa·s) flows through it at an average velocity of 2 mm/s. Determine the Reynolds number and the volumetric flow rate assuming rectangular geometry (aspect ratio = 1, with $Q = \Delta P \cdot w^3 h / (12 \mu L)$ is not required; use $Q = v \cdot w \cdot h$ for rectangular approximation). **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Architecture and Power Management (7 marks):** **Block diagram (3 marks):** The closed-loop insulin system consists of five functional blocks (as shown in the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.2): 1. **Glucose Sensor** (microneedle + GOx enzyme electrode) — measures interstitial glucose amperometrically. 2. **Signal Conditioning & ADC** — converts nA-level current to digital glucose concentration. 3. **Controller ASIC** — runs proportional-integral-derivative (PID) algorithm to compute required insulin dose. 4. **Microactuator & Microneedle Array** — delivers the calculated dose via thermally triggered phase-change polymer. 5. **Power Management Unit (PMU)** — harvests energy, regulates voltage, manages the duty cycle of sensing and actuation. **Role of PMU (4 marks — 0.5 each sub-point):** - **Energy harvesting interface:** Accepts input from piezo, thermoelectric, or biofuel cell sources (typically 100 mV – 1 V open circuit). - **Cold-start circuit:** Boosts the harvested voltage to a usable 3.3 V even when input is <300 mV. - **Maximum Power Point Tracking (MPPT):** Dynamically matches the harvester's internal impedance to the load for peak power transfer. - **Energy storage management:** Charges a thin-film solid-state battery or supercapacitor at the correct C-rate. - **Load switching:** Activates the sensor, ADC, and radio only when needed (sleep current <1 µA). - **Regulation:** Provides stable rails to the ASIC even as harvester output fluctuates with patient motion. - **Safety:** Includes under-voltage lockout and over-current protection for patient safety. - **Bidirectional control:** Can throttle the microheater actuator in case of hypoglycemia alert (safety interlock). --- **(b) Reynolds Number and Volumetric Flow Rate (7 marks):** **Given:** - $w = 80\ \mu\text{m} = 80 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{m}$ - $L = 5\ \text{mm} = 5 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m}$ - $\rho = 1025\ \text{kg/m}^3$ - $\mu = 0.0012\ \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ - $v = 2\ \text{mm/s} = 2 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m/s}$ **Step 1:** Write the Reynolds number formula (1 mark). $$Re = \frac{\rho v L_c}{\mu}$$ Here the characteristic length $L_c$ is the channel width $w = 80\ \mu\text{m}$ for rectangular microchannels. **Step 2:** Substitute (1 mark). $$Re = \frac{1025 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 80 \times 10^{-6}}{0.0012}$$ **Step 3:** Numerator calculation (1 mark). $$1025 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 2.05$$ $$2.05 \times 80 \times 10^{-6} = 1.64 \times 10^{-4}$$ **Step 4:** Final division (1 mark). $$Re = \frac{1.64 \times 10^{-4}}{1.2 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1367$$ $$\boxed{Re \approx 0.137}$$ **Step 5: Interpretation (1 mark).** Since $Re \ll 2300$, the flow is **laminar**. Mixing (if needed) must be enhanced using passive structures (Tesla mixer, staggered herringbone), not by turbulence. **Step 6:** Volumetric flow rate (1 mark). $$Q = v \times w \times h$$ Assuming square cross-section, $h = w = 80\ \mu\text{m}$: $$Q = 2 \times 10^{-3} \times 80 \times 10^{-6} \times 80 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$Q = 1.28 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$ Convert to more practical units: $$Q = 1.28 \times 10^{-11} \times 10^6\ \mu\text{L/s} = 1.28 \times 10^{-5}\ \mu\text{L/s}$$ $$\boxed{Q \approx 12.8\ \text{nL/s}}$$ **Step 7: Interpretation (1 mark).** This is a *nanoliter-per-second* flow regime. For reference, an insulin pump needs ~0.5 µL per dose, so this channel would take ~40 s to deliver a single dose — perfectly within the response-time budget of a wearable patch. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 4, Model — Choice 2]** **(a)** Explain the **piezoelectric energy harvesting mechanism** for an implantable cardiac Bio-MEMS device. List the key parameters that govern the harvested power. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the **diffusion time** for a glucose molecule to traverse a microchannel of width 100 µm, given the diffusivity of glucose in water at body temperature is $6.7 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$. Comment on how this diffusion time scales with channel width. **(7 marks)** --- **Model Solution:** **(a) Piezoelectric Energy Harvesting in Cardiac Bio-MEMS (7 marks):** **Mechanism (4 marks):** A thin PZT (lead zirconate titanate) or PVDF (polyvinylidene fluoride) cantilever or diaphragm is mechanically coupled to the heart wall. With each heartbeat, the cardiac muscle contracts and relaxes, producing a periodic strain on the piezoelectric element. The piezoelectric effect converts this **mechanical strain directly into electrical charge** (per the constitutive relation $D = d \cdot \sigma + \varepsilon \cdot E$, where $d$ is the piezoelectric coefficient and $\sigma$ is stress). The generated charge accumulates on the electrodes and is rectified (using a full-bridge diode rectifier and smoothing capacitor) before being fed to the PMU. The harvested energy is then used to power a low-duty-cycle ECG transmitter that sends a wireless signal to an external monitor. **Key parameters governing harvested power (3 marks — 0.5 each):** 1. **Piezoelectric charge coefficient $d_{31}$** — higher $d_{31}$ gives more charge per unit stress. 2. **Diaphragm/beam area $A$** — larger area captures more strain energy. 3. **Young's modulus $E$** — couples the strain to the stress field. 4. **Mechanical frequency $f$** — must match the heart rate or its harmonic for resonance. 5. **Mechanical quality factor $Q_m$** — higher $Q$ gives sharper resonance, more energy per cycle. 6. **Coupling coefficient $k^2$** — fraction of mechanical energy converted to electrical. --- **(b) Diffusion Time Derivation (7 marks):** **Step 1:** State the 1D diffusion equation (1 mark). $$\langle x^2 \rangle = 2 D t$$ **Step 2:** Identify the boundary condition. The glucose molecule starts at the channel wall ($x = 0$) and must reach the opposite wall at $x = L$ (1 mark). $$\langle x^2 \rangle = L^2$$ **Step 3:** Solve for $t_{diff}$ (1 mark). $$t_{diff} = \frac{L^2}{2 D}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute values: $L = 100\ \mu\text{m} = 10^{-4}\ \text{m}$, $D = 6.7 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{m}^2/\text{s}$ (1 mark). $$t_{diff} = \frac{(10^{-4})^2}{2 \times 6.7 \times 10^{-10}} = \frac{10^{-8}}{1.34 \times 10^{-9}}$$ **Step 5:** Final calculation (1 mark). $$t_{diff} = \frac{10^{-8}}{1.34 \times 10^{-9}} = \frac{10}{1.34} \approx 7.46\ \text{s}$$ $$\boxed{t_{diff} \approx 7.5\ \text{s}}$$ **Step 6:** Comment on scaling (2 marks): The diffusion time scales **quadratically with channel width**: $$t_{diff} \propto L^2$$ - If $L$ is halved from 100 µm to 50 µm, $t_{diff}$ drops by a factor of 4 (to ~1.87 s). - If $L$ is increased tenfold to 1 mm, $t_{diff}$ jumps to ~746 s (~12 minutes). - This is why Bio-MEMS designers prefer **sub-100 µm channels** for fast biosensing. However, going too small causes clogging by cells, so the practical sweet spot is $L = 30$ – $100\ \mu\text{m}$. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing Bo and Re:** Bo compares *gravity vs. surface tension*; Re compares *inertia vs. viscosity*. Mixing them up is a guaranteed -2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{2}$ in diffusion:** The formula is $t = L^2 / (2D)$, not $L^2 / D$. This is a common careless error. > 3. **Mixing up units:** Reynolds number is dimensionless — if you report it with units, you have an error. > 4. **Insufficient PMU explanation in 14-mark answers:** Many students only say "the PMU regulates voltage." Examiners want to see MPPT, cold-start, duty cycling, and safety interlocks explicitly mentioned. > 5. **Not drawing a block diagram in part (a) questions:** KTU valuation keys specifically allocate 2–3 marks for a clear block diagram. Text-only answers cap at 70% of the marks for that part. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Bio-MEMS Based Applications** - ☐ **Definition:** Bio-MEMS = micro-scale devices integrating mechanical + electrical + biological functions for medical applications. - ☐ **Four application pillars:** biosensors, microfluidics, drug delivery, neural interfaces. - ☐ **Three dimensionless numbers to memorize:** $Re$ (inertia/viscosity), $Ca$ (viscous/surface tension), $Bo$ (gravity/surface tension). - ☐ **At micro-scale:** surface tension and viscosity dominate; gravity and inertia become irrelevant. - ☐ **Laminar flow always:** $Re \ll 2300$ in Bio-MEMS channels → no turbulent mixing. - ☐ **Diffusion time formula:** $t_{diff} = L^2 / (2D)$ — quadratically scales with channel width. - ☐ **Hagen-Poiseuille for rectangular channels:** $Q = \Delta P \cdot w^3 h / (12 \mu L)$. - ☐ **Power budget:** Bio-MEMS operates in µW–mW range; energy harvesting is the bottleneck. - ☐ **Power sources ranked by density:** Triboelectric > Piezoelectric > Thermoelectric > RF > Photovoltaic > Biofuel. - ☐ **Self-powered insulin pump architecture:** GOx sensor → ASIC → microheater → microneedle → PMU with MPPT. - ☐ **Lab-on-Chip functions:** sample inlet → filter → mixer → reaction → detection → readout. - ☐ **Capillary-driven flow** works only when $Bo \ll 1$ (true for $L < 1$ mm). - ☐ **Piezoelectric harvested power** scales with $d_{31}^2 \cdot E \cdot A \cdot f$ — square of charge coefficient dominates. - ☐ **SVR scales as $1/L$** — explains why micro-devices are faster and more sensitive. - ☐ **Common exam trap:** "Why does $L^2$ matter?" — answer: because both Bo and diffusion time scale as $L^2$, the micro-scale is qualitatively different from macro-scale. - ☐ **Real-world exemplars** to cite in answers: continuous glucose monitor (CGM), cochlear implant, artificial pancreas, neural dust, smart bandage, electronic skin. ---

topics.title: Harvesting for RF Sensors and ID Tags
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Harvesting for RF Sensors and ID Tags # ⚡ MODULE 4 — RF Energy Harvesting for Sensors & ID Tags ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **RF (Radio Frequency) Energy Harvesting** is the process of capturing, rectifying, and storing ambient or dedicated radio-frequency electromagnetic radiation (typically from 3 kHz to 300 GHz) from the surrounding environment, and converting it into usable Direct Current (DC) electrical energy to power autonomous wireless sensors, passive/semi-passive **RFID tags**, and energy-constrained IoT edge nodes. The complete front-end is called a **Rectenna** — a portmanteau of *Rectifier + Antenna* — which is the heart of every RF harvesting system. ### 1.2 Key Building Block — The Rectenna > [!NOTE] > A **Rectenna (Rectifying Antenna)** is an antenna integrated with a non-linear rectifying circuit (usually Schottky diodes) that converts incident RF electromagnetic waves into DC voltage. It typically consists of four cascading stages: **Antenna → Matching Network → Rectifier (Diode) → DC Pass Filter / Smoothing Capacitor**. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "Solar Panels for the Electromagnetic Spectrum" Imagine you are standing under a Wi-Fi router or near a TV broadcast tower. The air around you is filled with invisible electromagnetic "currents" carrying energy. Your phone, router, and TV antenna are designed to *decode* the information in these waves, but a **rectenna** is designed to *eat* the energy itself. | Analogy Element | Real-World Counterpart | |---|---| | Sunlight in solar panels | Ambient RF radiation (TV, Wi-Fi, cellular) | | Photovoltaic cell | Schottky diode (rectifier) | | Solar panel mount | Matching network + antenna | | Battery in a solar lantern | Storage capacitor / supercapacitor | Think of an **RFID tag** stuck on a library book. It has no battery — yet when you walk it past the reader, the tag wakes up and transmits its ID. *Where does the energy come from?* The reader's RF field itself! This is the most widespread example of RF harvesting on Earth. ### 1.4 Why This Matters for KTU / NEP 2020 > [!IMPORTANT] > According to KTU's Outcome-Based Education framework, this topic directly maps to **CO4: Design and analyze energy harvesting systems for IoT devices**, combining electromagnetic theory, analog circuit design, and power management — the trifecta of modern IoT hardware. ### 1.5 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants - **Speed of light:** $c = 3 \times 10^8$ **m/s** - **Free-space impedance:** $Z_0 = 377$ **Ω** - **Typical harvestable RF power density:** $0.01$ to **$10\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2$** (urban ambient) - **Schottky diode threshold:** $\approx 0.2$ to **0.4 V** (turn-on voltage) - **RF harvesting frequency bands:** GSM (900/1800 MHz), Wi-Fi (2.4/5 GHz), LTE, DTV, ISM - **Conversion efficiency ($\eta$):** typically **1 % to 60 %** depending on input power and frequency --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Architecture of an RF Energy Harvesting System The complete signal chain, in order, is: 1. **RF Source (Ambient or Dedicated Transmitter)** - Examples: TV broadcast tower, GSM base station, Wi-Fi AP, dedicated RFID reader. - Emits an EM wave with Power $P_t$ and Gain $G_t$. 2. **Free-Space Propagation Channel** - The wave spreads out following the **Friis Transmission Equation**. - Suffers from **path loss** ($L_p$), multipath fading, and polarization mismatch. 3. **Receiving Antenna** - Captures a portion of the wave based on its effective aperture $A_e$. - Outputs an RF signal at the feed point. 4. **Impedance Matching Network** - Ensures maximum power transfer (S11 < -10 dB). - Typically a microstrip line, LC network, or transformer. 5. **Rectifier (Diode or Voltage Multiplier)** - The non-linear element that converts AC (RF) to DC. - Schottky diodes are preferred for low forward voltage. 6. **DC Pass Filter / Smoothing Capacitor** - Removes the high-frequency RF ripple. - Produces a stable DC output voltage $V_{DC}$. 7. **Energy Storage / Power Management Unit (PMU)** - Supercapacitor, thin-film battery, or storage capacitor. - Boost converter to step up the low DC voltage to usable levels (e.g., 1.8 V, 3.3 V). > [!NOTE] > For IoT sensors, the harvested voltage is often too low (200–500 mV) to directly power a microcontroller. This is why **ultra-low-voltage boost converters** (e.g., TI BQ25504, ADP5090) are critical PMU components. ## 2.2 Sources of RF Energy (KTU Exam Favorite) | Source | Frequency | Power Density (typ.) | Range | |---|---|---|---| | **TV Broadcast (VHF/UHF)** | 47–870 MHz | $0.1$ – $10\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2$ | 1–10 km | | **GSM 900/1800** | 900/1800 MHz | $0.01$ – $1\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2$ | 0.1–5 km | | **Wi-Fi (2.4/5 GHz)** | 2.4/5 GHz | $0.001$ – $0.1\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2$ | 10–50 m | | **Dedicated RFID Reader** | 860–960 MHz (UHF) | up to $100\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2$ | 1–10 m | | **LoRa / NB-IoT** | 433/868/915 MHz | $< 0.01\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2$ | 1–10 km | ## 2.3 RFID — The King Application of RF Harvesting RFID systems come in three flavors — understanding the energy flow is critical: | Type | Power Source | Operating Principle | |---|---|---| | **Passive RFID** | **Harvests** RF energy from reader | No battery; rectenna powers the chip | | **Semi-Passive (Semi-Active)** | Battery for IC, RF for comms | Harvests RF for uplink; battery for logic | | **Active RFID** | Internal battery | Independent; battery powers both IC and TX | > [!IMPORTANT] > In **passive UHF RFID (EPC Class-1 Gen-2)**, the tag harvests energy from the reader's continuous-wave (CW) signal using a dipole antenna and a Dickson charge pump, achieving a read range of up to **10–12 m** with reader EIRP of 4 W (regulated). ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $\lambda = \dfrac{c}{f}$ | Wavelength | m | | 2 | $P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2$ | Friis received power (free space) | W | | 3 | $S = \dfrac{P_t G_t}{4\pi R^2}$ | Power flux density (Poynting) | $\text{W/m}^2$ | | 4 | $A_e = \dfrac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$ | Effective aperture of RX antenna | $\text{m}^2$ | | 5 | $P_r = S \cdot A_e$ | Received power from flux density | W | | 6 | $L_p = \left(\dfrac{4\pi R}{\lambda}\right)^2$ | Free-space path loss (ratio) | unitless | | 7 | $L_p(\text{dB}) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(R) + 20\log_{10}(f)$ | Path loss in dB (R in km, f in MHz) | dB | | 8 | $V_{DC} = n(V_p - V_d)$ | Dickson multiplier output (n stages) | V | | 9 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{DC}}{P_{RF,in}} \times 100\%$ | RF-to-DC conversion efficiency | % | | 10 | $E_{stored} = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Energy in storage capacitor | J | | 11 | $I_{load,max} = \dfrac{P_{harv}}{V_{DC}}$ | Maximum load current | A | > [!WARNING] > In KTU exams, $V_d$ (diode drop) is the **key loss** in rectifiers. Always subtract it when computing the output of a voltage multiplier. Students commonly forget this and lose 2 marks. ## 2.5 Engineering Utility - **Smart Agriculture:** Soil moisture sensors powered by ambient GSM/Wi-Fi. - **Supply Chain:** Billions of passive RFID tags eliminating batteries. - **Smart Cities:** Battery-less streetlight sensors harvesting from LTE base stations. - **Wearable IoT:** Devices charged by ambient Wi-Fi in offices. - **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** Wireless condition-monitoring sensors in factories. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Received Power from Friis Equation (Most Important for KTU) **Starting Point (definition):** The power flux density at distance $R$ from an isotropic radiator of power $P_t$ is given by the surface area of a sphere: $$S = \dfrac{P_t}{4\pi R^2}$$ **Step 1:** Real antennas are not isotropic. Replace $P_t$ with the **Effective Isotropic Radiated Power (EIRP)**: $$S = \dfrac{P_t G_t}{4\pi R^2}$$ **Step 2:** The receiving antenna intercepts the flux density over its **effective aperture** $A_e$. For any antenna, the relationship between gain and aperture is: $$A_e = \dfrac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$ **Step 3:** The power delivered to the matched load at the receiver's terminals is: $$P_r = S \cdot A_e = \dfrac{P_t G_t}{4\pi R^2} \cdot \dfrac{G_r \lambda^2}{4\pi}$$ **Step 4:** Substitute $c = f \lambda$ so that $\lambda^2 = c^2 / f^2$. Final **Friis Transmission Equation**: $$\boxed{P_r = P_t G_t G_r \left(\dfrac{\lambda}{4\pi R}\right)^2 = \dfrac{P_t G_t G_r c^2}{(4\pi R f)^2}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Key insight:** $P_r \propto \dfrac{1}{R^2}$ in free space — this is why doubling the distance quarters the received power. Also $P_r \propto \dfrac{1}{f^2}$ — **lower frequencies harvest better** for the same distance. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Voltage Multiplier (Dickson Charge Pump) Output A single diode gives a peak DC of $V_p - V_d$. A **N-stage Dickson multiplier** stacks them: **Step 1:** Each stage adds the peak-to-peak RF voltage minus two diode drops: $$V_{stage} = 2 V_p - 2 V_d$$ **Step 2:** For $n$ stages in cascade, total open-circuit output is: $$V_{DC,open} = 2 n (V_p - V_d)$$ **Step 3:** Under load current $I_L$, voltage drops across the source impedance $R_s$ per stage. Final loaded form: $$\boxed{V_{DC} = 2 n (V_p - V_d) - n I_L R_s}$$ Where $V_p$ = peak RF voltage at rectifier input, $V_d$ = diode forward drop (≈ 0.3 V for Schottky), $R_s$ = parasitic series resistance. **Step 4:** Substituting $V_p = \sqrt{2 P_r \cdot Z_{in}}$ where $Z_{in}$ is the matched input impedance (usually 50 Ω): $$V_{DC} = 2 n \left(\sqrt{2 P_r Z_{in}} - V_d\right) - n I_L R_s$$ ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Energy Stored in a Harvester's Capacitor **Step 1:** A capacitor charged to voltage $V$ stores electrostatic energy: $$E = \int_0^Q V \, dq = \int_0^Q \dfrac{q}{C} \, dq = \dfrac{Q^2}{2C}$$ **Step 2:** Since $Q = CV$: $$\boxed{E = \dfrac{1}{2} C V^2}$$ **Step 3:** For a sensor that consumes average power $P_s$ and must operate for time $T$: $$C_{min} = \dfrac{2 P_s T}{V_{DC}^2 - V_{min}^2}$$ > [!NOTE] > $V_{min}$ is the minimum voltage the PMU/boost converter needs to operate. For the TI BQ25504, $V_{min} \approx 0.33$ V cold-start; for the ADP5090, $\approx 0.38$ V. ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style 14-mark problem) **Problem:** A UHF RFID reader transmits $P_t = 4$ W EIRP at $f = 868$ MHz. A passive tag uses a dipole antenna with $G_r = 2$ dBi located at $R = 5$ m. Calculate: (a) The received RF power at the tag. (b) The open-circuit DC voltage from a 4-stage Dickson multiplier, given $V_d = 0.3$ V and matched $Z_{in} = 50$ Ω. **Solution:** **(a) Received Power:** Convert $G_r$ from dBi: $G_r = 10^{2/10} = 1.585$ Compute wavelength: $\lambda = c/f = (3 \times 10^8)/(868 \times 10^6) = 0.3456$ m Apply Friis: $$P_r = 4 \times 1 \times 1.585 \times \left(\dfrac{0.3456}{4\pi \times 5}\right)^2$$ Calculate the bracket term: $\dfrac{0.3456}{62.832} = 0.0055$ Square: $0.0055^2 = 3.025 \times 10^{-5}$ Final: $P_r = 4 \times 1.585 \times 3.025 \times 10^{-5} = 1.918 \times 10^{-4}$ W $\approx 192\ \mu$W $$\boxed{P_r \approx 192\ \mu\text{W} = -7.17\ \text{dBm}}$$ **(b) Dickson Output:** Compute peak input voltage: $$V_p = \sqrt{2 P_r Z_{in}} = \sqrt{2 \times 1.918 \times 10^{-4} \times 50} = \sqrt{0.01918} = 0.1385\ \text{V}$$ Apply $V_{DC} = 2 n (V_p - V_d)$ with $n = 4$: $$V_{DC} = 2 \times 4 \times (0.1385 - 0.3) = 8 \times (-0.1615) = -1.292\ \text{V}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Negative result!** This is physically impossible and means the **received power is below the rectifier's threshold**. To fix: increase the number of stages (more stacking), or use a more sensitive zero-bias Schottky diode (e.g., SMS7630 with $V_d = 0.09$ V), or reduce the read range to improve $P_r$. Retry with $V_d = 0.09$ V: $V_{DC} = 8 \times (0.1385 - 0.09) = 8 \times 0.0485 = 0.388$ V → just enough to cold-start a boost converter. This is exactly the design challenge of modern RFID. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete RF Energy Harvesting System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[RFSRC
RF Source
TV/Wi-Fi/GSM/RFID Reader] -->|EM Wave| B[RXANT
Receiving Antenna
Dipole/Patch/Loop] B -->|Matched RF| C[MATCH
Impedance Matching
LC Network] C -->|RF signal| D[RECT
Rectifier
Schottky Diode] D -->|Pulsed DC| E[FILT
DC Smoothing
Capacitor] E -->|Stable DC| F[PMU
Power Management
Boost Converter] F -->|3.3V / 1.8V| G[LOAD
IoT Sensor / RFID Chip
MCU + Transmitter] F -->|Excess| H[STORE
Storage
Supercap / Thin Battery] H -.->|Replenish| F ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Dickson Charge Pump (4-Stage) ```mermaid flowchart TB RFIN[RF Input from Antenna] --> C1[Coupling Cap C1] C1 --> D1[Diode D1] D1 --> N1[Node 1] N1 --> C2[Cap C2] C2 --> D2[Diode D2] D2 --> N2[Node 2] N2 --> C3[Cap C3] C3 --> D3[Diode D3] D3 --> N3[Node 3] N3 --> C4[Cap C4] C4 --> D4[Diode D4] D4 --> OUT[V DC Output] OUT --> CLOAD[Smoothing Cap C L] CLOAD --> GND1[Ground] ``` ## 4.3 Architecture Flow — RF Energy Conversion Stages ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Stage 1: Electromagnetic Wave
in Air] --> P2[Stage 2: Antenna
Converts EM to RF Voltage] P2 --> P3[Stage 3: Matching Network
Maximizes Power Transfer] P3 --> P4[Stage 4: Rectifier
AC to Pulsed DC] P4 --> P5[Stage 5: Filter
Removes RF Ripple] P5 --> P6[Stage 6: PMU / Boost
Steps up Voltage] P6 --> P7[Stage 7: Storage
Stores Excess Energy] P7 --> P8[Stage 8: Sensor Load
MCU / RFID IC Powers Up] ``` ## 4.4 Signal Flow — Input RF vs Output DC Waveform ```mermaid flowchart LR S1[Input: Sinusoidal RF
10 MHz Carrier] -->|Schottky Rectifier| S2[Half-Wave Rectified
DC + Ripple] S2 -->|Capacitor Filter| S3[Smoothed DC
Approx Constant V] S3 -->|Boost Converter| S4[Regulated DC
3.3 V Output] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) > **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]** **Q1. Define a Rectenna. List its four essential components.** *(Module 4, 3 marks, CO4, Bloom: Remember/Understand)* **Model Answer:** A **Rectenna (Rectifying Antenna)** is a device that converts RF electromagnetic energy into DC electrical energy. Its four essential components are: 1. **Receiving antenna** — captures incident EM waves. 2. **Impedance matching network** — ensures maximum power transfer. 3. **Rectifier (Schottky diode or voltage multiplier)** — converts RF AC into DC. 4. **DC pass filter / smoothing capacitor** — removes high-frequency ripple to produce a clean DC output. > *Marking tip: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for the four components. Use bullet points for clarity.* --- **Q2. Differentiate between Passive, Semi-Passive, and Active RFID tags with respect to their energy source.** *(Module 4, 3 marks, CO4, Bloom: Understand)* **Model Answer:** | Feature | Passive | Semi-Passive | Active | |---|---|---|---| | Energy Source | Harvested RF from reader | Battery (for IC), RF (for comms) | Internal battery | | Range | 1–10 m | 10–30 m | Up to 100 m | | Cost | Very low | Moderate | High | | Lifetime | Unlimited | Battery-limited | Battery-limited | *Passive tags have no battery; semi-passive has a battery to power the IC but still harvests RF for uplink; active tags are fully self-powered.* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam – Module 4 Pattern, ESE Model]** --- ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) **Q. (a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an RF energy harvesting system for powering an IoT sensor. Discuss the role of each block. *(7 marks, CO4, Bloom: Understand/Analyze)* **(b)** A dedicated RF source transmits $P_t = 1$ W EIRP at $f = 2.4$ GHz. The receiving antenna has $G_r = 6$ dBi and is at a distance of $R = 3$ m. Using the Friis transmission equation, compute: (i) The free-space path loss in dB. (ii) The received power $P_r$ in mW and dBm. *(7 marks, CO4, Bloom: Apply)* **Model Solution for Q.A(a):** The RF energy harvesting system consists of the following cascaded blocks: 1. **RF Source:** Ambient or dedicated transmitter (TV, Wi-Fi, GSM, RFID reader) emits EM waves. 2. **Receiving Antenna:** A microstrip patch, dipole, or loop antenna tuned to the operating frequency. Its effective aperture $A_e$ determines how much energy is captured. 3. **Impedance Matching Network:** An LC circuit or microstrip stub that matches the antenna's impedance (typically 50 Ω) to the rectifier's input impedance, minimizing reflection (S11 < -10 dB). 4. **Rectifier:** A Schottky diode (HSMS-2850, SMS7630) or a multi-stage **Dickson/Greinacher voltage multiplier** that performs AC-to-DC conversion. Schottky diodes are preferred for their low forward voltage (~0.2 V) and fast switching. 5. **DC Smoothing Filter:** A shunt capacitor that filters out the high-frequency RF ripple, producing a steady DC voltage. 6. **Power Management Unit (PMU):** A ultra-low-voltage boost converter (e.g., TI BQ25504) that steps up the harvested voltage (typically 0.3–0.8 V) to a regulated 1.8 V or 3.3 V rail. 7. **Energy Storage:** A supercapacitor, thin-film lithium battery, or large electrolytic capacitor stores excess energy to power the load during RF-dark periods. 8. **Load:** The IoT sensor node — MCU (e.g., MSP430, Cortex-M0+), sensor (temperature, humidity), and transmitter (BLE, LoRa). > *Award 1 mark for each correctly explained block; the diagram itself earns 1–2 marks.* **Model Solution for Q.A(b):** **(i) Path Loss in dB:** $$L_p(\text{dB}) = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(R_{\text{km}}) + 20\log_{10}(f_{\text{MHz}})$$ With $R = 3\ \text{m} = 0.003$ km, $f = 2400$ MHz: $$L_p = 32.44 + 20\log_{10}(0.003) + 20\log_{10}(2400)$$ $$L_p = 32.44 + (-50.46) + 67.60 = \boxed{49.58\ \text{dB}}$$ **(ii) Received Power:** Convert $G_r$ from dBi: $G_r = 10^{6/10} = 3.981$ (linear) Apply Friis equation in dB form: $$P_r(\text{dBm}) = P_t(\text{dBm}) + G_t(\text{dBi}) + G_r(\text{dBi}) - L_p(\text{dB})$$ With $P_t = 1$ W $= 30$ dBm, $G_t = 0$ dBi (isotropic), $G_r = 6$ dBi: $$P_r(\text{dBm}) = 30 + 0 + 6 - 49.58 = -13.58\ \text{dBm}$$ Convert to mW: $P_r = 10^{(-13.58/10)} = 10^{-1.358} = 0.0438$ mW $\approx 43.8\ \mu$W $$\boxed{P_r \approx 43.8\ \mu\text{W} = -13.58\ \text{dBm}}$$ --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q. (a)** Explain the working principle of a **Dickson charge pump** voltage multiplier used in RF energy harvesters. Derive the expression for its DC output voltage. *(7 marks, CO4, Bloom: Analyze)* **(b)** Compare the typical power densities available from **TV broadcast, GSM, Wi-Fi, and dedicated RFID reader** sources. Explain why passive UHF RFID is the most commercially successful application of RF energy harvesting. *(7 marks, CO4, Bloom: Understand/Evaluate)* **Model Solution for Q.B(a):** The **Dickson charge pump** is the most widely used rectifier topology in RF energy harvesting and RFID tags. **Working Principle:** - The circuit consists of $n$ identical stages, each containing a **coupling capacitor** and a **Schottky diode**. - The RF input is AC-coupled through $C_1$ to the first diode $D_1$, which rectifies one half-cycle. - The output of stage 1 becomes a DC level shifted by $V_p - V_d$. - This DC level acts as the "AC ground" for the next stage, which adds another $V_p - V_d$ on top. - This cascading process continues, multiplying the DC voltage stage by stage. **Derivation of Output Voltage:** For a single stage, during the negative half-cycle, $C_1$ charges to $V_p$ (peak RF). On the positive half-cycle, the node voltage rises to $2V_p$, and $D_1$ conducts, transferring charge to $C_2$ at voltage $V_p - V_d$. For $n$ stages with no load current, the open-circuit output is: $$V_{DC,oc} = 2n(V_p - V_d)$$ Under load current $I_L$ with per-stage series resistance $R_s$: $$\boxed{V_{DC} = 2n(V_p - V_d) - n I_L R_s}$$ Where: - $V_p$ = peak RF voltage at the input - $V_d$ = forward voltage drop of each diode - $n$ = number of stages - $I_L$ = DC load current - $R_s$ = parasitic series resistance of diodes and capacitors **Design Trade-off:** Increasing $n$ boosts $V_{DC}$ but also increases $R_s$ and leakage, reducing efficiency. Typical designs use $n = 4$ to $n = 10$. > *Award 3 marks for working, 2 for derivation, 2 for the boxed equation and explanation.* **Model Solution for Q.B(b):** **Power Density Comparison:** | Source | Frequency | Power Density | Use Case | |---|---|---|---| | TV Broadcast (UHF) | 470–870 MHz | $0.1$ – $10\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2$ | High ambient power, low frequency | | GSM 900/1800 | 900/1800 MHz | $0.01$ – $1\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2$ | Ubiquitous in cities | | Wi-Fi 2.4/5 GHz | 2.4/5 GHz | $0.001$ – $0.1\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2$ | Indoor, short range | | UHF RFID Reader | 860–960 MHz | $10$ – $100\ \mu\text{W/cm}^2$ | **Controlled, high power** | **Why Passive UHF RFID is Most Successful:** 1. **Controlled power environment** — the reader's CW field is designed to be strong (regulated EIRP up to 4 W), giving reliable energy at 1–10 m. 2. **Standardized protocol (EPC Gen-2)** — global interoperability across vendors. 3. **Low cost** — tag ICs cost < $0.10 in volume, making disposability feasible. 4. **Dickson multiplier integration** — CMOS-compatible charge pumps are easily integrated on-chip. 5. **Diverse use cases** — supply chain, retail, toll collection, livestock tracking, access control — totaling **20+ billion tags/year**. > *Award 4 marks for table/comparison, 3 marks for justification of UHF RFID's success.* --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common mistakes students make in this topic (KTU Board Examiner's perspective):** > 1. **Confusing Friis equation form:** Some students write $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi R)^2$ but plug in $R$ in km or $f$ in GHz without unit conversion. **Always use SI units (m, Hz) inside the formula.** > 2. **Forgetting diode drop:** When asked for Dickson output, omitting $V_d$ costs 2 marks. Always subtract it. > 3. **Mixing dB and linear:** Gains like "6 dBi" must be converted to linear ($10^{0.6} = 3.98$) before substituting into Friis. > 4. **Writing "Rectifier = Antenna":** The antenna and rectifier are **separate** blocks. A rectenna is their *combination*; do not use the words interchangeably. > 5. **Ignoring efficiency:** Don't claim a 100 % RF-to-DC conversion. State realistic η (1–60 %). --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Rectenna = Antenna + Matching + Rectifier + Filter** — these four blocks are mandatory in every answer. - ✅ **Friis equation:** $P_r = P_t G_t G_r (\lambda / 4\pi R)^2$ — the cornerstone formula. - ✅ **Path loss in dB:** $L_p = 32.44 + 20\log R_{\text{km}} + 20\log f_{\text{MHz}}$ — memorize this number **32.44**. - ✅ **Dickson output:** $V_{DC} = 2n(V_p - V_d) - nI_L R_s$ — always subtract diode drop. - ✅ **Lower frequency = better harvesting** for a given distance (because $P_r \propto 1/f^2$). - ✅ **Passive RFID** has no battery and is the **flagship application** of RF harvesting. - ✅ **Schottky diodes** (HSMS-2850, SMS7630) are preferred for their low $V_d$ (~0.2 V). - ✅ **Storage energy:** $E = \frac{1}{2}CV^2$ — derived from capacitor physics. - ✅ **PMU/Boost converter** is essential to step up sub-1 V harvested voltage to usable 1.8/3.3 V. - ✅ **Ambient RF density** is tiny (μW/cm²) — efficiency and ultra-low-power design are critical. - ✅ **EIRP** = $P_t G_t$ is the regulated quantity for RFID readers (typ. 4 W max). - ✅ **Crossover to Module 5:** harvested energy must be *managed* — duty cycling, MPPT, and energy-aware scheduling follow next. ---

topics.title: Powering Wireless SHM Sensor Nodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECIT746 - ENERGY HARVESTING & POWER MANAGEMENT IN IOT > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Powering Wireless SHM Sensor Nodes ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Structural Health Monitoring (SHM)** is a continuous, automated process of damage detection and characterization in engineering structures (bridges, aircraft, pipelines, high-rise buildings, wind turbines, etc.) through the deployment of spatially distributed sensor networks. A **Wireless SHM Sensor Node** is a self-contained, battery-less or battery-assisted embedded measurement unit that performs localized sensing (vibration, strain, acoustic emission, temperature, humidity), local signal processing, and wireless data transmission to a central base station, with energy supplied either by finite storage or by ambient energy harvesting. **Powering Wireless SHM Sensor Nodes** refers to the holistic design discipline that ensures **energy autonomy** and **operational longevity** (typically 10–25 years for civil infrastructure) by combining ambient energy harvesting, ultra-low-power electronics, efficient power management, and intelligent duty cycling. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The emphasis is on the *complete power supply chain* — harvesting mechanism → conditioning → storage → regulation → load management — specifically tuned for the harsh, inaccessible deployment environments of SHM systems. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Doctor Inside the Bridge" Imagine placing a tiny **lifelong doctor** inside a 50-year-old concrete bridge. This doctor: - Must *continuously* monitor the bridge's "heartbeat" (vibration) and "pulse" (strain), - Cannot come out for a battery change (battery replacement is prohibitively expensive), - Must survive 25 years of heat, cold, rain, and vibration, - Has a *self-generating* energy supply from the bridge's own movement. This "doctor" is the wireless SHM sensor node, and the *energy infrastructure that keeps it alive* is what we study under "Powering Wireless SHM Sensor Nodes." > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** The challenge is not just *generating* enough energy (often only µW to mW), but managing it intelligently because the harvested energy is **intermittent, low-magnitude, and unpredictable**. ### 1.3 Why Conventional Batteries Fail for SHM | Constraint | Conventional Battery | SHM Requirement | |-------------|---------------------|-----------------| | Lifetime | 2–5 years | 10–25+ years | | Access | Easy replacement | Inaccessible, hazardous | | Capacity density | High (Wh/kg) | Irrelevant if inaccessible | | Maintenance | Periodic | **Zero maintenance** mandated | | Environmental | Disposal issues | Must be eco-friendly | | Cost over lifetime | Low initially, high recurrent | Must be **zero-recurrent** | This mismatch is the fundamental driver for **energy harvesting–based powering** in SHM nodes. ### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Ambient vibration levels in civil structures:** 0.1 – 10 m/s² (typical); up to 50 m/s² near machinery - **Dominant vibration frequencies in bridges/buildings:** **1 – 100 Hz** (low-frequency, broad-band) - **Available piezoelectric power density:** 10 – 500 µW/cm³ - **Solar irradiance (outdoor):** 100 – 1000 W/m² - **Typical SHM node sleep current:** **< 1 µA** - **Active TX current (BLE/ZigBee):** 5 – 20 mA - **Targeted energy budget per node:** 50 – 500 µW average ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The SHM Sensor Node Power Architecture — Five Functional Blocks A wireless SHM sensor node's power chain consists of **five sequential blocks**: 1. **Energy Harvester (Transducer):** Converts ambient energy (vibration, light, thermal) into electrical energy. 2. **Power Conditioning Circuit (PCC):** Rectification (AC→DC for piezo/EM), voltage regulation, impedance matching, MPPT. 3. **Energy Storage Element (ESE):** Stores excess energy — supercapacitor, thin-film Li-ion, or hybrid. 4. **Power Management Unit (PMU):** Intelligent switching between harvest/store/load modes; cold-start circuitry. 5. **Load (Sensor + MCU + Radio):** The actual sensing, computation, and communication subsystem. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Pattern:** When asked to "explain the power architecture," examiners expect the above five blocks in a *flow diagram* with clear arrows, plus a brief note on the function of each block. This is a **3-mark short answer** in most Part A papers. ### 2.2 Ambient Energy Sources for SHM — Comparative Analysis | Source | Power Density | Availability in SHM | Dominant Use Case | |--------|--------------|---------------------|-------------------| | **Vibration (Piezoelectric)** | 10 – 500 µW/cm³ | High (always present in vibrating structures) | Bridges, buildings, aircraft | | **Vibration (Electromagnetic)** | 1 – 100 µW/cm³ | High | Rotating machinery, pipelines | | **Photovoltaic (Solar)** | 10 – 15 mW/cm² | Daylight only | Outdoor structures | | **Thermoelectric (TEG)** | 10 – 100 µW/cm² | Pipes, engines | Industrial boilers, exhausts | | **RF Harvesting** | 0.1 – 1 µW/cm² | Urban areas | Indoor/short-range nodes | | **Airflow/Wind** | 0.1 – 10 mW/cm² | Exposed sites | Towers, chimneys | > [!NOTE] > For **civil-infrastructure SHM** (bridges, buildings), **piezoelectric vibration harvesting** is the *de facto* standard because structures vibrate 24/7 due to traffic, wind, and seismic micro-tremors. ### 2.3 Piezoelectric Energy Harvesting — Operational Principle A **piezoelectric transducer (PZT, e.g., PZT-5A, PZT-5H, PVDF)** generates voltage when mechanically stressed. The most common SHM configuration is the **cantilever beam with tip mass** tuned to the host structure's dominant frequency. **Energy conversion chain:** $$\text{Mechanical Vibration} \rightarrow \text{Strain in PZT} \rightarrow \text{Charge on electrodes} \rightarrow \text{AC Voltage} \rightarrow \text{Rectified DC}$$ ### 2.4 Electromagnetic Energy Harvesting — Operational Principle A permanent magnet moving through a coil induces an EMF via **Faraday's Law**. Used where rotary or linear motion exists (e.g., traffic-induced bridge vibrations with proper inertial frames). ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units / Notes | |---|---------|----------------|---------------| | 1 | $P_{harv} = \frac{1}{2} m \zeta Y^2 \omega^3$ | Harvested power (cantilever) | m=tip mass, ζ=damping ratio, Y=displacement, ω=angular freq | | 2 | $V_{oc} = g_{33} \cdot t \cdot \sigma$ | Open-circuit piezo voltage | g₃₃=piezo voltage coeff, t=thickness, σ=stress | | 3 | $Q = d_{31} \cdot F \cdot n$ | Generated charge | d₃₁=piezo strain coeff, F=force, n=no. of layers | | 4 | $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$ | Cantilever natural frequency | k=stiffness, m=effective mass | | 5 | $P_{avg} = E_{harv} \cdot \eta_{PCC} - P_{load} \cdot t_{active}/T_{cycle}$ | Energy neutral operation | η = efficiency; T = duty cycle | | 6 | $E_{store} = \frac{1}{2} C V^2$ | Energy in supercapacitor | C=capacitance, V=voltage | | 7 | $V_{out} = N \cdot B \cdot l \cdot v$ | EMF in electromagnetic harvester | N=turns, B=flux density, l=coil length, v=velocity | | 8 | $P_{load} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{sleep} + (V_{DD} \cdot I_{active} \cdot DC)$ | Total load power | DC = duty cycle = t_on/T | | 9 | $E_{budget} = P_{load,avg} \cdot t_{mission}$ | Mission energy requirement | t_mission in seconds | | 10 | $\eta_{MPPT} = P_{out}/P_{in}$ | MPPT efficiency | Typically 70–95% for vibration harvesters | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Formula 5 (energy neutral condition) is the most-tested. Always explicitly show that for *infinite operation*: $E_{harv} \cdot \eta_{PCC} \geq E_{load}$. ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Hong Kong–Zhuhai–Macao Bridge:** 4,000+ piezoelectric vibration harvesters powering SHM nodes. - **Boeing 787 wing spars:** Piezo-powered sensor nodes for fatigue monitoring. - **Offshore wind turbine towers:** Hybrid solar-wind-vibration harvesters. - **Oil & gas pipelines:** Thermoelectric + vibration hybrid for continuous corrosion monitoring. ## 3.
