Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Cantilever Piezoelectric Harvester Power Output **Starting point:** A cantilever beam of length $L$, width $w$, thickness $t_p$ (piezo layer), with tip mass $M$, excited by base acceleration $a(t) = A\omega^2 \sin(\omega t)$. **Step 1 — Equation of motion (base excitation):** The relative displacement $z(t)$ between tip mass and base obeys: $$m\ddot{z} + c\dot{z} + kz = -m\ddot{y}(t)$$ where $\ddot{y}(t) = A\omega^2 \sin(\omega t)$ is the base acceleration. **Step 2 — Steady-state amplitude of relative motion:** For sinusoidal excitation at the natural frequency $\omega_n$, the magnification factor $M_r$ gives the steady-state relative displacement amplitude: $$Z = \frac{A\omega^2}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$ **Step 3 — Power dissipated by structural damping:** The average mechanical power dissipated by the damper over one cycle is: $$P_{mech} = \frac{1}{2} c \omega^2 Z^2$$ **Step 4 — Damping ratio substitution:** Since $c = 2\zeta\sqrt{km} = 2\zeta m \omega_n$, and at resonance $\omega = \omega_n$, the expression simplifies to: $$P_{mech} = \frac{1}{2} (2\zeta m \omega_n)(\omega_n^2) \left(\frac{A}{\omega_n^2 / (2\zeta\omega_n^2)}\right)^2 \cdot \text{(resonance simplification)}$$ After algebraic reduction, the **maximum harvested power at resonance** is: $$P_{max} = \frac{1}{2} m \zeta A^2 \omega_n^3 \cdot \eta_{coupling}$$ where $\eta_{coupling}$ is the electromechanical coupling efficiency (typically 0.2–0.5 for PZT cantilevers). **Step 5 — Simplified final form (the standard KTU result):** $$\boxed{P_{harv} = \frac{1}{2} m \zeta Y^2 \omega_n^3}$$ This is the **canonical SHM harvester power equation** — directly testable in KTU 14-mark problems. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Energy-Neutral Operation Constraint **Given:** A sensor node with average load power $P_{load,avg}$, harvesting at $P_{harv}$ with conditioning efficiency $\eta_{PCC}$, and storage capacity $E_{store,max}$. **Step 1 — Define energy harvested in one duty cycle T:** $$E_{harv} = P_{harv} \cdot \eta_{PCC} \cdot T$$ **Step 2 — Define energy consumed by load in one duty cycle:** $$E_{load} = P_{sleep} \cdot T + (P_{active} - P_{sleep}) \cdot t_{on}$$ where $t_{on}$ is the active sensing + transmission window. **Step 3 — Net energy balance per cycle:** $$\Delta E = E_{harv} - E_{load}$$ **Step 4 — Condition for indefinite operation (energy neutral):** For sustainable operation, the net energy must be non-negative over the long term: $$\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \Delta E_i \geq 0$$ This reduces to the steady-state condition: $$\boxed{P_{harv} \cdot \eta_{PCC} \geq P_{load,avg} = P_{sleep} + DC \cdot (P_{active} - P_{sleep})}$$ where $DC = t_{on}/T$ is the duty cycle. **Step 5 — Maximum sustainable duty cycle:** Rearranging for the duty cycle: $$DC_{max} = \frac{P_{harv}\eta_{PCC} - P_{sleep}}{P_{active} - P_{sleep}}$$ This gives the **maximum percentage of time** the node can be active while still being energy-autonomous. ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Supercapacitor Sizing for a Wireless SHM Node **Given:** Mission duration $t_{mission} = 10$ years, average load $P_{load} = 100 \,\mu W$, no harvesting (worst-case storage-only). **Step 1 — Total mission energy:** $$E_{mission} = P_{load} \cdot t_{mission}$$ Substituting: $$E_{mission} = (100 \times 10^{-6}) \times (10 \times 365.25 \times 24 \times 3600)$$ **Step 2 — Convert to Wh:** $$E_{mission} = 100 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.156 \times 10^8 = 31{,}560 \text{ J} = 8.77 \text{ Wh}$$ **Step 3 — Supercapacitor stored energy:** $$E_{store} = \frac{1}{2} C (V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2)$$ For $V_{max} = 2.7\,V$, $V_{min} = 0.5\,V$ (usable range of typical supercap): $$E_{store} = \frac{1}{2} C (2.7^2 - 0.5^2) = \frac{1}{2} C (7.04)$$ **Step 4 — Required capacitance:** $$C = \frac{2 E_{mission}}{V_{max}^2 - V_{min}^2} = \frac{2 \times 31{,}560}{7.04} = 8{,}966 \text{ F}$$ **Step 5 — Practical interpretation:** This is why **storage-only powering is impractical** for 10-year SHM missions — supercapacitor self-discharge (~5–10% per day) would deplete the node within weeks. **Energy harvesting is mandatory** to *replenish* the storage continuously. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Note:** This derivation is a classic KTU 14-mark question. Always include the *practical conclusion* (why storage alone fails) to earn full marks. ### 3.4 Numerical Example — Designing a Bridge SHM Piezo Node **Problem:** A wireless SHM node on a bridge uses a PZT cantilever harvester. Given: - Tip mass: $m = 5\,g = 5 \times 10^{-3}\,kg$ - Damping ratio: $\zeta = 0.02$ - Vibration amplitude: $Y = 1\,mm = 10^{-3}\,m$ - Natural frequency: $f_n = 25\,Hz$, so $\omega_n = 2\pi(25) = 157.08\,rad/s$ - Coupling efficiency: $\eta = 0.3$ **Solution:** $$P_{harv} = \frac{1}{2} m \zeta Y^2 \omega_n^3$$ $$P_{harv} = \frac{1}{2} \times (5 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.02 \times (10^{-3})^2 \times (157.08)^3$$ Computing stepwise: $$\omega_n^3 = (157.08)^3 = 3.875 \times 10^{6}$$ $$P_{harv} = 0.5 \times 0.005 \times 0.02 \times 10^{-6} \times 3.875 \times 10^{6}$$ $$P_{harv} = 0.5 \times 0.005 \times 0.02 \times 3.875$$ $$P_{harv} = 1.9375 \times 10^{-4}\,W = 193.75\,\mu W$$ After conditioning ($\eta_{PCC} = 0.7$): $$P_{avail} = 193.75 \times 0.7 = 135.6\,\mu W$$ **Conclusion:** This is sufficient to power a typical SHM node (50–100 µW average). ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Complete Power Architecture of a Wireless SHM Sensor Node ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Ambient Energy Source
Vibration, Light, Thermal, RF] --> B[Energy Harvester
PZT, EM, PV, TEG] B --> C[Power Conditioning
Rectifier, MPPT, Impedance Match] C --> D[Energy Storage
Supercapacitor / Thin-Film Battery] D --> E[Power Management Unit
PMU with Cold Start] E --> F1[Sensing Subsystem
Accelerometer, Strain Gauge] E --> F2[Processing Unit
Low-Power MCU] E --> F3[Wireless Radio
BLE / ZigBee / LoRa] F1 --> G[Central Base Station
via Wireless Link] F2 --> G F3 --> G H[External Trigger
Wake-up Signal] --> E ``` ### 4.2 State Machine: PMU Power Management States ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> SLEEP SLEEP --> HARVEST : Vibration detected HARVEST --> STORE : Vstore below Vmax HARVEST --> REGULATE : Vstore above Vmax STORE --> REGULATE : Energy threshold reached REGULATE --> ACTIVE : Vstore exceeds Vthreshold ACTIVE --> SLEEP : After duty cycle window ACTIVE --> PROTECT : Vstore drops below Vmin PROTECT --> SLEEP : Recovery REGULATE --> HARVEST : Continuous replenishment ``` ### 4.3 Detailed Cantilever Piezoelectric Harvester Schematic ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Base Excitation
Bridge Vibration] --> B[Cantilever Beam
PZT-5A Layer] B --> C[Tip Mass
Tuning Element] B --> D[Electrodes
Top and Bottom] D --> E[Full-Bridge Rectifier
Schottky Diodes] E --> F[Smoothing Capacitor] F --> G[MPPT Controller
P&O Algorithm] G --> H[Supercapacitor Bank
1F to 10F] H --> I[Voltage Regulator
LDO / Buck-Boost] I --> J[Sensor Node Load
3.3V / 1.8V Rails] K[Wake-up Timer
Ultra-Low Power] --> J ``` ### 4.4 Energy Flow Analysis Chart ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Input Power
P_in: 1000 µW] -->|Harvest| P2[PZT Conversion
η1: 30%] P2 -->|Rectification| P3[Rectifier Loss
η2: 85%] P3 -->|MPPT Loss| P4[MPPT Efficiency
η3: 90%] P4 -->|Storage Loss| P5[Supercap Self-Discharge
η4: 95%/day] P5 -->|Regulation| P6[LDO Efficiency
η5: 80%] P6 --> P7[Useful Output
P_out: ~175 µW] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Cascading efficiency formula:** $\eta_{total} = \eta_1 \times \eta_2 \times \eta_3 \times \eta_4 \times \eta_5$. This is a common KTU sub-question in 14-mark problems. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### Part A — 3-Mark Questions **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** *List any three reasons why conventional batteries are unsuitable for powering wireless SHM sensor nodes deployed in remote civil infrastructure.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Limited operational lifetime** (2–5 years) compared to the 10–25 year service life of structures, requiring expensive and physically difficult battery replacements. 2. **Inaccessibility of deployment sites** — bridges, high-rise buildings, offshore platforms, and inside aircraft wings make manual battery swaps hazardous and economically unviable. 3. **Environmental concerns** — battery leakage can damage the host structure and cause ecological pollution, especially in large-scale deployments with thousands of nodes. *(1 mark for each valid point; 3 points required for full marks)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** *Define "energy neutral operation" of a wireless SHM sensor node.* **Model Answer (3 marks):** Energy neutral operation is the steady-state condition in which the **average electrical energy harvested from the ambient environment**, after accounting for the efficiency of the power conditioning circuitry, is **greater than or equal to the average energy consumed** by the sensor, processing, and communication subsystems over a complete duty cycle. Mathematically: $$P_{harv} \cdot \eta_{PCC} \geq P_{load,avg}$$ When this condition is satisfied over the long term, the storage element neither depletes nor saturates, ensuring **perpetual, maintenance-free operation** of the node. *(2 marks for definition + equation, 1 mark for perpetual operation implication)* --- ### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- #### ✅ Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model] **(a)** Draw the complete block diagram of a wireless SHM sensor node's power supply chain and explain the function of each block. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A PZT cantilever harvester for a bridge SHM node has the following specifications: - Tip mass $m = 8\,g$, damping ratio $\zeta = 0.025$, vibration displacement $Y = 0.8\,mm$, natural frequency $f_n = 30\,Hz$, electromechanical coupling efficiency $\eta = 0.35$, rectifier + MPPT efficiency $\eta_{PCC} = 0.75$. - Calculate: (i) Harvested mechanical power, (ii) Net DC power available to the load, (iii) Comment on whether this is sufficient for a typical SHM node requiring 120 µW average. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** | Block | Function | |-------|----------| | 1. Energy Harvester | Converts ambient vibration/light/thermal energy to electrical form (PZT, PV, TEG) | | 2. Power Conditioning | Rectifies AC, performs impedance matching, MPPT for maximum transfer | | 3. Energy Storage | Buffers intermittent energy (supercapacitor, thin-film battery) | | 4. Power Management Unit | Controls charge/discharge, cold-start, voltage regulation, duty cycling | | 5. Load Subsystem | Sensors + MCU + Radio — actual SHM function | *(1 mark per block + 2 marks for diagram = 7 marks)* **Model Solution (b):** **(i) Harvested mechanical power:** $$\omega_n = 2\pi f_n = 2\pi \times 30 = 188.5\,rad/s$$ $$\omega_n^3 = 188.5^3 = 6.696 \times 10^{6}\,rad^3/s^3$$ $$P_{mech} = \frac{1}{2} \times (8 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.025 \times (0.8 \times 10^{-3})^2 \times 6.696 \times 10^{6}$$ $$P_{mech} = 0.5 \times 0.008 \times 0.025 \times 6.4 \times 10^{-7} \times 6.696 \times 10^{6}$$ $$P_{mech} = 0.5 \times 0.008 \times 0.025 \times 4.286$$ $$P_{mech} = 4.286 \times 10^{-4}\,W = 428.6\,\mu W$$ After electromechanical coupling: $$P_{elec} = 428.6 \times 0.35 = 150\,\mu W \quad \text{...(i) 3 marks}$$ **(ii) Net DC power available:** $$P_{DC} = P_{elec} \times \eta_{PCC} = 150 \times 0.75 = 112.5\,\mu W \quad \text{...(ii) 2 marks}$$ **(iii) Comparison with 120 µW requirement:** $P_{DC} = 112.5\,\mu W < 120\,\mu W$. Therefore, the harvester is **insufficient by 7.5 µW** under current design parameters. **Recommendations:** (a) increase tip mass, (b) increase displacement, (c) optimize coupling, or (d) supplement with a secondary harvester (e.g., solar). *(2 marks)* --- #### ✅ Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] (Alternative Choice) **(a)** Compare piezoelectric, electromagnetic, and photovoltaic energy harvesting techniques for SHM applications. Discuss the dominant choice for civil-infrastructure monitoring with justification. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Derive the energy-neutral operation condition for a duty-cycled wireless SHM node, clearly defining all terms. If the harvested power is $P_{harv} = 300\,\mu W$, $\eta_{PCC} = 0.8$, $P_{sleep} = 1\,\mu W$, $P_{active} = 10\,mW$, find the maximum sustainable duty cycle. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution (a):** | Parameter | Piezoelectric | Electromagnetic | Photovoltaic | |-----------|--------------|-----------------|--------------| | Source | Vibration strain | Motion + magnetic field | Light | | Output | AC voltage | AC voltage | DC current | | Power density | 10–500 µW/cm³ | 1–100 µW/cm³ | 10–15 mW/cm² | | Indoor suitability | Excellent | Good | Poor | | 24/7 availability | Yes | Yes | No (daylight only) | | Frequency match | Tunable to structure | Tunable | N/A | | Size for same power | Smallest | Medium | Largest | **Dominant choice for civil SHM:** **Piezoelectric** because: 1. Civil structures vibrate continuously due to traffic, wind, micro-seismic activity — *guaranteed 24/7 source*. 2. Harvesters can be *frequency-tuned* to structural resonance using tip mass adjustment. 3. Compatible with MEMS fabrication, enabling *scalable, low-cost* deployment of thousands of nodes. 4. No dependence on weather/daylight unlike PV. *(4 marks for table + 3 marks for justification = 7 marks)* **Model Solution (b):** **Derivation:** For a duty cycle $DC = t_{on}/T$ where $T$ is the full cycle period and $t_{on}$ is the active duration: Average power consumed: $$P_{load,avg} = P_{sleep} \cdot (1 - DC) + P_{active} \cdot DC$$ Energy harvested per cycle: $$E_{harv} = P_{harv} \cdot \eta_{PCC} \cdot T$$ Energy consumed per cycle: $$E_{load} = P_{sleep}(T - t_{on}) + P_{active} \cdot t_{on} = P_{sleep} \cdot T + (P_{active} - P_{sleep}) t_{on}$$ For energy neutral operation: $E_{harv} \geq E_{load}$: $$P_{harv} \cdot \eta_{PCC} \cdot T \geq P_{sleep} \cdot T + (P_{active} - P_{sleep}) t_{on}$$ Dividing by T: $$P_{harv} \cdot \eta_{PCC} \geq P_{sleep} + (P_{active} - P_{sleep}) \cdot DC$$ Solving for $DC$: $$DC_{max} = \frac{P_{harv} \cdot \eta_{PCC} - P_{sleep}}{P_{active} - P_{sleep}}$$ *(3 marks for derivation)* **Numerical substitution:** $$DC_{max} = \frac{(300 \times 10^{-6}) \times 0.8 - (1 \times 10^{-6})}{(10 \times 10^{-3}) - (1 \times 10^{-6})}$$ $$DC_{max} = \frac{240 \times 10^{-6} - 1 \times 10^{-6}}{9.999 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$DC_{max} = \frac{239 \times 10^{-6}}{9.999 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0239 = 2.39\%$$ *(4 marks for calculation — show every step)* **Conclusion:** The node can be active for only ~2.39% of the time while remaining energy-autonomous. --- ### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — vibration amplitude must be in **meters** in the SI version of the formula. mm → m conversion is missed by 60% of students. > 2. **Mixing RMS and peak values** — $Y$ in the power formula is the **peak** displacement, not RMS. > 3. **Cubic frequency dependence** — Many students incorrectly use $\omega$ instead of $\omega^3$. A 10 Hz vs 100 Hz error changes power by **1000×**. > 4. **Neglecting cascaded efficiencies** — Always multiply efficiencies: $\eta_{total} = \eta_{rect} \times \eta_{MPPT} \times \eta_{reg}$. Partial credit is given for the right structure. > 5. **In duty-cycle problems, forgetting $P_{sleep}$** — Even during "off" time, the node consumes µA-level sleep current. This term *cannot* be dropped. > 6. **Diagram omissions** — When asked for a "block diagram," examiners expect **labeled arrows** showing signal/power flow direction. A diagram without arrows loses 2 of 7 marks. --- ### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ SHM = Structural Health Monitoring; nodes must operate **10–25 years** without maintenance. - ✅ Power chain = **Harvester → Conditioning → Storage → PMU → Load** (5 blocks, memorize in order). - ✅ **Piezoelectric** vibration harvesting is the **dominant choice** for civil SHM (24/7 availability, tunable, scalable). - ✅ Canonical harvester power: $P_{harv} = \frac{1}{2} m \zeta Y^2 \omega_n^3$ — *memorize the formula, the cubic $\omega$ dependence, and the units*. - ✅ Energy neutral condition: $P_{harv} \cdot \eta_{PCC} \geq P_{sleep} + DC(P_{active} - P_{sleep})$. - ✅ Total system efficiency = **product** of cascaded stage efficiencies (rectifier × MPPT × regulator). - ✅ Storage-only powering is **impractical** for long missions due to supercapacitor self-discharge (~5–10%/day). - ✅ **Duty cycling** is the *single most important* power-saving strategy in SHM nodes (target DC < 1%). - ✅ Hybrid harvesting (piezo + solar, piezo + thermal) is increasingly used in modern SHM deployments. - ✅ Always include the **practical engineering conclusion** in numerical answers — it is worth 1–2 marks. - ✅ PMU must implement **cold-start circuitry** (harvested voltage is often < 0.5 V initially, must be boosted to usable 2.5–3.3 V). - ✅ Cantilever natural frequency: $f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ — tune by adjusting tip mass $m$ to match host structure's dominant frequency.
